*Jinze Zhang,Jian Zhong,Li Lin,Jiaxiong Li,Ke Ma,Naiyang Li,Meng Li,Yuan Pan,Zeyu Meng,Mengyun Zhou,Shang Huang,Shilong Yu,Zhengyu Duan,Sutong Li,Honghui Xia,Juping Liu,Dan Liang,Yantao Wei,Xiaoying Tang,Jin Yuan,Peng Xiao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Foundation-model-driven end-to-end OCT diagnosis (FOCUS) automates 3D OCT interpretation across quality, abnormality, and disease classification, achieving near-expert performance with strong cross-center validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome multi-stage, single-slice AI limitations and enable autonomous, scalable retinal diagnosis from 3D OCT volumes.

Method: Sequential pipeline: (1) EfficientNetV2-S for image quality assessment; (2) fine-tuned Vision Foundation Model for abnormality detection and multi-disease classification; (3) unified adaptive aggregation to fuse 2D slice predictions into 3D patient-level diagnoses. Training on 3,300 patients (40,672 slices) with external validation on 1,345 patients (18,498 slices) across four-tier centers and diverse OCT devices.

Result: High performance on internal tests: quality 99.01% F1, abnormality 97.46% F1, patient-level 94.39% F1. Real-world validation: 90.22–95.24% F1. In human-machine comparison, F1: abnormality 95.47% (FOCUS) vs 90.91% (expert), multi-disease 93.49% vs 91.35%, with better efficiency.

Conclusion: FOCUS demonstrates a practical, scalable blueprint for autonomous OCT-based retinal screening, achieving end-to-end automation with strong cross-center generalization; highlights the promise of foundation-model-driven clinical pipelines while implying deployment considerations.

Abstract: Optical coherence tomography (OCT) has revolutionized retinal disease diagnosis with its high-resolution and three-dimensional imaging nature, yet its full diagnostic automation in clinical practices remains constrained by multi-stage workflows and conventional single-slice single-task AI models. We present Full-process OCT-based Clinical Utility System (FOCUS), a foundation model-driven framework enabling end-to-end automation of 3D OCT retinal disease diagnosis. FOCUS sequentially performs image quality assessment with EfficientNetV2-S, followed by abnormality detection and multi-disease classification using a fine-tuned Vision Foundation Model. Crucially, FOCUS leverages a unified adaptive aggregation method to intelligently integrate 2D slices-level predictions into comprehensive 3D patient-level diagnosis. Trained and tested on 3,300 patients (40,672 slices), and externally validated on 1,345 patients (18,498 slices) across four different-tier centers and diverse OCT devices, FOCUS achieved high F1 scores for quality assessment (99.01%), abnormally detection (97.46%), and patient-level diagnosis (94.39%). Real-world validation across centers also showed stable performance (F1: 90.22%-95.24%). In human-machine comparisons, FOCUS matched expert performance in abnormality detection (F1: 95.47% vs 90.91%) and multi-disease diagnosis (F1: 93.49% vs 91.35%), while demonstrating better efficiency. FOCUS automates the image-to-diagnosis pipeline, representing a critical advance towards unmanned ophthalmology with a validated blueprint for autonomous screening to enhance population scale retinal care accessibility and efficiency.

</details>


### [57] [PQTNet: Pixel-wise Quantitative Thermography Neural Network for Estimating Defect Depth in Polylactic Acid Parts by Additive Manufacturing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03314)
*Lei Deng,Wenhao Huang,Chao Yang,Haoyuan Zheng,Yinbin Tian,Yue Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PQT-Net uses an EfficientNetV2-S backbone with a Residual Regression Head to estimate per-pixel defect depth in PLA AM parts. It introduces a novel 2D stripe representation of time-series thermography data for full temporal heat diffusion, achieving MAE 0.0094 mm and R>0.99, outperforming other DL models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Defect depth quantification in additively manufactured components remains challenging for non-destructive testing; there is a need for precise, per-pixel quantitative defect characterization, particularly for PLA parts.

Method: A Pixel-wise Quantitative Thermography Neural Network (PQT-Net) with a pre-trained EfficientNetV2-S backbone and a custom Residual Regression Head (RRH). A novel data augmentation converts thermal sequence data into 2D stripe images that preserve the complete temporal evolution of heat diffusion for each pixel; comparative experiments against other deep learning models.

Result: PQT-Net achieves a minimum MAE of 0.0094 mm and a coefficient of determination R > 99%, outperforming competing models in quantitative defect depth estimation for AM PLA parts.

Conclusion: PQT-Net demonstrates high-precision, per-pixel defect depth quantification in additive manufacturing, indicating strong potential for robust quantitative NDT of AM components and suggesting effectiveness of the 2D stripe thermography representation and RRH-based refinement.

Abstract: Defect depth quantification in additively manufactured (AM) components remains a significant challenge for non-destructive testing (NDT). This study proposes a Pixel-wise Quantitative Thermography Neural Network (PQT-Net) to address this challenge for polylactic acid (PLA) parts. A key innovation is a novel data augmentation strategy that reconstructs thermal sequence data into two-dimensional stripe images, preserving the complete temporal evolution of heat diffusion for each pixel. The PQT-Net architecture incorporates a pre-trained EfficientNetV2-S backbone and a custom Residual Regression Head (RRH) with learnable parameters to refine outputs. Comparative experiments demonstrate the superiority of PQT-Net over other deep learning models, achieving a minimum Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 0.0094 mm and a coefficient of determination (R) exceeding 99%. The high precision of PQT-Net underscores its potential for robust quantitative defect characterization in AM.

</details>


### [58] [Invisible Clean-Label Backdoor Attacks for Generative Data Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03316)
*Ting Xiang,Jinhui Zhao,Changjian Chen,Zhuo Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: InvLBA presents an invisible latent-space clean-label backdoor attack on generative data augmentation, achieving higher attack success and robustness with theoretical generalization guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative data augmentation is vulnerable to clean-label backdoors; pixel-level attacks on generated images underperform; need latent-space triggers to preserve invisibility and attack efficacy.

Method: Proposes InvLBA: perturb latent features of generative models to embed backdoor triggers at the latent level; provides theoretical generalization guarantees for clean accuracy and attack success; evaluates across multiple datasets; robust against state-of-the-art defenses.

Result: Achieves an average 46.43% higher attack success rate; minimal reduction in clean accuracy; strong robustness against SOTA defenses; empirical validation on multiple datasets; theoretical bounds on generalization.

Conclusion: Latent perturbations in generative augmentation offer a potent, invisible backdoor channel with solid theoretical guarantees; highlights need for defense strategies targeting latent spaces and generative pipelines.

Abstract: With the rapid advancement of image generative models, generative data augmentation has become an effective way to enrich training images, especially when only small-scale datasets are available. At the same time, in practical applications, generative data augmentation can be vulnerable to clean-label backdoor attacks, which aim to bypass human inspection. However, based on theoretical analysis and preliminary experiments, we observe that directly applying existing pixel-level clean-label backdoor attack methods (e.g., COMBAT) to generated images results in low attack success rates. This motivates us to move beyond pixel-level triggers and focus instead on the latent feature level. To this end, we propose InvLBA, an invisible clean-label backdoor attack method for generative data augmentation by latent perturbation. We theoretically prove that the generalization of the clean accuracy and attack success rates of InvLBA can be guaranteed. Experiments on multiple datasets show that our method improves the attack success rate by 46.43% on average, with almost no reduction in clean accuracy and high robustness against SOTA defense methods.

</details>


### [59] [MedSAM-Agent: Empowering Interactive Medical Image Segmentation with Multi-turn Agentic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03320)
*Shengyuan Liu,Liuxin Bao,Qi Yang,Wanting Geng,Boyun Zheng,Chenxin Li,Wenting Chen,Houwen Peng,Yixuan Yuan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MedSAM-Agent reframes interactive medical image segmentation as a multi-step autonomous decision-making process, using hybrid prompting and a two-stage training regime with outcome verification and clinically faithful rewards to achieve state-of-the-art results across diverse modalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current autonomous segmentation via MLLMs relies on single-turn, rigid interactions and lacks process-level supervision, hindering adaptive tool use and leading to redundant actions; a multi-turn, supervised framework is needed to exploit interactive tools and improve efficiency.

Method: Hybrid prompting for expert-curated trajectory generation; two-stage training with multi-turn end-to-end outcome verification and a clinical-fidelity process reward design to promote interaction parsimony and decision efficiency.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across 6 medical modalities and 21 datasets; demonstrates effective unification of autonomous medical reasoning with iterative optimization.

Conclusion: MedSAM-Agent provides a scalable framework that couples autonomous reasoning with iterative, clinically faithful optimization, reducing redundant interactions and improving segmentation performance.

Abstract: Medical image segmentation is evolving from task-specific models toward generalizable frameworks. Recent research leverages Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) as autonomous agents, employing reinforcement learning with verifiable reward (RLVR) to orchestrate specialized tools like the Segment Anything Model (SAM). However, these approaches often rely on single-turn, rigid interaction strategies and lack process-level supervision during training, which hinders their ability to fully exploit the dynamic potential of interactive tools and leads to redundant actions. To bridge this gap, we propose MedSAM-Agent, a framework that reformulates interactive segmentation as a multi-step autonomous decision-making process. First, we introduce a hybrid prompting strategy for expert-curated trajectory generation, enabling the model to internalize human-like decision heuristics and adaptive refinement strategies. Furthermore, we develop a two-stage training pipeline that integrates multi-turn, end-to-end outcome verification with a clinical-fidelity process reward design to promote interaction parsimony and decision efficiency. Extensive experiments across 6 medical modalities and 21 datasets demonstrate that MedSAM-Agent achieves state-of-the-art performance, effectively unifying autonomous medical reasoning with robust, iterative optimization. Code is available \href{https://github.com/CUHK-AIM-Group/MedSAM-Agent}{here}.

</details>


### [60] [PWAVEP: Purifying Imperceptible Adversarial Perturbations in 3D Point Clouds via Spectral Graph Wavelets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03333)
*Haoran Li,Renyang Liu,Hongjia Liu,Chen Wang,Long Yin,Jian Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A plug-and-play, non-invasive spectral-domain defense for 3D point clouds that purifies data by saliency-guided removal of highly adversarial points and graph-wavelet filtering of moderately salient points, improving robustness without model modification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adversarial attacks on 3D point clouds threaten recognition accuracy; defenders need effective, non-invasive methods that do not require retraining, invasive modifications, or auxiliary data access.

Method: Compute spectral graph wavelet domain saliency and local sparsity scores per point. Use a hierarchical scheme to remove the most salient (hardly recoverable) adversarial outliers and apply a graph-wavelet-based spectral filter to attenuate high-frequency components on a broader set of moderately salient points, thereby suppressing adversarial noise.

Result: Extensive evaluations show PWAVEP achieves superior accuracy and robustness compared to existing purification approaches, advancing state-of-the-art in 3D point cloud purification. Code is provided at the authors' repository.

Conclusion: PWAVEP offers a non-invasive, plug-and-play purification framework in the spectral domain that effectively neutralizes adversarial perturbations on 3D point clouds through saliency-guided point removal and spectral filtering.

Abstract: Recent progress in adversarial attacks on 3D point clouds, particularly in achieving spatial imperceptibility and high attack performance, presents significant challenges for defenders. Current defensive approaches remain cumbersome, often requiring invasive model modifications, expensive training procedures or auxiliary data access. To address these threats, in this paper, we propose a plug-and-play and non-invasive defense mechanism in the spectral domain, grounded in a theoretical and empirical analysis of the relationship between imperceptible perturbations and high-frequency spectral components. Building upon these insights, we introduce a novel purification framework, termed PWAVEP, which begins by computing a spectral graph wavelet domain saliency score and local sparsity score for each point. Guided by these values, PWAVEP adopts a hierarchical strategy, it eliminates the most salient points, which are identified as hardly recoverable adversarial outliers. Simultaneously, it applies a spectral filtering process to a broader set of moderately salient points. This process leverages a graph wavelet transform to attenuate high-frequency coefficients associated with the targeted points, thereby effectively suppressing adversarial noise. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that the proposed PWAVEP achieves superior accuracy and robustness compared to existing approaches, advancing the state-of-the-art in 3D point cloud purification. Code and datasets are available at https://github.com/a772316182/pwavep

</details>


### [61] [Composable Visual Tokenizers with Generator-Free Diagnostics of Learnability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03339)
*Bingchen Zhao,Qiushan Guo,Ye Wang,Yixuan Huang,Zhonghua Zhai,Yu Tian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CompTok learns compositional visual tokenizers via a token-conditioned diffusion decoder, enforced by an InfoGAN-style objective and token-swapping with adversarial flow regularization, achieving state-of-the-art class-conditioned generation and enabling semantic editing through token manipulation; also introduces two token-space metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To yield interpretable, compositional visual tokens that enable flexible, controllable image generation and editing, addressing the difficulty of learning with standard tokenizers and enabling token-based manipulation.

Method: Train a token-conditioned diffusion decoder; apply InfoGAN-style objective to force token usage; augment training with swapped token subsets across images to promote compositional control; use an adversarial flow regularizer to keep swapped generations on the natural image manifold; introduce two metrics to characterize token-space landscape.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on class-conditioned image generation; enables high-level semantic edits via token swapping; improves the proposed token-space metrics; supports SOTA generators on class-conditioned generation.

Conclusion: CompTok provides a compositional tokenization framework that improves controllability and editability in diffusion-based generation; the metrics offer a quantitative lens into token-space structure; potential trade-offs include training complexity and reliance on adversarial regularization.

Abstract: We introduce CompTok, a training framework for learning visual tokenizers whose tokens are enhanced for compositionality. CompTok uses a token-conditioned diffusion decoder. By employing an InfoGAN-style objective, where we train a recognition model to predict the tokens used to condition the diffusion decoder using the decoded images, we enforce the decoder to not ignore any of the tokens. To promote compositional control, besides the original images, CompTok also trains on tokens formed by swapping token subsets between images, enabling more compositional control of the token over the decoder. As the swapped tokens between images do not have ground truth image targets, we apply a manifold constraint via an adversarial flow regularizer to keep unpaired swap generations on the natural-image distribution. The resulting tokenizer not only achieves state-of-the-art performance on image class-conditioned generation, but also demonstrates properties such as swapping tokens between images to achieve high level semantic editing of an image. Additionally, we propose two metrics that measures the landscape of the token space that can be useful to describe not only the compositionality of the tokens, but also how easy to learn the landscape is for a generator to be trained on this space. We show in experiments that CompTok can improve on both of the metrics as well as supporting state-of-the-art generators for class conditioned generation.

</details>


### [62] [Tiled Prompts: Overcoming Prompt Underspecification in Image and Video Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03342)
*Bryan Sangwoo Kim,Jonghyun Park,Jong Chul Ye*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A per-tile prompt framework (Tiled Prompts) for image/video super-resolution that assigns a distinct prompt to each latent tile during diffusion-based SR, addressing prompt underspecification and misguidance from global prompts and improving perceptual quality, text alignment, and reducing hallucinations and tile artifacts with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global captions are too coarse for high-resolution SR: they underspecify localized details and can misguide diffusion guidance, especially under classifier-free guidance, leading to artifacts and hallucinations.

Method: Generate tile-specific prompts for each latent tile and perform super-resolution under locally text-conditioned posteriors, enabling high-information, localized guidance with minimal overhead; unifies image and video SR.

Result: Experiments on high-resolution real-world images and videos show consistent gains in perceptual quality and text alignment, with reduced hallucinations and tile-level artifacts compared with global-prompt baselines.

Conclusion: Tiled Prompts offer a unified, scalable framework for text-conditioned SR that mitigates prompt sparsity and misguidance while delivering higher-quality, better-aligned outputs.

Abstract: Text-conditioned diffusion models have advanced image and video super-resolution by using prompts as semantic priors, but modern super-resolution pipelines typically rely on latent tiling to scale to high resolutions, where a single global caption causes prompt underspecification. A coarse global prompt often misses localized details (prompt sparsity) and provides locally irrelevant guidance (prompt misguidance) that can be amplified by classifier-free guidance. We propose Tiled Prompts, a unified framework for image and video super-resolution that generates a tile-specific prompt for each latent tile and performs super-resolution under locally text-conditioned posteriors, providing high-information guidance that resolves prompt underspecification with minimal overhead. Experiments on high resolution real-world images and videos show consistent gains in perceptual quality and text alignment, while reducing hallucinations and tile-level artifacts relative to global-prompt baselines.

</details>


### [63] [Z3D: Zero-Shot 3D Visual Grounding from Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03361)
*Nikita Drozdov,Andrey Lemeshko,Nikita Gavrilov,Anton Konushin,Danila Rukhovich,Maksim Kolodiazhnyi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Z3D is a flexible zero-shot 3D visual grounding pipeline that localizes 3D objects from multi-view images using high-quality 3D bounding proposals from zero-shot segmentation and prompt-based reasoning with VLMs; it achieves state-of-the-art among zero-shot methods on ScanRefer and Nr3D, with code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zero-shot 3D visual grounding from multi-view imagery lacks geometric supervision and object priors. The study identifies bottlenecks in prior zero-shot methods and aims to close the gap by combining strong zero-shot 3D proposals with advanced VLM-driven reasoning.

Method: Introduce Z3D, a universal grounding pipeline that operates on multi-view images (optionally using camera poses and depth maps). It comprises (i) a state-of-the-art zero-shot 3D instance segmentation stage to generate high-quality 3D bounding box proposals, and (ii) prompt-based segmentation leveraging full VLM capabilities for language-driven reasoning to select targets from proposals.

Result: On ScanRefer and Nr3D benchmarks, Z3D achieves state-of-the-art performance among zero-shot methods, validating the effectiveness of combining zero-shot 3D proposals with prompt-based, VLM-driven reasoning.

Conclusion: Z3D provides a flexible, high-performance zero-shot 3D visual grounding solution that works with multi-view imagery and optional geometric cues, establishing a new zero-shot baseline and offering publicly available code.

Abstract: 3D visual grounding (3DVG) aims to localize objects in a 3D scene based on natural language queries. In this work, we explore zero-shot 3DVG from multi-view images alone, without requiring any geometric supervision or object priors. We introduce Z3D, a universal grounding pipeline that flexibly operates on multi-view images while optionally incorporating camera poses and depth maps. We identify key bottlenecks in prior zero-shot methods causing significant performance degradation and address them with (i) a state-of-the-art zero-shot 3D instance segmentation method to generate high-quality 3D bounding box proposals and (ii) advanced reasoning via prompt-based segmentation, which utilizes full capabilities of modern VLMs. Extensive experiments on the ScanRefer and Nr3D benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance among zero-shot methods. Code is available at https://github.com/col14m/z3d .

</details>


### [64] [Symbol-Aware Reasoning with Masked Discrete Diffusion for Handwritten Mathematical Expression Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03370)
*Takaya Kawakatsu,Ryo Ishiyama*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A discrete diffusion framework for HMER reframes the task as iterative symbolic refinement via multi-step remasking, improving structural consistency and robustness; achieves state-of-the-art results on MathWriting and CROHME.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive HMER models suffer exposure bias and syntactic inconsistencies when handling diverse 2D symbol layouts; a non-autoregressive iterative approach that enforces structural consistency is desirable.

Method: Discrete diffusion with multi-step remasking; progressive refinement of both symbols and structural relations; symbol-aware tokenization; Random-Masking Mutual Learning; decouples dependencies and leverages diffusion steps to refine symbols and their 2D structure.

Result: MathWriting: CER 5.56%, EM 60.42% outperforming strong Transformer and commercial baselines; consistent gains on CROHME 2014--2023; demonstrates robustness and generalization of the approach.

Conclusion: Discrete diffusion provides a new paradigm for structure-aware visual recognition beyond generative modeling and can better handle diverse handwriting styles and complex 2D structures.

Abstract: Handwritten Mathematical Expression Recognition (HMER) requires reasoning over diverse symbols and 2D structural layouts, yet autoregressive models struggle with exposure bias and syntactic inconsistency. We present a discrete diffusion framework that reformulates HMER as iterative symbolic refinement instead of sequential generation. Through multi-step remasking, the proposal progressively refines both symbols and structural relations, removing causal dependencies and improving structural consistency. A symbol-aware tokenization and Random-Masking Mutual Learning further enhance syntactic alignment and robustness to handwriting diversity. On the MathWriting benchmark, the proposal achieves 5.56\% CER and 60.42\% EM, outperforming strong Transformer and commercial baselines. Consistent gains on CROHME 2014--2023 demonstrate that discrete diffusion provides a new paradigm for structure-aware visual recognition beyond generative modeling.

</details>


### [65] [Multi-Resolution Alignment for Voxel Sparsity in Camera-Based 3D Semantic Scene Completion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03371)
*Zhiwen Yang,Yuxin Peng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Multi-Resolution Alignment (MRA) to alleviate voxel sparsity in camera-based 3D semantic scene completion by introducing three auxiliary supervision modules: Multi-resolution View Transformer for cross-resolution 3D feature alignment, Cubic Semantic Anisotropy for voxel-level instance significance within cubic neighborhoods, and Critical Distribution Alignment to enforce cross-resolution feature distribution consistency via a circulated loss. Code available at GitHub.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Voxel sparsity and voxel-label-only supervision hinder optimization efficiency and performance in camera-based 3D semantic scene completion; there is a need for auxiliary, instance-aware supervision that leverages multi-resolution 3D features.

Method: 1) Multi-resolution View Transformer projects 2D image features into multi-resolution 3D features and performs scene-level alignment using discriminative seed features. 2) Cubic Semantic Anisotropy computes instance-level semantic significance of each voxel by comparing its semantics with neighboring voxels within a cubic neighborhood. 3) Critical Distribution Alignment selects critical voxels as instance-level anchors guided by anisotropy and applies a circulated loss to enforce feature distribution consistency across resolutions.

Result: The framework aims to mitigate voxel sparsity and improve optimization efficiency and model performance through auxiliary supervision across multi-resolution features and instance-aware guidance; experimental results are not provided in the abstract, but code is released at the referenced GitHub repository.

Conclusion: The proposed Multi-Resolution Alignment (MRA) offers a scalable solution for camera-based SSC by integrating scene- and instance-level alignment across multi-resolution 3D features and a distribution-alignment mechanism to better leverage voxels in sparse scenes.

Abstract: Camera-based 3D semantic scene completion (SSC) offers a cost-effective solution for assessing the geometric occupancy and semantic labels of each voxel in the surrounding 3D scene with image inputs, providing a voxel-level scene perception foundation for the perception-prediction-planning autonomous driving systems. Although significant progress has been made in existing methods, their optimization rely solely on the supervision from voxel labels and face the challenge of voxel sparsity as a large portion of voxels in autonomous driving scenarios are empty, which limits both optimization efficiency and model performance. To address this issue, we propose a \textit{Multi-Resolution Alignment (MRA)} approach to mitigate voxel sparsity in camera-based 3D semantic scene completion, which exploits the scene and instance level alignment across multi-resolution 3D features as auxiliary supervision. Specifically, we first propose the Multi-resolution View Transformer module, which projects 2D image features into multi-resolution 3D features and aligns them at the scene level through fusing discriminative seed features. Furthermore, we design the Cubic Semantic Anisotropy module to identify the instance-level semantic significance of each voxel, accounting for the semantic differences of a specific voxel against its neighboring voxels within a cubic area. Finally, we devise a Critical Distribution Alignment module, which selects critical voxels as instance-level anchors with the guidance of cubic semantic anisotropy, and applies a circulated loss for auxiliary supervision on the critical feature distribution consistency across different resolutions. The code is available at https://github.com/PKU-ICST-MIPL/MRA_TIP.

</details>


### [66] [SLIM-Diff: Shared Latent Image-Mask Diffusion with Lp loss for Data-Scarce Epilepsy FLAIR MRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03372)
*Mario Pascual-González,Ariadna Jiménez-Partinen,R. M. Luque-Baena,Fátima Nagib-Raya,Ezequiel López-Rubio*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SLIM-Diff presents a compact, geometry-aware joint diffusion model for MRI FCD lesion synthesis, achieving strong x0-prediction performance and showing trade-offs between L_p penalties for image fidelity and lesion mask morphology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address instability and memorization in joint image+mask diffusion models for subtle FCD lesions in epilepsy MRI by enforcing tight anatomy–lesion coupling with a compact architecture and geometry-aware loss.

Method: A single shared-bottleneck U-Net processes a 2-channel input (image+mask) to jointly model anatomy and lesion geometry. The training optimizes a tunable L_p loss on geometry, in addition to a canonical DDPM-style objective (ε-prediction with L2). The framework compares x0-prediction vs ε-prediction and investigates different L_p penalties.

Result: x0-prediction consistently outperforms ε-prediction for joint synthesis. Fractional sub-quadratic penalties (L1.5) improve image fidelity; L2 better preserves lesion mask morphology. The approach yields a compact model with tight geometry coupling; code and weights are released.

Conclusion: SLIM-Diff demonstrates that geometry-aware loss weighting and a single, shared representation can stabilize joint MRI synthesis of subtle lesions, balancing image fidelity and accurate lesion morphology, with an accessible implementation.

Abstract: Focal cortical dysplasia (FCD) lesions in epilepsy FLAIR MRI are subtle and scarce, making joint image--mask generative modeling prone to instability and memorization. We propose SLIM-Diff, a compact joint diffusion model whose main contributions are (i) a single shared-bottleneck U-Net that enforces tight coupling between anatomy and lesion geometry from a 2-channel image+mask representation, and (ii) loss-geometry tuning via a tunable $L_p$ objective. As an internal baseline, we include the canonical DDPM-style objective ($ε$-prediction with $L_2$ loss) and isolate the effect of prediction parameterization and $L_p$ geometry under a matched setup. Experiments show that $x_0$-prediction is consistently the strongest choice for joint synthesis, and that fractional sub-quadratic penalties ($L_{1.5}$) improve image fidelity while $L_2$ better preserves lesion mask morphology. Our code and model weights are available in https://github.com/MarioPasc/slim-diff

</details>


### [67] [Unifying Watermarking via Dimension-Aware Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03373)
*Jiale Meng,Runyi Hu,Jie Zhang,Zheming Lu,Ivor Tsang,Tianwei Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DiM unifies watermarking as a dimension-aware mapping problem. By varying embedding/extraction dimensionalities, a single framework yields diverse behaviors—from preserving payload structure to enabling spatial/spatiotemporal localization—demonstrated in a video setting where dimensions control tamper localization, local embedding control, and temporal-order recovery without changing architecture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep watermarking methods often use similar encoder-decoder architectures but differ in functional behavior. A principled, unified framework is needed to explain and design these behaviors by treating watermark payloads as dimensional entities.

Method: Introduce DiM, a multi-dimensional watermarking framework that represents payloads with different dimensionalities (1D binary messages, 2D spatial masks, 3D spatiotemporal structures). Analyze how embedding/extraction dimensional configurations determine watermarking behavior. Instantiate DiM in the video domain to exploit spatiotemporal representations and explore a variety of dimension mappings.

Result: Experiments show that changing only the embedding and extraction dimensions (without altering network architecture) yields distinct watermarking capabilities: spatiotemporal tamper localization, local embedding control, and recovery of temporal order under frame disruptions. Cross-dimensional mappings enable localization, while same-dimensional mappings preserve payload structure and offer fine-grained control.

Conclusion: DiM provides a unified, dimension-aware view of watermarking where embedding/extraction dimensionality governs behavior. The video-domain instantiation demonstrates a broad set of capabilities achievable through dimension configuration alone, suggesting a principled design path for controllable watermarking.

Abstract: Deep watermarking methods often share similar encoder-decoder architectures, yet differ substantially in their functional behaviors. We propose DiM, a new multi-dimensional watermarking framework that formulates watermarking as a dimension-aware mapping problem, thereby unifying existing watermarking methods at the functional level. Under DiM, watermark information is modeled as payloads of different dimensionalities, including one-dimensional binary messages, two-dimensional spatial masks, and three-dimensional spatiotemporal structures. We find that the dimensional configuration of embedding and extraction largely determines the resulting watermarking behavior. Same-dimensional mappings preserve payload structure and support fine-grained control, while cross-dimensional mappings enable spatial or spatiotemporal localization. We instantiate DiM in the video domain, where spatiotemporal representations enable a broader set of dimension mappings. Experiments demonstrate that varying only the embedding and extraction dimensions, without architectural changes, leads to different watermarking capabilities, including spatiotemporal tamper localization, local embedding control, and recovery of temporal order under frame disruptions.

</details>


### [68] [From Vicious to Virtuous Cycles: Synergistic Representation Learning for Unsupervised Video Object-Centric Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03390)
*Hyun Seok Seong,WonJun Moon,Jae-Pil Heo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SRL proposes a synergistic encoder–decoder training loop for unsupervised object-centric learning, using a warm-up slot regularization to align high-frequency encoder features with the decoder’s spatially coherent outputs, achieving state-of-the-art on video OC benchmarks; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In reconstruction-based training for slot-based object-centric models, a mismatch arises: sharp, high-frequency encoder attention conflicts with blurry decoder reconstructions, creating a vicious cycle that degrades supervision and representation quality.

Method: Introduce Synergistic Representation Learning (SRL), where the encoder’s sharpness helps deblur semantic boundaries inside the decoder output and the decoder’s spatially consistent reconstructions denoise the encoder’s features. A warm-up phase with a slot regularization objective initially enforces distinct entity slots to stabilize training and bridge encoder–decoder representations.

Result: SRL achieves state-of-the-art results on video object-centric learning benchmarks; code is publicly available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: Bridging the representational gap between encoder and decoder via mutual refinement improves unsupervised object-centric learning and establishes SRL as a general mechanism to harmonize high-frequency encoder signals with spatially coherent decoder outputs.

Abstract: Unsupervised object-centric learning models, particularly slot-based architectures, have shown great promise in decomposing complex scenes. However, their reliance on reconstruction-based training creates a fundamental conflict between the sharp, high-frequency attention maps of the encoder and the spatially consistent but blurry reconstruction maps of the decoder. We identify that this discrepancy gives rise to a vicious cycle: the noisy feature map from the encoder forces the decoder to average over possibilities and produce even blurrier outputs, while the gradient computed from blurry reconstruction maps lacks high-frequency details necessary to supervise encoder features. To break this cycle, we introduce Synergistic Representation Learning (SRL) that establishes a virtuous cycle where the encoder and decoder mutually refine one another. SRL leverages the encoder's sharpness to deblur the semantic boundary within the decoder output, while exploiting the decoder's spatial consistency to denoise the encoder's features. This mutual refinement process is stabilized by a warm-up phase with a slot regularization objective that initially allocates distinct entities per slot. By bridging the representational gap between the encoder and decoder, SRL achieves state-of-the-art results on video object-centric learning benchmarks. Codes are available at https://github.com/hynnsk/SRL.

</details>


### [69] [UnHype: CLIP-Guided Hypernetworks for Dynamic LoRA Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03410)
*Piotr Wójcik,Maksym Petrenko,Wojciech Gromski,Przemysław Spurek,Maciej Zieba*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A hypernetwork-based framework (UnHype) enhances LoRA-based unlearning for diffusion models by dynamically generating adaptive LoRA weights from CLIP embeddings, enabling single- and multi-concept, context-aware, scalable unlearning with stable training; demonstrated on object, celebrity, and explicit-content erasure; code at GitHub.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To mitigate risks from large-scale diffusion models by enabling reliable unlearning of targeted concepts. Existing LoRA-based unlearning often struggles with semantic adaptability, related concepts, and multi-concept scalability, limiting safe deployment.

Method: Introduce UnHype: a hypernetwork that plugs into single- and multi-concept LoRA training. The hypernetwork generates adaptive LoRA weights conditioned on CLIP embeddings during inference, enabling context-aware unlearning. Applicable to Stable Diffusion and modern flow-based T2I models. Supports both single and multi-concept erasure.

Result: The approach exhibits stable training and effective concept control. Demonstrated across object erasure, celebrity erasure, and explicit content removal, showing versatility and practical efficacy. Repository: https://github.com/gmum/UnHype

Conclusion: UnHype provides scalable, context-aware unlearning by dynamically generating LoRA weights via a hypernetwork guided by CLIP embeddings, offering improvements over standard LoRA and facilitating plug-and-play deployment in contemporary diffusion and flow-based T2I models.

Abstract: Recent advances in large-scale diffusion models have intensified concerns about their potential misuse, particularly in generating realistic yet harmful or socially disruptive content. This challenge has spurred growing interest in effective machine unlearning, the process of selectively removing specific knowledge or concepts from a model without compromising its overall generative capabilities. Among various approaches, Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) has emerged as an effective and efficient method for fine-tuning models toward targeted unlearning. However, LoRA-based methods often exhibit limited adaptability to concept semantics and struggle to balance removing closely related concepts with maintaining generalization across broader meanings. Moreover, these methods face scalability challenges when multiple concepts must be erased simultaneously. To address these limitations, we introduce UnHype, a framework that incorporates hypernetworks into single- and multi-concept LoRA training. The proposed architecture can be directly plugged into Stable Diffusion as well as modern flow-based text-to-image models, where it demonstrates stable training behavior and effective concept control. During inference, the hypernetwork dynamically generates adaptive LoRA weights based on the CLIP embedding, enabling more context-aware, scalable unlearning. We evaluate UnHype across several challenging tasks, including object erasure, celebrity erasure, and explicit content removal, demonstrating its effectiveness and versatility. Repository: https://github.com/gmum/UnHype.

</details>


### [70] [Socratic-Geo: Synthetic Data Generation and Geometric Reasoning via Multi-Agent Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03414)
*Zhengbo Jiao,Shaobo Wang,Zifan Zhang,Wei Wang,Bing Zhao,Hu Wei,Linfeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Autonomous multi-agent framework (Socratic-Geo) connects data synthesis with model learning for geometric reasoning under data scarcity; Teacher-Solver-Generator roles enable iterative, targeted augmentation and open-source image generation, achieving strong state-of-the-art-like results with limited seed data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle the bottleneck of scarce high-quality image-text pairs for geometric reasoning in multimodal LLMs; reduce reliance on expensive human annotation and inefficient random exploration; ensure image-text fidelity through solver-guided augmentation and programmatic image generation.

Method: 1) Teacher generates parameterized Python scripts with reflective feedback on solvability (Reflect) and visual validity (RePI); 2) Solver uses preference learning to optimize reasoning; failure paths guide Teacher's targeted augmentation; 3) Generator learns image generation from accumulated image-code-instruction triplets, distilling programmatic drawing intelligence; starts from 108 seed problems; a closed-loop, autonomous multi-agent framework.

Result: Socratic-Solver achieves 49.11 on six benchmarks using one-quarter of baseline data, beating strong baselines by 2.43 points. Socratic-Generator attains 42.4% on GenExam, a new open-source SOTA, surpassing Seedream-4.0 and approaching Gemini-2.5-Flash-Image.

Conclusion: Autonomous coupling of data synthesis and learning via multi-agent interaction enables efficient geometric reasoning with limited data, advancing open-source capabilities for image-text understanding and programmatic image generation.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have significantly advanced vision-language understanding. However, even state-of-the-art models struggle with geometric reasoning, revealing a critical bottleneck: the extreme scarcity of high-quality image-text pairs. Human annotation is prohibitively expensive, while automated methods fail to ensure fidelity and training effectiveness. Existing approaches either passively adapt to available images or employ inefficient random exploration with filtering, decoupling generation from learning needs. We propose Socratic-Geo, a fully autonomous framework that dynamically couples data synthesis with model learning through multi-agent interaction. The Teacher agent generates parameterized Python scripts with reflective feedback (Reflect for solvability, RePI for visual validity), ensuring image-text pair purity. The Solver agent optimizes reasoning through preference learning, with failure paths guiding Teacher's targeted augmentation. Independently, the Generator learns image generation capabilities on accumulated "image-code-instruction" triplets, distilling programmatic drawing intelligence into visual generation. Starting from only 108 seed problems, Socratic-Solver achieves 49.11 on six benchmarks using one-quarter of baseline data, surpassing strong baselines by 2.43 points. Socratic-Generator achieves 42.4% on GenExam, establishing new state-of-the-art for open-source models, surpassing Seedream-4.0 (39.8%) and approaching Gemini-2.5-Flash-Image (43.1%).

</details>


### [71] [ConsistentRFT: Reducing Visual Hallucinations in Flow-based Reinforcement Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03425)
*Xiaofeng Tan,Jun Liu,Yuanting Fan,Bin-Bin Gao,Xi Jiang,Xiaochen Chen,Jinlong Peng,Chengjie Wang,Hongsong Wang,Feng Zheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RFT on flow-based models creates visual hallucinations due to limited exploration and trajectory imitation; ConsistentRFT uses Dynamic Granularity Rollout (DGR) and Consistent Policy Gradient Optimization (CPGO) to reduce hallucinations and improve out-of-domain performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve alignment of flow-based models and reduce perceptual hallucinations during reinforcement fine-tuning.

Method: Dynamic Granularity Rollout (DGR) to balance global semantics and local details via dynamic noise scheduling; Consistent Policy Gradient Optimization (CPGO) to align the current policy with a stable prior, preserving cross-step consistency.

Result: Significant hallucination reductions: 49% lower low-level and 38% lower high-level perceptual hallucinations on average; out-of-domain metrics improved by 5.1% over FLUX1.dev, which dropped by 0.4% baseline.

Conclusion: Proposes ConsistentRFT as a general framework to mitigate visual hallucinations in RFT for flow-based models, with demonstrated gains in both in-domain and out-of-domain metrics.

Abstract: Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RFT) on flow-based models is crucial for preference alignment. However, they often introduce visual hallucinations like over-optimized details and semantic misalignment. This work preliminarily explores why visual hallucinations arise and how to reduce them. We first investigate RFT methods from a unified perspective, and reveal the core problems stemming from two aspects, exploration and exploitation: (1) limited exploration during stochastic differential equation (SDE) rollouts, leading to an over-emphasis on local details at the expense of global semantics, and (2) trajectory imitation process inherent in policy gradient methods, distorting the model's foundational vector field and its cross-step consistency. Building on this, we propose ConsistentRFT, a general framework to mitigate these hallucinations. Specifically, we design a Dynamic Granularity Rollout (DGR) mechanism to balance exploration between global semantics and local details by dynamically scheduling different noise sources. We then introduce a Consistent Policy Gradient Optimization (CPGO) that preserves the model's consistency by aligning the current policy with a more stable prior. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ConsistentRFT significantly mitigates visual hallucinations, achieving average reductions of 49\% for low-level and 38\% for high-level perceptual hallucinations. Furthermore, ConsistentRFT outperforms other RFT methods on out-of-domain metrics, showing an improvement of 5.1\% (v.s. the baseline's decrease of -0.4\%) over FLUX1.dev. This is \href{https://xiaofeng-tan.github.io/projects/ConsistentRFT}{Project Page}.

</details>


### [72] [Hierarchical Concept-to-Appearance Guidance for Multi-Subject Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03448)
*Yijia Xu,Zihao Wang,Jinshi Cui*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Hierarchical Concept-to-Appearance Guidance (CAG) for multi-subject image generation. Introduces VAE dropout training to force reliance on robust semantic signals from a Visual Language Model (VLM). Integrates VLM-derived correspondences into a correspondence-aware masked attention module in the Diffusion Transformer (DiT) to bind attributes to matched reference regions, aiming for precise multi-subject composition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome identity inconsistency and weak compositional control in multi-subject diffusion-based generation by moving from implicit, diffusion-only associations to explicit, structured supervision from high-level concepts to fine-grained appearances.

Method: A two-level approach: (1) Conceptual level uses a VAE dropout training strategy that randomly omits reference VAE features, promoting semantic guidance from a VLM. (2) Appearance level integrates VLM-derived correspondences into a correspondence-aware masked attention module within the Diffusion Transformer (DiT), constraining each text token to attend only to its matched reference regions.

Result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art performance on multi-subject image generation, with substantial improvements in prompt following and subject consistency.

Conclusion: Explicit, hierarchical supervision from concepts to appearances improves identity preservation and compositional control in multi-subject image generation compared to diffusion-model-only approaches.

Abstract: Multi-subject image generation aims to synthesize images that faithfully preserve the identities of multiple reference subjects while following textual instructions. However, existing methods often suffer from identity inconsistency and limited compositional control, as they rely on diffusion models to implicitly associate text prompts with reference images. In this work, we propose Hierarchical Concept-to-Appearance Guidance (CAG), a framework that provides explicit, structured supervision from high-level concepts to fine-grained appearances. At the conceptual level, we introduce a VAE dropout training strategy that randomly omits reference VAE features, encouraging the model to rely more on robust semantic signals from a Visual Language Model (VLM) and thereby promoting consistent concept-level generation in the absence of complete appearance cues. At the appearance level, we integrate the VLM-derived correspondences into a correspondence-aware masked attention module within the Diffusion Transformer (DiT). This module restricts each text token to attend only to its matched reference regions, ensuring precise attribute binding and reliable multi-subject composition. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on the multi-subject image generation, substantially improving prompt following and subject consistency.

</details>


### [73] [Contextualized Visual Personalization in Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03454)
*Yeongtak Oh,Sangwon Yu,Junsung Park,Han Cheol Moon,Jisoo Mok,Sungroh Yoon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CoViP introduces a reinforcement-learning-based post-training framework for contextualized visual personalization, focusing on personalized image captioning via caption-augmented generation, with diagnostic evaluations; it improves personalization across VLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLMs struggle to connect new images with a user's accumulated visual-textual context, hindering personalized responses. Formalizes this as contextualized visual personalization.

Method: Unified framework CoViP; treat personalized image captioning as core task; reinforcement-learning-based post-training; caption-augmented generation; diagnostic evaluations to prevent textual shortcuts.

Result: Existing open-source and proprietary VLMs show substantial limitations in personalization; CoViP improves personalized image captioning and provides holistic gains on downstream personalization tasks.

Conclusion: CoViP is a crucial stage for enabling robust, generalizable contextualized visual personalization.

Abstract: Despite recent progress in vision-language models (VLMs), existing approaches often fail to generate personalized responses based on the user's specific experiences, as they lack the ability to associate visual inputs with a user's accumulated visual-textual context. We newly formalize this challenge as contextualized visual personalization, which requires the visual recognition and textual retrieval of personalized visual experiences by VLMs when interpreting new images. To address this issue, we propose CoViP, a unified framework that treats personalized image captioning as a core task for contextualized visual personalization and improves this capability through reinforcement-learning-based post-training and caption-augmented generation. We further introduce diagnostic evaluations that explicitly rule out textual shortcut solutions and verify whether VLMs truly leverage visual context. Extensive experiments demonstrate that existing open-source and proprietary VLMs exhibit substantial limitations, while CoViP not only improves personalized image captioning but also yields holistic gains across downstream personalization tasks. These results highlight CoViP as a crucial stage for enabling robust and generalizable contextualized visual personalization.

</details>


### [74] [Inlier-Centric Post-Training Quantization for Object Detection Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03472)
*Minsu Kim,Dongyeun Lee,Jaemyung Yu,Jiwan Hur,Giseop Kim,Junmo Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: InlierQ is an inlier-centric post-training quantization method for object detection that separates task-relevant information from anomalies using gradient-aware volume saliency and EM-based posterior fitting, enabling drop-in quantization with only 64 calibration samples and reducing quantization error across 2D/3D camera and LiDAR detectors on COCO and nuScenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Task-irrelevant morphologies (background clutter, sensor noise) cause redundant activations and skew activation ranges, hindering effective bit allocation; there is no clear criterion to separate anomalies from informative signals, risking loss of useful info if suppressed.

Method: Compute gradient-aware volume saliency scores per volume, classify as inlier or anomaly, fit a posterior over scores with Expectation-Maximization, suppress anomalies while preserving inliers; label-free, drop-in quantization using only 64 calibration samples.

Result: On COCO and nuScenes, consistent reductions in quantization error for camera-based (2D/3D) and LiDAR-based (3D) detectors.

Conclusion: InlierQ provides a practical, low-calibration, anomaly-aware post-training quantization framework that improves preservation of informative features in object detection across modalities.

Abstract: Object detection is pivotal in computer vision, yet its immense computational demands make deployment slow and power-hungry, motivating quantization. However, task-irrelevant morphologies such as background clutter and sensor noise induce redundant activations (or anomalies). These anomalies expand activation ranges and skew activation distributions toward task-irrelevant responses, complicating bit allocation and weakening the preservation of informative features. Without a clear criterion to distinguish anomalies, suppressing them can inadvertently discard useful information. To address this, we present InlierQ, an inlier-centric post-training quantization approach that separates anomalies from informative inliers. InlierQ computes gradient-aware volume saliency scores, classifies each volume as an inlier or anomaly, and fits a posterior distribution over these scores using the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm. This design suppresses anomalies while preserving informative features. InlierQ is label-free, drop-in, and requires only 64 calibration samples. Experiments on the COCO and nuScenes benchmarks show consistent reductions in quantization error for camera-based (2D and 3D) and LiDAR-based (3D) object detection.

</details>


### [75] [Decoupling Skeleton and Flesh: Efficient Multimodal Table Reasoning with Disentangled Alignment and Structure-aware Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03491)
*Yingjie Zhu,Xuefeng Bai,Kehai Chen,Yang Xiang,Youcheng Pan,Xiaoqiang Zhou,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Disentangled Structure-Content (DiSCo) to adapt LVLMs to table reasoning with minimal annotation and no external tools; builds Table-GLS for global-to-local structured reasoning; shows strong generalization to unseen tables.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Table reasoning with LVLMs is hindered by complex layouts and tight structure-content coupling; existing methods rely on heavy supervision, RL, or external tools; need efficient, annotation-light, tool-free adaptation.

Method: First, DiSCo disentangles structure and content during multimodal alignment to ground semantic information on table structure; then Table-GLS applies global-to-local structure-guided reasoning with structured exploration and evidence-grounded inference.

Result: Experiments across diverse benchmarks show improved table understanding and reasoning, efficient adaptation, and strong generalization to unseen table structures.

Conclusion: Proposes a practical, annotation-efficient framework for LVLM table reasoning, combining structure-content disentanglement with global-to-local reasoning, enabling robust performance without external tools.

Abstract: Reasoning over table images remains challenging for Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) due to complex layouts and tightly coupled structure-content information. Existing solutions often depend on expensive supervised training, reinforcement learning, or external tools, limiting efficiency and scalability. This work addresses a key question: how to adapt LVLMs to table reasoning with minimal annotation and no external tools? Specifically, we first introduce DiSCo, a Disentangled Structure-Content alignment framework that explicitly separates structural abstraction from semantic grounding during multimodal alignment, efficiently adapting LVLMs to tables structures. Building on DiSCo, we further present Table-GLS, a Global-to-Local Structure-guided reasoning framework that performs table reasoning via structured exploration and evidence-grounded inference. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that our framework efficiently enhances LVLM's table understanding and reasoning capabilities, particularly generalizing to unseen table structures.

</details>


### [76] [Semantic Routing: Exploring Multi-Layer LLM Feature Weighting for Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03510)
*Bozhou Li,Yushuo Guan,Haolin Li,Bohan Zeng,Yiyan Ji,Yue Ding,Pengfei Wan,Kun Gai,Yuanxing Zhang,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A unified convex fusion framework with lightweight gates fuses multi-layer LLM hidden states for DiT-based text-to-image generation; depth-wise semantic routing outperforms time-wise and joint fusion, improving alignment and compositionality, while time-wise fusion can hurt fidelity due to trajectory mismatch under classifier-free guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Leverage semantic hierarchies across LLM layers and non-stationary denoising dynamics across diffusion time/depth to improve text-conditioned diffusion generation, which current approaches often treat conditioning statically or from a single LLM layer.

Method: Propose a unified normalized convex fusion framework with lightweight gates to organize LLM hidden states along time-wise, depth-wise, and joint axes. Investigate three fusion strategies and identify Depth-wise Semantic Routing as superior. Provide trajectory-aware conditioning analysis and ablations. 

Result: Depth-wise Semantic Routing consistently improves text-image alignment and compositional generation (e.g., +9.97 on GenAI-Bench Counting). Purely time-wise fusion can degrade visual fidelity due to a trajectory mismatch between nominal timesteps and effective SNR under classifier-free guidance. The approach establishes Convex fusion with gating as a robust conditioning mechanism and positions depth-wise routing as a strong baseline.

Conclusion: Depth-wise routing provides effective, hierarchy-aware conditioning for DiT-based diffusion models and underscores the importance of trajectory-aware signals for time-dependent conditioning. Time-wise fusion should be used with caution, and a unified, convex, multi-axis fusion framework offers a stable path to leverage multi-layer LLM representations.

Abstract: Recent DiT-based text-to-image models increasingly adopt LLMs as text encoders, yet text conditioning remains largely static and often utilizes only a single LLM layer, despite pronounced semantic hierarchy across LLM layers and non-stationary denoising dynamics over both diffusion time and network depth. To better match the dynamic process of DiT generation and thereby enhance the diffusion model's generative capability, we introduce a unified normalized convex fusion framework equipped with lightweight gates to systematically organize multi-layer LLM hidden states via time-wise, depth-wise, and joint fusion. Experiments establish Depth-wise Semantic Routing as the superior conditioning strategy, consistently improving text-image alignment and compositional generation (e.g., +9.97 on the GenAI-Bench Counting task). Conversely, we find that purely time-wise fusion can paradoxically degrade visual generation fidelity. We attribute this to a train-inference trajectory mismatch: under classifier-free guidance, nominal timesteps fail to track the effective SNR, causing semantically mistimed feature injection during inference. Overall, our results position depth-wise routing as a strong and effective baseline and highlight the critical need for trajectory-aware signals to enable robust time-dependent conditioning.

</details>


### [77] [Interpretable Logical Anomaly Classification via Constraint Decomposition and Instruction Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03530)
*Xufei Zhang,Xinjiao Zhou,Ziling Deng,Dongdong Geng,Jianxiong Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Logical Anomaly Classification (LAC) and LogiCls—vision–language framework with subquery reasoning, synthetic chain-of-thought supervision, and difficulty-aware resampling to classify and explain logical anomalies in industrial images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial quality assurance requires not only detecting anomalies but also identifying the violated logical rules and providing interpretable explanations, which binary detectors cannot offer.

Method: Decompose complex logical constraints into verifiable subqueries; utilize a vision–language model (VLM) with grounding to answer subqueries; generate chain-of-thought (CoT) supervision via a data-centric instruction synthesis pipeline that couples precise grounding annotations with diverse image–text augmentations; stabilize training with a difficulty-aware resampling strategy emphasizing hard subqueries and long-tail constraint types.

Result: LogiCls achieves robust, interpretable, and accurate logical anomaly classification in industrial images, delivering both predicted violation categories and their evidence trails across extensive experiments.

Conclusion: The framework unifies anomaly detection and fine-grained violation classification in a single inference step, enabling interpretable, logic-sensitive QA for industrial inspection and potentially improving QA workflows.

Abstract: Logical anomalies are violations of predefined constraints on object quantity, spatial layout, and compositional relationships in industrial images. While prior work largely treats anomaly detection as a binary decision, such formulations cannot indicate which logical rule is broken and therefore offer limited value for quality assurance. We introduce Logical Anomaly Classification (LAC), a task that unifies anomaly detection and fine-grained violation classification in a single inference step. To tackle LAC, we propose LogiCls, a vision-language framework that decomposes complex logical constraints into a sequence of verifiable subqueries. We further present a data-centric instruction synthesis pipeline that generates chain-of-thought (CoT) supervision for these subqueries, coupling precise grounding annotations with diverse image-text augmentations to adapt vision language models (VLMs) to logic-sensitive reasoning. Training is stabilized by a difficulty-aware resampling strategy that emphasizes challenging subqueries and long tail constraint types. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LogiCls delivers robust, interpretable, and accurate industrial logical anomaly classification, providing both the predicted violation categories and their evidence trails.

</details>


### [78] [PnP-U3D: Plug-and-Play 3D Framework Bridging Autoregression and Diffusion for Unified Understanding and Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03533)
*Yongwei Chen,Tianyi Wei,Yushi Lan,Zhaoyang Lyu,Shangchen Zhou,Xudong Xu,Xingang Pan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: We present a unified AR+diffusion framework for 3D understanding and generation that uses autoregressive modeling for understanding and diffusion for generation, bridged by a lightweight transformer to enable cross-modal interaction with LLM priors, achieving state-of-the-art results and enabling 3D editing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing attempts to unify 3D tasks under a single autoregressive paradigm suffer from forced signal quantization and high training costs, hindering effective interaction between understanding and generation; a hybrid approach leveraging pretrained models can reduce cost while preserving capabilities.

Method: Use autoregressive next-token prediction for 3D understanding and continuous diffusion for 3D generation. A lightweight transformer bridges the LLM feature space and the 3D diffusion conditional space to enable cross-modal information exchange while preserving pretrained priors.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse 3D understanding and generation benchmarks and excels in 3D editing tasks.

Conclusion: A unified AR+diffusion framework is a promising direction for general-purpose 3D intelligence, enabling effective interaction between understanding and generation while leveraging pretrained models to control cost.

Abstract: The rapid progress of large multimodal models has inspired efforts toward unified frameworks that couple understanding and generation. While such paradigms have shown remarkable success in 2D, extending them to 3D remains largely underexplored. Existing attempts to unify 3D tasks under a single autoregressive (AR) paradigm lead to significant performance degradation due to forced signal quantization and prohibitive training cost. Our key insight is that the essential challenge lies not in enforcing a unified autoregressive paradigm, but in enabling effective information interaction between generation and understanding while minimally compromising their inherent capabilities and leveraging pretrained models to reduce training cost. Guided by this perspective, we present the first unified framework for 3D understanding and generation that combines autoregression with diffusion. Specifically, we adopt an autoregressive next-token prediction paradigm for 3D understanding, and a continuous diffusion paradigm for 3D generation. A lightweight transformer bridges the feature space of large language models and the conditional space of 3D diffusion models, enabling effective cross-modal information exchange while preserving the priors learned by standalone models. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse 3D understanding and generation benchmarks, while also excelling in 3D editing tasks. These results highlight the potential of unified AR+diffusion models as a promising direction for building more general-purpose 3D intelligence.

</details>


### [79] [Constrained Dynamic Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03538)
*Zihan Zheng,Zhenglong Wu,Xuanxuan Wang,Houqiang Zhong,Xiaoyun Zhang,Qiang Hu,Guangtao Zhai,Wenjun Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Budget-constrained dynamic Gaussian Splatting (CDGS) enforces a user-defined Gaussian budget via a differentiable budget controller, achieving high-quality dynamic 3D reconstruction under memory limits with >3x compression and strict <2% error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic Gaussian Splatting yields high-fidelity 4D reconstruction but suffers memory blow-up; conventional pruning cannot guarantee quality at fixed budgets. A budget-aware training mechanism is needed.

Method: Introduce a differentiable budget controller guided by a multi-modal unified importance score combining geometry, motion, and perceptual cues; decouple static/dynamic optimization with adaptive capacity allocation; three-phase training; dual-mode hybrid compression; ensure adherence to target Gaussian count.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art under various capacity limits, delivering optimal rendering quality with about 3x compression; hardware error bound < ~2% under the budget; pushes the Pareto frontier of rate-distortion.

Conclusion: Provides a principled budget-aware framework enabling efficient dynamic scene reconstruction on edge devices while maintaining rendering quality and improving rate-distortion performance.

Abstract: While Dynamic Gaussian Splatting enables high-fidelity 4D reconstruction, its deployment is severely hindered by a fundamental dilemma: unconstrained densification leads to excessive memory consumption incompatible with edge devices, whereas heuristic pruning fails to achieve optimal rendering quality under preset Gaussian budgets. In this work, we propose Constrained Dynamic Gaussian Splatting (CDGS), a novel framework that formulates dynamic scene reconstruction as a budget-constrained optimization problem to enforce a strict, user-defined Gaussian budget during training. Our key insight is to introduce a differentiable budget controller as the core optimization driver. Guided by a multi-modal unified importance score, this controller fuses geometric, motion, and perceptual cues for precise capacity regulation. To maximize the utility of this fixed budget, we further decouple the optimization of static and dynamic elements, employing an adaptive allocation mechanism that dynamically distributes capacity based on motion complexity. Furthermore, we implement a three-phase training strategy to seamlessly integrate these constraints, ensuring precise adherence to the target count. Coupled with a dual-mode hybrid compression scheme, CDGS not only strictly adheres to hardware constraints (error < 2%}) but also pushes the Pareto frontier of rate-distortion performance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CDGS delivers optimal rendering quality under varying capacity limits, achieving over 3x compression compared to state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [80] [Cut to the Mix: Simple Data Augmentation Outperforms Elaborate Ones in Limited Organ Segmentation Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03555)
*Chang Liu,Fuxin Fan,Annette Schwarz,Andreas Maier*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Evaluates four inter-image data augmentation strategies for multi-organ segmentation under limited data; CutMix, CarveMix, ObjectAug, and AnatoMix improve segmentation accuracy versus nnUNet baseline, with Dice score gains of 4.9, 2.0, and 1.9, respectively; results improve further with traditional DA; code released for benchmarking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical image segmentation with DL is data-hungry; clinical data scarcity motivates designing augmentation strategies that operate across images and organs to synthesize diverse, realistic samples and regularize models.

Method: Apply four inter-image augmentation methods (CutMix, CarveMix, ObjectAug, AnatoMix) to two organ segmentation datasets; compare against a state-of-the-art nnUNet baseline with/without traditional data augmentation; report average Dice scores and robustness of the methods (e.g., CutMix performs well despite producing visually implausible images); provide publicly available implementation.

Result: CutMix, CarveMix, and AnatoMix yield average Dice improvements of 4.9, 2.0, and 1.9 points over nnUNet without DA; combining with traditional DA affords further gains; CutMix is notably robust even when images look misaligned or 'wrong'.

Conclusion: Inter-image/organ-level augmentation is beneficial for multi-organ segmentation with limited data; among the methods, CutMix is particularly robust and effective; integrating these strategies with traditional DA can maximize gains; authors release code to support benchmarking.

Abstract: Multi-organ segmentation is a widely applied clinical routine and automated organ segmentation tools dramatically improve the pipeline of the radiologists. Recently, deep learning (DL) based segmentation models have shown the capacity to accomplish such a task. However, the training of the segmentation networks requires large amount of data with manual annotations, which is a major concern due to the data scarcity from clinic. Working with limited data is still common for researches on novel imaging modalities. To enhance the effectiveness of DL models trained with limited data, data augmentation (DA) is a crucial regularization technique. Traditional DA (TDA) strategies focus on basic intra-image operations, i.e. generating images with different orientations and intensity distributions. In contrast, the interimage and object-level DA operations are able to create new images from separate individuals. However, such DA strategies are not well explored on the task of multi-organ segmentation. In this paper, we investigated four possible inter-image DA strategies: CutMix, CarveMix, ObjectAug and AnatoMix, on two organ segmentation datasets. The result shows that CutMix, CarveMix and AnatoMix can improve the average dice score by 4.9, 2.0 and 1.9, compared with the state-of-the-art nnUNet without DA strategies. These results can be further improved by adding TDA strategies. It is revealed in our experiments that Cut-Mix is a robust but simple DA strategy to drive up the segmentation performance for multi-organ segmentation, even when CutMix produces intuitively 'wrong' images. Our implementation is publicly available for future benchmarks.

</details>


### [81] [ELIQ: A Label-Free Framework for Quality Assessment of Evolving AI-Generated Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03558)
*Xinyue Li,Zhiming Xu,Zhichao Zhang,Zhaolin Cai,Sijing Wu,Xiongkuo Min,Yitong Chen,Guangtao Zhai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ELIQ provides a label-free framework to assess evolving AI-generated images by automatically constructing positive and aspect-specific negative pairs, then fine-tuning a multimodal model into a quality-aware critic to predict two-dimensional quality (visual quality and prompt-image alignment) using lightweight fusion and a Quality Query Transformer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rapid evolution of generative models invalidates static labels; scalable, label-free quality assessment is needed that covers both general distortions and AIGC-specific distortions while generalizing to user-generated content.

Method: Automatically generate positive and aspect-specific negative pairs for various distortions (including AIGC-specific ones); instruction-tune a pre-trained multimodal model into a quality-aware critic; apply lightweight gated fusion and a Quality Query Transformer to predict two-dimensional quality scores; enable transferable supervision without human annotations.

Result: ELIQ outperforms existing label-free methods across benchmarks, generalizes from AI-generated content to user-generated content without modification, and demonstrates scalable, label-free quality assessment under evolving generative models; code will be released after publication.

Conclusion: ELIQ enables scalable, label-free assessment of visual quality and prompt-image alignment for evolving AI-generated content by crafting self-supervised supervisory signals and adapting a multimodal critic, addressing the need for continual quality monitoring as generative models advance.

Abstract: Generative text-to-image models are advancing at an unprecedented pace, continuously shifting the perceptual quality ceiling and rendering previously collected labels unreliable for newer generations. To address this, we present ELIQ, a Label-free Framework for Quality Assessment of Evolving AI-generated Images. Specifically, ELIQ focuses on visual quality and prompt-image alignment, automatically constructs positive and aspect-specific negative pairs to cover both conventional distortions and AIGC-specific distortion modes, enabling transferable supervision without human annotations. Building on these pairs, ELIQ adapts a pre-trained multimodal model into a quality-aware critic via instruction tuning and predicts two-dimensional quality using lightweight gated fusion and a Quality Query Transformer. Experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that ELIQ consistently outperforms existing label-free methods, generalizes from AI-generated content (AIGC) to user-generated content (UGC) scenarios without modification, and paves the way for scalable and label-free quality assessment under continuously evolving generative models. The code will be released upon publication.

</details>


### [82] [SlowFocus: Enhancing Fine-grained Temporal Understanding in Video LLM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03589)
*Ming Nie,Dan Ding,Chunwei Wang,Yuanfan Guo,Jianhua Han,Hang Xu,Li Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces SlowFocus for Vid-LLMs to balance frame-level token quality with video-level temporal information by segment-focused dense sampling and multi-frequency mixing attention; introduces training strategies and a FineAction-CGR benchmark; shows superior performance on public benchmarks and the new dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vid-LLMs currently struggle to concurrently preserve high-quality frame-level semantic tokens and adequate video-level temporal coverage, hindering fine-grained video understanding.

Method: Proposes SlowFocus: (1) identify the query-related temporal segment from the question, (2) perform dense sampling on that segment to extract local high-frequency features, (3) apply a multi-frequency mixing attention to fuse local high-frequency details with global low-frequency contexts. Also introduces training strategies to boost temporal grounding and detailed temporal reasoning, and a new FineAction-CGR benchmark to assess fine-grained temporal understanding. Evaluates on existing benchmarks and FineAction-CGR.

Result: Demonstrates superiority of SlowFocus across public video understanding benchmarks and the FineAction-CGR dataset.
