Conclusion: StepNav advances reliable trajectory generation for practical autonomous navigation by leveraging structured, multimodal priors derived from variational principles, integrated with flow-matching and optimal-control refinement to outperform unstructured-prior generative planners.

Abstract: Visual navigation is fundamental to autonomous systems, yet generating reliable trajectories in cluttered and uncertain environments remains a core challenge. Recent generative models promise end-to-end synthesis, but their reliance on unstructured noise priors often yields unsafe, inefficient, or unimodal plans that cannot meet real-time requirements. We propose StepNav, a novel framework that bridges this gap by introducing structured, multimodal trajectory priors derived from variational principles. StepNav first learns a geometry-aware success probability field to identify all feasible navigation corridors. These corridors are then used to construct an explicit, multi-modal mixture prior that initializes a conditional flow-matching process. This refinement is formulated as an optimal control problem with explicit smoothness and safety regularization. By replacing unstructured noise with physically-grounded candidates, StepNav generates safer and more efficient plans in significantly fewer steps. Experiments in both simulation and real-world benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in robustness, efficiency, and safety over state-of-the-art generative planners, advancing reliable trajectory generation for practical autonomous navigation. The code has been released at https://github.com/LuoXubo/StepNav.

</details>


### [109] [AROLA: A Modular Layered Architecture for Scaled Autonomous Racing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02730)
*Fam Shihata,Mohammed Abdelazim,Ahmed Hussein*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AROLA is a modular ROS 2-based software stack for autonomous racing paired with a Race Monitor for real-time logging and standardized post-race analysis; validated on RoboRacer hardware and in simulation; aims to enhance reproducibility and rapid module replacement on scaled platforms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fragmented and monolithic autonomous racing software architectures hinder rapid development, benchmarking, and reproducibility. A modular, interoperable stack with standardized interfaces and evaluation tools is needed for scaled platforms.

Method: Proposes AROLA: a layered, modular architecture where sensing, pre-processing, perception, localization and mapping, planning, behavior, control, and actuation are connected via standardized ROS 2 interfaces, avoiding custom message definitions. Introduces Race Monitor as a lightweight framework to log lap timing, trajectory quality, and computational load in real time and to generate standardized post-race analyses. Validation conducted in simulation and on RoboRacer hardware, including deployment at the 2025 RoboRacer IV25 competition.

Result: Showing that modularity, transparent interfaces, and systematic evaluation can accelerate development and improve reproducibility in scaled autonomous racing.

Conclusion: ARO LA and Race Monitor provide a reproducible, extensible framework enabling rapid module replacement and objective benchmarking, potentially accelerating progress in scaled autonomous racing.

Abstract: Autonomous racing has advanced rapidly, particularly on scaled platforms, and software stacks must evolve accordingly. In this work, AROLA is introduced as a modular, layered software architecture in which fragmented and monolithic designs are reorganized into interchangeable layers and components connected through standardized ROS 2 interfaces. The autonomous-driving pipeline is decomposed into sensing, pre-processing, perception, localization and mapping, planning, behavior, control, and actuation, enabling rapid module replacement and objective benchmarking without reliance on custom message definitions. To support consistent performance evaluation, a Race Monitor framework is introduced as a lightweight system through which lap timing, trajectory quality, and computational load are logged in real time and standardized post-race analyses are generated. AROLA is validated in simulation and on hardware using the RoboRacer platform, including deployment at the 2025 RoboRacer IV25 competition. Together, AROLA and Race Monitor demonstrate that modularity, transparent interfaces, and systematic evaluation can accelerate development and improve reproducibility in scaled autonomous racing.

</details>


### [110] [PokeNet: Learning Kinematic Models of Articulated Objects from Human Observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02741)
*Anmol Gupta,Weiwei Gu,Omkar Patil,Jun Ki Lee,Nakul Gopalan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PokeNet estimates articulation models from a single demonstration without prior object knowledge, predicting joint parameters, manipulation order, and state trajectories from point-cloud sequences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Articulation modeling for manipulation often requires prior knowledge about objects and multiple views; occluded joints and ordering constraints hinder real-world robotic manipulation.

Method: An end-to-end framework that takes a sequence of point-cloud observations of a human interacting with an unknown object to predict joint parameters, infer manipulation order, and track joint states over time, without relying on prior object knowledge; evaluated in simulation and real-world settings with superior performance.

Result: Joint axis and state estimation accuracy improved by over 27% on diverse and unseen objects; demonstrated gains in both simulation and real-world environments.

Conclusion: The approach enables robust articulation modeling from minimal input, enabling better planning and manipulation of novel and complex articulated objects without prior knowledge.

Abstract: Articulation modeling enables robots to learn joint parameters of articulated objects for effective manipulation which can then be used downstream for skill learning or planning. Existing approaches often rely on prior knowledge about the objects, such as the number or type of joints. Some of these approaches also fail to recover occluded joints that are only revealed during interaction. Others require large numbers of multi-view images for every object, which is impractical in real-world settings. Furthermore, prior works neglect the order of manipulations, which is essential for many multi-DoF objects where one joint must be operated before another, such as a dishwasher. We introduce PokeNet, an end-to-end framework that estimates articulation models from a single human demonstration without prior object knowledge. Given a sequence of point cloud observations of a human manipulating an unknown object, PokeNet predicts joint parameters, infers manipulation order, and tracks joint states over time. PokeNet outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, improving joint axis and state estimation accuracy by an average of over 27% across diverse objects, including novel and unseen categories. We demonstrate these gains in both simulation and real-world environments.

</details>


### [111] [Bimanual High-Density EMG Control for In-Home Mobile Manipulation by a User with Quadriplegia](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02773)
*Jehan Yang,Eleanor Hodgson,Cindy Sun,Zackory Erickson,Doug Weber*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A first-in-kind system enabling a quadriplegic to teleoperate a mobile manipulator at home via bimanual high-density EMG sleeves, integrated with vision, language, and motion-planning for shared autonomy, validated by a 12-day in-home study supporting daily living tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address access barriers for people with cervical spinal cord injury by providing an intuitive, hands-free control interface for home robot manipulation, overcoming limitations of conventional joysticks/keyboards.

Method: Develop two fabric-integrated, forearm-HDEMG sleeves worn on both arms to capture residual neuromotor activity for real-time gesture-based robot control; integrate vision, language, and motion-planning modules to form a shared autonomy teleoperation framework; conduct a 12-day in-home study to test real-world use.

Result: Demonstrated real-time EMG-based control of a mobile manipulator in a home environment; the shared autonomy framework enhanced robustness for navigation-intensive tasks; validated long-term, in-home usability over twelve days.

Conclusion: The work establishes feasibility of EMG-driven, gesture-based control for home mobile manipulation with a shared autonomy layer, enabling activities of daily living in real homes and highlighting the potential for practical assistive robotics deployment.

Abstract: Mobile manipulators in the home can enable people with cervical spinal cord injury (cSCI) to perform daily physical household tasks that they could not otherwise do themselves. However, paralysis in these users often limits access to traditional robot control interfaces such as joysticks or keyboards. In this work, we introduce and deploy the first system that enables a user with quadriplegia to control a mobile manipulator in their own home using bimanual high-density electromyography (HDEMG). We develop a pair of custom, fabric-integrated HDEMG forearm sleeves, worn on both arms, that capture residual neuromotor activity from clinically paralyzed degrees of freedom and support real-time gesture-based robot control. Second, by integrating vision, language, and motion planning modules, we introduce a shared autonomy framework that supports robust and user-driven teleoperation, with particular benefits for navigation-intensive tasks in home environments. Finally, to demonstrate the system in the wild, we present a twelve-day in-home user study evaluating real-time use of the wearable EMG interface for daily robot control. Together, these system components enable effective robot control for performing activities of daily living and other household tasks in a real home environment.

</details>


### [112] [Adaptive Linear Path Model-Based Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02831)
*Yutaka Shimizu,Masayoshi Tomizuka*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a Linear Path Model-Based Diffusion (LP-MBD) framework and an Adaptive LP-MBD (ALP-MBD) for diffusion-based robotic control. LP-MBD replaces the variance-preserving schedule with a linear, flow-matching-inspired probability path to simplify scheduling; ALP-MBD uses reinforcement learning to adapt diffusion steps and noise levels based on task/environment, enhancing robustness and real-time efficiency across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-model–based control in robotics is highly sensitive to scheduling hyperparameters. A decoupled, interpretable schedule and an adaptive mechanism are needed to reduce tuning complexity and improve robustness in real-world tasks.

Method: Replace the variance-preserving diffusion schedule with a flow-matching-inspired linear probability path to yield a geometrically interpretable, decoupled parameterization. Extend with reinforcement learning to adjust diffusion steps and noise levels according to task complexity and environmental conditions. Validate on numerical studies, Brax benchmarks, and mobile-robot trajectory tracking.

Result: LP-MBD reduces scheduling tuning while maintaining strong performance. ALP-MBD improves robustness, adaptability, and real-time efficiency across tasks and environments.

Conclusion: LP-MBD provides a stable, interpretable foundation for diffusion-based control; ALP-MBD builds on it with RL-driven adaptation to boost robustness and efficiency in practical robotic applications.

Abstract: The interest in combining model-based control approaches with diffusion models has been growing. Although we have seen many impressive robotic control results in difficult tasks, the performance of diffusion models is highly sensitive to the choice of scheduling parameters, making parameter tuning one of the most critical challenges. We introduce Linear Path Model-Based Diffusion (LP-MBD), which replaces the variance-preserving schedule with a flow-matching-inspired linear probability path. This yields a geometrically interpretable and decoupled parameterization that reduces tuning complexity and provides a stable foundation for adaptation. Building on this, we propose Adaptive LP-MBD (ALP-MBD), which leverages reinforcement learning to adjust diffusion steps and noise levels according to task complexity and environmental conditions. Across numerical studies, Brax benchmarks, and mobile-robot trajectory tracking, LP-MBD simplifies scheduling while maintaining strong performance, and ALP-MBD further improves robustness, adaptability, and real-time efficiency.

</details>


### [113] [Language Movement Primitives: Grounding Language Models in Robot Motion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02839)
*Yinlong Dai,Benjamin A. Christie,Daniel J. Evans,Dylan P. Losey,Simon Stepputtis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes Language Movement Primitives (LMPs) to ground VLM reasoning in Dynamic Movement Primitives for zero-shot tabletop manipulation; achieves 80% success across 20 real-world tasks, outperforming ~31% baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between high-level language-based task planning and low-level embodied motion in robotics; current VLMs struggle to ground reasoning into motion, while robotics foundations require fine-tuning; need a representation that is both expressive and grounded.

Method: Ground VLM outputs in DMP parameterization; VLMs reason over natural language and set DMP parameters to produce continuous, stable trajectories; form a pipeline that outputs a sequence of DMP motions for manipulation tasks.

Result: Empirical evaluation on 20 real-world tabletop tasks shows 80% success vs 31% baselines; videos available on the project website.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that grounding VLM-derived task planning in DMPs can bridge high-level reasoning and low-level control; indicates promise for zero-shot manipulation and broader applicability.

Abstract: Enabling robots to perform novel manipulation tasks from natural language instructions remains a fundamental challenge in robotics, despite significant progress in generalized problem solving with foundational models. Large vision and language models (VLMs) are capable of processing high-dimensional input data for visual scene and language understanding, as well as decomposing tasks into a sequence of logical steps; however, they struggle to ground those steps in embodied robot motion. On the other hand, robotics foundation models output action commands, but require in-domain fine-tuning or experience before they are able to perform novel tasks successfully. At its core, there still remains the fundamental challenge of connecting abstract task reasoning with low-level motion control. To address this disconnect, we propose Language Movement Primitives (LMPs), a framework that grounds VLM reasoning in Dynamic Movement Primitive (DMP) parameterization. Our key insight is that DMPs provide a small number of interpretable parameters, and VLMs can set these parameters to specify diverse, continuous, and stable trajectories. Put another way: VLMs can reason over free-form natural language task descriptions, and semantically ground their desired motions into DMPs -- bridging the gap between high-level task reasoning and low-level position and velocity control. Building on this combination of VLMs and DMPs, we formulate our LMP pipeline for zero-shot robot manipulation that effectively completes tabletop manipulation problems by generating a sequence of DMP motions. Across 20 real-world manipulation tasks, we show that LMP achieves 80% task success as compared to 31% for the best-performing baseline. See videos at our website: https://collab.me.vt.edu/lmp

</details>


### [114] [Kino-PAX$^+$: Near-Optimal Massively Parallel Kinodynamic Sampling-based Motion Planner](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02846)
*Nicolas Perrault,Qi Heng Ho,Morteza Lahijanian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A massively parallel kinodynamic sampling-based motion planner with asymptotic near-optimal guarantees, delivering real-time performance and lower solution costs than existing serial and GPU-based planners.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome real-time constraints in high-dimensional kinodynamic motion planning by leveraging parallelism while preserving guarantees on optimality, addressing the gap where parallel SBMPs lacked optimization assurances.

Method: Decompose traditionally serial operations into three massively parallel subroutines; construct a sparse tree of dynamically feasible trajectories; prioritize computation on the most promising nodes within local neighborhoods for propagation and refinement; maintain probabilistic δ-robust completeness.

Result: Achieves asymptotic δ-robust near-optimality; finds solutions up to 1,000× faster than serial methods and yields lower costs than a state-of-the-art GPU-based planner.

Conclusion: Kino-PAX+ demonstrates that massive parallelization in kinodynamic SBMP can deliver real-time, near-optimal solutions, combining speedups with cost improvements over existing approaches.

Abstract: Sampling-based motion planners (SBMPs) are widely used for robot motion planning with complex kinodynamic constraints in high-dimensional spaces, yet they struggle to achieve \emph{real-time} performance due to their serial computation design. Recent efforts to parallelize SBMPs have achieved significant speedups in finding feasible solutions; however, they provide no guarantees of optimizing an objective function. We introduce Kino-PAX$^{+}$, a massively parallel kinodynamic SBMP with asymptotic near-optimal guarantees. Kino-PAX$^{+}$ builds a sparse tree of dynamically feasible trajectories by decomposing traditionally serial operations into three massively parallel subroutines. The algorithm focuses computation on the most promising nodes within local neighborhoods for propagation and refinement, enabling rapid improvement of solution cost. We prove that, while maintaining probabilistic $δ$-robust completeness, this focus on promising nodes ensures asymptotic $δ$-robust near-optimality. Our results show that Kino-PAX$^{+}$ finds solutions up to three orders of magnitude faster than existing serial methods and achieves lower solution costs than a state-of-the-art GPU-based planner.

</details>


### [115] [Latent Perspective-Taking via a Schrödinger Bridge in Influence-Augmented Local Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02857)
*Kevin Alcedo,Pedro U. Lima,Rachid Alami*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Neuro-symbolic, factored belief-space model with an amortized Schrödinger Bridge for perspective-shifting enables socially-aware planning under uncertainty, using an Influence-Augmented Local Model and MiniGrid demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle intractable nested-belief inference in Interactive POMDPs and Bayesian Theory of Mind by learning structured mental models and a belief-space perspective-shift estimator to reason about others' hidden mental-states in open-world settings.

Method: Introduce Influence-Augmented Local Model; instantiate a neuro-symbolic world model as a factored discrete Dynamic Bayesian Network; implement a perspective-shift operator as an amortized Schrödinger Bridge over local dynamics to transport egocentric beliefs into other-centric beliefs; perform decision-time mental-state planning within model-based RL; apply to a MiniGrid social navigation task with preliminary results.

Result: Architecture enables synthesis of socially-aware policies in model-based RL; preliminary MiniGrid results demonstrate the viability of decision-time mental-state planning over belief space.

Conclusion: The framework offers a scalable approach to reasoning about others’ hidden mental-states by combining structured, learnable mental models with belief-space planning, enabling social intelligence in open environments; further work needed on scalable evaluation and real-world experiments.

Abstract: Operating in environments alongside humans requires robots to make decisions under uncertainty. In addition to exogenous dynamics, they must reason over others' hidden mental-models and mental-states. While Interactive POMDPs and Bayesian Theory of Mind formulations are principled, exact nested-belief inference is intractable, and hand-specified models are brittle in open-world settings. We address both by learning structured mental-models and an estimator of others' mental-states. Building on the Influence-Based Abstraction, we instantiate an Influence-Augmented Local Model to decompose socially-aware robot tasks into local dynamics, social influences, and exogenous factors. We propose (a) a neuro-symbolic world model instantiating a factored, discrete Dynamic Bayesian Network, and (b) a perspective-shift operator modeled as an amortized Schrödinger Bridge over the learned local dynamics that transports factored egocentric beliefs into other-centric beliefs. We show that this architecture enables agents to synthesize socially-aware policies in model-based reinforcement learning, via decision-time mental-state planning (a Schrödinger Bridge in belief space), with preliminary results in a MiniGrid social navigation task.

</details>


### [116] [IMAGINE: Intelligent Multi-Agent Godot-based Indoor Networked Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02858)
*Tiago Leite,Maria Conceição,António Grilo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A scalable MARL framework enables cooperative, autonomous UAV exploration in GNSS-denied indoor environments using ND-POMDPs, continuous actions, high-fidelity simulation, and limited inter-agent communication, achieving emergent collaboration and robust learning through curriculum-based training and ablations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Coordinate perception, decision-making, and coordination in unknown GNSS-denied spaces where centralized control, discrete actions, and permanent connectivity assumptions fail; enable robust, scalable collaboration among multiple UAVs under communication and sensing constraints.

Method: MARL in a 2D indoor setting with continuous action spaces, ND-POMDP formulation, each UAV equipped with LiDAR and sharing sensor data and local occupancy maps with neighbors. Use Godot for high-fidelity simulation; address limited communication range, bandwidth, and latency. Conduct extensive ablations on MARL paradigms, reward design, NN architectures/memory, and POMDP formulations. Employ Curriculum Learning (five increasingly complex levels) to accelerate training and improve robustness.

Result: Demonstrates that scalable MARL with simplified architectures and curriculum learning yields rapid, robust autonomous exploration in indoor environments, with emergent cooperative behaviors under uncertainty. The approach overcomes prior limitations (discrete actions, centralized setups, permanent connectivity, static assumptions) and provides a strong foundation for transferring learned strategies to physical robots.

Conclusion: The proposed MARL framework, high-fidelity simulation, and curriculum-driven training offer a viable path toward practical deployment of cooperative UAV strategies in GNSS-denied scenarios, emphasizing scalability, data-sharing among agents, and robustness to communication constraints.

Abstract: The exploration of unknown, Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) denied environments by an autonomous communication-aware and collaborative group of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) presents significant challenges in coordination, perception, and decentralized decision-making. This paper implements Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) to address these challenges in a 2D indoor environment, using high-fidelity game-engine simulations (Godot) and continuous action spaces. Policy training aims to achieve emergent collaborative behaviours and decision-making under uncertainty using Network-Distributed Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (ND-POMDPs). Each UAV is equipped with a Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) sensor and can share data (sensor measurements and a local occupancy map) with neighbouring agents. Inter-agent communication constraints include limited range, bandwidth and latency. Extensive ablation studies evaluated MARL training paradigms, reward function, communication system, neural network (NN) architecture, memory mechanisms, and POMDP formulations. This work jointly addresses several key limitations in prior research, namely reliance on discrete actions, single-agent or centralized formulations, assumptions of a priori knowledge and permanent connectivity, inability to handle dynamic obstacles, short planning horizons and architectural complexity in Recurrent NNs/Transformers. Results show that the scalable training paradigm, combined with a simplified architecture, enables rapid autonomous exploration of an indoor area. The implementation of Curriculum-Learning (five increasingly complex levels) also enabled faster, more robust training. This combination of high-fidelity simulation, MARL formulation, and computational efficiency establishes a strong foundation for deploying learned cooperative strategies in physical robotic systems.

</details>


### [117] [Accelerating Structured Chain-of-Thought in Autonomous Vehicles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02864)
*Yi Gu,Yan Wang,Yuxiao Chen,Yurong You,Wenjie Luo,Yue Wang,Wenhao Ding,Boyi Li,Heng Yang,Boris Ivanovic,Marco Pavone*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Parallel decoding for template-structured Chain-of-Thought (CoT) in autonomous driving accelerates reasoning via a dependency-graph decomposition and parallel sub-task generation, yielding 3-4x CoT speedups and lower end-to-end latency while preserving downstream benefits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CoT improves decision-making in vision-language-action models but is hindered by autoregressive latency unsuitable for real-time autonomous driving.

Method: Decompose CoT into a dependency graph of sub-tasks (e.g., object identification, traffic-rule summarization) that can be generated in parallel; perform multiple independent steps concurrently in a single forward pass; template-structured CoT with parallel decoding.

Result: 3-4× speedup in CoT generation and substantial end-to-end latency reduction across multiple model architectures; downstream performance improvements from CoT are preserved.

Conclusion: Parallel decoding for template-structured CoT is effective in speeding up real-time autonomous driving reasoning without sacrificing benefit of CoT.

Abstract: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning enhances the decision-making capabilities of vision-language-action models in autonomous driving, but its autoregressive nature introduces significant inference latency, making it impractical for real-time applications. To address this, we introduce FastDriveCoT, a novel parallel decoding method that accelerates template-structured CoT. Our approach decomposes the reasoning process into a dependency graph of distinct sub-tasks, such as identifying critical objects and summarizing traffic rules, some of which can be generated in parallel. By generating multiple independent reasoning steps concurrently within a single forward pass, we significantly reduce the number of sequential computations. Experiments demonstrate a 3-4$\times$ speedup in CoT generation and a substantial reduction in end-to-end latency across various model architectures, all while preserving the original downstream task improvements brought by incorporating CoT reasoning.

</details>


### [118] [Moving On, Even When You're Broken: Fail-Active Trajectory Generation via Diffusion Policies Conditioned on Embodiment and Task](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02895)
*Gilberto G. Briscoe-Martinez,Yaashia Gautam,Rahul Shetty,Anuj Pasricha,Marco M. Nicotra,Alessandro Roncone*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DEFT is a diffusion-based trajectory generator conditioned on embodiment and task constraints that enables fail-active manipulation under actuation failures, achieving robust real-world and simulation performance and outperforming baselines by up to 2x.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots operating in the presence of actuator faults risk unsafe or incomplete task execution. A fail-active controller capable of completing tasks despite failures can improve reliability and safety.

Method: DEFT uses a diffusion-based trajectory generator conditioned on the robot's current embodiment and task constraints to produce feasible trajectories under various actuator failure modes. It supports constrained and unconstrained motions and is evaluated on a 7-DoF arm in both simulated fault scenarios (thousands of joint-failure cases across tasks) and real-world tasks.

Result: In simulation, DEFT outperformed a baseline by up to 2× across thousands of joint-failure cases and generalized to unseen failures. In real-world experiments, DEFT succeeded on drawer manipulation and whiteboard erasing tasks where classical methods failed.

Conclusion: DEFT enables fail-active manipulation across arbitrary failure configurations and real-world deployments, demonstrating robustness and practical viability across failure types and tasks.

Abstract: Robot failure is detrimental and disruptive, often requiring human intervention to recover. Maintaining safe operation under impairment to achieve task completion, i.e. fail-active operation, is our target. Focusing on actuation failures, we introduce DEFT, a diffusion-based trajectory generator conditioned on the robot's current embodiment and task constraints. DEFT generalizes across failure types, supports constrained and unconstrained motions, and enables task completion under arbitrary failure. We evaluated DEFT in both simulation and real-world scenarios using a 7-DoF robotic arm. In simulation over thousands of joint-failure cases across multiple tasks, DEFT outperformed the baseline by up to 2 times. On failures unseen during training, it continued to outperform the baseline, indicating robust generalization in simulation. Further, we performed real-world evaluations on two multi-step tasks, drawer manipulation and whiteboard erasing. These experiments demonstrated DEFT succeeding on tasks where classical methods failed. Our results show that DEFT achieves fail-active manipulation across arbitrary failure configurations and real-world deployments.

</details>


### [119] [Modular Isoperimetric Soft Robotic Truss for Lunar Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02915)
*Mihai Stanciu,Isaac Weaver,Adam Rose,James Wade,Kaden Paxton,Chris Paul,Spencer Stowell,Nathan Usevitch*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A large-scale inflatable-truss robotic system using fabric tubes to form transformable triangles connected by a spherical vertex joint. It operates untethered after initial inflation, demonstrates a 12-DOF solar array with 60° tilt and 360° sweep, and a 14-DOF locomotion gait. With a stowed:deployed volume of 1:18.3, it enables modular, shape-adaptive lunar infrastructure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for lightweight, modular, reconfigurable, and untethered robotic systems capable of rapid deployment and in-situ assembly for lunar and deep-space operations. Key challenges include scalable deployable structures, power autonomy, and reliable joints under harsh space conditions.

Method: Triangular modules are formed by continuous inflated fabric tubes routed through two robotic roller units and a connecting unit. A spherical joint allows up to three triangles to join at a vertex, enabling truss assemblies beyond a single octahedron. Inflation locally reduces bending stiffness at pinch points; motors translate rollers along the tube to shift the pinch point by lengthening one edge and shortening another, preserving a constant perimeter (isoperimetric) without extra compressed air. After initial inflation, the system remains untethered. Demonstrations include a 12-DOF solar array capable of 60° tilt and 360° sweep, and a 14-DOF locomotion device using a step-and-slide gait.

Result: Stowed-to-deployed volume ratio of 1:18.3. The system supports a 12-DOF solar array with 60° tilt and full rotational sweep, and a 14-DOF locomotion mechanism via a step-and-slide gait, illustrating large-scale, shape-adaptive reconfigurability without continuous air supply.

Conclusion: The modular, inflatable-truss approach offers a scalable, untethered, shape-adaptive architecture suitable for sustainable lunar operations and broader space missions, enabling rapid deployment and reconfigurable infrastructure and locomotion.

Abstract: We introduce a large-scale robotic system designed as a lightweight, modular, and reconfigurable structure for lunar applications. The system consists of truss-like robotic triangles formed by continuous inflated fabric tubes routed through two robotic roller units and a connecting unit. A newly developed spherical joint enables up to three triangles to connect at a vertex, allowing construction of truss assemblies beyond a single octahedron. When deflated, the triangles compact to approximately the volume of the roller units, achieving a stowed-to-deployed volume ratio of 1:18.3. Upon inflation, the roller units pinch the tubes, locally reducing bending stiffness to form effective joints. Electric motors then translate the roller units along the tube, shifting the pinch point by lengthening one edge while shortening another at the same rate, thereby preserving a constant perimeter (isoperimetric). This shape-changing process requires no additional compressed air, enabling untethered operation after initial inflation. We demonstrate the system as a 12-degree-of-freedom solar array capable of tilting up to 60 degrees and sweeping 360 degrees, and as a 14-degree-of-freedom locomotion device using a step-and-slide gait. This modular, shape-adaptive system addresses key challenges for sustainable lunar operations and future space missions.

</details>


### [120] [Embodiment-Aware Generalist Specialist Distillation for Unified Humanoid Whole-Body Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02960)
*Quanquan Peng,Yunfeng Lin,Yufei Xue,Jiangmiao Pang,Weinan Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: EAGLE presents an iterative generalist–specialist distillation framework that learns a single policy to control multiple heterogeneous humanoid whole-body controllers without per-robot reward tuning, achieving robust cross-embodiment performance in both simulation and real robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Heterogeneous humanoids differ in dynamics, DoFs, and kinematic topology, making a single WBC policy transfer difficult. A generalist policy capable of handling multiple embodiments and enabling richer behaviors beyond walking is highly desirable for scalable, fleet-level humanoid control.

Method: Iteratively fork embodiment-specific specialists from the current generalist, refine each on its respective robot, then distill new skills back into the generalist using training on the pooled set of embodiments. Repeat until convergence. The approach eliminates per-robot reward tuning and validates on multiple hardware (Unitree H1, G1, Fourier N1) with five simulated robots and four real-world experiments.

Result: The method achieves high tracking accuracy and robustness across diverse humanoids and settings, outperforming alternative methods in both simulation and real-world tests, demonstrating scalable, fleet-level control capabilities.

Conclusion: EAGLE provides a unified, generalist policy capable of controlling heterogeneous humanoids through iterative specialization and distillation, reducing the need for robot-specific reward design and advancing scalable deployment of humanoid WBCs.

Abstract: Humanoid Whole-Body Controllers trained with reinforcement learning (RL) have recently achieved remarkable performance, yet many target a single robot embodiment. Variations in dynamics, degrees of freedom (DoFs), and kinematic topology still hinder a single policy from commanding diverse humanoids. Moreover, obtaining a generalist policy that not only transfers across embodiments but also supports richer behaviors-beyond simple walking to squatting, leaning-remains especially challenging. In this work, we tackle these obstacles by introducing EAGLE, an iterative generalist-specialist distillation framework that produces a single unified policy that controls multiple heterogeneous humanoids without per-robot reward tuning. During each cycle, embodiment-specific specialists are forked from the current generalist, refined on their respective robots, and new skills are distilled back into the generalist by training on the pooled embodiment set. Repeating this loop until performance convergence produces a robust Whole-Body Controller validated on robots such as Unitree H1, G1, and Fourier N1. We conducted experiments on five different robots in simulation and four in real-world settings. Through quantitative evaluations, EAGLE achieves high tracking accuracy and robustness compared to other methods, marking a step toward scalable, fleet-level humanoid control. See more details at https://eagle-wbc.github.io/

</details>


### [121] [RPL: Learning Robust Humanoid Perceptive Locomotion on Challenging Terrains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03002)
*Yuanhang Zhang,Younggyo Seo,Juyue Chen,Yifu Yuan,Koushil Sreenath,Pieter Abbeel,Carmelo Sferrazza,Karen Liu,Rocky Duan,Guanya Shi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RPL is a two-stage training framework for robust multi-directional humanoid locomotion on challenging terrains with payloads. It first learns terrain-specific experts using privileged height maps and then distills them into a transformer policy that fuses multi-view depth data. It introduces depth feature scaling by velocity and random side masking for robust distillation, and employs an efficient, parallel depth rendering pipeline. Real-world tests show locomotion with a 2 kg payload across slopes, variable stair steps, and stepping stones with gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable robust, directionally versatile humanoid locomotion on complex terrains with payloads, addressing the gap where training relies on privileged sensory information but deployment requires robust perception under varied viewpoints and sensor noise.

Method: Stage 1: train terrain-specific expert policies using privileged height-map observations to master decoupled locomotion and manipulation across terrains. Stage 2: distill these experts into a transformer-based policy that exploits multiple depth cameras. During distillation, apply depth feature scaling based on commanded velocity and random side masking to handle asymmetric depth observations. Develop an efficient multi-depth system that ray-casts against dynamic robot meshes and static terrain meshes in parallel, modeling latency, noise, and dropout.

Result: Real-world experiments demonstrate robust multi-directional locomotion with a 2 kg payload across diverse terrains, including 20° slopes, stairs with steps of 22, 25, and 30 cm, and 25 cm × 25 cm stepping stones separated by 60 cm gaps.

Conclusion: RPL provides a scalable framework for robust 2.5D/4D-like humanoid locomotion on challenging terrains with payloads by combining privileged-training with robust distillation and efficient depth rendering.

Abstract: Humanoid perceptive locomotion has made significant progress and shows great promise, yet achieving robust multi-directional locomotion on complex terrains remains underexplored. To tackle this challenge, we propose RPL, a two-stage training framework that enables multi-directional locomotion on challenging terrains, and remains robust with payloads. RPL first trains terrain-specific expert policies with privileged height map observations to master decoupled locomotion and manipulation skills across different terrains, and then distills them into a transformer policy that leverages multiple depth cameras to cover a wide range of views. During distillation, we introduce two techniques to robustify multi-directional locomotion, depth feature scaling based on velocity commands and random side masking, which are critical for asymmetric depth observations and unseen widths of terrains. For scalable depth distillation, we develop an efficient multi-depth system that ray-casts against both dynamic robot meshes and static terrain meshes in massively parallel environments, achieving a 5-times speedup over the depth rendering pipelines in existing simulators while modeling realistic sensor latency, noise, and dropout. Extensive real-world experiments demonstrate robust multi-directional locomotion with payloads (2kg) across challenging terrains, including 20° slopes, staircases with different step lengths (22 cm, 25 cm, 30 cm), and 25 cm by 25 cm stepping stones separated by 60 cm gaps.

</details>


### [122] [Training and Simulation of Quadrupedal Robot in Adaptive Stair Climbing for Indoor Firefighting: An End-to-End Reinforcement Learning Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03087)
*Baixiao Huang,Baiyu Huang,Yu Hou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A two-stage end-to-end RL framework trains quadruped stair-climbing and navigation skills, transferring from abstract pyramid terrain to realistic indoor stair geometries, using a centerline-based, unified learning approach with local height-map perception; shows cross-staircase generalization and analyzes performance across increasing stair difficulty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Indoor search tasks in hazardous environments require robust navigation and locomotion on varied stair geometries. End-to-end RL offers joint optimization of navigation and locomotion, and transfer from simple to realistic terrains can reduce data requirements and improve adaptability.

Method: Stage 1: Train Unitree Go2 quadrupeds on pyramid-stair terrain in Isaac Lab to learn stair-climbing through end-to-end RL. Stage 2: Transfer the learned policy to realistic indoor staircases (straight, L-shaped, spiral) within the Isaac Lab engine, training on the target geometries. Introduce a centerline-based navigation formulation that enables unified learning of navigation and locomotion without hierarchical planning, using only local height-map perception.

Result: Demonstrates policy generalization across diverse staircases using a single centerline-based representation and local height-map cues. Provides empirical analysis of success, efficiency, and failure modes as stair difficulty increases, indicating effective transfer from abstract terrain to realistic stair topologies in simulation.

Conclusion: A two-stage, end-to-end RL framework with centerline navigation can enable robust stair climbing and navigation across various indoor stair shapes, illustrating cross-domain generalization with minimal perceptual input. Further work should address real-world transfer, dynamic obstacles, and richer stair geometries.

Abstract: Quadruped robots are used for primary searches during the early stages of indoor fires. A typical primary search involves quickly and thoroughly looking for victims under hazardous conditions and monitoring flammable materials. However, situational awareness in complex indoor environments and rapid stair climbing across different staircases remain the main challenges for robot-assisted primary searches. In this project, we designed a two-stage end-to-end deep reinforcement learning (RL) approach to optimize both navigation and locomotion. In the first stage, the quadrupeds, Unitree Go2, were trained to climb stairs in Isaac Lab's pyramid-stair terrain. In the second stage, the quadrupeds were trained to climb various realistic indoor staircases in the Isaac Lab engine, with the learned policy transferred from the previous stage. These indoor staircases are straight, L-shaped, and spiral, to support climbing tasks in complex environments. This project explores how to balance navigation and locomotion and how end-to-end RL methods can enable quadrupeds to adapt to different stair shapes. Our main contributions are: (1) A two-stage end-to-end RL framework that transfers stair-climbing skills from abstract pyramid terrain to realistic indoor stair topologies. (2) A centerline-based navigation formulation that enables unified learning of navigation and locomotion without hierarchical planning. (3) Demonstration of policy generalization across diverse staircases using only local height-map perception. (4) An empirical analysis of success, efficiency, and failure modes under increasing stair difficulty.

</details>


### [123] [A Unified Candidate Set with Scene-Adaptive Refinement via Diffusion for End-to-End Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03112)
*Zhengfei Wu,Shuaixi Pan,Shuohan Chen,Shuo Yang,Yanjun Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CdDrive preserves a fixed trajectory vocabulary for stable routine coverage while augmenting with scene-adaptive, vocabulary-conditioned diffusion candidates; both are scored by a shared module, enabling robust performance across routine and highly interactive scenarios. A Horizon-Aware Trajectory Noise Adapter (HATNA) improves diffusion candidates’ smoothness and continuity. Evaluations on NAVSIM v1/v2 show leading performance with ablations confirming components; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end autonomous driving uses multimodal planning with multiple trajectory candidates. A fixed vocabulary ensures coverage in common scenarios but may miss optimal options in complex interactions; scene-adaptive refinement can over-correct in simple cases. There is a need to integrate stable vocabulary with adaptive generation while controlling perturbations.

Method: Maintain original vocabulary candidates and generate additional scene-adaptive candidates via vocabulary-conditioned diffusion denoising. Jointly score both candidate sets with a shared selection module. Introduce HATNA to improve diffusion candidates' smoothness via temporal smoothing and horizon-aware noise modulation. Evaluate on NAVSIM v1/v2 and perform ablations; code released.

Result: CdDrive achieves leading performance on NAVSIM v1 and v2 benchmarks. Ablation studies demonstrate the contribution of preserving vocabulary, diffusion augmentation, shared scoring, and the HATNA component to accuracy and trajectory quality.

Conclusion: The combination of a stable trajectory vocabulary with diffusion-based, vocabulary-conditioned augmentation and horizon-aware smoothing yields robust and high-quality planning across routine and highly interactive driving scenarios. The approach offers a practical path to balance coverage and adaptability in multimodal trajectory planning.

Abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving is increasingly adopting a multimodal planning paradigm that generates multiple trajectory candidates and selects the final plan, making candidate-set design critical. A fixed trajectory vocabulary provides stable coverage in routine driving but often misses optimal solutions in complex interactions, while scene-adaptive refinement can cause over-correction in simple scenarios by unnecessarily perturbing already strong vocabulary trajectories.We propose CdDrive, which preserves the original vocabulary candidates and augments them with scene-adaptive candidates generated by vocabulary-conditioned diffusion denoising. Both candidate types are jointly scored by a shared selection module, enabling reliable performance across routine and highly interactive scenarios. We further introduce HATNA (Horizon-Aware Trajectory Noise Adapter) to improve the smoothness and geometric continuity of diffusion candidates via temporal smoothing and horizon-aware noise modulation. Experiments on NAVSIM v1 and NAVSIM v2 demonstrate leading performance, and ablations verify the contribution of each component. Code: https://github.com/WWW-TJ/CdDrive.

</details>


### [124] [Multi-function Robotized Surgical Dissector for Endoscopic Pulmonary Thromboendarterectomy: Preclinical Study and Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03147)
*Runfeng Zhu,Xin Zhong,Qingxiang Zhao,Jing Lin,Zhong Wu,Kang Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A slender concentric push/pull robot (CPPR) dissector for pulmonary thromboendarterectomy (PTE) enables endoscopic access to deep, thin branch pulmonary arteries with a 3.5 mm diameter, dual channels, and internal camera wiring. An optimization-based kinematics model achieves ~2 mm tip-position accuracy over a 60 mm tool length; validated with physics tests and ex vivo porcine lung simulations, suggesting potential to upgrade traditional PTE to endoscopic PTE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chronic severe pulmonary thromboembolism requires PTE, but conventional rigid dissectors lack distal dexterity to reach small PA branches. There is a need for a slender, dexterous, endoscopic-capable tool to access tortuous distal PA branches.

Method: Develop a hollow, slender CPPR-based dissector with 3.5 mm outer diameter, two internal channels for irrigation and tip tool, and space for an endoscopic camera signal. Implement an optimization-based kinematics model to achieve precise tip positioning under open-loop control (2 mm accuracy along 60 mm length). Evaluate mechanical properties (stiffness, accuracy, maneuverability) and perform ex vivo porcine lung surgery simulations to assess dexterity and applicability to endoscopic PTE.

Result: Characterized basic mechanical performance (stiffness, motion accuracy, maneuverability). Demonstrated dexterity and advantages for PTE through ex vivo porcine lung simulations, indicating feasibility of endoscopic PTE.

Conclusion: The CPPR-based slender dissector shows promise to upgrade PTE to an endoscopic procedure by enabling deep, dexterous manipulation in thin PA branches, with validated kinematic accuracy and ex vivo proof-of-concept.

Abstract: Patients suffering chronic severe pulmonary thromboembolism need Pulmonary Thromboendarterectomy (PTE) to remove the thromb and intima located inside pulmonary artery (PA). During the surgery, a surgeon holds tweezers and a dissector to delicately strip the blockage, but available tools for this surgery are rigid and straight, lacking distal dexterity to access into thin branches of PA. Therefore, this work presents a novel robotized dissector based on concentric push/pull robot (CPPR) structure, enabling entering deep thin branch of tortuous PA. Compared with conventional rigid dissectors, our design characterizes slenderness and dual-segment-bending dexterity. Owing to the hollow and thin-walled structure of the CPPR-based dissector as it has a slender body of 3.5mm in diameter, the central lumen accommodates two channels for irrigation and tip tool, and space for endoscopic camera's signal wire. To provide accurate surgical manipulation, optimization-based kinematics model was established, realizing a 2mm accuracy in positioning the tip tool (60mm length) under open-loop control strategy. As such, with the endoscopic camera, traditional PTE is possible to be upgraded as endoscopic PTE. Basic physic performance of the robotized dissector including stiffness, motion accuracy and maneuverability was evaluated through experiments. Surgery simulation on ex vivo porcine lung also demonstrates its dexterity and notable advantages in PTE.

</details>


### [125] [When Attention Betrays: Erasing Backdoor Attacks in Robotic Policies by Reconstructing Visual Tokens](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03153)
*Xuetao Li,Pinhan Fu,Wenke Huang,Nengyuan Pan,Songhua Yang,Kaiyan Zhao,Guancheng Wan,Mengde Li,Jifeng Xuan,Miao Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Bera is a test-time defense against backdoors in vision-language-action (VLA) models for robotics. It detects anomalous attention cues at deep layers, localizes latent-space anomalies, masks suspicious regions, and reconstructs clean images to disrupt trigger-action mappings, all without retraining. It maintains nominal performance while substantially reducing backdoor success across tasks and platforms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Downstream fine-tuning of VLA models in robotics is vulnerable to backdoors trained on poisoned data. Current defenses either lack mechanistic insight into multimodal backdoors or require expensive full-model retraining. There is a need for an efficient, training-free defense that preserves performance.

Method: At test time, identify tokens with anomalous attention via latent-space localization in deep layers; mask suspicious regions using deep-layer cues; reconstruct a trigger-free image to break the trigger-unsafe-action mapping; no retraining or training-pipeline changes required. Evaluate across embodied platforms and tasks.

Result: Bera preserves nominal performance, significantly reduces backdoor attack success rates, and restores benign behavior from backdoored outputs across multiple embodied platforms.

Conclusion: Bera offers a practical, training-free defense for robotic VLA systems by erasing backdoors at inference through attention-based anomaly detection, region masking, and image reconstruction, enhancing security with minimal impact on functionality.

Abstract: Downstream fine-tuning of vision-language-action (VLA) models enhances robotics, yet exposes the pipeline to backdoor risks. Attackers can pretrain VLAs on poisoned data to implant backdoors that remain stealthy but can trigger harmful behavior during inference. However, existing defenses either lack mechanistic insight into multimodal backdoors or impose prohibitive computational costs via full-model retraining. To this end, we uncover a deep-layer attention grabbing mechanism: backdoors redirect late-stage attention and form compact embedding clusters near the clean manifold. Leveraging this insight, we introduce Bera, a test-time backdoor erasure framework that detects tokens with anomalous attention via latent-space localization, masks suspicious regions using deep-layer cues, and reconstructs a trigger-free image to break the trigger-unsafe-action mapping while restoring correct behavior. Unlike prior defenses, Bera requires neither retraining of VLAs nor any changes to the training pipeline. Extensive experiments across multiple embodied platforms and tasks show that Bera effectively maintains nominal performance, significantly reduces attack success rates, and consistently restores benign behavior from backdoored outputs, thereby offering a robust and practical defense mechanism for securing robotic systems.

</details>


### [126] [Estimation of Ground Reaction Forces from Kinematic Data during Locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03177)
*Gautami Golani,Dong Anh Khoa To,Ananda Sidarta,Arun-Kumar Kaliya-Perumal,Oliver Roberts,Lek Syn Lim,Jim Patton,Domenico Campolo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A marker-based, force-plate-free framework estimates ground reaction forces (GRFs) from kinematic data by computing the center of mass (CoM) from 16 body segments and decomposing GRFs via a minimization-based approach, enabling gait analysis without force plates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Force plates limit clinical workflows due to setup complexity and expense; a kinematic-only method would broaden accessibility for gait analysis and clinical deployment.

Method: Use motion capture-derived kinematics from 16 body segments to estimate CoM; compute GRFs derived from these estimates; decompose GRFs into components using a minimization-based optimization; identify gait stance phases.

Result: Experimental results show viability of estimating CoM and GRFs from kinematic data alone, supporting force-plate-free gait analysis.

Conclusion: A force-plate-free approach can provide clinically meaningful kinetic measures from marker data, enabling wider deployment of gait analysis without dedicated force plates.

Abstract: Ground reaction forces (GRFs) provide fundamental insight into human gait mechanics and are widely used to assess joint loading, limb symmetry, balance control, and motor function. Despite their clinical relevance, the use of GRF remains underutilised in clinical workflows due to the practical limitations of force plate systems. In this work, we present a force-plate-free approach for estimating GRFs using only marker-based motion capture data. This kinematics only method to estimate and decompose GRF makes it well suited for widespread clinical depolyment. By using kinematics from sixteen body segments, we estimate the centre of mass (CoM) and compute GRFs, which are subsequently decomposed into individual components through a minimization-based approach. Through this framework, we can identify gait stance phases and provide access to clinically meaningful kinetic measures without a dedicated force plate system. Experimental results demonstrate the viability of CoM and GRF estimation based solely on kinematic data, supporting force-plate-free gait analysis.

</details>


### [127] [Hierarchical Proportion Models for Motion Generation via Integration of Motion Primitives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03188)
*Yu-Han Shu,Toshiaki Tsuji,Sho Sakaino*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Hierarchical imitation learning with motion primitives and proportion-based synthesis uses a two-layer architecture to improve data efficiency and adaptability. It demonstrates three proportion models and shows that sampling-based and playback-based variants offer stability and adaptability in real-robot pick-and-place tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve data efficiency and adaptability of imitation learning for complex, long-horizon robotic tasks by combining reusable motion primitives with a proportion-based integration mechanism.

Method: Two-layer IL framework: upper layer does long-term planning; lower layer learns individual motion primitives. Proportions determine how primitives are blended. Three variants explore trade-offs: learning-based proportion model (trainable proportions), sampling-based proportion model (proportions sampled during execution), and playback-based proportion model (proportions driven by playback trajectories).

Result: Real-robot pick-and-place experiments show the method can generate complex motions not present in the primitive set. The sampling-based and playback-based proportion models deliver more stable and adaptable motion generation than a standard hierarchical model.

Conclusion: Proportion-based motion integration enhances data efficiency and adaptability in imitation learning. Among the variants, sampling-based and playback-based approaches offer favorable stability and adaptability. This approach is promising for practical robotic tasks but may require further investigation into proportion estimation and scalability.

Abstract: Imitation learning (IL) enables robots to acquire human-like motion skills from demonstrations, but it still requires extensive high-quality data and retraining to handle complex or long-horizon tasks. To improve data efficiency and adaptability, this study proposes a hierarchical IL framework that integrates motion primitives with proportion-based motion synthesis. The proposed method employs a two-layer architecture, where the upper layer performs long-term planning, while a set of lower-layer models learn individual motion primitives, which are combined according to specific proportions. Three model variants are introduced to explore different trade-offs between learning flexibility, computational cost, and adaptability: a learning-based proportion model, a sampling-based proportion model, and a playback-based proportion model, which differ in how the proportions are determined and whether the upper layer is trainable. Through real-robot pick-and-place experiments, the proposed models successfully generated complex motions not included in the primitive set. The sampling-based and playback-based proportion models achieved more stable and adaptable motion generation than the standard hierarchical model, demonstrating the effectiveness of proportion-based motion integration for practical robot learning.

</details>


### [128] [HUSKY: Humanoid Skateboarding System via Physics-Aware Whole-Body Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03205)
*Jinrui Han,Dewei Wang,Chenyun Zhang,Xinzhe Liu,Ping Luo,Chenjia Bai,Xuelong Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes HUSKY, a learning-based physics-aware whole-body control framework for humanoid skateboarding to handle underactuated, non-holonomic dynamics; introduces tilt-steer coupling, AMP-based pushing motions, physics-guided lean-to-steer, and trajectory-guided transitions; validated on Unitree G1 with real-world maneuvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of dynamic, highly interactive, underactuated humanoid robotics tasks beyond static environments—specifically stable dynamic maneuvering on a skateboard with non-holonomic constraints and human-object coupling.

Method: Model the board dynamics, propose HUSKY architecture; integrate physics-aware whole-body control; incorporate Adversarial Motion Priors to learn pushing; use physics-guided lean-to-steer; trajectory-guided mode switching.

Result: Experimental validation on Unitree G1 shows stable and agile maneuvering on skateboards in real-world scenarios; project page provided.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates that integrating principled dynamics modeling with data-driven priors and trajectory guidance enables robust dynamic locomotion tasks in humanoid-vehicle systems, enabling future exploration of similar dynamic human-robot interactions.

Abstract: While current humanoid whole-body control frameworks predominantly rely on the static environment assumptions, addressing tasks characterized by high dynamism and complex interactions presents a formidable challenge. In this paper, we address humanoid skateboarding, a highly challenging task requiring stable dynamic maneuvering on an underactuated wheeled platform. This integrated system is governed by non-holonomic constraints and tightly coupled human-object interactions. Successfully executing this task requires simultaneous mastery of hybrid contact dynamics and robust balance control on a mechanically coupled, dynamically unstable skateboard. To overcome the aforementioned challenges, we propose HUSKY, a learning-based framework that integrates humanoid-skateboard system modeling and physics-aware whole-body control. We first model the coupling relationship between board tilt and truck steering angles, enabling a principled analysis of system dynamics. Building upon this, HUSKY leverages Adversarial Motion Priors (AMP) to learn human-like pushing motions and employs a physics-guided, heading-oriented strategy for lean-to-steer behaviors. Moreover, a trajectory-guided mechanism ensures smooth and stable transitions between pushing and steering. Experimental results on the Unitree G1 humanoid platform demonstrate that our framework enables stable and agile maneuvering on skateboards in real-world scenarios. The project page is available on https://husky-humanoid.github.io/.

</details>


### [129] [Depth Completion in Unseen Field Robotics Environments Using Extremely Sparse Depth Measurements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03209)
*Marco Job,Thomas Stastny,Eleni Kelasidi,Roland Siegwart,Michael Pantic*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a depth completion approach for field robotics: train on synthetic data using sparse depth cues to predict dense metric depth; uses a synthetic dataset pipeline with textured 3D meshes and novel viewpoint synthesis; achieves 53 ms per frame on Jetson AGX Orin; shows competitive real-world performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of reliable scale cues, texture scarcity, and limited datasets that hinder monocular depth estimation in unstructured field environments.

Method: A depth completion model trained on synthetic data, leveraging extremely sparse depth measurements as input and producing dense metric depth. A synthetic pipeline generates multiple realistic datasets using textured SfM meshes and photorealistic rendering with novel viewpoints to simulate field robotics scenarios.

Result: End-to-end latency of 53 ms/frame on Nvidia Jetson AGX Orin; competitive performance across diverse real-world field robotics scenarios.

Conclusion: Synthetic-data-driven depth completion can enable real-time dense depth estimation in field robotics with monocular cameras, addressing scale and texture issues while delivering real-time performance on embedded hardware.

Abstract: Autonomous field robots operating in unstructured environments require robust perception to ensure safe and reliable operations. Recent advances in monocular depth estimation have demonstrated the potential of low-cost cameras as depth sensors; however, their adoption in field robotics remains limited due to the absence of reliable scale cues, ambiguous or low-texture conditions, and the scarcity of large-scale datasets. To address these challenges, we propose a depth completion model that trains on synthetic data and uses extremely sparse measurements from depth sensors to predict dense metric depth in unseen field robotics environments. A synthetic dataset generation pipeline tailored to field robotics enables the creation of multiple realistic datasets for training purposes. This dataset generation approach utilizes textured 3D meshes from Structure from Motion and photorealistic rendering with novel viewpoint synthesis to simulate diverse field robotics scenarios. Our approach achieves an end-to-end latency of 53 ms per frame on a Nvidia Jetson AGX Orin, enabling real-time deployment on embedded platforms. Extensive evaluation demonstrates competitive performance across diverse real-world field robotics scenarios.

</details>


### [130] [Omnidirectional Solid-State mmWave Radar Perception for UAV Power Line Collision Avoidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03229)
*Nicolaj Haarhøj Malle,Emad Ebeid*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: mmWave multi-module radar for omnidirectional UAV power-line sensing enabling detection and avoidance at ~10 m, 1.2 mm wire detection, supports safe autonomous and manual flight.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce collision risk with power lines for human and autonomous UAVs; conventional sensors offer limited omnidirectional coverage and range; need robust perception in adverse weather and clutter.

Method: Mount several compact solid-state mmWave radar modules to synthesize an omnidirectional field of view around a small UAV; characterize sensing behavior in power-line environments; develop a robust detection-and-avoidance algorithm tailored to the radar behavior; validate via field experiments on real power lines; measure detection at up to 10 m, avoidance at >10 m/s, wire diameter detection of 1.2 mm.

Result: Field experiments demonstrate reliable detection and collision avoidance; 10 m detection range; successful avoidance at flight speeds up to 10 m/s; detection of 1.2 mm wires; supports addition of safety layer for UAVs.

Conclusion: Omnidirectional MMWave radar array is viable as an extra safety layer for UAVs operating near power lines; can enhance autonomy and manual flight safety in power-line environments.

Abstract: Detecting and estimating distances to power lines is a challenge for both human UAV pilots and autonomous systems, which increases the risk of unintended collisions. We present a mmWave radar-based perception system that provides spherical sensing coverage around a small UAV for robust power line detection and avoidance. The system integrates multiple compact solid-state mmWave radar modules to synthesize an omnidirectional field of view while remaining lightweight. We characterize the sensing behavior of this omnidirectional radar arrangement in power line environments and develop a robust detection-and-avoidance algorithm tailored to that behavior. Field experiments on real power lines demonstrate reliable detection at ranges up to 10 m, successful avoidance maneuvers at flight speeds upwards of 10 m/s, and detection of wires as thin as 1.2 mm in diameter. These results indicate the approach's suitability as an additional safety layer for both autonomous and manual UAV flight.

</details>


### [131] [A thin and soft optical tactile sensor for highly sensitive object perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03248)
*Yanchen Shen,Kohei Tsuji,Haruto Koizumi,Jiseon Hong,Tomoaki Niiyama,Hiroyuki Kuwabara,Hayato Ishida,Jun Hiramitsu,Mitsuhito Mase,Satoshi Sunada*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A thin, alignment-free speckle-based optical tactile sensor uses deformation-induced speckle changes in soft silicone to achieve 40 mN RMSE in force sensing and 93.3% texture classification accuracy across nine textures, enabling compact tactile sensing for soft robots and wearables.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for compact, alignment-tolerant tactile sensing in soft robotics and wearables, overcoming bulky lens-camera optical assemblies.

Method: A soft silicone tactile sensor generates internal speckle patterns; deformation alters speckle, enabling force estimation via regression and texture recognition via machine learning, with an alignment-free, compact optical readout.

Result: RMSE of 40 mN for force measurement and 93.33% classification accuracy over nine textured surfaces (including Mahjong tiles).

Conclusion: Demonstrates a compact, fabricable, and mechanically compliant optical tactile sensing paradigm that links optical speckle readouts with flexible, shape-adaptive architectures for soft robotics and wearable haptics.

Abstract: Tactile sensing is crucial in robotics and wearable devices for safe perception and interaction with the environment. Optical tactile sensors have emerged as promising solutions, as they are immune to electromagnetic interference and have high spatial resolution. However, existing optical approaches, particularly vision-based tactile sensors, rely on complex optical assemblies that involve lenses and cameras, resulting in bulky, rigid, and alignment-sensitive designs. In this study, we present a thin, compact, and soft optical tactile sensor featuring an alignment-free configuration. The soft optical sensor operates by capturing deformation-induced changes in speckle patterns generated within a soft silicone material, thereby enabling precise force measurements and texture recognition via machine learning. The experimental results show a root-mean-square error of 40 mN in the force measurement and a classification accuracy of 93.33% over nine classes of textured surfaces, including Mahjong tiles. The proposed speckle-based approach provides a compact, easily fabricated, and mechanically compliant platform that bridges optical sensing with flexible shape-adaptive architectures, thereby demonstrating its potential as a novel tactile-sensing paradigm for soft robotics and wearable haptic interfaces.

</details>


### [132] [Collision Detection with Analytical Derivatives of Contact Kinematics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03250)
*Anup Teejo Mathew,Anees Peringal,Daniele Caradonna,Frederic Boyer,Federico Renda*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: iDCOL is a differentiable collision detection framework using strictly convex implicit surfaces, leveraging geometric regularization to ensure smooth, unique contact maps, solved via a fixed-size nonlinear system and Newton method; analytical derivatives are obtained with the Implicit Function Theorem; open-source C++ implementation with validation on multi-body and soft-robot interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Degenerate geometries yield non-smooth contact maps, hindering gradient-based optimization in robotics; a smooth, unique contact kinematics map is needed for differentiable planning and control.

Method: Regularize shapes into strictly convex implicit surfaces via geometric scaling-based convex optimization to derive a fixed-size nonlinear system; apply the Implicit Function Theorem to the residual to obtain analytic derivatives; develop a fast Newton-based solver; provide open-source C++ implementation.

Result: Demonstrates robustness across extensive collision simulations and benchmarking; shows applicability to gradient-based kinematic path planning and differentiable contact physics, including multi-body rigid collisions and soft-robot interaction.

Conclusion: Geometric regularization to convex implicit representations restores smooth, unique contact maps and enables differentiable contact kinematics; iDCOL offers an efficient solver and open-source implementation suitable for differentiable robotics.

Abstract: Differentiable contact kinematics are essential for gradient-based methods in robotics, yet the mapping from robot state to contact distance, location, and normal becomes non-smooth in degenerate configurations of shapes with zero or undefined curvature. We address this inherent limitation by selectively regularizing such geometries into strictly convex implicit representations, restoring uniqueness and smoothness of the contact map. Leveraging this geometric regularization, we develop iDCOL, an implicit differentiable collision detection and contact kinematics framework. iDCOL represents colliding bodies using strictly convex implicit surfaces and computes collision detection and contact kinematics by solving a fixed-size nonlinear system derived from a geometric scaling-based convex optimization formulation. By applying the Implicit Function Theorem to the resulting system residual, we derive analytical derivatives of the contact kinematic quantities. We develop a fast Newton-based solver for iDCOL and provide an open-source C++ implementation of the framework. The robustness of the approach is evaluated through extensive collision simulations and benchmarking, and applicability is demonstrated in gradient-based kinematic path planning and differentiable contact physics, including multi-body rigid collisions and a soft-robot interaction example.

</details>


### [133] [RDT2: Exploring the Scaling Limit of UMI Data Towards Zero-Shot Cross-Embodiment Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03310)
*Songming Liu,Bangguo Li,Kai Ma,Lingxuan Wu,Hengkai Tan,Xiao Ouyang,Hang Su,Jun Zhu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RDT2 is a 7B vision-language-model-based robotic foundation model enabling zero-shot deployment to unseen embodiments for open-vocabulary tasks, trained on 10k+ hours of demonstrations via an embodiment-agnostic interface (UMI) using a three-stage recipe that aligns discrete language with continuous control (RVQ, flow-matching, distillation). It claims zero-shot generalization across objects, scenes, instructions, and platforms, and outperforms baselines on dexterous, long-horizon tasks (e.g., table tennis).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data scarcity, architectural inefficiency, and poor cross-embodiment generalization in Vision-Language-Action robotics by building a scalable, embodiment-agnostic foundation model capable of zero-shot transfer to new hardware and tasks.

Method: Collect a large, diverse open-source robotic dataset (~10k+ hours) using an enhanced embodiment-agnostic UMI. Train a 7B VLM with a three-stage recipe: Residual Vector Quantization (RVQ) to bridge discrete linguistic tokens with continuous control, flow-matching for sequence alignment in action spaces, and distillation for real-time inference.

Result: RDT2 purportedly generalizes zero-shot to unseen objects, scenes, instructions, and robotic platforms. It reportedly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on dexterous, long-horizon, and dynamic tasks such as playing table tennis.

Conclusion: RDT2 demonstrates strong cross-domain generalization and real-time capability, representing a significant step toward universal, platform-agnostic robotic intelligence. The approach hinges on large-scale data, embodiment-agnostic interfaces, and a discrete-continuous alignment strategy; further evaluation details (metrics, ablations, and limits) would clarify robustness and generalization bounds.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models hold promise for generalist robotics but currently struggle with data scarcity, architectural inefficiencies, and the inability to generalize across different hardware platforms. We introduce RDT2, a robotic foundation model built upon a 7B parameter VLM designed to enable zero-shot deployment on novel embodiments for open-vocabulary tasks. To achieve this, we collected one of the largest open-source robotic datasets--over 10,000 hours of demonstrations in diverse families--using an enhanced, embodiment-agnostic Universal Manipulation Interface (UMI). Our approach employs a novel three-stage training recipe that aligns discrete linguistic knowledge with continuous control via Residual Vector Quantization (RVQ), flow-matching, and distillation for real-time inference. Consequently, RDT2 becomes one of the first models that simultaneously zero-shot generalizes to unseen objects, scenes, instructions, and even robotic platforms. Besides, it outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in dexterous, long-horizon, and dynamic downstream tasks like playing table tennis. See https://rdt-robotics.github.io/rdt2/ for more information.

</details>


### [134] [Manipulation via Force Distribution at Contact](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03350)
*Haegu Lee,Yitaek Kim,Casper Hewson Rask,Christoffer Sloth*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces Force-Distributed Line Contact (FDLC) to replace point contact in contact-rich manipulation, combined with a bi-level optimization framework (lower-level contact force optimization, upper-level iLQR trajectory optimization) to yield efficient, robust trajectories demonstrated on a box-rotating task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Point-contact models are computationally efficient but fail to capture frictional dynamics and torque generation observed in human manipulation; a more expressive contact model is needed for robust contact-rich manipulation.

Method: Define and implement the FDLC model; construct a bi-level optimization framework where the lower level solves an optimization problem for contact force distribution along the contact line, and the upper level uses iLQR for trajectory optimization; perform comparative analysis against conventional point-contact models; validate on a box-rotation task.

Result: FDLC supports non-uniform force distributions along the contact line, enabling more efficient and robust trajectories with lower control effort and less robotic motion compared to point-contact models.

Conclusion: FDLC provides a more expressive and efficient framework for contact-rich manipulation than point-contact models, improving trajectory efficiency and robustness; future work could extend to more complex contact geometries and real-world hardware validation.

Abstract: Efficient and robust trajectories play a crucial role in contact-rich manipulation, which demands accurate mod- eling of object-robot interactions. Many existing approaches rely on point contact models due to their computational effi- ciency.
