Simple contact models are computationally efficient but inherently limited for achieving human-like, contact-rich ma- nipulation, as they fail to capture key frictional dynamics and torque generation observed in human manipulation. This study introduces a Force-Distributed Line Contact (FDLC) model in contact-rich manipulation and compares it against conventional point contact models. A bi-level optimization framework is constructed, in which the lower-level solves an optimization problem for contact force computation, and the upper-level optimization applies iLQR for trajectory optimization. Through this framework, the limitations of point contact are demon- strated, and the benefits of the FDLC in generating efficient and robust trajectories are established. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is validated by a box rotating task, demonstrating that FDLC enables trajectories generated via non-uniform force distributions along the contact line, while requiring lower control effort and less motion of the robot.

</details>


### [135] [Learning-based Adaptive Control of Quadruped Robots for Active Stabilization on Moving Platforms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03367)
*Minsung Yoon,Heechan Shin,Jeil Jeong,Sung-Eui Yoon*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A learning-based active stabilization framework enables a quadruped robot to balance on six-DOF moving platforms using a self-balancing policy and state estimators, trained across diverse platform motions; it outperforms three baselines, with ablations validating each component.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Balancing on moving platforms with independent platform motions induces complex, varying inertia disturbances; robust stabilization requires adaptive posture control and accurate state inference from proprioception.

Method: A self-balancing policy adjusts the robot’s posture in response to platform motion. Proprioceptive sensors feed estimators that infer robot and platform states. Platform trajectory generation and scheduling generate systematic training across diverse platform motions.

Result: The approach achieves superior balancing performance across multiple metrics compared to three baselines; ablation studies and estimator evaluations substantiate the contribution and effectiveness of each component.

Conclusion: LAS-MP demonstrates effective platform-aware stabilization for quadrupeds on moving platforms; its component teamwork—policy, estimators, and trajectory-based training—yields robust performance, with potential for real-world deployment and extension to broader platforms.

Abstract: A quadruped robot faces balancing challenges on a six-degrees-of-freedom moving platform, like subways, buses, airplanes, and yachts, due to independent platform motions and resultant diverse inertia forces on the robot. To alleviate these challenges, we present the Learning-based Active Stabilization on Moving Platforms (\textit{LAS-MP}), featuring a self-balancing policy and system state estimators. The policy adaptively adjusts the robot's posture in response to the platform's motion. The estimators infer robot and platform states based on proprioceptive sensor data. For a systematic training scheme across various platform motions, we introduce platform trajectory generation and scheduling methods. Our evaluation demonstrates superior balancing performance across multiple metrics compared to three baselines. Furthermore, we conduct a detailed analysis of the \textit{LAS-MP}, including ablation studies and evaluation of the estimators, to validate the effectiveness of each component.

</details>


### [136] [PlanTRansformer: Unified Prediction and Planning with Goal-conditioned Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03376)
*Constantin Selzer,Fabina B. Flohr*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PlanTRansformer (PTR) unifies prediction and planning via a Gaussian Mixture Transformer, incorporating goal-conditioning, feasibility, interaction, and lane topology; uses teacher-student masking to align predictions with planning when intents are unknown; shows notable gains and is architecture-agnostic.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the mismatch between multimodal, intent-unspecified predictions and constraint-driven planning by enforcing feasibility and collision avoidance while aligning training objectives.

Method: A unified Gaussian Mixture Transformer (PlanTRansformer) that performs goal-conditioned prediction with dynamic feasibility, interaction awareness, and lane-level topology reasoning; employs a teacher-student training strategy that progressively masks surrounding agent commands to simulate inference where intents are unavailable; architecture-agnostic to enable integration with existing Transformer-based predictors.

Result: Quantitative gains: 4.3% marginal mAP and 3.5% joint mAP improvements over Motion Transformer (MTR); 15.5% reduction in planning error at 5s horizon compared with GameFormer.

Conclusion: PTR provides a cohesive, architecture-agnostic framework that aligns prediction with planning constraints, improving feasibility, interaction handling, and planning performance; applicable to a range of Transformer-based predictors.

Abstract: Trajectory prediction and planning are fundamental yet disconnected components in autonomous driving. Prediction models forecast surrounding agent motion under unknown intentions, producing multimodal distributions, while planning assumes known ego objectives and generates deterministic trajectories. This mismatch creates a critical bottleneck: prediction lacks supervision for agent intentions, while planning requires this information. Existing prediction models, despite strong benchmarking performance, often remain disconnected from planning constraints such as collision avoidance and dynamic feasibility. We introduce Plan TRansformer (PTR), a unified Gaussian Mixture Transformer framework integrating goal-conditioned prediction, dynamic feasibility, interaction awareness, and lane-level topology reasoning. A teacher-student training strategy progressively masks surrounding agent commands during training to align with inference conditions where agent intentions are unavailable. PTR achieves 4.3%/3.5% improvement in marginal/joint mAP compared to the baseline Motion Transformer (MTR) and 15.5% planning error reduction at 5s horizon compared to GameFormer. The architecture-agnostic design enables application to diverse Transformer-based prediction models. Project Website: https://github.com/SelzerConst/PlanTRansformer

</details>


### [137] [Enhancing Navigation Efficiency of Quadruped Robots via Leveraging Personal Transportation Platforms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03397)
*Minsung Yoon,Sung-Eui Yoon*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RL-ATR enables quadruped robots to ride a transporter using a learned policy and state estimators to extend long-range navigation, with simulation validation and ablations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quadrupeds struggle with long-range efficiency due to legged locomotion energy costs and navigation limits. Incorporating a transporter (e.g., Segway-like device) can extend range, but requires policy coordination with transporter dynamics and resolving sensor ambiguities in non-inertial frames.

Method: Develop a reinforcement learning policy for transporter riding that accounts for transporter control dynamics. Introduce two state estimators to infer unobservable robot and transporter states and to resolve sensor ambiguities in non-inertial frames. Validate via simulation across multiple transporter-robot models and perform ablations to quantify component contributions.

Result: Simulation demonstrates proficient command tracking across models and reduced energy consumption compared with legged locomotion. Ablation studies quantify contributions of individual components.

Conclusion: RL-ATR broadens quadruped locomotion modalities, potentially increasing operational range and efficiency via transporter-assisted mobility.

Abstract: Quadruped robots face limitations in long-range navigation efficiency due to their reliance on legs. To ameliorate the limitations, we introduce a Reinforcement Learning-based Active Transporter Riding method (\textit{RL-ATR}), inspired by humans' utilization of personal transporters, including Segways. The \textit{RL-ATR} features a transporter riding policy and two state estimators. The policy devises adequate maneuvering strategies according to transporter-specific control dynamics, while the estimators resolve sensor ambiguities in non-inertial frames by inferring unobservable robot and transporter states. Comprehensive evaluations in simulation validate proficient command tracking abilities across various transporter-robot models and reduced energy consumption compared to legged locomotion. Moreover, we conduct ablation studies to quantify individual component contributions within the \textit{RL-ATR}. This riding ability could broaden the locomotion modalities of quadruped robots, potentially expanding the operational range and efficiency.

</details>


### [138] [Deep-Learning-Based Control of a Decoupled Two-Segment Continuum Robot for Endoscopic Submucosal Dissection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03406)
*Yuancheng Shao,Yao Zhang,Jia Gu,Zixi Chen,Di Wu,Yuqiao Chen,Bo Lu,Wenjie Liu,Cesare Stefanini,Peng Qi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GRU-based control for a dual-segment continuum robot (DESectBot) enhances precision and performance in endoscopic submucosal dissection tasks compared with IK, MPC, FNN, and LSTM controllers, demonstrated through trajectory tracking, fixed-position pose control, peg transfer, and ex vivo demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD) is technically demanding and single-segment robotic tools offer limited dexterity. A dual-segment continuum robot with integrated forceps and GRU-based controllers is proposed to improve dexterity, precision, and reliability for ESD.

Method: Develop DESectBot, a decoupled dual-seg continuum robot with integrated surgical forceps enabling 6-DoF tip dexterity. Implement GRU-based controllers for simultaneous tip position and orientation control, and benchmark against Jacobian-based IK, MPC, FNN, and LSTM. Evaluate on nested-rectangle and Lissajous trajectory tracking, orientation control at fixed position (four poses), peg transfer task, and an ex vivo ESD demonstration.

Result: GRU/controller achieved lowest RMSE in position/orientation: 1.11 mm/4.62° and 0.81 mm/2.59° for two tracking tasks. Orientation control RMSE at fixed pose: 0.14 mm and 0.72°. Peg transfer: 100% success (120/120) with mean transfer time 11.8 s; standard deviation better than novices. Ex vivo demonstration showed sufficient stiffness to divide thick gastric mucosa and adequate workspace for large lesions. Overall, GRU-based control markedly improves precision, reliability, and usability in ESD training scenarios.

Conclusion: GRU-based control significantly enhances precision, reliability, and usability of DESectBot for ESD, outperforming alternative controllers in multiple task modalities and demonstrated feasibility in ex vivo tissue handling.

Abstract: Manual endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD) is technically demanding, and existing single-segment robotic tools offer limited dexterity. These limitations motivate the development of more advanced solutions. To address this, DESectBot, a novel dual segment continuum robot with a decoupled structure and integrated surgical forceps, enabling 6 degrees of freedom (DoFs) tip dexterity for improved lesion targeting in ESD, was developed in this work. Deep learning controllers based on gated recurrent units (GRUs) for simultaneous tip position and orientation control, effectively handling the nonlinear coupling between continuum segments, were proposed. The GRU controller was benchmarked against Jacobian based inverse kinematics, model predictive control (MPC), a feedforward neural network (FNN), and a long short-term memory (LSTM) network. In nested-rectangle and Lissajous trajectory tracking tasks, the GRU achieved the lowest position/orientation RMSEs: 1.11 mm/ 4.62° and 0.81 mm/ 2.59°, respectively. For orientation control at a fixed position (four target poses), the GRU attained a mean RMSE of 0.14 mm and 0.72°, outperforming all alternatives. In a peg transfer task, the GRU achieved a 100% success rate (120 success/120 attempts) with an average transfer time of 11.8s, the STD significantly outperforms novice-controlled systems. Additionally, an ex vivo ESD demonstration grasping, elevating, and resecting tissue as the scalpel completed the cut confirmed that DESectBot provides sufficient stiffness to divide thick gastric mucosa and an operative workspace adequate for large lesions.These results confirm that GRU-based control significantly enhances precision, reliability, and usability in ESD surgical training scenarios.

</details>


### [139] [Learning-based Initialization of Trajectory Optimization for Path-following Problems of Redundant Manipulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03418)
*Minsung Yoon,Mincheul Kang,Daehyung Park,Sung-Eui Yoon*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Learning-based initialization for trajectory optimization in redundant manipulators using example-guided reinforcement learning with a null-space imitation reward; improves TO optimality and efficiency in simulation and real 7-DoF experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TO performance hinges on initial trajectories; the search space is vast and task constraints are often unknown in configuration space, making high-quality initialization challenging and time-consuming.

Method: Propose a learning-based method that generates initial trajectories via example-guided reinforcement learning. Introduce a null-space projected imitation reward to enforce null-space constraints and capture kinematically feasible motions from expert demonstrations. Evaluate in simulation against three baselines and validate on a real 7-DoF manipulator.

Result: Demonstrates improved optimality, efficiency, and applicability of TO with the proposed initializer; performance exceeds baselines in simulation; feasible real-world execution with a 7-DoF manipulator.

Conclusion: The approach enables faster, higher-quality initial trajectories for TO in redundant manipulators, increasing practicality and bringing simulation results closer to real-world performance; potential for broader adoption with more demonstrations and diverse tasks.

Abstract: Trajectory optimization (TO) is an efficient tool to generate a redundant manipulator's joint trajectory following a 6-dimensional Cartesian path. The optimization performance largely depends on the quality of initial trajectories. However, the selection of a high-quality initial trajectory is non-trivial and requires a considerable time budget due to the extremely large space of the solution trajectories and the lack of prior knowledge about task constraints in configuration space. To alleviate the issue, we present a learning-based initial trajectory generation method that generates high-quality initial trajectories in a short time budget by adopting example-guided reinforcement learning. In addition, we suggest a null-space projected imitation reward to consider null-space constraints by efficiently learning kinematically feasible motion captured in expert demonstrations. Our statistical evaluation in simulation shows the improved optimality, efficiency, and applicability of TO when we plug in our method's output, compared with three other baselines. We also show the performance improvement and feasibility via real-world experiments with a seven-degree-of-freedom manipulator.

</details>


### [140] [ProAct: A Benchmark and Multimodal Framework for Structure-Aware Proactive Response](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03430)
*Xiaomeng Zhu,Fengming Zhu,Weijie Zhou,Ye Tian,Zhenlin Hu,Yufei Huang,Yuchun Guo,Xinyu Wu,Zhengyou Zhang,Fangzhen Lin,Xuantang Xiong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ProAct-75 introduces a 75-task benchmark with 91k annotated steps and explicit task graphs to train/evaluate proactive agents; ProAct-Helper is a baseline MLLM-based agent using state grounding and entropy-driven search to enable parallel action execution, achieving notable improvements over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Proactive agents require specialized datasets and baselines; current resources are insufficient for training and evaluating agents that continuously monitor the environment and act toward higher-level objectives like assistance and safety.

Method: Construct ProAct-75 with 75 tasks spanning domains (assistance, maintenance, safety monitoring); 91,581 step-level annotations enriched with explicit task graphs encoding dependencies and parallel execution; ProAct-Helper uses a multimodal LLM grounded in state detection and employs task-graph driven entropy-based heuristic search to select actions, enabling independent parallel threads rather than mirroring the human's next step.

Result: ProAct-Helper outperforms strong closed-source models, increasing trigger detection mF1 by 6.21%, saving 0.25 more steps in online one-step decision, and boosting the rate of parallel actions by 15.58%.

Conclusion: The proposed task-graph grounded framework demonstrates viability for proactive agents; ProAct-75 provides a rich resource for training/evaluating proactive behavior across domains, while ProAct-Helper demonstrates practical gains from state grounding and parallelism via task graphs.

Abstract: While passive agents merely follow instructions, proactive agents align with higher-level objectives, such as assistance and safety by continuously monitoring the environment to determine when and how to act. However, developing proactive agents is hindered by the lack of specialized resources. To address this, we introduce ProAct-75, a benchmark designed to train and evaluate proactive agents across diverse domains, including assistance, maintenance, and safety monitoring. Spanning 75 tasks, our dataset features 91,581 step-level annotations enriched with explicit task graphs. These graphs encode step dependencies and parallel execution possibilities, providing the structural grounding necessary for complex decision-making. Building on this benchmark, we propose ProAct-Helper, a reference baseline powered by a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) that grounds decision-making in state detection, and leveraging task graphs to enable entropy-driven heuristic search for action selection, allowing agents to execute parallel threads independently rather than mirroring the human's next step. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ProAct-Helper outperforms strong closed-source models, improving trigger detection mF1 by 6.21%, saving 0.25 more steps in online one-step decision, and increasing the rate of parallel actions by 15.58%.

</details>


### [141] [Model-based Optimal Control for Rigid-Soft Underactuated Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03435)
*Daniele Caradonna,Nikhil Nair,Anup Teejo Mathew,Daniel Feliu Talegón,Imran Afgan,Egidio Falotico,Cosimo Della Santina,Federico Renda*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Evaluates three optimal-control schemes (Direct Collocation, DDP, NMPC) for swing-up tasks in high-order rigid-soft continuum robots using a Geometric Variable Strain model with analytical derivatives; demonstrates simulation feasibility and trade-offs, aided by implicit integration and warm-starts for stiffness and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic control of continuum soft robots is challenging due to intrinsic input constraints and high-dimensional, stiff dynamics. Quasi-static or low-order models fail to capture true continuum behavior; there is a need for accurate, efficient model-based control with tractable derivatives.

Method: Employ the Geometric Variable Strain model to obtain analytical derivatives. Compare Direct Collocation, Differential Dynamic Programming, and Nonlinear Model Predictive Control under implicit integration and warm-start strategies to handle stiff dynamics and actuation constraints. Test on three high-order soft-robot benchmarks: Soft Cart-Pole, Soft Pendubot, and Soft Furuta Pendulum.

Result: In simulation, the three methods demonstrate feasibility for dynamic swing-up with underactuated soft systems. The study discusses performance and computational trade-offs among methods, and shows that implicit integration and warm starts enhance numerical robustness and efficiency for stiff continuum dynamics.

Conclusion: Analytical derivatives from the Geometric Variable Strain model enable efficient optimization across three optimal-control strategies. For high-order soft robots, implicit integration and warm-starts are crucial to robustness and computational efficiency. The work provides guidance on method selection and highlights the viability of Direct Collocation, DDP, and NMPC for dynamic tasks in rigid-soft systems.

Abstract: Continuum soft robots are inherently underactuated and subject to intrinsic input constraints, making dynamic control particularly challenging, especially in hybrid rigid-soft robots. While most existing methods focus on quasi-static behaviors, dynamic tasks such as swing-up require accurate exploitation of continuum dynamics. This has led to studies on simple low-order template systems that often fail to capture the complexity of real continuum deformations. Model-based optimal control offers a systematic solution; however, its application to rigid-soft robots is often limited by the computational cost and inaccuracy of numerical differentiation for high-dimensional models. Building on recent advances in the Geometric Variable Strain model that enable analytical derivatives, this work investigates three optimal control strategies for underactuated soft systems-Direct Collocation, Differential Dynamic Programming, and Nonlinear Model Predictive Control-to perform dynamic swing-up tasks. To address stiff continuum dynamics and constrained actuation, implicit integration schemes and warm-start strategies are employed to improve numerical robustness and computational efficiency. The methods are evaluated in simulation on three Rigid-Soft and high-order soft benchmark systems-the Soft Cart-Pole, the Soft Pendubot, and the Soft Furuta Pendulum- highlighting their performance and computational trade-offs.

</details>


### [142] [HetroD: A High-Fidelity Drone Dataset and Benchmark for Autonomous Driving in Heterogeneous Traffic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03447)
*Yu-Hsiang Chen,Wei-Jer Chang,Christian Kotulla,Thomas Keutgens,Steffen Runde,Tobias Moers,Christoph Klas,Wei Zhan,Masayoshi Tomizuka,Yi-Ting Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HetroD is a dataset and benchmark for autonomous driving in heterogeneous traffic dominated by vulnerable road users (VRUs). It uses drone-based, high-fidelity annotations and HD maps to enable forecasting, planning, and simulation tasks, highlighting gaps in current models for unstructured maneuvers and dense multi-agent interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current autonomous driving datasets focus on structured, lane-disciplined traffic and underrepresent heterogeneous traffic with VRUs and complex interactions (e.g., hook turns, lane splitting). There is a need for data and benchmarks that capture real-world, mixed-agent dynamics to improve robustness.

Method: Large-scale drone-based data collection with centimeter-level annotations, HD maps, and traffic signal states. A modular toolkit extracts per-agent scenarios. Provides standardized benchmarks for forecasting, planning, and simulation tasks. Dataset includes 65.4k high-fidelity trajectories, ~70% VRUs.

Result: State-of-the-art prediction and planning models struggle on HetroD: difficulty predicting lateral VRU movements, handling unstructured maneuvers, and performing in dense, multi-agent settings.

Conclusion: HetroD fills a critical gap by offering a standardized dataset and benchmark suite for heterogeneous traffic, enabling the development of more robust VRU-aware autonomous driving methods.

Abstract: We present HetroD, a dataset and benchmark for developing autonomous driving systems in heterogeneous environments. HetroD targets the critical challenge of navi- gating real-world heterogeneous traffic dominated by vulner- able road users (VRUs), including pedestrians, cyclists, and motorcyclists that interact with vehicles. These mixed agent types exhibit complex behaviors such as hook turns, lane splitting, and informal right-of-way negotiation. Such behaviors pose significant challenges for autonomous vehicles but remain underrepresented in existing datasets focused on structured, lane-disciplined traffic. To bridge the gap, we collect a large- scale drone-based dataset to provide a holistic observation of traffic scenes with centimeter-accurate annotations, HD maps, and traffic signal states. We further develop a modular toolkit for extracting per-agent scenarios to support downstream task development. In total, the dataset comprises over 65.4k high- fidelity agent trajectories, 70% of which are from VRUs. HetroD supports modeling of VRU behaviors in dense, het- erogeneous traffic and provides standardized benchmarks for forecasting, planning, and simulation tasks. Evaluation results reveal that state-of-the-art prediction and planning models struggle with the challenges presented by our dataset: they fail to predict lateral VRU movements, cannot handle unstructured maneuvers, and exhibit limited performance in dense and multi-agent scenarios, highlighting the need for more robust approaches to heterogeneous traffic. See our project page for more examples: https://hetroddata.github.io/HetroD/

</details>


### [143] [CMR: Contractive Mapping Embeddings for Robust Humanoid Locomotion on Unstructured Terrains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03511)
*Qixin Zeng,Hongyin Zhang,Shangke Lyu,Junxi Jin,Donglin Wang,Chao Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A contractive latent-space learning framework (CMR) improves robustness of humanoid locomotion under observation noise by fusing contrastive representation learning with Lipschitz regularization, providing theoretical return-gap bounds and empirical gains in noisy settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust disturbance rejection in humanoid locomotion on unstructured terrains is hindered by unreliable sensing and model mismatch. Perception aids terrain awareness but sensor noise and sim-to-real gaps destabilize policies. The work aims to bound return gaps due to observation noise when latent dynamics are contractive.

Method: Introduce Contractive Mapping for Robustness (CMR), mapping high-dimensional observations to a latent space that attenuates local perturbations over time. Combines contrastive representation learning with Lipschitz regularization to preserve task geometry while controlling sensitivity. Can be integrated into modern DRL as an auxiliary loss with minimal extra effort.

Result: The authors provide theoretical analysis that bounds the return gap under observation noise for contractive latent dynamics, and empirical humanoid experiments show CMR outperforms other locomotion algorithms under increased noise.

Conclusion: CMR offers a principled approach to robustness by enforcing contraction in latent dynamics, achieving provable return-gap bounds and practical performance gains in noisy perception scenarios, with easy integration into DRL pipelines.

Abstract: Robust disturbance rejection remains a longstanding challenge in humanoid locomotion, particularly on unstructured terrains where sensing is unreliable and model mismatch is pronounced. While perception information, such as height map, enhances terrain awareness, sensor noise and sim-to-real gaps can destabilize policies in practice. In this work, we provide theoretical analysis that bounds the return gap under observation noise, when the induced latent dynamics are contractive. Furthermore, we present Contractive Mapping for Robustness (CMR) framework that maps high-dimensional, disturbance-prone observations into a latent space, where local perturbations are attenuated over time. Specifically, this approach couples contrastive representation learning with Lipschitz regularization to preserve task-relevant geometry while explicitly controlling sensitivity. Notably, the formulation can be incorporated into modern deep reinforcement learning pipelines as an auxiliary loss term with minimal additional technical effort required. Further, our extensive humanoid experiments show that CMR potently outperforms other locomotion algorithms under increased noise.

</details>


### [144] [Investigating the Influence of Spatial Ability in Augmented Reality-assisted Robot Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03544)
*Nicolas Leins,Jana Gonnermann-Müller,Malte Teichmann,Sebastian Pokutta*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AR-assisted robot programming did not improve overall learning experience but mitigated the influence of spatial ability on learning experience, acting as a compensatory aid for learners with lower spatial abilities; suggests designing personalized AR-based learning to accommodate cognitive diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address inconsistent findings on AR in education by examining whether individual differences in spatial ability moderate AR learning effects and whether AR can compensate for lower spatial skills.

Method: Between-subjects experiment (N=71) comparing conventional robot programming vs AR-assisted approach via head-mounted display; spatial ability measured with Mental Rotation Test; learning experience quantified with SUS and cognitive load measures.

Result: AR did not significantly enhance learning experience compared with conventional methods. However, AR moderated the relation between spatial ability and learning experience: in the control condition, higher spatial ability predicted better SUS scores and lower extraneous cognitive load; in AR, these relations were not observed, indicating AR mitigates the disadvantage of lower spatial ability.

Conclusion: AR may provide compensatory support by reducing dependence on learner spatial abilities, informing the design of personalized AR learning environments that accommodate diverse cognitive profiles; further research is needed to explore the compensatory mechanism and its design implications.

Abstract: Augmented Reality (AR) offers promising opportunities to enhance learning, but its mechanisms and effects are not yet fully understood. As learning becomes increasingly personalized, considering individual learner characteristics becomes more important. This study investigates the moderating effect of spatial ability on learning experience with AR in the context of robot programming. A between-subjects experiment ($N=71$) compared conventional robot programming to an AR-assisted approach using a head-mounted display. Participants' spatial ability was assessed using the Mental Rotation Test. The learning experience was measured through the System Usability Scale (SUS) and cognitive load. The results indicate that AR support does not significantly improve the learning experience compared to the conventional approach. However, AR appears to have a compensatory effect on the influence of spatial ability. In the control group, spatial ability was significantly positively associated with SUS scores and negatively associated with extraneous cognitive load, indicating that higher spatial ability predicts a better learning experience. In the AR condition, these relationships were not observable, suggesting that AR mitigated the disadvantage typically experienced by learners with lower spatial abilities. These findings suggest that AR can serve a compensatory function by reducing the influence of learner characteristics. Future research should further explore this compensatory role of AR to guide the design of personalized learning environments that address diverse learner needs and reduce barriers for learners with varying cognitive profiles.

</details>


### [145] [AffordanceGrasp-R1:Leveraging Reasoning-Based Affordance Segmentation with Reinforcement Learning for Robotic Grasping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03547)
*Dingyi Zhou,Mu He,Zhuowei Fang,Xiangtong Yao,Yinlong Liu,Alois Knoll,Hu Cao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A reasoning-driven affordance segmentation framework for robotic grasping (AffordanceGrasp-R1) that combines chain-of-thought cold-start reasoning with reinforcement learning, and a context-aware pipeline that generates grasp candidates from the global scene point cloud and filters them with instruction-conditioned affordance masks; achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks and demonstrates robust generalization in real-world, language-conditioned manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance deduction and spatial grounding in robotic grasping by integrating reasoning with perception, and to improve context-awareness and generalization for language-conditioned manipulation.

Method: Employ a chain-of-thought (CoT) cold-start strategy to guide reasoning, coupled with reinforcement learning to strengthen deduction. Redesign grasp planning to (i) generate candidates from the global scene point cloud, and (ii) filter them using instruction-conditioned affordance masks.

Result: Presents consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods on benchmark datasets. Real-world robotic grasping tests validate robustness and generalization under complex language-conditioned manipulation scenarios.

Conclusion: The approach integrates reasoning and perception to deliver context-aware, language-conditioned grasping with improved performance and robustness, indicating strong potential for scalable robotic manipulation systems.

Abstract: We introduce AffordanceGrasp-R1, a reasoning-driven affordance segmentation framework for robotic grasping that combines a chain-of-thought (CoT) cold-start strategy with reinforcement learning to enhance deduction and spatial grounding. In addition, we redesign the grasping pipeline to be more context-aware by generating grasp candidates from the global scene point cloud and subsequently filtering them using instruction-conditioned affordance masks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AffordanceGrasp-R1 consistently outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods on benchmark datasets, and real-world robotic grasping evaluations further validate its robustness and generalization under complex language-conditioned manipulation scenarios.

</details>


### [146] [Multi-Player, Multi-Strategy Quantum Game Model for Interaction-Aware Decision-Making in Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03571)
*Karim Essalmi,Fernando Garrido,Fawzi Nashashibi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A novel Quantum Game Decision-Making (QGDM) framework integrates classical game theory with quantum ideas (superposition, entanglement, interference) to improve multi-agent, multi-strategy decision-making in automated driving. Validated in simulation across roundabouts, merging, and highways, showing higher success and lower collision rates than classical baselines, especially under high interaction, with real-time performance on standard hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical game-theoretic models struggle to capture uncertain/irrational human behavior and inter-agent interactions in traffic. There is a need for interaction-aware decision-making that can handle multi-agent coupling under uncertainty without requiring quantum hardware.

Method: Introduce QGDM which blends classical game theory with quantum game-theoretic concepts. Encodes players, strategies, and payoffs in a quantum-inspired framework allowing superposition and entanglement to capture interaction effects. Real-time capable on standard computers. Evaluation via simulations (roundabouts, merging, highways) comparing to multiple baselines.

Result: In simulation, QGDM significantly improves success rates and reduces collision rates compared with classical approaches, with pronounced gains in scenarios with high interaction among agents.

Conclusion: QGDM is a pioneering application of quantum game theory to automated driving decision-making, demonstrating promising performance gains in simulated, interactive settings; future work should address real-world validation, scalability, and robustness to modeling assumptions.

Abstract: Although significant progress has been made in decision-making for automated driving, challenges remain for deployment in the real world. One challenge lies in addressing interaction-awareness. Most existing approaches oversimplify interactions between the ego vehicle and surrounding agents, and often neglect interactions among the agents themselves. A common solution is to model these interactions using classical game theory. However, its formulation assumes rational players, whereas human behavior is frequently uncertain or irrational. To address these challenges, we propose the Quantum Game Decision-Making (QGDM) model, a novel framework that combines classical game theory with quantum mechanics principles (such as superposition, entanglement, and interference) to tackle multi-player, multi-strategy decision-making problems. To the best of our knowledge, this is one of the first studies to apply quantum game theory to decision-making for automated driving. QGDM runs in real time on a standard computer, without requiring quantum hardware. We evaluate QGDM in simulation across various scenarios, including roundabouts, merging, and highways, and compare its performance with multiple baseline methods. Results show that QGDM significantly improves success rates and reduces collision rates compared to classical approaches, particularly in scenarios with high interaction.

</details>


### [147] [Human-in-the-Loop Failure Recovery with Adaptive Task Allocation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03603)
*Lorena Maria Genua,Nikita Boguslavskii,Zhi Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Adaptive failure allocation (ARFA) assigns robot failure recoveries to human operators by estimating operator capabilities and selecting the highest expected reward, improving efficiency and workload distribution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable reliable operation of autonomous mobile manipulators and humanoid assistive robots in dynamic, unstructured environments by reducing robot idle time and operator workload through effective human-robot collaboration.

Method: Model operator capabilities with continuously updated beliefs based on actual performance. For each failure, compute a reward function using operator capability estimates, task urgency, and current workload; assign the failure to the operator with the highest expected reward.

Result: Simulations and user studies show ARFA outperforms random allocation, significantly reducing robot idle time, improving overall system performance, and achieving a more balanced workload among operators.

Conclusion: An adaptive, reward-based allocation framework can enhance human-robot collaboration for failure recovery in autonomous assistive robots, yielding improved efficiency and workload balance.

Abstract: Since the recent Covid-19 pandemic, mobile manipulators and humanoid assistive robots with higher levels of autonomy have increasingly been adopted for patient care and living assistance. Despite advancements in autonomy, these robots often struggle to perform reliably in dynamic and unstructured environments and require human intervention to recover from failures. Effective human-robot collaboration is essential to enable robots to receive assistance from the most competent operator, in order to reduce their workload and minimize disruptions in task execution. In this paper, we propose an adaptive method for allocating robotic failures to human operators (ARFA). Our proposed approach models the capabilities of human operators, and continuously updates these beliefs based on their actual performance for failure recovery. For every failure to be resolved, a reward function calculates expected outcomes based on operator capabilities and historical data, task urgency, and current workload distribution. The failure is then assigned to the operator with the highest expected reward. Our simulations and user studies show that ARFA outperforms random allocation, significantly reducing robot idle time, improving overall system performance, and leading to a more distributed workload among operators.

</details>


### [148] [Self-supervised Physics-Informed Manipulation of Deformable Linear Objects with Non-negligible Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03623)
*Youyuan Long,Gokhan Solak,Sara Zeynalpour,Heng Zhang,Arash Ajoudani*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SPiD is a physics-informed self-supervised framework for deformable linear object manipulation (rope) that combines an extended mass-spring model, a differentiable neural controller trained with task-oriented costs, and a self-supervised DAgger loop, achieving fast, robust rope stabilization and trajectory tracking with strong sim-to-real generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic manipulation of deformable linear objects (e.g., ropes) is challenging due to complex, uncertain dynamics, nonuniform mass distributions, and distribution shifts between simulation and reality. There is a need for data-efficient, robust learning that can adapt during deployment without heavy expert supervision.

Method: Extend a light-weight mass-spring model to better capture rope dynamics; train a neural controller via end-to-end optimization through a differentiable object model using a task-oriented loss; propose a self-supervised DAgger variant that detects distribution shift during deployment and performs offline self-correction; develop an affordable markerless rope perception method; evaluate on rope stabilization and trajectory tracking tasks in simulation and real-world robotics; test generalization across unseen initial states, rope lengths, masses, non-uniform mass distributions, external disturbances, and robustness to noisy/low-frequency state updates.

Result: Demonstrates fast and smooth rope stabilization and accurate trajectory tracking, with generalization to unseen initial states, rope lengths, masses, non-uniform mass distributions, and disturbances; robustness to noisy, low-frequency state updates; good sim-to-real transfer; data-efficient learning.

Conclusion: SPiD offers a data-efficient, robust, physically grounded framework for dynamic manipulation of deformable linear objects, with strong sim-to-real generalization, and extends to rope trajectory tracking; the combination of an extended differentiable physics model and self-supervised learning components enables robust control under distribution shifts.

Abstract: We address dynamic manipulation of deformable linear objects by presenting SPiD, a physics-informed self-supervised learning framework that couples an accurate deformable object model with an augmented self-supervised training strategy. On the modeling side, we extend a mass-spring model to more accurately capture object dynamics while remaining lightweight enough for high-throughput rollouts during self-supervised learning. On the learning side, we train a neural controller using a task-oriented cost, enabling end-to-end optimization through interaction with the differentiable object model. In addition, we propose a self-supervised DAgger variant that detects distribution shift during deployment and performs offline self-correction to further enhance robustness without expert supervision. We evaluate our method primarily on the rope stabilization task, where a robot must bring a swinging rope to rest as quickly and smoothly as possible. Extensive experiments in both simulation and the real world demonstrate that the proposed controller achieves fast and smooth rope stabilization, generalizing across unseen initial states, rope lengths, masses, non-uniform mass distributions, and external disturbances. Additionally, we develop an affordable markerless rope perception method and demonstrate that our controller maintains performance with noisy and low-frequency state updates. Furthermore, we demonstrate the generality of the framework by extending it to the rope trajectory tracking task. Overall, SPiD offers a data-efficient, robust, and physically grounded framework for dynamic manipulation of deformable linear objects, featuring strong sim-to-real generalization.

</details>


### [149] [Variance-Reduced Model Predictive Path Integral via Quadratic Model Approximation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03639)
*Fabian Schramm,Franki Nguimatsia Tiofack,Nicolas Perrin-Gilbert,Marc Toussaint,Justin Carpentier*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hybrid variance-reduced MPPI using a model-guided prior derived from a quadratic approximation to reduce variance and improve sample efficiency, demonstrated to enhance convergence in low-sample regimes across benchmarks and control tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sampling-based controllers like MPPI suffer from high variance and poor data efficiency; incorporating a prior model can concentrate samples where informative, improving performance.

Method: Decompose the objective into a known approximate model plus a residual. Use a quadratic approximation to derive a closed-form prior that concentrates samples. The prior integrates with MPPI and is agnostic to the source of geometric information; it can be built from exact derivatives, Gauss-/Quasi-Newton approximations, or gradient-free randomized smoothing.

Result: Empirical validation on standard optimization benchmarks, a nonlinear underactuated cart-pole task, and a contact-rich manipulation problem with non-smooth dynamics shows faster convergence and better performance in low-sample regimes compared to standard MPPI.

Conclusion: The framework enhances practicality of sample-based control by reducing sample requirements, generalizes across modeling choices, and is applicable to scenarios with expensive or limited sampling.

Abstract: Sampling-based controllers, such as Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) methods, offer substantial flexibility but often suffer from high variance and low sample efficiency. To address these challenges, we introduce a hybrid variance-reduced MPPI framework that integrates a prior model into the sampling process. Our key insight is to decompose the objective function into a known approximate model and a residual term. Since the residual captures only the discrepancy between the model and the objective, it typically exhibits a smaller magnitude and lower variance than the original objective. Although this principle applies to general modeling choices, we demonstrate that adopting a quadratic approximation enables the derivation of a closed-form, model-guided prior that effectively concentrates samples in informative regions. Crucially, the framework is agnostic to the source of geometric information, allowing the quadratic model to be constructed from exact derivatives, structural approximations (e.g., Gauss- or Quasi-Newton), or gradient-free randomized smoothing. We validate the approach on standard optimization benchmarks, a nonlinear, underactuated cart-pole control task, and a contact-rich manipulation problem with non-smooth dynamics. Across these domains, we achieve faster convergence and superior performance in low-sample regimes compared to standard MPPI. These results suggest that the method can make sample-based control strategies more practical in scenarios where obtaining samples is expensive or limited.

</details>


### [150] [MVP-LAM: Learning Action-Centric Latent Action via Cross-Viewpoint Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03668)
*Jung Min Lee,Dohyeok Lee,Seokhun Ju,Taehyun Cho,Jin Woo Koo,Li Zhao,Sangwoo Hong,Jungwoo Lee*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MVP-LAM learns discrete latent actions from time-synchronized multi-view videos using a cross-viewpoint reconstruction objective, yielding action-centric latent representations that enhance action prediction and improve VLA pretraining, with benefits in OOD performance and downstream manipulation benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To scale robot learning beyond embodiment-specific datasets by extracting latent actions from diverse human videos that reflect true agent actions, even without ground-truth labels, and to reduce reliance on viewpoint-specific cues for robust representations.

Method: Propose Multi-View Point Latent Action Model (MVP-LAM) that learns discrete latent actions via a cross-viewpoint reconstruction objective: a latent action inferred from one view should explain future observations in another view, trained on time-synchronized multi-view data (Bridge V2). The latent actions are then used to pretrain vision-language-action (VLA) models.

Result: Latent actions produced by MVP-LAM are more action-centric and show higher mutual information with ground-truth actions; improved action prediction, including under distribution shifts; pretraining VLA models with these latent actions leads to better downstream manipulation performance on SIMPLER and LIBERO-Long benchmarks.

Conclusion: Cross-viewpoint reconstruction enables learning informative, viewpoint-robust latent actions from multi-view data, improving both action understanding and downstream VLA-pretraining for robot manipulation.

Abstract: Learning \emph{latent actions} from diverse human videos enables scaling robot learning beyond embodiment-specific robot datasets, and these latent actions have recently been used as pseudo-action labels for vision-language-action (VLA) model pretraining. To make VLA pretraining effective, latent actions should contain information about the underlying agent's actions despite the absence of ground-truth labels. We propose \textbf{M}ulti-\textbf{V}iew\textbf{P}oint \textbf{L}atent \textbf{A}ction \textbf{M}odel (\textbf{MVP-LAM}), which learns discrete latent actions that are highly informative about ground-truth actions from time-synchronized multi-view videos. MVP-LAM trains latent actions with a \emph{cross-viewpoint reconstruction} objective, so that a latent action inferred from one view must explain the future in another view, reducing reliance on viewpoint-specific cues. On Bridge V2, MVP-LAM produces more action-centric latent actions, achieving higher mutual information with ground-truth actions and improved action prediction, including under out-of-distribution evaluation. Finally, pretraining VLAs with MVP-LAM latent actions improves downstream manipulation performance on the SIMPLER and LIBERO-Long benchmarks.

</details>


### [151] [A Scene Graph Backed Approach to Open Set Semantic Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03781)
*Martin Günther,Felix Igelbrink,Oscar Lima,Lennart Niecksch,Marian Renz,Martin Atzmueller*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a 3D Semantic Scene Graph (3DSSG) as the core backend for Open Set Semantic Mapping, enabling real-time, topologically consistent updates and facilitating knowledge-driven reasoning with sub-symbolic and symbolic integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decoupled perception and representation in current Open Set Mapping and 3DSSGs leads to inconsistency and scalability limits for large-scale, real-world environments.

Method: Incremental scene graph prediction to infer and update the 3DSSG in real time during exploration; mapping process uses 3DSSG as the primary knowledge representation; supports flat and hierarchical topologies; explicit spatial grounding.

Result: Map remains topologically consistent and computationally efficient during extended operations; provides a stable, verifiable structure for downstream knowledge-driven frameworks.

Conclusion: This architecture bridges sub-symbolic sensory data and high-level symbolic reasoning, enabling interpretable and trustworthy reasoning with knowledge graphs, ontologies, and LLMs.

Abstract: While Open Set Semantic Mapping and 3D Semantic Scene Graphs (3DSSGs) are established paradigms in robotic perception, deploying them effectively to support high-level reasoning in large-scale, real-world environments remains a significant challenge. Most existing approaches decouple perception from representation, treating the scene graph as a derivative layer generated post hoc. This limits both consistency and scalability. In contrast, we propose a mapping architecture where the 3DSSG serves as the foundational backend, acting as the primary knowledge representation for the entire mapping process.
  Our approach leverages prior work on incremental scene graph prediction to infer and update the graph structure in real-time as the environment is explored. This ensures that the map remains topologically consistent and computationally efficient, even during extended operations in large-scale settings. By maintaining an explicit, spatially grounded representation that supports both flat and hierarchical topologies, we bridge the gap between sub-symbolic raw sensor data and high-level symbolic reasoning. Consequently, this provides a stable, verifiable structure that knowledge-driven frameworks, ranging from knowledge graphs and ontologies to Large Language Models (LLMs), can directly exploit, enabling agents to operate with enhanced interpretability, trustworthiness, and alignment to human concepts.

</details>


### [152] [BridgeV2W: Bridging Video Generation Models to Embodied World Models via Embodiment Masks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03793)
*Yixiang Chen,Peiyan Li,Jiabing Yang,Keji He,Xiangnan Wu,Yuan Xu,Kai Wang,Jing Liu,Nianfeng Liu,Yan Huang,Liang Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: BridgeV2W aligns coordinate-space actions with pixel-space video via embodiment masks and a ControlNet-style integration, enabling unified embodied world models with improved video quality and downstream task support.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Key challenges include misalignment between coordinate-space actions and pixel-space videos, sensitivity to camera viewpoint, and non-unified architectures across embodiments.

Method: Convert actions to pixel-aligned embodiment masks rendered from URDF and camera parameters; inject via a ControlNet-style pathway into a pretrained video generation model; add view-specific conditioning to handle camera viewpoints; unify architecture across embodiments; introduce a flow-based motion loss to emphasize dynamic/task-relevant regions.

Result: Experiments on single-arm (DROID) and dual-arm (AgiBot-G1) datasets show improved video-generation quality over prior state-of-the-art, including unseen viewpoints/scenes; downstream demonstrations on policy evaluation and goal-conditioned planning.

Conclusion: BridgeV2W delivers a unified embodied world model across embodiments, mitigating action-to-video misalignment and viewpoint sensitivity, with practical benefits for downstream robotic tasks.

Abstract: Embodied world models have emerged as a promising paradigm in robotics, most of which leverage large-scale Internet videos or pretrained video generation models to enrich visual and motion priors. However, they still face key challenges: a misalignment between coordinate-space actions and pixel-space videos, sensitivity to camera viewpoint, and non-unified architectures across embodiments. To this end, we present BridgeV2W, which converts coordinate-space actions into pixel-aligned embodiment masks rendered from the URDF and camera parameters. These masks are then injected into a pretrained video generation model via a ControlNet-style pathway, which aligns the action control signals with predicted videos, adds view-specific conditioning to accommodate camera viewpoints, and yields a unified world model architecture across embodiments. To mitigate overfitting to static backgrounds, BridgeV2W further introduces a flow-based motion loss that focuses on learning dynamic and task-relevant regions. Experiments on single-arm (DROID) and dual-arm (AgiBot-G1) datasets, covering diverse and challenging conditions with unseen viewpoints and scenes, show that BridgeV2W improves video generation quality compared to prior state-of-the-art methods. We further demonstrate the potential of BridgeV2W on downstream real-world tasks, including policy evaluation and goal-conditioned planning. More results can be found on our project website at https://BridgeV2W.github.io .

</details>


### [153] [Conformal Reachability for Safe Control in Unknown Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03799)
*Xinhang Ma,Junlin Wu,Yiannis Kantaros,Yevgeniy Vorobeychik*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Probabilistic verification framework for unknown dynamical systems that combines conformal prediction with reachability to guarantee safety; trains controllers to maximize reward and planning horizon under probabilistic safety; validated across seven tasks in four domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional safe-control methods assume known/deterministic dynamics or finite state spaces, limiting real-world applicability; need safety guarantees under uncertainty and unknown dynamics.

Method: Use conformal prediction to compute valid uncertainty intervals for the unknown dynamics at each time step; perform reachability analysis within these bounds to ensure safety; develop a training algorithm that optimizes nominal reward while increasing planning horizon with probabilistic safety guarantees.

Result: Empirically, learned policies achieve the strongest provable safety guarantees while maintaining high average reward across seven safe-control settings spanning four domains (cartpole, lane following, drone control, safe navigation) for both affine and nonlinear safety specifications.

Conclusion: Provides a general framework for provably safe control under unknown dynamics, enabling longer-horizon planning with robust safety guarantees across diverse domains, with practical applicability.

Abstract: Designing provably safe control is a core problem in trustworthy autonomy. However, most prior work in this regard assumes either that the system dynamics are known or deterministic, or that the state and action space are finite, significantly limiting application scope. We address this limitation by developing a probabilistic verification framework for unknown dynamical systems which combines conformal prediction with reachability analysis. In particular, we use conformal prediction to obtain valid uncertainty intervals for the unknown dynamics at each time step, with reachability then verifying whether safety is maintained within the conformal uncertainty bounds. Next, we develop an algorithmic approach for training control policies that optimize nominal reward while also maximizing the planning horizon with sound probabilistic safety guarantees. We evaluate the proposed approach in seven safe control settings spanning four domains -- cartpole, lane following, drone control, and safe navigation -- for both affine and nonlinear safety specifications. Our experiments show that the policies we learn achieve the strongest provable safety guarantees while still maintaining high average reward.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [154] [UNSO: Unified Newton Schulz Orthogonalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02500)
*Chen Hu,Qianxi Zhao,Yuming Li,Mingyu Zhou,Xiyin Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unified Newton-Schulz Orthogonalization (UNSO) reframes NS iterations into a unified, learnable polynomial framework to improve efficiency and stability for orthogonalization tasks on the Stiefel manifold and related optimizers; it avoids polynomial expansion and uses learnable coefficients to prune insignificant terms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address inefficiency and instability of conventional Newton-Schulz iterations, which rely on repeated matrix products in high dimensions.

Method: Consolidates the NS iteration into a single unified framework, analyzes the contribution of each matrix power, removes insignificant terms, and introduces a learnable polynomial with trainable coefficients. The approach avoids explicit polynomial expansion and optimizes the coefficients to achieve stable convergence.

Result: Reportedly achieves outstanding performance with stable convergence; the learnable coefficients enable effective pruning of terms and reduce computational burden. Code is provided for reproducibility.

Conclusion: UNSO provides a flexible, learnable alternative to classical NS iterations for orthogonalization tasks, potentially reducing computational cost while maintaining or improving convergence on the Stiefel manifold and related optimization problems.

Abstract: The Newton-Schulz (NS) iteration has gained increasing interest for its role in the Muon optimizer and the Stiefel manifold. However, the conventional NS iteration suffers from inefficiency and instability. Although various improvements have been introduced to NS iteration, they fail to deviate from the conventional iterative paradigm, which could increase computation burden largely due to the matrix products along the long dimension repeatedly. To address this, we consolidate the iterative structure into a unified framework, named Unified Newton-Schulz Orthogonalization (UNSO). To do so, we could avoid a polynomial expansion. Instead, we evaluate the role of each matrix power, remove the insignificant terms, and provide a recommended polynomial with learnable coefficients. These learnable coefficients are then optimized, and achieve an outstanding performance with stable convergence. The code of our method is available: https://github.com/greekinRoma/Unified_Newton_Schulz_Orthogonalization.

</details>


### [155] [Augmenting Parameter-Efficient Pre-trained Language Models with Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02501)
*Saurabh Anand,Shubham Malaviya,Manish Shukla,Sachin Lodha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PEFT with compacters and layer freezing combined with LLM-based data labeling and fallback for low-confidence predictions to tackle data drift and label scarcity in cybersecurity; experimental validation across domain-specific tasks shows improved reliability and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome data drift and scarcity of labeled data in cybersecurity ML, aiming for more reliable, robust models suitable for real-world deployment.

Method: Apply parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) using compacters and selective layer freezing; introduce two LLM-based strategies—(1) LLMs label unlabeled data, (2) LLMs serve as fallbacks for low-confidence predictions; evaluate on multiple cybersecurity downstream tasks.

Result: Empirical demonstration that combining PEFT with LLMs enhances reliability and robustness across cybersecurity tasks.

Conclusion: Hybrid approach of PEFT and LLMs is beneficial for real-world cybersecurity applications, offering improved performance and resilience against data-related challenges.

Abstract: Training AI models in cybersecurity with help of vast datasets offers significant opportunities to mimic real-world behaviors effectively. However, challenges like data drift and scarcity of labelled data lead to frequent updates of models and the risk of overfitting. To address these challenges, we used parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques for pre-trained language models wherein we combine compacters with various layer freezing strategies. To enhance the capabilities of these pre-trained language models, in this work we introduce two strategies that use large language models. In the first strategy, we utilize large language models as data-labelling tools wherein they generate labels for unlabeled data. In the second strategy, large language modes are utilized as fallback mechanisms for predictions having low confidence scores. We perform comprehensive experimental analysis on the proposed strategies on different downstream tasks specific to cybersecurity domain. We empirically demonstrate that by combining parameter-efficient pre-trained models with large language models, we can improve the reliability and robustness of models, making them more suitable for real-world cybersecurity applications.

</details>


### [156] [Sparse Adapter Fusion for Continual Learning in NLP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02502)
*Min Zeng,Xi Chen,Haiqin Yang,Yike Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAFM dynamically fuses sparse adapters across tasks via decision and tuning stages to minimize parameters while maintaining or improving performance in continual NLP learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address inefficiencies in parameter reuse across tasks, mitigate catastrophic forgetting when tasks are dissimilar, and avoid unnecessary parameter growth by sharing knowledge among similar tasks.

Method: Two-stage approach. Decision stage uses architecture search to decide whether to add, reuse, or insert an empty adapter, prioritizing reuse and empty adapters to reduce parameters. Tuning stage applies a layer-wise loss to encourage differentiation between adapters, promoting knowledge capture within the same task. Experimental protocol compares to SOTA on continual learning benchmarks; results show parameter efficiency (under 60% of parameters) with comparable or superior performance.

Result: SAFM achieves state-of-the-art-like performance while using less than 60% of the parameters, outperforming several SOTA methods.

Conclusion: SAFM provides effective parameter-efficient continual learning for NLP by adaptively fusing adapters, enabling knowledge sharing among tasks while avoiding catastrophic forgetting and excessive parameter growth.

Abstract: Continual learning in natural language processing plays a crucial role in adapting to evolving data and preventing catastrophic forgetting. Despite significant progress, existing methods still face challenges, such as inefficient parameter reuse across tasks, risking catastrophic forgetting when tasks are dissimilar, and the unnecessary introduction of new parameters for each task, which hampers knowledge sharing among similar tasks. To tackle these issues, we propose a Sparse Adapter Fusion Method (SAFM), which dynamically fuses old and new adapters to address these challenges. SAFM operates in two stages: the decision stage and the tuning stage. In the decision stage, SAFM determines whether to incorporate a new adapter, reuse an existing one, or add an empty adapter. The architecture search procedure, designed to prioritize reusing or adding empty adapters, minimizes parameter consumption and maximizes reuse. In the tuning stage, SAFM especially facilitates a layer-wise loss to encourage differentiation between adapters, effectively capturing knowledge within the same task. Experimental results consistently show that SAFM outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods, achieving comparable performance while utilizing less than 60% of the parameters.

</details>


### [157] [Learning ORDER-Aware Multimodal Representations for Composite Materials Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02513)
*Xinyao Li,Hangwei Qian,Jingjing Li,Ivor Tsang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ORDER is a multimodal pretraining framework introducing ordinal awareness to model continuous composite properties, enabling interpolation and better predictions in data-scarce regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Composite materials exhibit continuous, nonlinear design spaces without well-defined graphs. Existing graph-centric or discrete multimodal approaches fail to capture fiber distributions and heterogenous data. A need exists for a multimodal framework that respects property ordinality and supports interpolation under data scarcity.

Method: ORDinal-aware imagE-tabulaR alignment (ORDER) trains multimodal representations with an explicit ordinal constraint, ensuring materials with similar target properties lie close in latent space. This preserves the continuous property landscape and enables interpolation between sparse designs. Evaluated on public Nanofiber-enforced composite data and a proprietary carbon fiber T700 simulation dataset, ORDER optimizes across property prediction, cross-modal retrieval, and microstructure generation.

Result: ORDER consistently outperforms state-of-the-art multimodal baselines on multiple tasks (property prediction, cross-modal retrieval, microstructure generation) across the two datasets.

Conclusion: Incorporating ordinal structure into multimodal pretraining effectively captures the continuous design space of composites, enabling meaningful interpolation and improved performance in low-data settings.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) has shown remarkable success in materials discovery and property prediction, particularly for crystalline and polymer systems where material properties and structures are dominated by discrete graph representations. Such graph-central paradigm breaks down on composite materials, which possess continuous and nonlinear design spaces that lack well-defined graph structures. General composite descriptors, e.g., fiber volume and misalignment angle, cannot fully capture the fiber distributions that fundamentally determine microstructural characteristics, necessitating the integration of heterogeneous data sources through multimodal learning. Existing alignment-oriented multimodal frameworks have proven effective on abundant crystal or polymer data under discrete, unique graph-property mapping assumptions, but fail to address the highly continuous composite design space under extreme data scarcity. In this work, we introduce ORDinal-aware imagE-tabulaR alignment (ORDER), a multimodal pretraining framework that establishes ordinality as a core principle for composite material representations. ORDER ensures that materials with similar target properties occupy nearby regions in the latent space, which effectively preserves the continuous nature of composite properties and enables meaningful interpolation between sparsely observed designs. We evaluate ORDER on a public Nanofiber-enforced composite dataset and an internally curated dataset that simulates the construction of carbon fiber T700 with diverse fiber distributions. ORDER achieves consistent improvements over state-of-the-art multimodal baselines across property prediction, cross-modal retrieval, and microstructure generation tasks.

</details>


### [158] [GraphDancer: Training LLMs to Explore and Reason over Graphs via Curriculum Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02518)
*Yuyang Bai,Zhuofeng Li,Ping Nie,Jianwen Xie,Yu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraphDancer is an RL-based framework that trains moderate-sized LLMs to navigate heterogeneous graphs by interleaving reasoning with function execution, using a graph-aware curriculum to handle increasing structural complexity. It achieves strong cross-domain generalization, outperforming larger baselines with a 3B model on unseen domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reasoning over graph-structured knowledge faces two main challenges: (1) precise, schema-driven function calls are required over graphs rather than simple text similarity, and (2) complex, multi-hop questions need iterative information seeking and aggregation. These issues motivate a learning framework that can learn structured navigation and reasoning.

Method: Introduce GraphDancer, a reinforcement learning framework that trains LLMs to perform graph navigation by alternating reasoning steps with function calls. To train effectively for moderate LLMs, propose a graph-aware curriculum with an easy-to-hard sampler that biases trajectories by structural complexity. Train on one domain and test on unseen domains and out-of-distribution question types.

Result: On a multi-domain benchmark, GraphDancer with a 3B backbone outperforms baselines that use a 14B backbone or GPT-4o-mini, demonstrating robust cross-domain generalization in graph exploration and reasoning. The authors provide code and models at a provided URL.

Conclusion: GraphDancer shows that structured graph reasoning for LLMs can be effectively learned with RL and curriculum-based training, enabling strong generalization for moderate-sized models in graph-structured knowledge scenarios.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly rely on external knowledge to improve factuality, yet many real-world knowledge sources are organized as heterogeneous graphs rather than plain text. Reasoning over such graph-structured knowledge poses two key challenges: (1) navigating structured, schema-defined relations requires precise function calls rather than similarity-based retrieval, and (2) answering complex questions often demands multi-hop evidence aggregation through iterative information seeking. We propose GraphDancer, a reinforcement learning (RL) framework that teaches LLMs to navigate graphs by interleaving reasoning and function execution. To make RL effective for moderate-sized LLMs, we introduce a graph-aware curriculum that schedules training by the structural complexity of information-seeking trajectories using an easy-to-hard biased sampler. We evaluate GraphDancer on a multi-domain benchmark by training on one domain only and testing on unseen domains and out-of-distribution question types. Despite using only a 3B backbone, GraphDancer outperforms baselines equipped with either a 14B backbone or GPT-4o-mini, demonstrating robust cross-domain generalization of graph exploration and reasoning skills. Our code and models can be found at https://yuyangbai.com/graphdancer/ .

</details>


### [159] [Scaled Dot-Product Attention implements projection of inputs onto a common surface](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02521)
*Terence D Sanger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SDPA can be reformulated as a projection of inputs onto a data-driven surface, revealing time- and context-dependent nonlinear dependencies; this reinterpretation speeds up computation and suggests extensions, and it offers a different view from self-attention, with applicability to time-series data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To place SDPA on a solid signal-processing footing, understand why it works beyond the query-key-value narrative, and explore its potential extensions and applicability to time-varying nonlinear dependencies.

Method: Rewrite SDPA as a projection of input vectors onto a common surface determined by the inputs themselves, deriving a time- and context-dependent nonlinear dependency structure; reinterpret the role of SDPA relative to self-attention and discuss speedups and extensions for time-series.

Result: The reformulated SDPA preserves equivalence with the standard formulation while enabling faster feedforward and learning and revealing nonlinear, time-varying dependencies that are context-dependent.

Conclusion: SDPA, in this framework, provides a compelling alternative to traditional self-attention for time-series data with local nonlinear dependencies, motivated by a surface-projection interpretation and with potential for new extensions.

Abstract: Scaled dot-product attention (SDPA) is a fundamental component responsible for the success of large-language models and other nonlinear signal processing applications. The rationale for SDPA has been based upon "query, key, value" concepts borrowed from database theory, but these concepts are difficult to reconcile with standard methods in mathematical signal processing. We show that SDPA can be rewritten in a different but mathematically equivalent form as a projection of the input vectors onto a common surface determined by the inputs themselves. Therefore SDPA discovers nonlinear dependencies in the input that are time-dependent and context-dependent. The rewritten form of SDPA permits increased speed of both feedforward and learning algorithms, but more importantly suggests potential extensions. In the context of language, we re-interpret the role of SDPA as finding a time-dependent contextual meaning determined by the surface on which the set of input vectors lies. Input token embeddings are then modified by the local context surface. This interpretation differs substantially from the concept of "self-attention", and provides a strong justification for the use of SDPA for time-series data with time-varying local nonlinear dependencies.

</details>


### [160] [IMU-1: Sample-Efficient Pre-training of Small Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02522)
*George Grigorev*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IMU-1 is a 430M-parameter language model trained on 72B tokens, achieving near-benchmark performance of models trained on 56x more data, using a validated recipe that combines architectural innovations (QK-norm attention, per-head gating, value residuals, LayerNorm scaling) with optimization advances (NorMuon with cautious weight decay, muP parametrization) and a three-stage training schedule with post-hoc checkpoint EMA; ablations are provided and code, weights, and data are released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Show that strong language-model performance can be achieved with data-efficient training by integrating targeted architectural and optimization techniques, and enable reproducibility through public release.

Method: Integrates architectural interventions (QK-norm attention, per-head gating, value residuals, LayerNorm scaling) with optimization advances (NorMuon with cautious weight decay, muP parametrization) and a three-stage training schedule plus post-hoc checkpoint EMA; supports ablations for each component and releases code, weights, and data.

Result: Demonstrates that a 430M-parameter model trained on 72B tokens can approach the benchmark performance of models trained on 56x more data; provides ablations for each component and public release of materials.

Conclusion: The combination of architectural and optimization techniques yields data-efficient, competitive LM performance with a small model size, and the authors provide reproducibility resources; further evaluation across tasks and datasets would bolster generalization claims.

Abstract: We present IMU-1, a 430M-parameter language model trained on 72B tokens that approaches the benchmark performance of models trained on 56x more data. We describe a validated training recipe combining recent architectural interventions (QK-norm attention, per-head gating, value residuals, LayerNorm scaling) with optimization advances (NorMuon with cautious weight decay, muP parametrization) and a three-stage training schedule with post-hoc checkpoint EMA. We provide ablations for each component and release code, weights and data to enable reproduction: https://huggingface.co/thepowerfuldeez/imu1_base

</details>


### [161] [The "Robert Boulton" Singularity: Semantic Tunneling and Manifold Unfolding in Recursive AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02526)
*Pengyue Hou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PPL is misleading for stability in recursive synthetic-data experiments; paper identifies 'Semantic Tunneling' where diversity collapses to a single attractor; proposes MNCIS with ASNC to 'unfold' the manifold and restore diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
