In this work, we perform a comprehensive comparative simulation study on the fairness and accuracy of predictive policing technologies in Baltimore. Our results suggest that the situation around bias in predictive policing is more complex than was previously assumed. While predictive policing exhibited bias due to feedback loops as was previously reported, we found that the traditional alternative, hot spots policing, had similar issues. Predictive policing was found to be more fair and accurate than hot spots policing in the short term, although it amplified bias faster, suggesting the potential for worse long-run behavior. In Baltimore, in some cases the bias in these systems tended toward over-policing in White neighborhoods, unlike in previous studies. Overall, this work demonstrates a methodology for city-specific evaluation and behavioral-tendency comparison of predictive policing systems, showing how such simulations can reveal inequities and long-term tendencies.

</details>


### [189] [IceBench-S2S: A Benchmark of Deep Learning for Challenging Subseasonal-to-Seasonal Daily Arctic Sea Ice Forecasting in Deep Latent Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02567)
*Jingyi Xu,Shengnan Wang,Weidong Yang,Siwei Tu,Lei Bai,Ben Fei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces IceBench-S2S, a first comprehensive benchmark for deep-learning-based Arctic sea ice concentration forecasting at a 180-day S2S horizon; framework compresses daily spatial data into a latent space and uses DL backbones to predict seasonal-scale sea ice variation; provides unified training/evaluation pipeline and guidance for model selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current DL sea-ice forecasts are largely limited to daily subseasonal scales or up to six months; there is a need to extend to seasonal (S2S) forecasts for operational planning (e.g., Arctic shipping, exploration). A standardized benchmark is required to fairly compare DL approaches and accelerate deployment.

Method: General framework that (1) compresses spatial features of daily sea ice data into a deep latent space, (2) concatenates temporal deep features, and (3) feeds them into DL-based forecasting backbones to forecast sea ice across successive 180-day periods; also provides unified training/evaluation pipeline and practical guidance for model selection.

Result: Proposes IceBench-S2S as the first comprehensive benchmark for DL-based 180-day S2S Arctic sea ice forecasting, enabling standardized evaluation of backbones and providing a practical pipeline for practitioners.

Conclusion: IceBench-S2S sets the foundation for evaluating and advancing DL methods toward real-world, long-range Arctic sea ice forecasting, addressing a critical gap between current daily forecasts and seasonal operational needs.

Abstract: Arctic sea ice plays a critical role in regulating Earth's climate system, significantly influencing polar ecological stability and human activities in coastal regions. Recent advances in artificial intelligence have facilitated the development of skillful pan-Arctic sea ice forecasting systems, where data-driven approaches showcase tremendous potential to outperform conventional physics-based numerical models in terms of accuracy, computational efficiency and forecasting lead times. Despite the latest progress made by deep learning (DL) forecasting models, most of their skillful forecasting lead times are confined to daily subseasonal scale and monthly averaged values for up to six months, which drastically hinders their deployment for real-world applications, e.g., maritime routine planning for Arctic transportation and scientific investigation. Extending daily forecasts from subseasonal to seasonal (S2S) scale is scientifically crucial for operational applications. To bridge the gap between the forecasting lead time of current DL models and the significant daily S2S scale, we introduce IceBench-S2S, the first comprehensive benchmark for evaluating DL approaches in mitigating the challenge of forecasting Arctic sea ice concentration in successive 180-day periods. It proposes a generalized framework that first compresses spatial features of daily sea ice data into a deep latent space. The temporally concatenated deep features are subsequently modeled by DL-based forecasting backbones to predict the sea ice variation at S2S scale. IceBench-S2S provides a unified training and evaluation pipeline for different backbones, along with practical guidance for model selection in polar environmental monitoring tasks.

</details>


### [190] [Mitigating Task-Order Sensitivity and Forgetting via Hierarchical Second-Order Consolidation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02568)
*Protik Nag,Krishnan Raghavan,Vignesh Narayanan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HTCL combines fast local adaptation with Hessian-regularized global consolidation in a hierarchical, multi-level framework to mitigate task-order variance in continual learning, achieving consistent accuracy gains and reduced variance across random task sequences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses high variance and order-dependency in continual learning by stabilizing knowledge consolidation while preserving fast adaptation; aims for multiscale knowledge integration beyond single-level CL.

Method: HTCL: fast local updates; conservative global consolidation via Hessian-regularized Taylor expansion; identify best intra-group task sequence; hierarchical extension to L levels; model-agnostic consolidation layer; theoretical guarantees.

Result: Empirical results show 7-25% mean accuracy gains and up to 68% reduction in final-accuracy variance across random task permutations, across datasets and baselines.

Conclusion: HTCL provides a versatile, theoretically grounded consolidation layer that improves accuracy and stability in continual learning and scales to multiple hierarchical levels, outperforming baselines across diverse settings.

Abstract: We introduce $\textbf{Hierarchical Taylor Series-based Continual Learning (HTCL)}$, a framework that couples fast local adaptation with conservative, second-order global consolidation to address the high variance introduced by random task ordering. To address task-order effects, HTCL identifies the best intra-group task sequence and integrates the resulting local updates through a Hessian-regularized Taylor expansion, yielding a consolidation step with theoretical guarantees. The approach naturally extends to an $L$-level hierarchy, enabling multiscale knowledge integration in a manner not supported by conventional single-level CL systems. Across a wide range of datasets and replay and regularization baselines, HTCL acts as a model-agnostic consolidation layer that consistently enhances performance, yielding mean accuracy gains of $7\%$ to $25\%$ while reducing the standard deviation of final accuracy by up to $68\%$ across random task permutations.

</details>


### [191] [Trajectory Consistency for One-Step Generation on Euler Mean Flows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02571)
*Zhiqi Li,Yuchen Sun,Duowen Chen,Jinjin He,Bo Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EMF: a flow-based generative framework using a linear surrogate to enforce long-horizon trajectory consistency, enabling one-step and few-step generation with lower training cost; achieves substantial savings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-range trajectory consistency is hard to supervise and optimize in flow-based generative models; need efficient, data-supervised approaches for maintaining consistency across long horizons.

Method: Derives a principled linear surrogate from the semigroup formulation of flow-based models, enabling direct supervision for long-horizon flow-map compositions. A JVP-free, unified training framework supporting u-prediction and x1-prediction, avoiding Jacobian computations and reducing memory/computation. Applies to one-step and few-step generation.

Result: Improved optimization stability and sample quality under fixed sampling budgets; ~50% reductions in training time and memory usage compared to existing one-step methods for image generation; demonstrated across image synthesis, particle-based geometry generation, and functional generation.

Conclusion: A principled, efficient approximation to trajectory consistency that preserves performance while substantially reducing training cost, with broad applicability to flow-based generation tasks.

Abstract: We propose \emph{Euler Mean Flows (EMF)}, a flow-based generative framework for one-step and few-step generation that enforces long-range trajectory consistency with minimal sampling cost. The key idea of EMF is to replace the trajectory consistency constraint, which is difficult to supervise and optimize over long time scales, with a principled linear surrogate that enables direct data supervision for long-horizon flow-map compositions. We derive this approximation from the semigroup formulation of flow-based models and show that, under mild regularity assumptions, it faithfully approximates the original consistency objective while being substantially easier to optimize. This formulation leads to a unified, JVP-free training framework that supports both $u$-prediction and $x_1$-prediction variants, avoiding explicit Jacobian computations and significantly reducing memory and computational overhead. Experiments on image synthesis, particle-based geometry generation, and functional generation demonstrate improved optimization stability and sample quality under fixed sampling budgets, together with approximately $50\%$ reductions in training time and memory consumption compared to existing one-step methods for image generation.

</details>


### [192] [Reward Shaping for Inference-Time Alignment: A Stackelberg Game Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02572)
*Haichuan Wang,Tao Lin,Lingkai Kong,Ce Li,Hezi Jiang,Milind Tambe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reward shaping within a Stackelberg framework offers a practical, near-optimal reward model under KL regularization, improving user utility in LLM alignment with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KL regularization with the base policy biases the model away from user preferences; amplifying rewards can mitigate bias but risks reward hacking; need a principled reward-model design under KL constraints.

Method: Model the reward-model optimization as a Stackelberg game and propose a simple reward-shaping scheme that approximates the optimal reward model; show compatibility with existing alignment pipelines and low overhead.

Result: Empirical evaluation in inference-time settings shows consistent improvements in average reward and win-tie rates (>66% vs baselines) across evaluation settings.

Conclusion: Reward shaping under KL-regularized Stackelberg optimization is effective and readily integrable for improving alignment with modest computational and implementation cost.

Abstract: Existing alignment methods directly use the reward model learned from user preference data to optimize an LLM policy, subject to KL regularization with respect to the base policy. This practice is suboptimal for maximizing user's utility because the KL regularization may cause the LLM to inherit the bias in the base policy that conflicts with user preferences. While amplifying rewards for preferred outputs can mitigate this bias, it also increases the risk of reward hacking. This tradeoff motivates the problem of optimally designing reward models under KL regularization. We formalize this reward model optimization problem as a Stackelberg game, and show that a simple reward shaping scheme can effectively approximate the optimal reward model. We empirically evaluate our method in inference-time alignment settings and demonstrate that it integrates seamlessly into existing alignment methods with minimal overhead. Our method consistently improves average reward and achieves win-tie rates exceeding 66% against all baselines, averaged across evaluation settings.

</details>


### [193] [Product Interaction: An Algebraic Formalism for Deep Learning Architectures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02573)
*Haonan Dong,Chun-Wun Cheng,Angelica I. Aviles-Rivero*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes product interactions, an algebraic framework to build neural networks via compositions of a multiplication operator on algebras, unifying linear, quadratic, and higher-order interactions; convolutional and equivariant nets are linear interactions, while attention and Mamba are higher-order.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a principled, unified algebraic lens for neural network architectures by expressing layers as product interactions of increasing order.

Method: Define a multiplication operator on suitable algebras; construct layers as compositions of this operator; categorize architectures by interaction order; show symmetry-constrained linear interactions yield convolutional/equivariant nets; higher-order interactions capture attention and Mamba.

Result: A unified construction for algebraic expressions in modern neural networks across multiple interaction orders; mapping of conventional architectures to linear, quadratic, and higher-order product interactions; establishes correspondence between symmetry constraints and network types.

Conclusion: Product interactions offer a principled framework to design and analyze neural network architectures, unifying diverse models under an algebraic, order-based approach and clarifying how common architectures arise from symmetry constraints and interaction order.

Abstract: In this paper, we introduce product interactions, an algebraic formalism in which neural network layers are constructed from compositions of a multiplication operator defined over suitable algebras. Product interactions provide a principled way to generate and organize algebraic expressions by increasing interaction order. Our central observation is that algebraic expressions in modern neural networks admit a unified construction in terms of linear, quadratic, and higher-order product interactions. Convolutional and equivariant networks arise as symmetry-constrained linear product interactions, while attention and Mamba correspond to higher-order product interactions.

</details>


### [194] [QuantLRM: Quantization of Large Reasoning Models via Fine-Tuning Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02581)
*Nan Zhang,Eugene Kwek,Yusen Zhang,Muyu Pan,Suhang Wang,Prasenjit Mitra,Rui Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QuantLRM uses weight-update signals from fine-tuning to guide weight-only quantization of Large Reasoning Models; it focuses on extreme updates (protecting both ends), fits restricted quadratic functions to updates, and derives channel importance by combining average quadratic values with zero-update counts, achieving consistent gains across RL-tuned models and enabling pseudo-fine-tuning for non-fine-tuned LRMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable more effective weight-only quantization for Large Reasoning Models by leveraging dynamics of weight updates during fine-tuning, especially extreme updates, rather than relying on activation or second-order statistics.

Method: Track weight updates during various fine-tuning regimes (supervised, direct preference optimization, RL fine-tuning). Fit restricted quadratic functions to the smallest and largest updates (protecting both ends). Compute channel importance as (average quadratic value of updates) × (count of zero-weight updates in the channel). Use this to guide quantization of LRMs. Validate on diverse benchmarks (AIME-120, FOLIO, temporal sequences, GPQA-Diamond) and across fine-tuning types; also apply pseudo-fine-tuning for non-fine-tuned LRMs.

Result: QuantLRM delivers consistent improvement in weight quantization performance across tested LRMs, with an average improvement of 6.55% on RL-fine-tuned models. It also provides performance gains for non-fine-tuned LRMs via pseudo-fine-tuning, broadening applicability.

Conclusion: Weight-update signals, especially extreme updates, offer valuable cues for channel-wise quantization in LRMs. The proposed restricted-quadratic fitting and the resulting channel-importance measure outperform activations or second-order cues, enabling effective weight-only quantization across varied fine-tuning regimes and tasks.

Abstract: Weight-only quantization is important for compressing Large Language Models (LLMs). Inspired by the spirit of classical magnitude pruning, we study whether the magnitude of weight updates during reasoning-incentivized fine-tuning can provide valuable signals for quantizing Large Reasoning Models (LRMs). We hypothesize that the smallest and largest weight updates during fine-tuning are more important than those of intermediate magnitude, a phenomenon we term "protecting both ends". Upon hypothesis validation, we introduce QuantLRM, which stands for weight quantization of LRMs via fine-tuning signals. We fit simple restricted quadratic functions on weight updates to protect both ends. By multiplying the average quadratic values with the count of zero weight updates of channels, we compute channel importance that is more effective than using activation or second-order information. We run QuantLRM to quantize various fine-tuned models (including supervised, direct preference optimization, and reinforcement learning fine-tuning) over four reasoning benchmarks (AIME-120, FOLIO, temporal sequences, and GPQA-Diamond) and empirically find that QuantLRM delivers a consistent improvement for LRMs quantization, with an average improvement of 6.55% on a reinforcement learning fine-tuned model. Also supporting non-fine-tuned LRMs, QuantLRM gathers effective signals via pseudo-fine-tuning, which greatly enhances its applicability.

</details>


### [195] [Copula-Based Aggregation and Context-Aware Conformal Prediction for Reliable Renewable Energy Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02583)
*Alireza Moradi,Mathieu Tanneau,Reza Zandehshahvar,Pascal Van Hentenryck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework to produce calibrated fleet-level probabilistic forecasts from heterogeneous site-level forecasts using copula-based dependence modeling and Context-Aware Conformal Prediction (CACP).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Operators lack fleet-level probabilistic models and must aggregate site-level forecasts; aggregation can miscalibrate due to complex cross-site dependencies. A calibrated, dependence-aware approach is needed to provide reliable, sharp fleet forecasts without building full fleet models.

Method: Integrates copula-based dependence modeling to capture cross-site correlations with Context-Aware Conformal Prediction (CACP) to correct miscalibration at the aggregated level, ensuring valid coverage and sharp prediction intervals.

Result: Empirical evaluation on large-scale solar datasets from MISO, ERCOT, and SPP shows near-nominal coverage with significantly sharper intervals than uncalibrated aggregation baselines, across multiple sites.

Conclusion: The Copula+CACP framework enables coherent, calibrated fleet-level probabilistic forecasts from site-level inputs, combining dependence-aware aggregation with valid calibration and improved sharpness.

Abstract: The rapid growth of renewable energy penetration has intensified the need for reliable probabilistic forecasts to support grid operations at aggregated (fleet or system) levels. In practice, however, system operators often lack access to fleet-level probabilistic models and instead rely on site-level forecasts produced by heterogeneous third-party providers. Constructing coherent and calibrated fleet-level probabilistic forecasts from such inputs remains challenging due to complex cross-site dependencies and aggregation-induced miscalibration. This paper proposes a calibrated probabilistic aggregation framework that directly converts site-level probabilistic forecasts into reliable fleet-level forecasts in settings where system-level models cannot be trained or maintained. The framework integrates copula-based dependence modeling to capture cross-site correlations with Context-Aware Conformal Prediction (CACP) to correct miscalibration at the aggregated level. This combination enables dependence-aware aggregation while providing valid coverage and maintaining sharp prediction intervals. Experiments on large-scale solar generation datasets from MISO, ERCOT, and SPP demonstrate that the proposed Copula+CACP approach consistently achieves near-nominal coverage with significantly sharper intervals than uncalibrated aggregation baselines.

</details>


### [196] [Hierarchical Entity-centric Reinforcement Learning with Factored Subgoal Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02722)
*Dan Haramati,Carl Qi,Tal Daniel,Amy Zhang,Aviv Tamar,George Konidaris*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hierarchical, entity-centric offline GCRL framework with a value-based RL agent and a conditional diffusion model for subgoal generation; modular training and post hoc composition improve long-horizon, multi-entity tasks with sparse rewards, achieving large gains and generalization to more entities/horizons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon goals in multi-entity environments are hard due to combinatorial state spaces and sparse rewards; subgoal decomposition and factored structure can improve scalability and generalization of GCRL.

Method: A two-level hierarchy: (1) a value-based GCRL agent; (2) a factored, subgoal-generating conditional diffusion model. The two are trained independently and composed post hoc through selective subgoal generation guided by the value function, enabling modularity and compatibility with existing GCRL algorithms. New benchmark tasks emphasize multi-entity challenges.

Result: The method consistently improves the base RL agent on image-based long-horizon tasks with sparse rewards, achieving over 150% higher success on the hardest task and generalizing to more entities and longer horizons.

Conclusion: A modular, scalable approach that leverages an entity-centric subgoal generator to enhance GCRL performance in multi-entity, high-dimensional settings; demonstrates strong empirical gains and generalization, with rollout videos provided.

Abstract: We propose a hierarchical entity-centric framework for offline Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning (GCRL) that combines subgoal decomposition with factored structure to solve long-horizon tasks in domains with multiple entities. Achieving long-horizon goals in complex environments remains a core challenge in Reinforcement Learning (RL). Domains with multiple entities are particularly difficult due to their combinatorial complexity. GCRL facilitates generalization across goals and the use of subgoal structure, but struggles with high-dimensional observations and combinatorial state-spaces, especially under sparse reward. We employ a two-level hierarchy composed of a value-based GCRL agent and a factored subgoal-generating conditional diffusion model. The RL agent and subgoal generator are trained independently and composed post hoc through selective subgoal generation based on the value function, making the approach modular and compatible with existing GCRL algorithms. We introduce new variations to benchmark tasks that highlight the challenges of multi-entity domains, and show that our method consistently boosts performance of the underlying RL agent on image-based long-horizon tasks with sparse rewards, achieving over 150% higher success rates on the hardest task in our suite and generalizing to increasing horizons and numbers of entities. Rollout videos are provided at: https://sites.google.com/view/hecrl

</details>


### [197] [Effective Frontiers: A Unification of Neural Scaling Laws](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02593)
*Jiaxuan Zou,Zixuan Gong,Ye Su,Huayi Tang,Yong Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A universal, architecture-agnostic framework for neural scaling laws based on progressive coverage of Zipfian patterns, introducing Effective Frontier k*, deriving scaling laws for N, D, C, and unifying Kaplan/Chinchilla via a Max-Bottleneck principle.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of universal intuition in neural scaling laws and move beyond architecture- or kernel-specific explanations by modeling learning as coverage of a long-tail pattern distribution.

Method: Abstract general learning tasks as coverage of a Zipfian pattern distribution; define Effective Frontier k*, relate reducible loss to tail probability with frontier truncation; derive N, D, C scaling laws via capacity, coverage, and optimization bottlenecks; unify regimes via Max-Bottleneck principle.

Result: Explicit scaling laws for model size N, dataset size D, and compute C; interpretation of each factor as capacity, coverage, and optimization bottlenecks; Kaplan and Chinchilla scaling laws presented as equilibrium solutions under different bottlenecks.

Conclusion: Provides a universal, frame-agnostic account of neural scaling laws; reveals a single constrained optimization governing trade-offs and reconciles previously claimed conflicting laws.

Abstract: Neural scaling laws govern the prediction power-law improvement of test loss with respect to model capacity ($N$), datasize ($D$), and compute ($C$). However, existing theoretical explanations often rely on specific architectures or complex kernel methods, lacking intuitive universality. In this paper, we propose a unified framework that abstracts general learning tasks as the progressive coverage of patterns from a long-tail (Zipfian) distribution. We introduce the Effective Frontier ($k_\star$), a threshold in the pattern rank space that separates learned knowledge from the unlearned tail. We prove that reducible loss is asymptotically determined by the probability mass of the tail a resource-dependent frontier truncation. Based on our framework, we derive the precise scaling laws for $N$, $D$, and $C$, attributing them to capacity, coverage, and optimization bottlenecks, respectively. Furthermore, we unify these mechanisms via a Max-Bottleneck principle, demonstrating that the Kaplan and Chinchilla scaling laws are not contradictory, but equilibrium solutions to the same constrained optimization problem under different active bottlenecks.

</details>


### [198] [Causal Flow Q-Learning for Robust Offline Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02847)
*Mingxuan Li,Junzhe Zhang,Elias Bareinboim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a confounding-robust causal offline RL framework using expressive flow-matching policies and a discriminator to handle unobserved confounding from perceptual mismatches in pixel-based demonstrations; optimizes worst-case performance and shows large empirical gains on 25 tasks (1.2x relative to confounding-unaware baselines).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline RL with pixel-based demonstrations is prone to unmeasured confounding due to perceptual mismatches between demonstrator and learner; standard policy gradients assume no confounding, leading to biased learning. A causal perspective is needed to model and mitigate these biases.

Method: Devise a causal offline RL objective that optimizes worst-case performance under confounding; implement expressive flow-matching policies trained from confounded demonstrations; employ a deep discriminator to quantify discrepancy between the target policy and the nominal behavioral policy; introduce a confounding-robust augmentation procedure.

Result: Empirical evaluation across 25 pixel-based tasks shows the proposed method achieves a 1.2x (120%) improvement in success rate over confounding-unaware, state-of-the-art offline RL baselines.

Conclusion: A causal, confounding-robust approach to offline RL with perceptual confounding is effective; flow-matching policies plus discriminator-based augmentation yield substantial empirical gains, validating the utility of a worst-case, bias-aware objective for offline learning with confounded data.

Abstract: Expressive policies based on flow-matching have been successfully applied in reinforcement learning (RL) more recently due to their ability to model complex action distributions from offline data. These algorithms build on standard policy gradients, which assume that there is no unmeasured confounding in the data. However, this condition does not necessarily hold for pixel-based demonstrations when a mismatch exists between the demonstrator's and the learner's sensory capabilities, leading to implicit confounding biases in offline data. We address the challenge by investigating the problem of confounded observations in offline RL from a causal perspective. We develop a novel causal offline RL objective that optimizes policies' worst-case performance that may arise due to confounding biases. Based on this new objective, we introduce a practical implementation that learns expressive flow-matching policies from confounded demonstrations, employing a deep discriminator to assess the discrepancy between the target policy and the nominal behavioral policy. Experiments across 25 pixel-based tasks demonstrate that our proposed confounding-robust augmentation procedure achieves a success rate 120\% that of confounding-unaware, state-of-the-art offline RL methods.

</details>


### [199] [Fubini Study geometry of representation drift in high dimensional data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02596)
*Arturo Tozzi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Projective-geometry framework for representation drift via the Fubini-Study metric that factors out gauge transformations, showing standard metrics overestimate drift; introduces a gauge-invariant drift and a monotone gauge-churn measure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parametrization-induced variation contaminates drift metrics; need invariants under gauge transformations to isolate intrinsic evolution of high-dimensional representations.

Method: Map representations to projective space and apply the Fubini-Study metric; identify gauge transformations (global rescalings, sign flips); construct representation trajectories; compare Euclidean, cosine, and Fubini-Study drift along trajectories; derive a computable difference between cosine and Fubini-Study as a monotone gauge-churn metric; validate on high-dimensional datasets.

Result: Euclidean and cosine drift overestimate change when gauge freedom is present; Fubini-Study drift is invariant to gauge; the difference cosine−Fubini-Study provides a monotone, computable gauge-churn measure; diagnostic for intrinsic evolution without model-specific assumptions.

Conclusion: Embeds representation dynamics in projective space to obtain a geometric criterion for representation stability; clarifies limits of angular distances; links data analysis with established geometry; yields observables directly testable in empirical workflows.

Abstract: High dimensional representation drift is commonly quantified using Euclidean or cosine distances, which presuppose fixed coordinates when comparing representations across time, training or preprocessing stages. While effective in many settings, these measures entangle intrinsic changes in the data with variations induced by arbitrary parametrizations. We introduce a projective geometric view of representation drift grounded in the Fubini Study metric, which identifies representations that differ only by gauge transformations such as global rescalings or sign flips. Applying this framework to empirical high dimensional datasets, we explicitly construct representation trajectories and track their evolution through cumulative geometric drift. Comparing Euclidean, cosine and Fubini Study distances along these trajectories reveals that conventional metrics systematically overestimate change whenever representations carry genuine projective ambiguity. By contrast, the Fubini Study metric isolates intrinsic evolution by remaining invariant under gauge-induced fluctuations. We further show that the difference between cosine and Fubini Study drift defines a computable, monotone quantity that directly captures representation churn attributable to gauge freedom. This separation provides a diagnostic for distinguishing meaningful structural evolution from parametrization artifacts, without introducing model-specific assumptions. Overall, we establish a geometric criterion for assessing representation stability in high-dimensional systems and clarify the limits of angular distances. Embedding representation dynamics in projective space connects data analysis with established geometric programs and yields observables that are directly testable in empirical workflows.

</details>


### [200] [ContextEvolve: Multi-Agent Context Compression for Systems Code Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02597)
*Hongyuan Su,Yu Zheng,Yong Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ContextEvolve: a tri-agent framework enabling RL-like search efficiency under parameter-blind constraints by decomposing optimization context into Summarizer, Navigator, and Sampler, achieving improved ADRS results in a text latent space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models can generate plausible but not necessarily correct systems solutions; achieving high-performance optimization under API-only access requires efficient search; existing training-free evolutionary methods have inefficient context utilization and undirected search.

Method: Three orthogonal agents: Summarizer condenses semantic state via code-to-language abstraction; Navigator extracts optimization direction from trajectory analysis; Sampler reshapes experience distribution via prioritized exemplar retrieval. The orchestration maps to RL components (state, policy, replay) in a textual latent space.

Result: On the ADRS benchmark, ContextEvolve outperforms baselines by 33.3% and reduces token consumption by 29.0%.

Conclusion: Demonstrates RL-level search efficiency under parameter-blind constraints through multi-agent orchestration in a textual latent space; releases code at the provided URL.

Abstract: Large language models are transforming systems research by automating the discovery of performance-critical algorithms for computer systems. Despite plausible codes generated by LLMs, producing solutions that meet the stringent correctness and performance requirements of systems demands iterative optimization. Test-time reinforcement learning offers high search efficiency but requires parameter updates infeasible under API-only access, while existing training-free evolutionary methods suffer from inefficient context utilization and undirected search. We introduce ContextEvolve, a multi-agent framework that achieves RL-level search efficiency under strict parameter-blind constraints by decomposing optimization context into three orthogonal dimensions: a Summarizer Agent condenses semantic state via code-to-language abstraction, a Navigator Agent distills optimization direction from trajectory analysis, and a Sampler Agent curates experience distribution through prioritized exemplar retrieval. This orchestration forms a functional isomorphism with RL-mapping to state representation, policy gradient, and experience replay-enabling principled optimization in a textual latent space. On the ADRS benchmark, ContextEvolve outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by 33.3% while reducing token consumption by 29.0%. Codes for our work are released at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ContextEvolve-ACC

</details>


### [201] [RAP: KV-Cache Compression via RoPE-Aligned Pruning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02599)
*Jihao Xin,Tian Lvu,David Keyes,Hatem Ltaief,Marco Canini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RoPE-Aligned Pruning (RAP) preserves RoPE's 2x2 rotation by pruning RoPE-aligned column pairs, enabling B absorption and avoiding KV reconstruction. Evaluated on LLaMA-3-8B and Mistral-7B, RAP reduces KV-Cache size, attention parameters, and FLOPs by 20–30% with maintained accuracy, and cuts attention latency to 83% (prefill) and 77% (decode) of baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context inference is bottlenecked by memory and compute in KV-Cache. Low-rank factorization (W ≈ A B) cannot be easily used with RoPE-based LLMs because latent KV states must reconstruct to full dimension, negating the absorption trick.

Method: Introduce RoPE-Aligned Pruning (RAP) that removes RoPE-aligned column pairs to keep RoPE's rotation structure intact while allowing the B matrix to absorb the remaining factors, thereby avoiding KV reconstruction. Evaluate on LLaMA-3-8B and Mistral-7B with metrics on KV-Cache, attention parameters, FLOPs, and latency across prefill and decode phases.

Result: Joint 20–30% reductions in KV-Cache, attention parameters, and FLOPs with preserved accuracy. Attention latency reduced to 83% of baseline during prefill and 77% during decode.

Conclusion: RAP offers a practical, RoPE-compatible pruning strategy that achieves cross-component efficiency gains (KV-Cache, attention, FLOPs) without sacrificing performance on RoPE-based LLMs.

Abstract: Long-context inference in large language models is increasingly bottlenecked by the memory and compute cost of the KV-Cache. Low-rank factorization compresses KV projections by writing $W \approx A * B$, where A produces latent KV states and B can be absorbed into downstream weights. In modern RoPE-based LLMs, this absorption fails: RoPE forces latent KV states to be reconstructed to full dimension, reintroducing substantial memory and compute overhead. We propose RoPE-Aligned Pruning (RAP), which prunes entire RoPE-aligned column pairs to preserve RoPE's 2x2 rotation structure, restore B absorption, and eliminate reconstruction. Our evaluation on LLaMA-3-8B and Mistral-7B shows that RAP enables joint reduction of KV-Cache, attention parameters, and FLOPs by 20-30%, all at once, while maintaining strong accuracy. Notably, RAP reduces attention latency to 83% (prefill) and 77% (decode) of baseline.

</details>


### [202] [Step-Wise Refusal Dynamics in Autoregressive and Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02600)
*Eliron Rahimi,Elad Hirshel,Rom Himelstein,Amit LeVi,Avi Mendelson,Chaim Baskin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces SRI, a step-wise internal dynamics framework to analyse refusal behavior in diffusion language models (and AR models), revealing sampling strategy as a key safety determinant and enabling efficient detectors with strong generalization on unseen attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how sampling mechanics influence refusal/jailbreak robustness beyond learned representations, and build interpretable, efficient safety tools for both AR and diffusion LMs.

Method: Develop a formal analytical framework for step-wise refusal dynamics; compare AR vs diffusion sampling; define the Step-Wise Refusal Internal Dynamics (SRI) signal; analyze its geometric structure to capture recovery dynamics; implement lightweight inference-time detectors based on SRI.

Result: SRI captures internal recovery dynamics; turns anomalous harmful generations into incomplete internal recovery events not detectable at the text level; detectors generalize to unseen attacks and achieve over 100x lower inference overhead while matching or surpassing existing defenses.

Conclusion: Sampling strategy, not just learned representations, governs safety behavior in sequence models. The SRI framework provides interpretable insights and practical, efficient detectors that generalize to new attacks for both AR and diffusion LMs.

Abstract: Diffusion language models (DLMs) have recently emerged as a promising alternative to autoregressive (AR) models, offering parallel decoding and controllable sampling dynamics while achieving competitive generation quality at scale. Despite this progress, the role of sampling mechanisms in shaping refusal behavior and jailbreak robustness remains poorly understood. In this work, we present a fundamental analytical framework for step-wise refusal dynamics, enabling comparison between AR and diffusion sampling. Our analysis reveals that the sampling strategy itself plays a central role in safety behavior, as a factor distinct from the underlying learned representations. Motivated by this analysis, we introduce the Step-Wise Refusal Internal Dynamics (SRI) signal, which supports interpretability and improved safety for both AR and DLMs. We demonstrate that the geometric structure of SRI captures internal recovery dynamics, and identifies anomalous behavior in harmful generations as cases of \emph{incomplete internal recovery} that are not observable at the text level. This structure enables lightweight inference-time detectors that generalize to unseen attacks while matching or outperforming existing defenses with over $100\times$ lower inference overhead.

</details>


### [203] [Discovering Data Manifold Geometry via Non-Contracting Flows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02611)
*David Vigouroux,Lucas Drumetz,Ronan Fablet,François Rousseau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unsupervised global reference frame learning on manifolds by learning ambient tangent vector fields that span tangent spaces; uses flow-based, non-shrinking objective inspired by flow matching to avoid integration; proves recovery of a global chart when possible; demonstrates tangent alignment on synthetic data and scalable CIFAR-10 results with competitive downstream performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcomes the limitations of isometric/objective assumptions of flat manifolds by directly learning tangent fields that define flows to a common reference point, yielding interpretable intrinsic coordinates tied to a global frame and scalable computation without integrating flows.

Method: Learn tangent vector fields in ambient space spanning manifold tangents; flows transport samples to a learnable reference point; enforce non-shrinking constraint to avoid collapse; use an integration-free objective inspired by flow matching; theoretical proof that minimization recovers a global coordinate chart when it exists.

Result: Empirically achieves correct tangent alignment and coherent global coordinates on synthetic manifolds; demonstrates scalability to CIFAR-10 and competitive downstream classification performance.

Conclusion: Provides a principled unsupervised approach for global coordinate discovery on data manifolds with a global frame, supported by theory and scalable empirical validation; suitable for unsupervised representation learning and interpretable coordinate systems.

Abstract: We introduce an unsupervised approach for constructing a global reference system by learning, in the ambient space, vector fields that span the tangent spaces of an unknown data manifold. In contrast to isometric objectives, which implicitly assume manifold flatness, our method learns tangent vector fields whose flows transport all samples to a common, learnable reference point. The resulting arc-lengths along these flows define interpretable intrinsic coordinates tied to a shared global frame. To prevent degenerate collapse, we enforce a non-shrinking constraint and derive a scalable, integration-free objective inspired by flow matching. Within our theoretical framework, we prove that minimizing the proposed objective recovers a global coordinate chart when one exists. Empirically, we obtain correct tangent alignment and coherent global coordinate structure on synthetic manifolds. We also demonstrate the scalability of our method on CIFAR-10, where the learned coordinates achieve competitive downstream classification performance.

</details>


### [204] [A Semi-Supervised Pipeline for Generalized Behavior Discovery from Animal-Borne Motion Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02618)
*Fatemeh Karimi Nejadasl,Judy Shamoun-Baranes,Eldar Rakhimberdiev*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Semi-supervised discovery for animal motion time series using KDE+HDR containment to detect novel behavioral clusters when labels are scarce and classes imbalanced.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Behavioral taxonomies from animal-borne sensors are hard due to scarce labels, severe class imbalance, and potential absence of some behaviors from annotated sets; the work targets generalized behavior discovery from short multivariate motion snippets for gulls across nine expert-annotated categories.

Method: Embed labeled data, perform label-guided clustering on embeddings of both labeled and unlabeled samples to form candidate behavior groups, and use a KDE+HDR containment score to quantify how a discovered cluster relates to known-class distributions; select the best-match containment as a novelty statistic.

Result: When entire behavior is withheld from supervision (novelty only in unlabeled pool), the method recovers a distinct cluster and the containment score indicates novelty via low overlap; a negative-control (no novel behavior) yields consistently higher overlaps with known classes.

Conclusion: HDR-based containment provides a practical, quantitative test for generalized class discovery in ecological motion time series under limited annotation and severe class imbalance.

Abstract: Learning behavioral taxonomies from animal-borne sensors is challenging because labels are scarce, classes are highly imbalanced, and behaviors may be absent from the annotated set. We study generalized behavior discovery in short multivariate motion snippets from gulls, where each sample is a sequence with 3-axis IMU acceleration (20 Hz) and GPS speed, spanning nine expert-annotated behavior categories. We propose a semi-supervised discovery pipeline that (i) learns an embedding function from the labeled subset, (ii) performs label-guided clustering over embeddings of both labeled and unlabeled samples to form candidate behavior groups, and (iii) decides whether a discovered group is truly novel using a containment score. Our key contribution is a KDE + HDR (highest-density region) containment score that measures how much a discovered cluster distribution is contained within, or contains, each known-class distribution; the best-match containment score serves as an interpretable novelty statistic. In experiments where an entire behavior is withheld from supervision and appears only in the unlabeled pool, the method recovers a distinct cluster and the containment score flags novelty via low overlap, while a negative-control setting with no novel behavior yields consistently higher overlaps. These results suggest that HDR-based containment provides a practical, quantitative test for generalized class discovery in ecological motion time series under limited annotation and severe class imbalance.

</details>


### [205] [daVinci-Agency: Unlocking Long-Horizon Agency Data-Efficiently](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02619)
*Mohan Jiang,Dayuan Fu,Junhao Shi,Ji Zeng,Weiye Si,Keyu Li,Xuefeng Li,Yang Xiao,Wenjie Li,Dequan Wang,Pengfei Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PR sequences provide scalable, real-world supervision for long-horizon agentic learning. daVinci-Agency uses chain-of-PRs to decompose tasks, enforce long-term consistency, and leverage bug-fix refinements, enabling data-efficient, project-aligned training with strong Toolathlon gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the data bottleneck for long-horizon, goal-directed behavior in LLMs. Real-world software evolution (pull requests) offers verifiable, iterative signals that encode long dependencies and refinement trajectories beyond synthetic data.

Method: Introduce daVinci-Agency that mines PR sequences as structured supervision via three mechanisms: (1) progressive task decomposition through continuous commits; (2) long-term consistency via unified functional objectives; (3) verifiable refinement from authentic bug-fix trajectories. The approach yields long trajectories (~85k tokens, 116 tool calls) and leverages fine-tuning of GLM-4.6 on 239 samples.

Result: Data-efficient improvements: notably a 47% relative gain on Toolathlon and broad benchmark gains when fine-tuning GLM-4.6 on the daVinci-Agency data. The PR-grounded structure preserves causal dependencies and iterative refinements for persistent goal-directed behavior.

Conclusion: PR-based supervision grounded in chain-of-PRs supports scalable, long-horizon learning that aligns with full-cycle project tasks, delivering substantial performance gains with limited data and maintaining functional coherence across iterations.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at short-term tasks, scaling them to long-horizon agentic workflows remains challenging. The core bottleneck lies in the scarcity of training data that captures authentic long-dependency structures and cross-stage evolutionary dynamics--existing synthesis methods either confine to single-feature scenarios constrained by model distribution, or incur prohibitive human annotation costs, failing to provide scalable, high-quality supervision. We address this by reconceptualizing data synthesis through the lens of real-world software evolution. Our key insight: Pull Request (PR) sequences naturally embody the supervision signals for long-horizon learning. They decompose complex objectives into verifiable submission units, maintain functional coherence across iterations, and encode authentic refinement patterns through bug-fix histories. Building on this, we propose daVinci-Agency, which systematically mines structured supervision from chain-of-PRs through three interlocking mechanisms: (1) progressive task decomposition via continuous commits, (2) long-term consistency enforcement through unified functional objectives, and (3) verifiable refinement from authentic bug-fix trajectories. Unlike synthetic trajectories that treat each step independently, daVinci-Agency's PR-grounded structure inherently preserves the causal dependencies and iterative refinements essential for teaching persistent goal-directed behavior and enables natural alignment with project-level, full-cycle task modeling. The resulting trajectories are substantial--averaging 85k tokens and 116 tool calls--yet remarkably data-efficient: fine-tuning GLM-4.6 on 239 daVinci-Agency samples yields broad improvements across benchmarks, notably achieving a 47% relative gain on Toolathlon. Beyond benchmark performance, our analysis confirms...

</details>


### [206] [Learning Consistent Causal Abstraction Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02623)
*Gabriele D'Acunto,Paolo Di Lorenzo,Sergio Barbarossa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Gaussian-CAN: a sheaf-theoretic causal abstraction framework learning consistent CA structures via edge-wise local Riemannian problems solved with a spectral iterative method (SPECTRAL); validated on synthetic data with successful CAN recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Advance explainability, trustworthiness, and robustness in AI by embedding causal knowledge into a sheaf/cosheaf framework and learning consistent causal abstractions across SCMs.

Method: Propose CAN: a Gaussian SCM-based, sheaf-theoretic framework where edge restriction maps are transposes of constructive linear causal abstractions adhering to a semantic embedding principle; edge stalks align with node stalks of detailed SCMs up to permutation. Decompose learning into edge-specific local Riemannian problems and solve with SPECTRAL—an iterative method with closed-form updates suitable for PD/PSD covariance matrices.

Result: On synthetic data, the method achieves competitive CA learning performance and successfully recovers diverse CAN structures.

Conclusion: The approach provides a tractable, nonconvex-avoiding pathway to learn consistent causal abstractions within a CAN, demonstrating practical viability and enabling structured causal knowledge transfer; future work may address real-world data, scalability, and robustness.

Abstract: Causal artificial intelligence aims to enhance explainability, trustworthiness, and robustness in AI by leveraging structural causal models (SCMs). In this pursuit, recent advances formalize network sheaves and cosheaves of causal knowledge. Pushing in the same direction, we tackle the learning of consistent causal abstraction network (CAN), a sheaf-theoretic framework where (i) SCMs are Gaussian, (ii) restriction maps are transposes of constructive linear causal abstractions (CAs) adhering to the semantic embedding principle, and (iii) edge stalks correspond--up to permutation--to the node stalks of more detailed SCMs. Our problem formulation separates into edge-specific local Riemannian problems and avoids nonconvex objectives. We propose an efficient search procedure, solving the local problems with SPECTRAL, our iterative method with closed-form updates and suitable for positive definite and semidefinite covariance matrices. Experiments on synthetic data show competitive performance in the CA learning task, and successful recovery of diverse CAN structures.

</details>


### [207] [Learning Better Certified Models from Empirically-Robust Teachers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02626)
*Alessandro De Palma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Distilling from empirically robust teachers improves certifiable robustness with competitive standard accuracy in ReLU networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between empirical robustness and verifiable robustness by transferring knowledge from adversarially trained teachers to certifiably robust students.

Method: Knowledge distillation in feature space using an objective that aligns student and teacher representations while training for certifiable robustness (on bounds from network relaxations).

Result: Consistent improvement over the state-of-the-art certified training methods across robust computer vision benchmarks.

Conclusion: Teacher-informed distillation is an effective approach to enhance certifiable robustness without severely compromising standard performance.

Abstract: Adversarial training attains strong empirical robustness to specific adversarial attacks by training on concrete adversarial perturbations, but it produces neural networks that are not amenable to strong robustness certificates through neural network verification. On the other hand, earlier certified training schemes directly train on bounds from network relaxations to obtain models that are certifiably robust, but display sub-par standard performance. Recent work has shown that state-of-the-art trade-offs between certified robustness and standard performance can be obtained through a family of losses combining adversarial outputs and neural network bounds. Nevertheless, differently from empirical robustness, verifiability still comes at a significant cost in standard performance. In this work, we propose to leverage empirically-robust teachers to improve the performance of certifiably-robust models through knowledge distillation. Using a versatile feature-space distillation objective, we show that distillation from adversarially-trained teachers consistently improves on the state-of-the-art in certified training for ReLU networks across a series of robust computer vision benchmarks.

</details>


### [208] [Performance of Small Language Model Pretraining on FABRIC: An Empirical Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02632)
*Praveen Rao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Evaluates pretraining strategies for small LLMs on commodity GPUs, comparing data, intra- and inter-operator (pipeline) parallelism and their combinations using Alpa and Ray; finds Alpa’s plans optimizing intra- and inter-operator parallelism perform best for geographically distributed GPUs; offers a systematic approach to select pretraining techniques to minimize training time and GPU usage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable efficient pretraining of small LLMs on limited data and accessible hardware; understand how parallelism strategies and network latency affect performance to guide practical deployment.

Method: Experimental testbed with GPT-2 medium and large models pretrained via open-source frameworks Alpa and Ray on GPU clusters (both homogeneous and heterogeneous); explore data parallelism, intra-operator parallelism, inter-operator/pipeline parallelism, and their combinations; vary hardware configurations and network latencies across geographically distributed GPUs to measure training performance (execution time).

Result: Alpa’s execution plans that jointly optimize intra-operator and inter-operator/pipeline parallelism consistently yield the best performance in distributed setups, particularly when inter-GPU latencies are tens of milliseconds; network latency is a key factor shaping speedups; a systematic approach is proposed to select the pretraining technique that balances speed and GPU usage.

Conclusion: The study provides practical guidelines for choosing pretraining strategies on commodity hardware, emphasizing the interplay between parallelism type and network latency; the proposed approach helps reduce GPU counts while achieving high training performance for small LLMs.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) require enormous computing power to pretrain on massive datasets. When limited datasets are available, smaller-sized LLMs are better choice to pretrain (on user-specified datasets) by following the scaling laws of LLMs. Using pretrained models, vector embeddings can be generated for raw data and stored using vector databases to support modern AI applications and semantic search. In this work, we investigate the performance of pretraining techniques for smaller-sized LLMs on an experimental testbed (with commodity GPUs) available to academic users at no charge. We consider data parallelism, intra-operator parallelism, and inter-operator/pipeline parallelism, and their combinations for pretraining. We set up different GPU clusters with homogeneous and heterogeneous GPU hardware. Furthermore, we investigate the impact of network latency on pretraining performance especially when GPUs are geographically distributed. We used GPT-2 medium and large models and pretrained them using open-source packages, namely, Alpa and Ray. We observed that Alpa's execution plans that collectively optimized intra-operator and inter-operator/pipeline parallelism consistently performed the best when GPUs were geographically distributed. This was especially true when the network latencies were in 10's of milliseconds. Based on the insights gained from the experiments, we propose a systematic approach for selecting the appropriate pretraining technique to achieve high training performance/lower execution time as well as to reduce the number of GPUs used.

</details>


### [209] [A Reduction from Delayed to Immediate Feedback for Online Convex Optimization with Improved Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02634)
*Alexander Ryabchenko,Idan Attias,Daniel M. Roy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reduction-based framework for online learning with delays that decomposes regret into a delay-free learning term and a delay-induced drift, enabling a delay-adaptive reduction from online linear optimization to delayed feedback; yields improved regret bounds for bandit convex optimization and first-order settings, with explicit delay-terms in terms of total delay and problem dimensions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address regret growth due to delayed feedback in online learning and bandit optimization, and to unify and simplify analyses while improving delay-dependent terms.

Method: Introduce a continuous-time model in which regret splits into a delay-independent learning term and a delay-induced drift term. Develop a delay-adaptive reduction that converts any online linear optimization algorithm into one that handles round-dependent delays. Apply the framework to bandit convex optimization and first-order feedback, yielding unified estimations and simpler analyses.

Result: Bandit convex optimization achieves O(sqrt(d_tot) + T^{3/4} sqrt(k)) regret, with the delay-dependent term improved from O(min{sqrt{T d_max}, (T d_tot)^{1/3}}) to O(sqrt(d_tot)). Under strong convexity, achieve O(min{σ_max ln T, sqrt(d_tot)} + (T^2 ln T)^{1/3} k^{2/3}), improving the delay-dependent term from O(d_max ln T) to O(min{σ_max ln T, sqrt(d_tot)}).

Conclusion: The delay-adaptive reduction framework provides a unified, simpler, and tighter analysis for online learning with delays, matching or surpassing state-of-the-art bounds across both bandit and first-order settings, with favorable dependence on total/maximum delays and the number of outstanding observations.

Abstract: We develop a reduction-based framework for online learning with delayed feedback that recovers and improves upon existing results for both first-order and bandit convex optimization. Our approach introduces a continuous-time model under which regret decomposes into a delay-independent learning term and a delay-induced drift term, yielding a delay-adaptive reduction that converts any algorithm for online linear optimization into one that handles round-dependent delays. For bandit convex optimization, we significantly improve existing regret bounds, with delay-dependent terms matching state-of-the-art first-order rates. For first-order feedback, we recover state-of-the-art regret bounds via a simpler, unified analysis. Quantitatively, for bandit convex optimization we obtain $O(\sqrt{d_{\text{tot}}} + T^{\frac{3}{4}}\sqrt{k})$ regret, improving the delay-dependent term from $O(\min\{\sqrt{T d_{\text{max}}},(Td_{\text{tot}})^{\frac{1}{3}}\})$ in previous work to $O(\sqrt{d_{\text{tot}}})$. Here, $k$, $T$, $d_{\text{max}}$, and $d_{\text{tot}}$ denote the dimension, time horizon, maximum delay, and total delay, respectively. Under strong convexity, we achieve $O(\min\{σ_{\text{max}} \ln T, \sqrt{d_{\text{tot}}}\} + (T^2\ln T)^{\frac{1}{3}} {k}^{\frac{2}{3}})$, improving the delay-dependent term from $O(d_{\text{max}} \ln T)$ in previous work to $O(\min\{σ_{\text{max}} \ln T, \sqrt{d_{\text{tot}}}\})$, where $σ_{\text{max}}$ denotes the maximum number of outstanding observations and may be considerably smaller than $d_{\text{max}}$.

</details>


### [210] [hSNMF: Hybrid Spatially Regularized NMF for Image-Derived Spatial Transcriptomics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02638)
*Md Ishtyaq Mahmud,Veena Kochat,Suresh Satpati,Jagan Mohan Reddy Dwarampudi,Humaira Anzum,Kunal Rai,Tania Banerjee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Spatial NMF variants SNMF and hSNMF improve spatial coherence and clustering in high-dimensional Xenium spatial transcriptomics data, via diffusion-based smoothing and a hybrid spatial-transcriptomic adjacency for Leiden clustering; validated on cholangiocarcinoma data; code available on GitHub.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of high dimensionality and pronounced spatial structure in high-resolution spatial transcriptomics by incorporating spatial context into matrix factorization and clustering, aiming for more coherent spatial domains and biologically meaningful clusters.

Method: SNMF diffuses each cell's NMF factor vector across the spatial neighborhood to enforce local spatial smoothness. hSNMF performs spatially regularized NMF and then clusters with Leiden on a hybrid adjacency that combines spatial proximity (contact-radius graph) and transcriptomic similarity, controlled by a mixing parameter alpha.

Result: On a cholangiocarcinoma dataset, SNMF and hSNMF show markedly improved spatial compactness (CHAOS < 0.004; Moran's I > 0.96), enhanced cluster separability (Silhouette > 0.12; DBI < 1.8), and higher biological coherence (cell-type meshed clustering and enrichment) relative to competing spatial baselines.

Conclusion: Spatially regularized NMF variants can achieve superior spatially coherent representations and clustering for high-resolution spatial transcriptomics data, with accessible open-source implementation for reuse.

Abstract: High-resolution spatial transcriptomics platforms, such as Xenium, generate single-cell images that capture both molecular and spatial context, but their extremely high dimensionality poses major challenges for representation learning and clustering. In this study, we analyze data from the Xenium platform, which captures high-resolution images of tumor microarray (TMA) tissues and converts them into cell-by-gene matrices suitable for computational analysis. We benchmark and extend nonnegative matrix factorization (NMF) for spatial transcriptomics by introducing two spatially regularized variants. First, we propose Spatial NMF (SNMF), a lightweight baseline that enforces local spatial smoothness by diffusing each cell's NMF factor vector over its spatial neighborhood. Second, we introduce Hybrid Spatial NMF (hSNMF), which performs spatially regularized NMF followed by Leiden clustering on a hybrid adjacency that integrates spatial proximity (via a contact-radius graph) and transcriptomic similarity through a tunable mixing parameter alpha. Evaluated on a cholangiocarcinoma dataset, SNMF and hSNMF achieve markedly improved spatial compactness (CHAOS < 0.004, Moran's I > 0.96), greater cluster separability (Silhouette > 0.12, DBI < 1.8), and higher biological coherence (CMC and enrichment) compared to other spatial baselines. Availability and implementation: https://github.com/ishtyaqmahmud/hSNMF

</details>


### [211] [MARA: Continuous SE(3)-Equivariant Attention for Molecular Force Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02671)
*Francesco Leonardi,Boris Bonev,Kaspar Riesen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Modular Angular-Radial Attention (MARA), a modular extension of spherical attention for SE(3) molecular tasks that is plug-and-play with existing models like MACE, improving energy/force accuracy and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed angular expansions in ML force fields constrain flexible weighting of local geometric interactions; there is a need for a learnable, geometry-aware attention mechanism operating on angular and radial coordinates.

Method: MARA operates directly on angular and radial coordinates of neighboring atoms to provide an efficient, equivariant interaction approximation. It enables continuous spherical attention and can be integrated into SE(3)-equivariant architectures in a plug-and-play fashion without major architectural changes.

Result: Across molecular benchmarks, MARA improves energy and force predictions, reduces high-error events, and enhances robustness.

Conclusion: Continuous spherical attention is a generalizable, effective geometric operator that increases expressiveness, stability, and reliability of atomistic models.

Abstract: Machine learning force fields (MLFFs) have become essential for accurate and efficient atomistic modeling. Despite their high accuracy, most existing approaches rely on fixed angular expansions, limiting flexibility in weighting local geometric interactions. We introduce Modular Angular-Radial Attention (MARA), a module that extends spherical attention -- originally developed for SO(3) tasks -- to the molecular domain and SE(3), providing an efficient approximation of equivariant interactions. MARA operates directly on the angular and radial coordinates of neighboring atoms, enabling flexible, geometrically informed, and modular weighting of local environments. Unlike existing attention mechanisms in SE(3)-equivariant architectures, MARA can be integrated in a plug-and-play manner into models such as MACE without architectural modifications. Across molecular benchmarks, MARA improves energy and force predictions, reduces high-error events, and enhances robustness. These results demonstrate that continuous spherical attention is an effective and generalizable geometric operator that increases the expressiveness, stability, and reliability of atomistic models.

</details>


### [212] [FlexRank: Nested Low-Rank Knowledge Decomposition for Adaptive Model Deployment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02680)
*Riccardo Zaccone,Stefanos Laskaridis,Marco Ciccone,Samuel Horváth*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlexRank enables budget-aware deployment by extracting a nested sequence of submodels from pretrained networks via low-rank decomposition and importance-based consolidation, allowing train-once, deploy-everywhere with graceful cost–performance trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The scale of modern DL models makes training from scratch costly and deployment inflexible; fixed-cost monoliths fail to exploit overparameterization across different compute budgets.

Method: Apply low-rank weight decomposition with nested, importance-based consolidation to derive a hierarchy of submodels with increasing capacity, enabling selective activation based on budget.

Result: Demonstrates a train-once, deploy-everywhere paradigm yielding cost-efficient submodels; enables graceful trade-offs without per-budget retraining.

Conclusion: FlexRank advances practical deployment of large models by enabling budget-adaptive submodels derived from pretrained weights.

Abstract: The growing scale of deep neural networks, encompassing large language models (LLMs) and vision transformers (ViTs), has made training from scratch prohibitively expensive and deployment increasingly costly. These models are often used as computational monoliths with fixed cost, a rigidity that does not leverage overparametrized architectures and largely hinders adaptive deployment across different cost budgets. We argue that importance-ordered nested components can be extracted from pretrained models, and selectively activated on the available computational budget. To this end, our proposed FlexRank method leverages low-rank weight decomposition with nested, importance-based consolidation to extract submodels of increasing capabilities. Our approach enables a "train-once, deploy-everywhere" paradigm that offers a graceful trade-off between cost and performance without training from scratch for each budget - advancing practical deployment of large models.

</details>


### [213] [Expert-Data Alignment Governs Generation Quality in Decentralized Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02685)
*Marcos Villagra,Bidhan Roy,Raihan Seraj,Zhiying Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: In decentralized diffusion models, generation quality is governed by expert-data alignment rather than denoising-trajectory stability; full ensemble routing is numerically stable but yields poor quality, while sparse Top-2 routing aligned with data clusters achieves best quality (lower FID).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand what governs generation quality in systems where multiple experts trained on disjoint data clusters can disagree, challenging the assumption that stability of the denoising trajectory drives quality.

Method: Empirical analysis across two DDM systems comparing routing strategies (full ensemble vs sparse Top-2). The study uses data-cluster distance analyses, per-expert performance, and disagreement metrics to assess how routing inputs align with the current denoising state and correlate with generation quality (FID).

Result: Full ensemble routing produced the most stable sampling dynamics and fastest numerical convergence but the worst generation quality (higher FID). Sparse Top-2 routing achieved the best generation quality (lower FID). Expert-data alignment emerged as the governing principle: routing tends to select experts whose training data clusters are closest to the current denoising state; selected experts yield more accurate predictions; disagreement among experts degrades quality.

Conclusion: Routing policies for DDM deployment should prioritize expert-data alignment over purely numerical stability metrics, to ensure higher-quality generations.

Abstract: Decentralized Diffusion Models (DDMs) route denoising through experts trained independently on disjoint data clusters, which can strongly disagree in their predictions. What governs the quality of generations in such systems? We present the first ever systematic investigation of this question. A priori, the expectation is that minimizing denoising trajectory sensitivity -- minimizing how perturbations amplify during sampling -- should govern generation quality. We demonstrate this hypothesis is incorrect: a stability-quality dissociation. Full ensemble routing, which combines all expert predictions at each step, achieves the most stable sampling dynamics and best numerical convergence while producing the worst generation quality (FID 47.9 vs. 22.6 for sparse Top-2 routing). Instead, we identify expert-data alignment as the governing principle: generation quality depends on routing inputs to experts whose training distribution covers the current denoising state. Across two distinct DDM systems, we validate expert-data alignment using (i) data-cluster distance analysis, confirming sparse routing selects experts with data clusters closest to the current denoising state, and (ii) per-expert analysis, showing selected experts produce more accurate predictions than non-selected ones, and (iii) expert disagreement analysis, showing quality degrades when experts disagree. For DDM deployment, our findings establish that routing should prioritize expert-data alignment over numerical stability metrics.

</details>


### [214] [Sparsely Supervised Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02699)
*Wenshuai Zhao,Zhiyuan Li,Yi Zhao,Mohammad Hassan Vali,Martin Trapp,Joni Pajarinen,Juho Kannala,Arno Solin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A sparse, masking-based training approach for diffusion models to enhance global spatial coherence and training stability, enabling up to 98% pixel masking with minimal code, yielding competitive FID and reduced memorization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models often produce locally plausible but globally inconsistent samples due to the inherent locality of their denoising steps; there is a need for a lightweight method to improve global coherence and training stability, especially on small datasets.

Method: Introduce sparsely supervised learning via a masking strategy for diffusion model training. During training, randomly mask a large fraction of pixels (up to 98%), providing sparse supervision to guide learning. The approach is simple to implement (a few lines of code).

Result: The method achieves competitive FID scores across multiple experiments, while avoiding training instability on small datasets. The masking reduces memorization and encourages the model to leverage essential contextual information during generation.

Conclusion: A lightweight masking-based sparsely supervised framework effectively improves global consistency and robustness of diffusion models with minimal code changes, and can help mitigate memorization and data sparsity issues.

Abstract: Diffusion models have shown remarkable success across a wide range of generative tasks. However, they often suffer from spatially inconsistent generation, arguably due to the inherent locality of their denoising mechanisms. This can yield samples that are locally plausible but globally inconsistent. To mitigate this issue, we propose sparsely supervised learning for diffusion models, a simple yet effective masking strategy that can be implemented with only a few lines of code. Interestingly, the experiments show that it is safe to mask up to 98\% of pixels during diffusion model training. Our method delivers competitive FID scores across experiments and, most importantly, avoids training instability on small datasets. Moreover, the masking strategy reduces memorization and promotes the use of essential contextual information during generation.

</details>


### [215] [Every Bit Counts: A Theoretical Study of Precision-Expressivity Tradeoffs in Quantized Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02707)
*Sayak Chakrabarti,Toniann Pitassi,Josh Alman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A precise one-bit threshold for quantized Transformer expressivity: with p bits you can implement a function Γ inspired by equality, but with p-1 bits you cannot; a one-layer softmax Transformer suffices for Γ at precision p, establishing a tight threshold and practical guidance for quantization budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantization speeds up inference but can reduce expressivity. Understanding the fundamental tradeoff clarifies why accuracy degrades and guides practical bit-width budgeting, especially for equality-like tasks.

Method: Construct explicit finite-precision one-layer Transformer circuits that implement Γ with p bits; prove impossibility with p-1 bits via communication complexity lower bounds; combine both directions to establish a tight threshold; analysis hinges on an equality-function-inspired Γ.

Result: We prove that there exists a function Γ computable by a one-layer softmax Transformer using p bits of precision but not with p−1 bits; this yields a tight one-bit threshold for expressivity under quantization.

Conclusion: This work links quantization precision to model expressivity, offering practical heuristics for choosing bit-width based on the length of equality checks required by a task, and motivates future methods to mitigate loss of expressivity under aggressive quantization.

Abstract: Quantization reduces the numerical precision of Transformer computations and is widely used to accelerate inference, yet its effect on expressivity remains poorly characterized. We demonstrate a fine-grained theoretical tradeoff between expressivity and precision: For every p we exhibit a function Γ, inspired by the equality function, and prove that a one-layer softmax Transformer can compute Γ, with p bits of precision, but not with p-1 bits of precision.
  This result concretely explains the widely observed phenomenon of empirical loss of expressivity when quantization is used. Practically, it suggests that tasks requiring equality-like comparisons (exact match, membership, etc.) are especially sensitive to quantization. Dropping even one bit can cross a threshold where the model cannot represent the needed comparison reliably. Thus, it paves the way for developing heuristics that will help practitioners choose how much quantization is possible: the precision should be chosen as a function of the length of equality to be checked for the specific task.
  Our proofs combine explicit finite-precision Transformer constructions with communication-complexity lower bounds, yielding a tight "one-bit" threshold.

</details>


### [216] [BinaryPPO: Efficient Policy Optimization for Binary Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02708)
*Punya Syon Pandey,Zhijing Jin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BinaryPPO reframes binary classification as an offline RL problem using a confidence-weighted PPO-like optimization to learn robust decision policies from static datasets, achieving large accuracy gains over SFT in noisy, imbalanced settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the brittleness of supervised fine-tuning for binary classification tasks under label noise, class imbalance, and sparse supervision by leveraging offline RL and a confidence-aware reward design to improve robustness and performance.

Method: Formulates binary classification as a reward-maximization task.
