Uses a variant of Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) with a confidence-weighted reward that penalizes uncertain or incorrect predictions. Trains entirely on static datasets (offline) without online interaction. Evaluates across eight domain-specific benchmarks and multiple model architectures.

Result: BinaryPPO improves accuracy by 40–60 percentage points and reaches up to 99% on several benchmarks, substantially outperforming supervised baselines. Analysis highlights the importance of reward shaping, advantage scaling, and policy stability for the observed gains.

Conclusion: Confidence-based reward design in an offline RL framing offers a robust alternative to supervised fine-tuning for binary classification, enabling significant performance gains in noisy or low-supervision settings.

Abstract: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is the standard approach for binary classification tasks such as toxicity detection, factuality verification, and causal inference. However, SFT often performs poorly in real-world settings with label noise, class imbalance, or sparse supervision. We introduce BinaryPPO, an offline reinforcement learning large language model (LLM) framework that reformulates binary classification as a reward maximization problem. Our method leverages a variant of Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) with a confidence-weighted reward function that penalizes uncertain or incorrect predictions, enabling the model to learn robust decision policies from static datasets without online interaction. Across eight domain-specific benchmarks and multiple models with differing architectures, BinaryPPO improves accuracy by 40-60 percentage points, reaching up to 99%, substantially outperforming supervised baselines. We provide an in-depth analysis of the role of reward shaping, advantage scaling, and policy stability in enabling this improvement. Overall, we demonstrate that confidence-based reward design provides a robust alternative to SFT for binary classification. Our code is available at https://github.com/psyonp/BinaryPPO.

</details>


### [217] [Maximum Likelihood Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02710)
*Fahim Tajwar,Guanning Zeng,Yueer Zhou,Yuda Song,Daman Arora,Yiding Jiang,Jeff Schneider,Ruslan Salakhutdinov,Haiwen Feng,Andrea Zanette*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MaxRL introduces a compute-indexed family of sample-based objectives that interpolate between standard RL and exact maximum likelihood, providing an unbiased policy-gradient estimator and convergence to maximum likelihood with infinite compute; empirically, it outperforms GRPO-based methods with up to 20x test-time speedups and scales well with data and compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In sampling-based, binary-feedback tasks (e.g., navigation, code generation, math problem solving), RL does not maximize the likelihood of correct rollouts; there is a need to directly maximize likelihood under a non-differentiable sampling process to improve correctness and efficiency.

Method: Define a compute-indexed family of objectives that interpolate between RL and exact maximum likelihood by allocating additional sampling compute; derive a simple, unbiased policy-gradient estimator; show convergence to maximum likelihood as compute grows to infinity.

Result: Empirically, MaxRL Pareto-dominates existing methods across tested models and tasks, achieving up to 20x test-time efficiency gains compared to a GRPO-trained baseline; demonstrates better scaling with more data and compute.

Conclusion: MaxRL is a promising framework for scaling reinforcement learning in correctness-based settings, offering a principled path to maximum-likelihood optimization under non-differentiable sampling while improving efficiency and scalability.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning is the method of choice to train models in sampling-based setups with binary outcome feedback, such as navigation, code generation, and mathematical problem solving. In such settings, models implicitly induce a likelihood over correct rollouts. However, we observe that reinforcement learning does not maximize this likelihood, and instead optimizes only a lower-order approximation. Inspired by this observation, we introduce Maximum Likelihood Reinforcement Learning (MaxRL), a sampling-based framework to approximate maximum likelihood using reinforcement learning techniques. MaxRL addresses the challenges of non-differentiable sampling by defining a compute-indexed family of sample-based objectives that interpolate between standard reinforcement learning and exact maximum likelihood as additional sampling compute is allocated. The resulting objectives admit a simple, unbiased policy-gradient estimator and converge to maximum likelihood optimization in the infinite-compute limit. Empirically, we show that MaxRL Pareto-dominates existing methods in all models and tasks we tested, achieving up to 20x test-time scaling efficiency gains compared to its GRPO-trained counterpart. We also observe MaxRL to scale better with additional data and compute. Our results suggest MaxRL is a promising framework for scaling RL training in correctness based settings.

</details>


### [218] [Towards Understanding Steering Strength](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02712)
*Magamed Taimeskhanov,Samuel Vaiter,Damien Garreau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First theoretical analysis of steering strength for latent perturbations in LLMs, deriving qualitative laws for how perturbation magnitude affects next-token probability, concept presence, and cross-entropy; reveals non-monotonic effects and is validated across 11 models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in understanding how the magnitude of steering (not just the direction) influences model behavior and performance in post-training control of LLMs.

Method: Theoretical analysis deriving relationships between steering strength and key quantities (next-token probability, concept presence, cross-entropy); formulation of qualitative laws; comprehensive empirical validation across eleven language models from small GPT-style to modern architectures.

Result: Identification of non-monotonic effects of steering strength; clear, testable qualitative laws governing its impact on targeted behaviors and probabilities; broad empirical confirmation across diverse models.

Conclusion: Provides a principled framework for choosing steering strength in latent-space steering, enabling more robust and predictable post-training control across architectures; the laws generalize across model sizes and types.

Abstract: A popular approach to post-training control of large language models (LLMs) is the steering of intermediate latent representations. Namely, identify a well-chosen direction depending on the task at hand and perturbs representations along this direction at inference time. While many propositions exist to pick this direction, considerably less is understood about how to choose the magnitude of the move, whereas its importance is clear: too little and the intended behavior does not emerge, too much and the model's performance degrades beyond repair. In this work, we propose the first theoretical analysis of steering strength. We characterize its effect on next token probability, presence of a concept, and cross-entropy, deriving precise qualitative laws governing these quantities. Our analysis reveals surprising behaviors, including non-monotonic effects of steering strength. We validate our theoretical predictions empirically on eleven language models, ranging from a small GPT architecture to modern models.

</details>


### [219] [Neural Probabilistic Amplitude Shaping for Nonlinear Fiber Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02716)
*Mohammad Taha Askari,Lutz Lampe,Amirhossein Ghazisaeidi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NPAS (neural probabilistic amplitude shaping) enables joint-distribution learning for coherent fiber systems and delivers a 0.5 dB SNR gain over sequence selection for DP-64QAM on a 205 km single-span link.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance spectral efficiency and robustness of coherent optical communications by learning optimal joint distributions of amplitude and other signal characteristics rather than using fixed shaping schemes.

Method: Propose neural probabilistic amplitude shaping as a joint-distribution learning framework; evaluate on dual-polarized 64-QAM over a single-span 205 km fiber link, comparing against sequence selection.

Result: Achieves a 0.5 dB SNR gain over sequence selection for DP-64QAM on the tested link.

Conclusion: Neural probabilistic amplitude shaping is an effective joint-distribution learning approach that yields measurable SNR gains in practical coherent fiber links.

Abstract: We introduce neural probabilistic amplitude shaping, a joint-distribution learning framework for coherent fiber systems. The proposed scheme provides a 0.5 dB signal-to-noise ratio gain over sequence selection for dual-polarized 64-QAM transmission across a single-span 205 km link.

</details>


### [220] [Automated Dysphagia Screening Using Noninvasive Neck Acoustic Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02725)
*Jade Chng,Rong Xing,Yunfei Luo,Kristen Linnemeyer-Risser,Tauhidur Rahman,Andrew Yousef,Philip A Weissbrod*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Portable, noninvasive acoustic sensing with ML detects dysphagia; AUC-ROC 0.904 across five train-test splits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address need for noninvasive, radiation-free dysphagia screening and scalable pharyngeal health monitoring.

Method: Collect neck-acoustic signals during swallowing; preprocess; apply applied ML; evaluate on 5 independent train-test splits; report abnormality detection performance.

Result: AUC-ROC 0.904; notable early-stage feasibility and potential utility.

Conclusion: Noninvasive acoustic sensing is feasible and scalable for pharyngeal health monitoring; warrants further validation and deployment studies.

Abstract: Pharyngeal health plays a vital role in essential human functions such as breathing, swallowing, and vocalization. Early detection of swallowing abnormalities, also known as dysphagia, is crucial for timely intervention. However, current diagnostic methods often rely on radiographic imaging or invasive procedures. In this study, we propose an automated framework for detecting dysphagia using portable and noninvasive acoustic sensing coupled with applied machine learning. By capturing subtle acoustic signals from the neck during swallowing tasks, we aim to identify patterns associated with abnormal physiological conditions. Our approach achieves promising test-time abnormality detection performance, with an AUC-ROC of 0.904 under 5 independent train-test splits. This work demonstrates the feasibility of using noninvasive acoustic sensing as a practical and scalable tool for pharyngeal health monitoring.

</details>


### [221] [Vector Quantized Latent Concepts: A Scalable Alternative to Clustering-Based Concept Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02726)
*Xuemin Yu,Ankur Garg,Samira Ebrahimi Kahou,Hassan Sajjad*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes VQLC, a discrete latent concept framework using VQ-VAE to map token representations to a discrete codebook, enabling scalable, human-interpretable concept explanations with comparable quality to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing post-hoc concept-based clustering (e.g., hierarchical clustering, K-Means) is either computationally infeasible on large-scale datasets or yields shallow/frequency-dominated clusters. There is a need for scalable, interpretable explanations of deep models.

Method: Introduce vector quantized latent concept (VQLC) built on the VQ-VAE architecture. The method learns a discrete codebook that maps continuous token representations to concept vectors, enabling efficient, discrete concept discovery for explanations.

Result: Empirical evaluations show that VQLC scales better than traditional clustering approaches while preserving a quality of human-understandable explanations that is comparable to baselines.

Conclusion: VQLC provides a scalable, interpretable framework for post-hoc concept-based explanations in large models by leveraging discrete latent concepts learned via a VQ-VAE, achieving favorable scalability-explanation quality trade-offs.

Abstract: Deep Learning models encode rich semantic information in their hidden representations. However, it remains challenging to understand which parts of this information models actually rely on when making predictions. A promising line of post-hoc concept-based explanation methods relies on clustering token representations. However, commonly used approaches such as hierarchical clustering are computationally infeasible for large-scale datasets, and K-Means often yields shallow or frequency-dominated clusters. We propose the vector quantized latent concept (VQLC) method, a framework built upon the vector quantized-variational autoencoder (VQ-VAE) architecture that learns a discrete codebook mapping continuous representations to concept vectors. We perform thorough evaluations and show that VQLC improves scalability while maintaining comparable quality of human-understandable explanations.

</details>


### [222] [RLAnything: Forge Environment, Policy, and Reward Model in Completely Dynamic RL System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02488)
*Yinjie Wang,Tianbao Xie,Ke Shen,Mengdi Wang,Ling Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLAnything is a dynamic co-adaptation framework for reinforcement learning that jointly optimizes environment, policy, and reward models via closed-loop signals and critic feedback, yielding consistent improvements on LLM/agentic benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To amplify learning signals and stabilize RL in LLM/agentic settings by integrating environment adaptation, reward modeling, and policy optimization in a unified loop.

Method: Train policy using step-wise and outcome signals; jointly train reward model using consistency feedback; automatic environment adaptation using critic signals from reward and policy models; closed-loop optimization with cross-feedback between components; empirical evaluation on OSWorld, AlfWorld, LiveBench; code release.

Result: Significant performance gains on multiple benchmarks (e.g., +9.1% on OSWorld; +18.7% and +11.9% on AlfWorld and LiveBench); reward-model optimization can outperform human labels; consistent gains across added components.

Conclusion: Component-wise integration in RLAnything yields robust improvements across LLM/agentic tasks, suggesting a versatile approach for enhancing RL systems; potential for broader applicability and further improvements via ablations and theoretical grounding.

Abstract: We propose RLAnything, a reinforcement learning framework that dynamically forges environment, policy, and reward models through closed-loop optimization, amplifying learning signals and strengthening the overall RL system for any LLM or agentic scenarios. Specifically, the policy is trained with integrated feedback from step-wise and outcome signals, while the reward model is jointly optimized via consistency feedback, which in turn further improves policy training. Moreover, our theory-motivated automatic environment adaptation improves training for both the reward and policy models by leveraging critic feedback from each, enabling learning from experience. Empirically, each added component consistently improves the overall system, and RLAnything yields substantial gains across various representative LLM and agentic tasks, boosting Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking by 9.1% on OSWorld and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct by 18.7% and 11.9% on AlfWorld and LiveBench, respectively. We also that optimized reward-model signals outperform outcomes that rely on human labels. Code: https://github.com/Gen-Verse/Open-AgentRL

</details>


### [223] [Search-Augmented Masked Diffusion Models for Constrained Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02727)
*Huu Binh Ta,Michael Cardei,Alvaro Velasquez,Ferdinando Fioretto*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SearchDiff adds a training-free, search-augmented step to discrete diffusion for sequences, enforcing hard constraints and non-differentiable properties during inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard discrete diffusion training optimizes a likelihood-based objective mainly matching the data distribution and provides no native mechanism to enforce hard constraints or optimize non-differentiable properties during inference.

Method: At each denoising step, model predictions define a proposal set that is optimized under a user-specified property, yielding a modified reverse transition that steers sampling toward feasible solutions; this integrates informed search into the reverse denoising process.

Result: Empirical results in biological design and symbolic reasoning show improved constraint satisfaction and property adherence, consistently outperforming discrete diffusion and autoregressive baselines.

Conclusion: SearchDiff is a training-free neurosymbolic inference framework that enhances constrained sequence generation by augmenting the reverse diffusion with an informed search, improving feasibility and property alignment.

Abstract: Discrete diffusion models generate sequences by iteratively denoising samples corrupted by categorical noise, offering an appealing alternative to autoregressive decoding for structured and symbolic generation. However, standard training targets a likelihood-based objective that primarily matches the data distribution and provides no native mechanism for enforcing hard constraints or optimizing non-differentiable properties at inference time. This work addresses this limitation and introduces Search-Augmented Masked Diffusion (SearchDiff), a training-free neurosymbolic inference framework that integrates informed search directly into the reverse denoising process. At each denoising step, the model predictions define a proposal set that is optimized under a user-specified property satisfaction, yielding a modified reverse transition that steers sampling toward probable and feasible solutions. Experiments in biological design and symbolic reasoning illustrate that SearchDiff substantially improves constraint satisfaction and property adherence, while consistently outperforming discrete diffusion and autoregressive baselines.

</details>


### [224] [CAPS: Unifying Attention, Recurrence, and Alignment in Transformer-based Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02729)
*Viresh Pati,Yubin Kim,Vinh Pham,Jevon Twitty,Shihao Yang,Jiecheng Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CAPS is a clock-weighted, structurally gated attention for time series that decouples global trends, local shocks, and seasonal patterns, combining SO(2) phase alignment with three gating paths (Riemann softmax, prefix-product gates, Clock baseline) in a single linear-complexity attention layer to improve forecasting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard softmax attention entangles temporal structures in time series and recurrent models struggle with long-range dependencies; there is a need for an attention mechanism that decouples global trends, local shocks, and seasonality while maintaining efficiency.

Method: Introduce CAPS: a time-series attention layer that decouples three temporal structures via SO(2) rotations for phase alignment and three additive gating paths (Riemann softmax, prefix-product gates, Clock baseline) modulated by a learned Clock mechanism, all within a single attention layer with linear complexity.

Result: Empirical results show CAPS surpasses vanilla softmax and linear attention on long- and short-term forecasting benchmarks and is competitive with seven strong baselines, with code released at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: CAPS provides a structured, clock-weighted attention mechanism that disentangles temporal patterns for improved time-series forecasting while retaining linear-time complexity, offering competitive performance and a reusable code implementation.

Abstract: This paper presents $\textbf{CAPS}$ (Clock-weighted Aggregation with Prefix-products and Softmax), a structured attention mechanism for time series forecasting that decouples three distinct temporal structures: global trends, local shocks, and seasonal patterns. Standard softmax attention entangles these through global normalization, while recent recurrent models sacrifice long-term, order-independent selection for order-dependent causal structure. CAPS combines SO(2) rotations for phase alignment with three additive gating paths -- Riemann softmax, prefix-product gates, and a Clock baseline -- within a single attention layer. We introduce the Clock mechanism, a learned temporal weighting that modulates these paths through a shared notion of temporal importance. Experiments on long- and short-term forecasting benchmarks surpass vanilla softmax and linear attention mechanisms and demonstrate competitive performance against seven strong baselines with linear complexity. Our code implementation is available at https://github.com/vireshpati/CAPS-Attention.

</details>


### [225] [TabPFN for Zero-shot Parametric Engineering Design Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02735)
*Ke Wang,Yifan Tang,Nguyen Gia Hien Vu,Faez Ahmed,G. Gary Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Zero-shot, data-efficient conditional design generation for parametric engineering using TabPFN; no task-specific training, sequential parameter generation based on target performance; evaluated on ship hull, BlendedNet aircraft, and UIUC airfoil with competitive diversity and low error, compared to diffusion models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the computational cost and data requirements of deep generative models in engineering design, enabling rapid deployment and easy integration into real-world workflows without retraining.

Method: Utilizes TabPFN to perform zero-shot, conditional generation of design parameters. The model is conditioned on target performance indicators and generates parameters sequentially. No task-specific training or fine-tuning is performed; uses a limited number of reference samples.

Result: Demonstrates competitive diversity in highly structured parametric design spaces, robustness to sampling variations, and low performance error (e.g., <2% for ship hull performance). Offers significant reductions in computational overhead and data needs relative to diffusion-based generative models.

Conclusion: Zero-shot, data-efficient parametric design generation via TabPFN shows practical promise for engineering design, enabling rapid deployment and adaptation to new design settings with easier integration into existing workflows.

Abstract: Deep generative models for engineering design often require substantial computational cost, large training datasets, and extensive retraining when design requirements or datasets change, limiting their applicability in real-world engineering design workflow. In this work, we propose a zero-shot generation framework for parametric engineering design based on TabPFN, enabling conditional design generation using only a limited number of reference samples and without any task-specific model training or fine-tuning. The proposed method generates design parameters sequentially conditioned on target performance indicators, providing a flexible alternative to conventional generative models. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is evaluated on three engineering design datasets, i.e., ship hull design, BlendedNet aircraft, and UIUC airfoil. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves competitive diversity across highly structured parametric design spaces, remains robust to variations in sampling, resolution and parameter dimensionality of geometry generation, and achieves a low performance error (e.g., less than 2% in generated ship hull designs' performance). Compared with diffusion-based generative models, the proposed framework significantly reduces computational overhead and data requirements while preserving reliable generation performance. These results highlight the potential of zero-shot, data-efficient generation as a practical and efficient tool for engineering design, enabling rapid deployment, flexible adaptation to new design settings, and ease of integration into real-world engineering workflows.

</details>


### [226] [TopoPrune: Robust Data Pruning via Unified Latent Space Topology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02739)
*Arjun Roy,Prajna G. Malettira,Manish Nagaraj,Kaushik Roy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TopoPrune is a topology-driven data-pruning framework that achieves robust, high-rate pruning via a two-scale approach: (i) topology-aware manifold approximation for a global low-dimensional embedding, and (ii) differentiable persistent homology for local topological optimization to rank samples by structural complexity, yielding strong accuracy and transferability (e.g., at 90% pruning).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current geometric pruning methods rely on extrinsic geometry and are unstable under latent-space perturbations, leading to degraded cross-architecture transfer and sensitivity to feature noise; an intrinsic, stable data representation is needed.

Method: TopoPrune consists of a two-scale pipeline: (1) a topology-aware manifold approximation to obtain a global low-dimensional embedding of the dataset, and (2) differentiable persistent homology to perform a local topological optimization on the embeddings, ranking samples by their structural complexity.

Result: The approach yields high accuracy and precision at high pruning rates (e.g., 90%), and due to the stability properties of topology, it is robust to latent feature noise and shows superior transferability across diverse network architectures.

Conclusion: A principled, topology-based framework for robust data-efficient learning, TopoPrune demonstrates stable pruning by leveraging intrinsic data structure, promising for cross-architecture robustness and data efficiency.

Abstract: Geometric data pruning methods, while practical for leveraging pretrained models, are fundamentally unstable. Their reliance on extrinsic geometry renders them highly sensitive to latent space perturbations, causing performance to degrade during cross-architecture transfer or in the presence of feature noise. We introduce TopoPrune, a framework which resolves this challenge by leveraging topology to capture the stable, intrinsic structure of data. TopoPrune operates at two scales, (1) utilizing a topology-aware manifold approximation to establish a global low-dimensional embedding of the dataset. Subsequently, (2) it employs differentiable persistent homology to perform a local topological optimization on the manifold embeddings, ranking samples by their structural complexity. We demonstrate that our unified dual-scale topological approach ensures high accuracy and precision, particularly at significant dataset pruning rates (e.g., 90%). Furthermore, through the inherent stability properties of topology, TopoPrune is (a) exceptionally robust to noise perturbations of latent feature embeddings and (b) demonstrates superior transferability across diverse network architectures. This study demonstrates a promising avenue towards stable and principled topology-based frameworks for robust data-efficient learning.

</details>


### [227] [Entropy-Guided Dynamic Tokens for Graph-LLM Alignment in Molecular Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02742)
*Zihao Jing,Qiuhao Zeng,Ruiyi Fang,Yan Sun,Boyu Wang,Pingzhao Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces EDT-Former, a dynamic-token transformer that aligns frozen graph encoders with LLMs via entropy-guided token selection, enabling efficient fine-tuning and state-of-the-art performance on molecular benchmarks without tuning the LLM backbone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Molecular graphs encode rich local and global structure (e.g., stereochemistry, substructures) but existing graph-LLM bridges use fixed-length tokens and require costly LLM fine-tuning, limiting efficiency and generalization.

Method: Entropy-guided dynamic token generation selects informative molecular patches and aligns them to LLM tokens, preserving local/global structure. The bridge preserves a frozen graph encoder (except embedding layer) and uses dynamic tokens to connect to LLMs, avoiding backpropagation through the LLM backbone.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on MoleculeQA, Molecule-oriented Mol-Instructions, and property-prediction benchmarks (TDC, MoleculeNet) with efficient finetuning and without tuning the LLM backbone.

Conclusion: EDT-Former offers scalable, generalizable multimodal molecular understanding by effectively bridging graph encoders and frozen LLMs through entropy-guided dynamic tokens, outperforming prior fixed-token approaches.

Abstract: Molecular understanding is central to advancing areas such as scientific discovery, yet Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle to understand molecular graphs effectively. Existing graph-LLM bridges often adapt the Q-Former-style connector with fixed-length static tokens, which is originally designed for vision tasks. These designs overlook stereochemistry and substructural context and typically require costly LLM-backbone fine-tuning, limiting efficiency and generalization. We introduce EDT-Former, an Entropy-guided Dynamic Token Transformer that generates tokens aligned with informative molecular patches, thereby preserving both local and global structural features for molecular graph understanding. Beyond prior approaches, EDT-Former enables alignment between frozen graph encoders and LLMs without tuning the LLM backbone (excluding the embedding layer), resulting in computationally efficient finetuning, and achieves stateof-the-art results on MoleculeQA, Molecule-oriented Mol-Instructions, and property prediction benchmarks (TDC, MoleculeNet), underscoring its effectiveness for scalable and generalizable multimodal molecular understanding

</details>


### [228] [On the Sample Efficiency of Inverse Dynamics Models for Semi-Supervised Imitation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02762)
*Sacha Morin,Moonsub Byeon,Alexia Jolicoeur-Martineau,Sébastien Lachapelle*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VM-IDM and IDM labeling converge to an IDM-based policy in a limit; IDM-based policies offer superior sample efficiency due to lower hypothesis class complexity and reduced stochasticity; experimental validation with UVA; improved LAPO algorithm for latent action policy learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand why IDM-based SSIL methods outperform pure behavior cloning, unify VM-IDM and IDM labeling under a single IDM-based policy, and propose improvements for latent action policy learning.

Method: Theoretically show equivalence of VM-IDM and IDM labeling in a limit; provide statistical learning theory arguments for efficiency; conduct experiments with unified video-action prediction architectures (UVA); develop an improved LAPO variant for latent action policy learning.

Result: Demonstrates equivalence of VM-IDM and IDM labeling to the IDM-based policy in the limit; identifies two sources of sample efficiency gains (lower complexity, lower stochasticity); supports claims with UVA experiments; introduces an improved LAPO algorithm.

Conclusion: IDM-based strategies organize SSIL more efficiently than naive behavior cloning; theoretical and empirical support for focusing on IDM learning; improved LAPO provides practical gains for latent action policy learning.

Abstract: Semi-supervised imitation learning (SSIL) consists in learning a policy from a small dataset of action-labeled trajectories and a much larger dataset of action-free trajectories. Some SSIL methods learn an inverse dynamics model (IDM) to predict the action from the current state and the next state. An IDM can act as a policy when paired with a video model (VM-IDM) or as a label generator to perform behavior cloning on action-free data (IDM labeling). In this work, we first show that VM-IDM and IDM labeling learn the same policy in a limit case, which we call the IDM-based policy. We then argue that the previously observed advantage of IDM-based policies over behavior cloning is due to the superior sample efficiency of IDM learning, which we attribute to two causes: (i) the ground-truth IDM tends to be contained in a lower complexity hypothesis class relative to the expert policy, and (ii) the ground-truth IDM is often less stochastic than the expert policy. We argue these claims based on insights from statistical learning theory and novel experiments, including a study of IDM-based policies using recent architectures for unified video-action prediction (UVA). Motivated by these insights, we finally propose an improved version of the existing LAPO algorithm for latent action policy learning.

</details>


### [229] [Exposing Vulnerabilities in Explanation for Time Series Classifiers via Dual-Target Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02763)
*Bohan Wang,Zewen Liu,Lu Lin,Hui Liu,Li Xiong,Ming Jin,Wei Jin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Demonstrates that explanation stability is not a reliable proxy for robustness in time-series models by introducing TSEF, a dual-target attack that fools both the classifier and the explainer to cause targeted misclassification while keeping explanations aligned with a reference rationale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Challenging the common assumption that temporal explanation consistency indicates robustness; expose vulnerabilities in time-series explainability and urge coupling-aware robustness evaluation for trustworthy systems.

Method: Introduce TSEF (Time Series Explanation Fooler), a dual-target attack that jointly perturbs classifier outputs and explainer outputs to realize targeted prediction changes while ensuring explanations remain plausible with respect to a chosen reference rationale.

Result: Across multiple datasets and explainer backbones, explanation stability is shown to be a misleading proxy for decision robustness; targeted misclassifications are achievable without obvious explanations deviating from a reference, highlighting decoupling between predictions and explanations.

Conclusion: Explain stability alone should not certify robustness in time-series models; robustness evaluation must couple classifier behavior and explanation cues to ensure trustworthy systems.

Abstract: Interpretable time series deep learning systems are often assessed by checking temporal consistency on explanations, implicitly treating this as evidence of robustness. We show that this assumption can fail: Predictions and explanations can be adversarially decoupled, enabling targeted misclassification while the explanation remains plausible and consistent with a chosen reference rationale. We propose TSEF (Time Series Explanation Fooler), a dual-target attack that jointly manipulates the classifier and explainer outputs. In contrast to single-objective misclassification attacks that disrupt explanation and spread attribution mass broadly, TSEF achieves targeted prediction changes while keeping explanations consistent with the reference. Across multiple datasets and explainer backbones, our results consistently reveal that explanation stability is a misleading proxy for decision robustness and motivate coupling-aware robustness evaluations for trustworthy time series tasks.

</details>


### [230] [Privately Fine-Tuned LLMs Preserve Temporal Dynamics in Tabular Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02766)
*Lucas Rosenblatt,Peihan Liu,Ryan McKenna,Natalia Ponomareva*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PATH: a DP synthetic framework that treats full longitudinal tables as the synthesis unit, leveraging privately fine-tuned autoregressive LLMs to preserve temporal coherence. Outperforms leading marginal mechanisms on long-range dependencies and state transitions while maintaining marginal fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitation of DP synthetic tabular data that assumes i.i.d. rows; longitudinal datasets (e.g., EHR) have temporal structure and sub-tables per user. Flattening histories to high-dimensional vectors fails to preserve temporal coherence even if marginals are accurate, necessitating a unit-of-synthesis approach for sequences.

Method: Introduce PATH, a generative framework that treats the entire user table as the unit of synthesis and uses autoregressive modeling via privately fine-tuned large language models to capture long-range temporal dependencies and state transitions in sequential event data.

Result: Empirical evaluations show PATH reduces distributional distance to real trajectories by over 60% and decreases state transition errors by nearly 50% compared with leading marginal mechanisms, while achieving comparable marginal fidelity.

Conclusion: Synthesis of full longitudinal tables with privately fine-tuned autoregressive LLMs (PATH) effectively captures long-range temporal dependencies that marginal methods miss, offering improved fidelity of synthetic longitudinal data without sacrificing marginal accuracy.

Abstract: Research on differentially private synthetic tabular data has largely focused on independent and identically distributed rows where each record corresponds to a unique individual. This perspective neglects the temporal complexity in longitudinal datasets, such as electronic health records, where a user contributes an entire (sub) table of sequential events. While practitioners might attempt to model such data by flattening user histories into high-dimensional vectors for use with standard marginal-based mechanisms, we demonstrate that this strategy is insufficient. Flattening fails to preserve temporal coherence even when it maintains valid marginal distributions. We introduce PATH, a novel generative framework that treats the full table as the unit of synthesis and leverages the autoregressive capabilities of privately fine-tuned large language models. Extensive evaluations show that PATH effectively captures long-range dependencies that traditional methods miss. Empirically, our method reduces the distributional distance to real trajectories by over 60% and reduces state transition errors by nearly 50% compared to leading marginal mechanisms while achieving similar marginal fidelity.

</details>


### [231] [Provable Effects of Data Replay in Continual Learning: A Feature Learning Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02767)
*Meng Ding,Jinhui Xu,Kaiyi Ji*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Full data replay can mitigate but not fully prevent forgetting in continual learning; the outcome depends on the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and the order of tasks. High-SNR tasks can aid learning of low-SNR tasks and reduce forgetting when replay is sufficient and well-ordered.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the theoretical limits of full data-replay strategies in continual learning, beyond memory-constrained settings, and to identify the key factors (SNR, task correlations, and ordering) that govern forgetting and recovery.

Method: Introduce a multi-view data model and analyze continual learning under full data replay for task-incremental binary classification across M tasks. Treat signal as information carried by earlier tasks and noise as interference from later tasks. Derive conditions under which forgetting occurs despite full replay and when retrieval of earlier tasks is possible. Propose that prioritizing higher-SNR tasks can benefit both high- and low-SNR tasks. Validate findings with synthetic and real-world experiments visualizing signal learning vs. noise memorization across varying SNRs and task correlations.

Result: The analysis shows that forgetting can persist under full replay when cumulative noise from later tasks eclipses the signal from earlier ones. Conversely, with sufficient accumulation of signal, replay can recover earlier tasks even if their initial learning was poor. A key insight is that orchestrating task order by signal strength (high-SNR tasks first) can not only improve their own learning but also facilitate learning of lower-SNR tasks and reduce forgetting. Experiments corroborate these dynamics across different SNR regimes and task correlations.

Conclusion: Full data replay offers important benefits but is not a universal remedy for forgetting. The interplay between signal accumulation and cumulative noise dictates outcomes, and task ordering by signal strength emerges as a practical strategy to enhance retention and transfer in continual learning. These results inform the design and theoretical study of replay-based CL systems and highlight directions for future work.

Abstract: Continual learning (CL) aims to train models on a sequence of tasks while retaining performance on previously learned ones. A core challenge in this setting is catastrophic forgetting, where new learning interferes with past knowledge. Among various mitigation strategies, data-replay methods, where past samples are periodically revisited, are considered simple yet effective, especially when memory constraints are relaxed. However, the theoretical effectiveness of full data replay, where all past data is accessible during training, remains largely unexplored. In this paper, we present a comprehensive theoretical framework for analyzing full data-replay training in continual learning from a feature learning perspective. Adopting a multi-view data model, we identify the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) as a critical factor affecting forgetting. Focusing on task-incremental binary classification across $M$ tasks, our analysis verifies two key conclusions: (1) forgetting can still occur under full replay when the cumulative noise from later tasks dominates the signal from earlier ones; and (2) with sufficient signal accumulation, data replay can recover earlier tasks-even if their initial learning was poor. Notably, we uncover a novel insight into task ordering: prioritizing higher-signal tasks not only facilitates learning of lower-signal tasks but also helps prevent catastrophic forgetting. We validate our theoretical findings through synthetic and real-world experiments that visualize the interplay between signal learning and noise memorization across varying SNRs and task correlation regimes.

</details>


### [232] [VerIde ECG Biometrics: Verification and Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02776)
*Scagnetto Arjuna*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ECG biometrics exhibit strong individual signatures at scale. Simple tabular fiducial features leak identity, and embedding-based models (ArcFace) improve re-identification further when moving from features to waveforms, driven by larger training data and consistent normalization. High verification accuracy at strict false-alarm rates and strong open-set DIR indicate meaningful privacy risks, suggesting anonymization by feature release is insufficient and robust privacy protocols are needed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess the scalability of ECG-based identification and the sufficiency of feature-level anonymization. Determine how well ECGs can be linked to individuals in open and closed-set settings, and quantify privacy risks for large-scale deployments.

Method: Compare two fronts: (1) tabular fiducial ECG features with a simple MLP embedding; (2) embedding-based models (ArcFace) applied to features and to raw ECG waveforms. Evaluations on a large-scale test set include verification metrics (TAR@FAR, EER) in all-vs-all, closed-set Rank@1/Rank@10, and open-set DIR with a two-stage shortlist + re-ranking pipeline. Consistent normalization across train/val/test is employed to maximize generalization.

Result: Tabular features already enable non-trivial identification with an MLP. Moving to embeddings yields substantial gains: waveform-based ArcFace embeddings outperform feature-based ones, with larger training sets and consistent normalization further boosting performance. On a large-scale test set (all-vs-all): TAR=0.908 at FAR=1e-3; TAR=0.820 at FAR=1e-4; EER=2.53%. Closed-set: Rank@1=0.812; Rank@10=0.910. Open-set: two-stage pipeline achieves DIR up to 0.976 at FAR=1e-3 and 1e-4.

Conclusion: ECG contains a measurable identifying signature. Privacy risk is real even with feature-level anonymization, and embedding-based re-identification further amplifies risk. The results underscore the need for privacy-preserving protocols and careful consideration of what to release in ECG datasets, especially for large-scale deployments.

Abstract: This work studies electrocardiogram (ECG) biometrics at large scale, evaluating how strongly an ECG can be linked to an individual and, consequently, how its anonymization may be compromised. We show that identity information is already present in tabular representations (fiducial features): even a simple MLP-based embedding network yields non-trivial performance, indicating that anonymization based solely on releasing features does not guarantee privacy. We then adopt embedding-based deep learning models (ArcFace), first on features and then on ECG waveforms, showing a performance jump when moving from tabular inputs to waveforms, and a further gain with larger training sets and consistent normalization across train/val/test. On a large-scale test set, verification achieves high TAR at strict FAR thresholds (TAR=0.908 @ FAR=1e-3; TAR=0.820 @ FAR=1e-4) with EER=2.53% (all-vs-all); closed-set identification yields Rank@1=0.812 and Rank@10=0.910. In open-set, a two-stage pipeline (top-K shortlist on embeddings + re-ranking) reaches DIR@FAR up to 0.976 at FAR=1e-3 and 1e-4. Overall, the results show that ECG carries a measurable individual signature: re-identification is already possible with tabular features and is further amplified by embedding-based models, making privacy implications and realistic operational protocols essential to consider.

</details>


### [233] [Cross-Temporal Attention Fusion (CTAF) for Multimodal Physiological Signals in Self-Supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02784)
*Arian Khorasani,Théophile Demazure*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A time-aware, self-supervised cross-temporal attention fusion (CTAF) for asynchronous EEG and peripheral data, achieving stronger alignment and competitive classification with few labels on K-EmoCon.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Asynchronous multimodal signals and CNS–ANS coupling in psychophysiological data complicate standard fusion; there is a need for alignment-aware, label-efficient fusion that preserves temporal correspondence.

Method: CTAF: a self-supervised module that learns soft bidirectional alignments via time-aware cross attention, a lightweight fusion gate, and alignment-regularized contrastive objectives with optional weak supervision.

Result: On K-EmoCon with leave-one-out CV, CTAF yields higher cosine margins for matched cross-modal pairs and better cross-modal token retrieval within 1 second, while remaining competitive with baselines on 3-bin accuracy and macro-F1 using few labels.

Conclusion: Introduces a time-aware fusion mechanism that directly models cross-modal correspondence, an alignment-driven self-supervised objective for EEG/physiology, and an evaluation protocol that quantifies alignment quality; results support CTAF as a strong, label-efficient approach for EEG–peripheral fusion under temporal asynchrony.

Abstract: We study multimodal affect modeling when EEG and peripheral physiology are asynchronous, which most fusion methods ignore or handle with costly warping. We propose Cross-Temporal Attention Fusion (CTAF), a self-supervised module that learns soft bidirectional alignments between modalities and builds a robust clip embedding using time-aware cross attention, a lightweight fusion gate, and alignment-regularized contrastive objectives with optional weak supervision. On the K-EmoCon dataset, under leave-one-out cross-validation evaluation, CTAF yields higher cosine margins for matched pairs and better cross-modal token retrieval within one second, and it is competitive with the baseline on three-bin accuracy and macro-F1 while using few labels. Our contributions are a time-aware fusion mechanism that directly models correspondence, an alignment-driven self-supervised objective tailored to EEG and physiology, and an evaluation protocol that measures alignment quality itself. Our approach accounts for the coupling between the central and autonomic nervous systems in psychophysiological time series. These results indicate that CTAF is a strong step toward label-efficient, generalizable EEG-peripheral fusion under temporal asynchrony.

</details>


### [234] [LEMON: Local Explanations via Modality-aware OptimizatioN](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02786)
*Yu Qin,Phillip Sloan,Raul Santos-Rodriguez,Majid Mirmehdi,Telmo de Menezes e Silva Filho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LEMON is a model-agnostic, local explainer for multimodal predictions that uses a modality-aware surrogate with group-structured sparsity to produce unified, modality- and feature-level explanations with low computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal explainability methods are often confined to a single modality, architecture-specific, or computationally expensive. A model-agnostic, efficient approach is needed to provide faithful, local explanations across modalities.

Method: LEMON fits a single modality-aware surrogate with group-structured sparsity, treating the predictor as a black box. It yields unified explanations that disentangle modality-level contributions and feature-level attributions, requiring relatively few forward passes and maintaining faithfulness under perturbations.

Result: Empirical evaluation on vision-language question answering and a clinical task with image, text, and tabular data shows competitive deletion-based faithfulness. LEMON reduces black-box evaluations by 35–67x and runtime by 2–8x compared to strong multimodal baselines, and performs well across different backbone architectures.

Conclusion: LEMON provides a faithful, efficient, model-agnostic framework for local multimodal explanations, enabling scalable interpretation by delivering modality- and feature-level attributions with substantially reduced computation.

Abstract: Multimodal models are ubiquitous, yet existing explainability methods are often single-modal, architecture-dependent, or too computationally expensive to run at scale. We introduce LEMON (Local Explanations via Modality-aware OptimizatioN), a model-agnostic framework for local explanations of multimodal predictions. LEMON fits a single modality-aware surrogate with group-structured sparsity to produce unified explanations that disentangle modality-level contributions and feature-level attributions. The approach treats the predictor as a black box and is computationally efficient, requiring relatively few forward passes while remaining faithful under repeated perturbations. We evaluate LEMON on vision-language question answering and a clinical prediction task with image, text, and tabular inputs, comparing against representative multimodal baselines. Across backbones, LEMON achieves competitive deletion-based faithfulness while reducing black-box evaluations by 35-67 times and runtime by 2-8 times compared to strong multimodal baselines.

</details>


### [235] [Structure-Preserving Learning Improves Geometry Generalization in Neural PDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02788)
*Benjamin D. Shaffer,Shawn Koohy,Brooks Kinch,M. Ani Hsieh,Nathaniel Trask*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Geo-NeW is a data-driven finite element method that jointly learns a differential operator and compatible reduced FE spaces on a geometry-discretized mesh. It preserves physical conservation laws via Finite Element Exterior Calculus, encodes geometry with a transformer, and delivers state-of-the-art steady-state PDE performance with strong generalization to unseen geometries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for real-time, structure-preserving PDE solvers that generalize across complex, unseen geometries. Traditional neural PDE solvers often lack guaranteed conservation or compatibility with FE structure. Integrating geometry-aware FE spaces with neural operators provides strong inductive biases and theoretical guarantees (existence/uniqueness) while enabling real-time predictions.

Method: Jointly learn a differential operator and compatible reduced finite element spaces on a discretized geometry. Use Finite Element Exterior Calculus to enforce conservation laws, and a transformer-based encoding of the mesh to connect geometry to learned spaces. Parameterize the constitutive model to ensure existence/uniqueness of solutions. The framework leverages Whitney forms as the basis and outputs predictions by solving the learned system, aiming for physics-consistent, geometry-aware neural PDEs.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple steady-state PDE benchmarks and shows significant improvement over conventional baselines on out-of-distribution geometries, with explicit conservation and structure preservation.

Conclusion: Geo-NeW advances neural PDE solvers by fusing geometry-informed FE discretizations with neural operators, delivering real-time, structure-preserving solutions that generalize to unseen domains. The approach offers a principled framework that can be extended to broader PDE classes and time-dependent problems.

Abstract: We aim to develop physics foundation models for science and engineering that provide real-time solutions to Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) which preserve structure and accuracy under adaptation to unseen geometries. To this end, we introduce General-Geometry Neural Whitney Forms (Geo-NeW): a data-driven finite element method. We jointly learn a differential operator and compatible reduced finite element spaces defined on the underlying geometry. The resulting model is solved to generate predictions, while exactly preserving physical conservation laws through Finite Element Exterior Calculus. Geometry enters the model as a discretized mesh both through a transformer-based encoding and as the basis for the learned finite element spaces. This explicitly connects the underlying geometry and imposed boundary conditions to the solution, providing a powerful inductive bias for learning neural PDEs, which we demonstrate improves generalization to unseen domains. We provide a novel parameterization of the constitutive model ensuring the existence and uniqueness of the solution. Our approach demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on several steady-state PDE benchmarks, and provides a significant improvement over conventional baselines on out-of-distribution geometries.

</details>


### [236] [Causality--Δ: Jacobian-Based Dependency Analysis in Flow Matching Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02793)
*Reza Rezvan,Gustav Gille,Moritz Schauer,Richard Torkar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: JVPs reveal latent dependency structure in flow-based generation; closed-form optimal drift/Jacobian for Gaussian and Gaussian mixtures; flows show local affine structure despite global nonlinearities; JVPs recover Jacobians in low dimensions; attribute-level JVP estimator in images aligns with empirical correlations on MNIST/CelebA; conditioning on small classifier-Jacobian norms reduces correlations (not a formal do-intervention).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how small latent perturbations propagate through flow-based transports to reveal the dependency structure of generated features.

Method: Derive closed-form expressions for the optimal drift and its Jacobian in Gaussian and mixture-of-Gaussian settings; validate by numerical JVPs in low-dimensional synthetic data; compose the flow with an attribute classifier to obtain an attribute-level JVP estimator on image data; analyze how conditioning on classifier-Jacobian norms affects observed correlations.

Result: Analytical JVPs match the true Jacobians in low-dimensional tests; globally nonlinear flows exhibit local affine structure; the attribute-level JVP estimator recovers empirical correlations on MNIST and CelebA; conditioning reduces correlations consistent with a common-cause interpretation, though not a formal intervention.

Conclusion: JVPs provide a practical lens to inspect and potentially control dependency structures in flow models, highlighting a retained local affine regime and offering a scalable proxy for correlation analysis without requiring causal interventions.

Abstract: Flow matching learns a velocity field that transports a base distribution to data. We study how small latent perturbations propagate through these flows and show that Jacobian-vector products (JVPs) provide a practical lens on dependency structure in the generated features. We derive closed-form expressions for the optimal drift and its Jacobian in Gaussian and mixture-of-Gaussian settings, revealing that even globally nonlinear flows admit local affine structure. In low-dimensional synthetic benchmarks, numerical JVPs recover the analytical Jacobians. In image domains, composing the flow with an attribute classifier yields an attribute-level JVP estimator that recovers empirical correlations on MNIST and CelebA. Conditioning on small classifier-Jacobian norms reduces correlations in a way consistent with a hypothesized common-cause structure, while we emphasize that this conditioning is not a formal do intervention.

</details>


### [237] [Joint Learning of Hierarchical Neural Options and Abstract World Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02799)
*Wasu Top Piriyakulkij,Wolfgang Lehrach,Kevin Ellis,Kevin Murphy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AgentOWL learns a joint abstract world model and hierarchical neural options to enable sample-efficient skill composition, showing data-efficiency gains on Object-Centric Atari games.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data inefficiency in model-free hierarchical RL when composing skills by introducing a compact, abstract world model that captures structure across states and time.

Method: Propose AgentOWL, which jointly and efficiently learns an abstract world model (state-and-time abstraction) and a set of hierarchical neural options (skills), enabling hierarchical planning and execution with fewer samples.

Result: Empirical evaluation on a subset of Object-Centric Atari games showing that AgentOWL can learn more skills with substantially less data than baseline methods.

Conclusion: Joint learning of an abstract world model and hierarchical options yields sample-efficient acquisition of composable skills, suggesting a promising direction for scalable skill composition in reinforcement learning.

Abstract: Building agents that can perform new skills by composing existing skills is a long-standing goal of AI agent research. Towards this end, we investigate how to efficiently acquire a sequence of skills, formalized as hierarchical neural options. However, existing model-free hierarchical reinforcement algorithms need a lot of data. We propose a novel method, which we call AgentOWL (Option and World model Learning Agent), that jointly learns -- in a sample efficient way -- an abstract world model (abstracting across both states and time) and a set of hierarchical neural options. We show, on a subset of Object-Centric Atari games, that our method can learn more skills using much less data than baseline methods.

</details>


### [238] [Membership Inference Attacks from Causal Principles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02819)
*Mathieu Even,Clément Berenfeld,Linus Bleistein,Tudor Cebere,Julie Josse,Aurélien Bellet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Causal framing of MIA evaluation; bias analysis; practical estimators with finite-sample guarantees; enables reliable memorization measurement without full retraining, robust under distribution shift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard MIA evaluation is computationally expensive for large models; one-run and zero-run methods compromise validity. A principled, statistically valid framework is needed to assess memorization and privacy risk under practical constraints.

Method: Frame memorization as the causal effect of including a data point in training; identify and formalize biases in existing protocols (interference among jointly included points; confounding from non-random membership). Derive causal analogues of MIA metrics and propose practical estimators for multi-run, one-run, and zero-run regimes with non-asymptotic (finite-sample) consistency guarantees.

Result: Empirical results on real-world data show the proposed estimators provide reliable memorization measurements when retraining is impractical and under distribution shift.

Conclusion: Provides a principled foundation for privacy evaluation in modern AI systems, delivering workable, finite-sample guarantees across regimes and enabling robust memorization assessment without full retraining.

Abstract: Membership Inference Attacks (MIAs) are widely used to quantify training data memorization and assess privacy risks. Standard evaluation requires repeated retraining, which is computationally costly for large models. One-run methods (single training with randomized data inclusion) and zero-run methods (post hoc evaluation) are often used instead, though their statistical validity remains unclear. To address this gap, we frame MIA evaluation as a causal inference problem, defining memorization as the causal effect of including a data point in the training set. This novel formulation reveals and formalizes key sources of bias in existing protocols: one-run methods suffer from interference between jointly included points, while zero-run evaluations popular for LLMs are confounded by non-random membership assignment. We derive causal analogues of standard MIA metrics and propose practical estimators for multi-run, one-run, and zero-run regimes with non-asymptotic consistency guarantees. Experiments on real-world data show that our approach enables reliable memorization measurement even when retraining is impractical and under distribution shift, providing a principled foundation for privacy evaluation in modern AI systems.

</details>


### [239] [From Tokens to Numbers: Continuous Number Modeling for SVG Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02820)
*Michael Ogezi,Martin Bell,Freda Shi,Ethan Smith*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Continuous Number Modeling (CNM) for SVG vector graphics: directly model numeric SVG parameters as continuous values to reduce tokenization artifacts, enabling faster training and better perceptual quality; train on 2M raster-to-SVG pairs with a multimodal transformer and RL fine-tuning; >30% speedup and improved fidelity; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SVGs offer efficiency and editability but numerically encoded parameters are burdensome when tokenized, hurting training speed, accuracy, and generalization. A continuous representation could align modeling with the data's intrinsic continuity.

Method: Introduce CNM to treat numbers as continuous values, train a multimodal transformer on 2M raster-to-SVG samples, and further refine via reinforcement learning using perceptual feedback to boost visual quality.

Result: Training speed improves by over 30% while achieving higher perceptual fidelity compared to token-based or other baselines.

Conclusion: CNM provides a practical and efficient path for high-quality vector generation with potential broader applicability; code is available at the linked repository.

Abstract: For certain image generation tasks, vector graphics such as Scalable Vector Graphics (SVGs) offer clear benefits such as increased flexibility, size efficiency, and editing ease, but remain less explored than raster-based approaches. A core challenge is that the numerical, geometric parameters, which make up a large proportion of SVGs, are inefficiently encoded as long sequences of tokens. This slows training, reduces accuracy, and hurts generalization. To address these problems, we propose Continuous Number Modeling (CNM), an approach that directly models numbers as first-class, continuous values rather than discrete tokens. This formulation restores the mathematical elegance of the representation by aligning the model's inputs with the data's continuous nature, removing discretization artifacts introduced by token-based encoding. We then train a multimodal transformer on 2 million raster-to-SVG samples, followed by fine-tuning via reinforcement learning using perceptual feedback to further improve visual quality. Our approach improves training speed by over 30% while maintaining higher perceptual fidelity compared to alternative approaches. This work establishes CNM as a practical and efficient approach for high-quality vector generation, with potential for broader applications. We make our code available http://github.com/mikeogezi/CNM.

</details>


### [240] [A Single Revision Step Improves Token-Efficient LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02828)
*Yingchuan Zhang,Terry Ma,Wenxuan Zhong,Ping Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PACER enables LLM reasoning traces to peer-review each other via a concise consensus packet, achieving competitive accuracy with fewer votes by refining conclusions through structured self-review guided by the packet; effective on challenging math benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the blind-spot in per-trace aggregation where confident but flawed traces can suppress the correct solution, especially as task difficulty increases; aims to turn simple consensus into collaborative logical refinement.

Method: Generate multiple traces; build a compact consensus packet with (i) unique candidate answers, (ii) aggregated confidence, and (iii) representative reasoning for each candidate. Each trace performs a targeted self-review conditioned on the packet to identify divergence points and adjust if flawed. Final predictions are obtained via confidence-weighted voting over revised traces; training-free and inference-only.

Result: On AIME and BRUMO benchmarks, PACER matches or exceeds the accuracy of 256-sample majority voting and significantly outperforms raw ensembles by enabling collaborative refinement of reasoning.

Conclusion: PACER demonstrates that trace-level peer-review can substantially improve reasoning accuracy without additional training, offering scalable, inference-only gains and potential applicability to broader reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) achieve higher accuracy on challenging reasoning tasks by scaling test-time compute through multiple trajectory sampling. However, standard aggregation methods like majority voting or individual confidence-based filtering face a fundamental "blind spot": they evaluate each trace in isolation. As problems scale in difficulty, models often generate hallucinated paths that exhibit misleadingly high confidence, causing the true solution to be suppressed by a narrow margin in traditional voting. We ask: can we enable traces to "peer-review" each other to resolve these near-miss errors?
  We introduce Packet-Conditioned Revision (PACER), a training-free, inference-only framework that enables reasoning traces to revise their conclusions through a structured coordination step. After a preliminary screening of generated traces, PACER constructs a compact consensus packet containing (i) unique candidate answers, (ii) their aggregated confidence scores, and (iii) representative reasoning summaries for each candidate answer. Individual traces then perform a targeted self-review conditioned on this packet, allowing them to identify specific logical junctions where they diverged from the broader consensus and pivot if their original reasoning is found to be flawed. Final predictions are obtained via confidence-weighted voting over these revised trajectories. On challenging competitive math benchmarks such as AIME and BRUMO, PACER matches or exceeds the accuracy of 256-sample majority voting, significantly outperforming raw ensemble baselines by transforming simple consensus into a collaborative logical refinement process.

</details>


### [241] [SC3D: Dynamic and Differentiable Causal Discovery for Temporal and Instantaneous Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02830)
*Sourajit Das,Dibyajyoti Chakraborthy,Romit Maulik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SC3D introduces a two-stage differentiable framework to jointly learn lag-specific causal graphs and instantaneous DAGs in multivariate time series, achieving improved stability and accuracy vs baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discovering causal structure in multivariate time series with both lagged and potentially instantaneous dependencies is challenging due to a combinatorial search space. A scalable, differentiable method that yields stable and accurate graphs is needed.

Method: Stage 1 performs node-wise prediction to preselect edges and generate masks for lagged and instantaneous edges. Stage 2 refines these masks by optimizing a likelihood with sparsity regularization and enforcing acyclicity on the instantaneous block, jointly learning lag-specific adjacency matrices and, if present, an instantaneous DAG.

Result: Numerical experiments on synthetic and benchmark dynamical systems show SC3D achieves improved stability and more accurate recovery of both lagged and instantaneous causal structures compared to existing temporal baselines.

Conclusion: A two-stage differentiable framework that decouples edge preselection from refinement effectively discovers both lagged and instantaneous causal structures in time series, outperforming baselines in stability and accuracy.

Abstract: Discovering causal structures from multivariate time series is a key problem because interactions span across multiple lags and possibly involve instantaneous dependencies. Additionally, the search space of the dynamic graphs is combinatorial in nature. In this study, we propose \textit{Stable Causal Dynamic Differentiable Discovery (SC3D)}, a two-stage differentiable framework that jointly learns lag-specific adjacency matrices and, if present, an instantaneous directed acyclic graph (DAG). In Stage 1, SC3D performs edge preselection through node-wise prediction to obtain masks for lagged and instantaneous edges, whereas Stage 2 refines these masks by optimizing a likelihood with sparsity along with enforcing acyclicity on the instantaneous block. Numerical results across synthetic and benchmark dynamical systems demonstrate that SC3D achieves improved stability and more accurate recovery of both lagged and instantaneous causal structures compared to existing temporal baselines.

</details>


### [242] [Koopman Autoencoders with Continuous-Time Latent Dynamics for Fluid Dynamics Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02832)
*Rares Grozavescu,Pengyu Zhang,Etienne Meunier,Mark Girolami*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Continuous-time Koopman autoencoder for data-driven turbulence surrogates with variable timesteps and matrix-exponential-consistent dynamics, enabling robust long-horizon forecasting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome autoregressive surrogate limitations (short-term accuracy vs long-horizon stability) in turbulent-flow modeling; leverage Koopman theory for linear latent evolution and temporal flexibility.

Method: Introduce a continuous-time Koopman framework where latent dynamics are integrated with numerical schemes; enables variable inference timesteps; dynamics approximates matrix exponential; evaluated on classical CFD benchmarks.

Result: Achieves robustness to temporal resolution, generalizes beyond training regimes, with learned dynamics closely matching the analytical matrix exponential and enabling efficient long-horizon forecasting.

Conclusion: Provides flexible, stable, and efficient data-driven surrogates for CFD by combining continuous-time latent linear dynamics with Koopman encoding, suitable for varying timesteps and extrapolation.

Abstract: Data-driven surrogate models have emerged as powerful tools for accelerating the simulation of turbulent flows. However, classical approaches which perform autoregressive rollouts often trade off between strong short-term accuracy and long-horizon stability. Koopman autoencoders, inspired by Koopman operator theory, provide a physics-based alternative by mapping nonlinear dynamics into a latent space where linear evolution is conducted. In practice, most existing formulations operate in a discrete-time setting, limiting temporal flexibility. In this work, we introduce a continuous-time Koopman framework that models latent evolution through numerical integration schemes. By allowing variable timesteps at inference, the method demonstrates robustness to temporal resolution and generalizes beyond training regimes. In addition, the learned dynamics closely adhere to the analytical matrix exponential solution, enabling efficient long-horizon forecasting. We evaluate the approach on classical CFD benchmarks and report accuracy, stability, and extrapolation properties.

</details>


### [243] [Tabula RASA: Exposing and Breaking the Relational Bottleneck in Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02834)
*Jonas Petersen,Camilla Mazzoleni,Riccardo Maggioni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RASA is a minimal transformer modification that adds structural inductive bias for multi-hop graph reasoning by (i) sparse adjacency masking to limit attention to graph-connected nodes and (ii) learnable edge-type biases, enabling stronger performance on deep-relational tasks like 3-hop knowledge graph QA, while depth lower bounds remain asymptotic.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformers excel generally but struggle with multi-hop relational reasoning over graphs. A complexity-theoretic lens (TC^0) suggests constant-depth transformers cannot solve certain graph problems (e.g., connectivity) without deeper networks. The authors seek a minimal architectural fix that biases attention toward graph structure to improve relational reasoning.

Method: Introduce RASA: (1) sparse adjacency masking restricting attention to graph-connected positions, reducing attention pattern search space from O(2^{n^2}) to O(2^m) for graphs with m edges; (2) learnable edge-type biases to encode relation-specific attention preferences. It preserves depth constraints but offers stronger inductive bias for graph-structured functions.

Result: Empirically, on MetaQA knowledge graph QA, RASA achieves 97.7% accuracy on 3-hop questions, surpassing EmbedKGQA (94.8%) by 2.9 percentage points. The advantage grows with reasoning depth, indicating the bias is especially beneficial for complex multi-hop queries.

Conclusion: Minimal architectural changes grounded in complexity-theoretic analysis can substantially improve multi-hop reasoning by injecting structural inductive bias. RASA does not remove asymptotic depth requirements but significantly reduces the pattern-search space, yielding stronger performance on deeper relational queries.

Abstract: Transformers achieve remarkable performance across many domains, yet struggle with tasks requiring multi-hop relational reasoning over structured data. We analyze this limitation through circuit complexity: standard transformers are $\mathsf{TC}^0$-complete and cannot solve graph connectivity in constant depth, implying $Ω(k)$ layers are necessary for $k$-hop reasoning regardless of model size or training data. We introduce RASA (Relation-Aware Sparse Attention), a minimal architectural modification that provides structural inductive bias for relational reasoning. RASA adds: (1) sparse adjacency masking that restricts attention to graph-connected positions, reducing the attention pattern search space from $O(2^{n^2})$ to $O(2^m)$ for graphs with $m$ edges; and (2) learnable edge-type biases that encode relation-specific attention preferences. While RASA does not circumvent asymptotic depth requirements, the exponential reduction in attention pattern space provides stronger inductive bias for learning graph-structured functions. Empirically, on the MetaQA knowledge graph QA benchmark, RASA achieves 97.7% accuracy on 3-hop questions, outperforming EmbedKGQA (94.8%) by 2.9 percentage points. Notably, RASA's advantage grows with reasoning depth, validating that structural inductive bias is most beneficial for complex multi-hop queries. Our results demonstrate that minimal architectural modifications, grounded in complexity-theoretic analysis, can substantially improve multi-hop reasoning.

</details>


### [244] [Semantics-Aware Generative Latent Data Augmentation for Learning in Low-Resource Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02841)
*Jae-Sung Bae,Minje Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Semantics-aware generative latent data augmentation (GeLDA) uses conditional diffusion in the latent space induced by foundation models to synthesize high-quality samples for data-scarce tasks, improving downstream performance; achieves state-of-the-art tail accuracy on ImageNet-LT and gains in zero-shot speech emotion recognition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data scarcity in downstream fine-tuning when using foundation models; need efficient, semantically guided augmentation in a compact latent space to leverage rich features without requiring large labeled datasets.

Method: GeLDA trains a conditional diffusion model in an FM-induced latent space. It uses auxiliary feature vectors encoding semantic relationships among classes/subdomains to condition generation, producing augmented samples that are then used for downstream fine-tuning.

Result: Zero-shot language-specific speech emotion recognition: +6.13 percentage points in unweighted average recall over the Whisper-large baseline. ImageNet-LT long-tailed classification: 74.7% tail-class accuracy, achieving new state-of-the-art.

Conclusion: GeLDA demonstrates effective, scalable data augmentation in the latent space of foundation models, enabling improved performance in low-resource settings and suggesting broad applicability to other tasks with semantically rich class structures.

Abstract: Despite strong performance in data-rich regimes, deep learning often underperforms in the data-scarce settings common in practice. While foundation models (FMs) trained on massive datasets demonstrate strong generalization by extracting general-purpose features, they can still suffer from scarce labeled data during downstream fine-tuning. To address this, we propose GeLDA, a semantics-aware generative latent data augmentation framework that leverages conditional diffusion models to synthesize samples in an FM-induced latent space. Because this space is low-dimensional and concentrates task-relevant information compared to the input space, GeLDA enables efficient, high-quality data generation. GeLDA conditions generation on auxiliary feature vectors that capture semantic relationships among classes or subdomains, facilitating data augmentation in low-resource domains.
