We validate GeLDA in two large-scale recognition tasks: (a) in zero-shot language-specific speech emotion recognition, GeLDA improves the Whisper-large baseline's unweighted average recall by 6.13%; and (b) in long-tailed image classification, it achieves 74.7% tail-class accuracy on ImageNet-LT, setting a new state-of-the-art result.

</details>


### [245] [Zero Sum SVD: Balancing Loss Sensitivity for Low Rank LLM Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02848)
*Ali Abbasi,Chayne Thrash,Haoran Qin,Shansita Sharma,Sepehr Seifi,Soheil Kolouri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ZS-SVD is a post-training method for global SVD component selection that yields heterogeneous per-matrix ranks under a zero-sum loss-change constraint, using activation whitening and first-order calibration loss estimates; optionally followed by a single projected gradient correction; shows consistent gains across diverse LLMs and compression ratios; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce memory and compute costs of large language models. Traditional SVD compression requires pre-determined or optimally allocated ranks, often using homogeneous ranks or expensive per-matrix optimization. A lightweight, automatic method to allocate heterogeneous ranks globally is desirable.

Method: Perform global singular component selection across the model in whitened coordinates using activation whitening. Estimate the first-order calibration loss change for each component and enforce a zero-sum rule so the cumulative predicted loss change is near zero, yielding heterogeneous ranks without solving a rank-allocation optimization. Optional additional step: a single projected gradient update after truncation (then re-truncate).

Result: Experiments across multiple LLM architectures show consistent gains across diverse benchmarks and compression ratios, validating the effectiveness of heterogeneous rank allocation via ZS-SVD. Code is available at the provided repository.

Conclusion: ZS-SVD provides a simple, post-training approach to automatically allocate heterogeneous SVD ranks across a model, avoiding expensive optimization and achieving improved performance under compression across various LLMs.

Abstract: Advances in large language models have driven strong performance across many tasks, but their memory and compute costs still hinder deployment. SVD-based compression reduces storage and can speed up inference via low-rank factors, yet performance depends on how rank is allocated under a global compression ratio. Prior methods often use homogeneous ranks for similarly sized matrices, despite large differences in loss sensitivity, or rely on expensive iterative pre-truncation optimization to determine per matrix ranks. We propose \textbf{Zero Sum SVD} (\textbf{ZS-SVD}), a post-training method that performs \emph{global} singular component selection using activation whitening and first-order calibration loss estimates in whitened coordinates. \textbf{ZS-SVD} prunes components across the whole model with a \textbf{zero sum} rule that keeps the cumulative predicted loss change near zero, automatically yielding heterogeneous ranks without solving a rank allocation optimization. Motivated by evidence that gradients near pretrained solutions exhibit low rank structure, we also introduce an optional lightweight correction that applies a \textbf{single} projected gradient update after truncation, followed by re-truncation. Extensive experiments across multiple LLM architectures show consistent gains across diverse benchmarks and compression ratios. Code is available at https://github.com/mint-vu/Zero-Sum-SVD

</details>


### [246] [Recurrent Equivariant Constraint Modulation: Learning Per-Layer Symmetry Relaxation from Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02853)
*Stefanos Pertigkiozoglou,Mircea Petrache,Shubhendu Trivedi,Kostas Daniilidis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RECM: a recurrent, layerwise constraint modulation mechanism that learns per-layer relaxation levels for equivariant networks from the training signal and input-target symmetry, with convergence guarantees and empirical gains on exact/approximate symmetry tasks (e.g., GEOM-Drugs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Strict equivariance can hinder optimization; existing relaxation approaches require task-specific tuning of target relaxation levels; a mechanism that adapts relaxation automatically from data and layer symmetry without prior targets is needed.

Method: Introduce Recurrent Equivariant Constraint Modulation (RECM), a layer-wise, recurrent mechanism that modulates equivariant constraint relaxation based on the training signal and the symmetry properties of each layer's input-target distribution. It learns relaxation levels without predefined targets and provably bounds relaxation by the layer's symmetry gap.

Result: With RECM, per-layer relaxation converges to values upper-bounded by the symmetry gap; fully symmetric layers recover full equivariance, while near-symmetric layers retain flexibility to learn non-symmetric solutions when warranted. Empirically, RECM outperforms prior methods across exact and approximate equivariant tasks, including molecular conformer generation on GEOM-Drugs.

Conclusion: RECM provides a principled, data-driven mechanism to balance equivariance and flexibility on a per-layer basis, with convergence guarantees tied to the symmetry gap and improved generalization across tasks with varying symmetry.

Abstract: Equivariant neural networks exploit underlying task symmetries to improve generalization, but strict equivariance constraints can induce more complex optimization dynamics that can hinder learning. Prior work addresses these limitations by relaxing strict equivariance during training, but typically relies on prespecified, explicit, or implicit target levels of relaxation for each network layer, which are task-dependent and costly to tune. We propose Recurrent Equivariant Constraint Modulation (RECM), a layer-wise constraint modulation mechanism that learns appropriate relaxation levels solely from the training signal and the symmetry properties of each layer's input-target distribution, without requiring any prior knowledge about the task-dependent target relaxation level. We demonstrate that under the proposed RECM update, the relaxation level of each layer provably converges to a value upper-bounded by its symmetry gap, namely the degree to which its input-target distribution deviates from exact symmetry. Consequently, layers processing symmetric distributions recover full equivariance, while those with approximate symmetries retain sufficient flexibility to learn non-symmetric solutions when warranted by the data. Empirically, RECM outperforms prior methods across diverse exact and approximate equivariant tasks, including the challenging molecular conformer generation on the GEOM-Drugs dataset.

</details>


### [247] [When pre-training hurts LoRA fine-tuning: a dynamical analysis via single-index models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02855)
*Gibbs Nwemadji,Bruno Loureiro,Jean Barbier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Excessive pre-training can slow down LoRA fine-tuning on single-index models trained with one-pass SGD; convergence depends on initial alignment and task nonlinearity; strong pre-training may prolong the search phase even when tasks align.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Question the common belief that pre-training unconditionally accelerates fine-tuning and provide a tractable, quantitative analysis of how pre-training strength affects optimization dynamics in LoRA fine-tuning.

Method: Theoretical analysis using a summary-statistics description of LoRA fine-tuning dynamics for a single-index model under one-pass SGD; derives how convergence rate depends on initial alignment and nonlinearity; yields a unified description of pre-training strength, task difficulty, and convergence behavior.

Result: The convergence rate is characterized as a function of initial fine-tuning alignment and task nonlinearity; findings show that stronger pre-training can induce a longer search phase and slow convergence, even under good alignment.

Conclusion: Pre-training strength and task difficulty jointly shape optimization dynamics; naive intuition (pre-training always helps) fails in this tractable model; results offer a framework to understand when pre-training assists or hinders LoRA fine-tuning.

Abstract: Pre-training on a source task is usually expected to facilitate fine-tuning on similar downstream problems. In this work, we mathematically show that this naive intuition is not always true: excessive pre-training can computationally slow down fine-tuning optimization. We study this phenomenon for low-rank adaptation (LoRA) fine-tuning on single-index models trained under one-pass SGD. Leveraging a summary statistics description of the fine-tuning dynamics, we precisely characterize how the convergence rate depends on the initial fine-tuning alignment and the degree of non-linearity of the target task. The key take away is that even when the pre-training and down- stream tasks are well aligned, strong pre-training can induce a prolonged search phase and hinder convergence. Our theory thus provides a unified picture of how pre-training strength and task difficulty jointly shape the dynamics and limitations of LoRA fine-tuning in a nontrivial tractable model.

</details>


### [248] [Late-Stage Generalization Collapse in Grokking: Detecting anti-grokking with Weightwatcher](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02859)
*Hari K Prakash,Charles H Martin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Identifies a third phase of grokking: anti-grokking, where test accuracy collapses to chance after initial generalization, while training accuracy remains perfect. Uses WeightWatcher (HTSR/SETOL) to detect Correlation Traps (large eigenvalues beyond MP bulk) and HTSR layer quality alpha deviating from 2.0 as primary/secondary signals; compares with other diagnostics; shows causality with forgetting/memorization and notes similar patterns in large LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Memorization lacks a precise definition; grokking describes a regime where training generalizes only temporarily. The paper claims a distinct post-generalization failure mode (anti-grokking) and seeks robust, data-free diagnostics to detect it, understand its mechanisms, and assess its presence in small models and large LLMs.

Method: Revisit two grokking benchmarks (3-layer MLP on MNIST subset; Transformer on modular addition) with extended training beyond standard. Apply WeightWatcher/HTSR-SETOL analysis on shuffled weight matrices to identify spectral anomalies (Correlation Traps) and track HTSR layer quality metric α. Compare against alternative diagnostics (L2 norms, activation sparsity, absolute weight entropy, local circuit complexity). Evaluate signals that do not require test/train data and test generalization collapse phenomena, including observations in large LLMs (OSS GPT-20/120B).

Result: Document a third phase: anti-grokking—test accuracy collapses to chance after a period of successful generalization while training accuracy remains perfect. Correlation Traps emergent as primary signal predicting anti-grokking; HTSR α deviates from 2.0 as secondary signal. Other diagnostics fail to detect anti-grokking. Correlation Traps can drive catastrophic forgetting or prototype memorization. Similar pathologies observed in large-scale LLMs.

Conclusion: Anti-grokking is a distinct post-generalization failure mode identifiable via spectral signals that do not require data access. Correlation Traps and HTSR α deviations provide practical diagnostics for monitoring and mitigating late-stage generalization collapse and inform understanding of memorization, catastrophic forgetting, and proto-memorization in neural networks and large LLMs.

Abstract: \emph{Memorization} in neural networks lacks a precise operational definition and is often inferred from the grokking regime, where training accuracy saturates while test accuracy remains very low. We identify a previously unreported third phase of grokking in this training regime: \emph{anti-grokking}, a late-stage collapse of generalization.
  We revisit two canonical grokking setups: a 3-layer MLP trained on a subset of MNIST and a transformer trained on modular addition, but extended training far beyond standard. In both cases, after models transition from pre-grokking to successful generalization, test accuracy collapses back to chance while training accuracy remains perfect, indicating a distinct post-generalization failure mode.
  To diagnose anti-grokking, we use the open-source \texttt{WeightWatcher} tool based on HTSR/SETOL theory. The primary signal is the emergence of \emph{Correlation Traps}: anomalously large eigenvalues beyond the Marchenko--Pastur bulk in the empirical spectral density of shuffled weight matrices, which are predicted to impair generalization. As a secondary signal, anti-grokking corresponds to the average HTSR layer quality metric $α$ deviating from $2.0$. Neither metric requires access to the test or training data.
  We compare these signals to alternative grokking diagnostic, including $\ell_2$ norms, Activation Sparsity, Absolute Weight Entropy, and Local Circuit Complexity. These track pre-grokking and grokking but fail to identify anti-grokking. Finally, we show that Correlation Traps can induce catastrophic forgetting and/or prototype memorization, and observe similar pathologies in large-scale LLMs, like OSS GPT 20/120B.

</details>


### [249] [A Geometry-Aware Efficient Algorithm for Compositional Entropic Risk Minimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02877)
*Xiyuan Wei,Linli Zhou,Bokun Wang,Chih-Jen Lin,Tianbao Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SCENT is a geometry-aware stochastic optimization method for compositional entropic risk minimization with Log-E-Exp losses. It uses a stochastic proximal mirror descent (SPMD) update with a negative-exponential induced Bregman divergence to solve the dual min–min problem, achieving O(1/√T) convergence for convex cases and showing empirical gains across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address optimization challenges (non-convergence, numerical instability, slow convergence) in entropic risk objectives and Log-E-Exp formulations at scale, where LogSumExp is expensive to compute.

Method: Formulate dual of entropic risk minimization as a min–min problem and apply a stochastic proximal mirror descent update for the dual variable using a Bregman divergence induced by a negative exponential function to capture the objective's geometry; name of algorithm: SCENT.

Result: Theoretical: SCENT achieves an O(1/√T) convergence rate for convex problems; SPMD offers advantages over standard SGD for the dual update. Empirical: SCENT outperforms baselines on extreme classification, partial AUC maximization, contrastive learning, and distributionally robust optimization.

Conclusion: SCENT provides a geometry-aware optimization framework for compositional entropic risk minimization, improving convergence stability and performance on large-scale risk objectives; the dual-min–min SPDM approach and negative-exponential Bregman geometry are central to its advantages.

Abstract: This paper studies optimization for a family of problems termed $\textbf{compositional entropic risk minimization}$, in which each data's loss is formulated as a Log-Expectation-Exponential (Log-E-Exp) function. The Log-E-Exp formulation serves as an abstraction of the Log-Sum-Exponential (LogSumExp) function when the explicit summation inside the logarithm is taken over a gigantic number of items and is therefore expensive to evaluate. While entropic risk objectives of this form arise in many machine learning problems, existing optimization algorithms suffer from several fundamental limitations including non-convergence, numerical instability, and slow convergence rates. To address these limitations, we propose a geometry-aware stochastic algorithm, termed $\textbf{SCENT}$, for the dual formulation of entropic risk minimization cast as a min--min optimization problem. The key to our design is a $\textbf{stochastic proximal mirror descent (SPMD)}$ update for the dual variable, equipped with a Bregman divergence induced by a negative exponential function that faithfully captures the geometry of the objective. Our main contributions are threefold: (i) we establish an $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ convergence rate of the proposed SCENT algorithm for convex problems; (ii) we theoretically characterize the advantages of SPMD over standard SGD update for optimizing the dual variable; and (iii) we demonstrate the empirical effectiveness of SCENT on extreme classification, partial AUC maximization, contrastive learning and distributionally robust optimization, where it consistently outperforms existing baselines.

</details>


### [250] [Mixture of Concept Bottleneck Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02886)
*Francesco De Santis,Gabriele Ciravegna,Giovanni De Felice,Arianna Casanova,Francesco Giannini,Michelangelo Diligenti,Mateo Espinosa Zarlenga,Pietro Barbiero,Johannes Schneider,Danilo Giordano*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Mixture of Concept Bottleneck Experts (M-CBEs) to expand CBMs along two axes: the number of experts and the functional form of each expert; presents Linear M-CBE and Symbolic M-CBE; shows that adjusting mixture size and expert form balances accuracy and interpretability per user/task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixing the task predictor to a single linear/Boolean expression in CBMs limits predictive accuracy and adaptability; a more flexible framework is needed to trade off interpretability and performance and to accommodate diverse user needs.

Method: Propose M-CBEs with multiple experts; instantiate Linear M-CBE (finite set of linear expressions) and Symbolic M-CBE (symbolic regression with user-specified operator vocabularies); employ a mixture mechanism to combine expert predictions grounded in concepts.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows that varying mixture size and expert form navigates the accuracy–interpretability trade-off and enables customization for different users and tasks.

Conclusion: M-CBEs broaden the CBM design space, offering flexible, interpretable yet accurate models and promising adaptability for user-specific interpretability requirements.

Abstract: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) promote interpretability by grounding predictions in human-understandable concepts. However, existing CBMs typically fix their task predictor to a single linear or Boolean expression, limiting both predictive accuracy and adaptability to diverse user needs. We propose Mixture of Concept Bottleneck Experts (M-CBEs), a framework that generalizes existing CBMs along two dimensions: the number of experts and the functional form of each expert, exposing an underexplored region of the design space. We investigate this region by instantiating two novel models: Linear M-CBE, which learns a finite set of linear expressions, and Symbolic M-CBE, which leverages symbolic regression to discover expert functions from data under user-specified operator vocabularies. Empirical evaluation demonstrates that varying the mixture size and functional form provides a robust framework for navigating the accuracy-interpretability trade-off, adapting to different user and task needs.

</details>


### [251] [Self-Soupervision: Cooking Model Soups without Labels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02890)
*Anthony Fuller,James R. Green,Evan Shelhamer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Self-Soupervision extends model soups to self-supervised learning, enabling combining diverse SSL ingredients and hyperparameters to improve robustness and transfer performance; yields gains on corrupted data and allows mixing SSL algorithms (e.g., MAE, MoCoV3, MMCR).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend the successful model-soup paradigm from supervised learning to SSL to leverage unlabeled data, enable transfer to new data sources, and improve robustness to distribution shifts by ensemble-like optimization over SSL ingredients.

Method: Construct a 'soup' by fine-tuning a base stock model into multiple SSL ingredients (potentially differing data sources and SSL hyperparameters/algorithms) and then mixing their parameters back into one model. Apply flavoring by training ingredients on new unlabeled data sources (for transfer or robustness). Evaluate robustness by applying Self-Souping on corrupted test data, then re-fine-tuning on uncorrupted training data. Demonstrate that soups can combine different SSL ingredients (MAE, MoCoV3, MMCR).

Result: Self-Soupervision achieves robustness gains (+3.5 percentage points on ImageNet-C and +7 percentage points on LAION-C). The approach can outperform any single SSL ingredient. Ingredients can differ in SSL hyperparameters and even in their SSL algorithms; soups combining MAE, MoCoV3, and MMCR yield higher accuracy than individual ingredients.

Conclusion: Self-Soupervision broadens the SSL toolbox by enabling heterogeneous, algorithm-diverse soups that improve robustness and transfer capabilities, suggesting a general framework for combining SSL ingredients into potent soups.

Abstract: Model soups are strange and strangely effective combinations of parameters. They take a model (the stock), fine-tune it into multiple models (the ingredients), and then mix their parameters back into one model (the soup) to improve predictions. While all known soups require supervised learning, and optimize the same loss on labeled data, our recipes for Self-\emph{Soup}ervision generalize soups to self-supervised learning (SSL). Our Self-Souping lets us flavor ingredients on new data sources, e.g. from unlabeled data from a task for transfer or from a shift for robustness. We show that Self-Souping on corrupted test data, then fine-tuning back on uncorrupted train data, boosts robustness by +3.5\% (ImageNet-C) and +7\% (LAION-C). Self-\emph{Soup}ervision also unlocks countless SSL algorithms to cook the diverse ingredients needed for more robust soups. We show for the first time that ingredients can differ in their SSL hyperparameters -- and more surprisingly, in their SSL algorithms. We cook soups of MAE, MoCoV3, and MMCR ingredients that are more accurate than any one single SSL ingredient.

</details>


### [252] [TraceNAS: Zero-shot LLM Pruning via Gradient Trace Correlation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02891)
*Prajna G. Malettira,Manish Nagaraj,Arjun Roy,Shubham Negi,Kaushik Roy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TraceNAS is a training-free NAS for joint depth-width pruning of LLMs, using a scale-invariant zero-shot proxy to align with the pretrained model's loss landscape. It enables ~10x GPU-hour savings and achieves competitive performance vs training-aware baselines on Llama/Qwen benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address global dependencies in structured pruning of LLMs while drastically reducing the computational cost compared to training-aware pruning.

Method: Jointly search over LLM depth and width pruning using a scale-invariant zero-shot proxy that measures loss landscape alignment with the pretrained model (training-free). The framework selects pruned architectures that maximize potential after pruning, validated on Llama and Qwen across commonsense and reasoning tasks, with a single GPU budget of ~8.5 hours.

Result: TraceNAS enables high-fidelity discovery of pruned models on a single GPU (8.5 hours) and achieves ~10x GPU-hour reduction versus training-aware methods, with competitive performance relative to baselines on Llama and Qwen benchmarks.

Conclusion: A training-free NAS approach that effectively captures global structural dependencies via a loss-landscape proxy, enabling efficient pruning of LLMs with competitive post-pruning potential.

Abstract: Structured pruning is essential for efficient deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs). The varying sensitivity of LLM sub-blocks to pruning necessitates the identification of optimal non-uniformly pruned models. Existing methods evaluate the importance of layers, attention heads, or weight channels in isolation. Such localized focus ignores the complex global structural dependencies that exist across the model. Training-aware structured pruning addresses global dependencies, but its computational cost can be just as expensive as post-pruning training. To alleviate the computational burden of training-aware pruning and capture global structural dependencies, we propose TraceNAS, a training-free Neural Architecture Search (NAS) framework that jointly explores structured pruning of LLM depth and width. TraceNAS identifies pruned models that maintain a high degree of loss landscape alignment with the pretrained model using a scale-invariant zero-shot proxy, effectively selecting models that exhibit maximal performance potential during post-pruning training. TraceNAS is highly efficient, enabling high-fidelity discovery of pruned models on a single GPU in 8.5 hours, yielding a 10$\times$ reduction in GPU-hours compared to training-aware methods. Evaluations on the Llama and Qwen families demonstrate that TraceNAS is competitive with training-aware baselines across commonsense and reasoning benchmarks.

</details>


### [253] [Controlled disagreement improves generalization in decentralized training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02899)
*Zesen Wang,Mikael Johansson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DSGD-AC preserves non-vanishing consensus errors via a time-dependent scaling, revealing them as structured perturbations that align with the dominant Hessian subspace to steer optimization toward flatter minima, outperforming both standard DSGD and centralized SGD on image classification and machine translation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the view that consensus errors in decentralized training are purely detrimental by showing they can act as useful perturbations that regularize optimization and promote generalization through alignment with the Hessian subspace.

Method: Propose Decentralized SGD with Adaptive Consensus (DSGD-AC) that intentionally maintains non-vanishing consensus errors using a time-dependent scaling mechanism; provide theoretical insight that these errors align with the dominant Hessian subspace; validate experimentally on image classification and machine translation benchmarks comparing against standard DSGD and centralized SGD.

Result: Non-vanishing consensus errors function as structured perturbations that guide optimization toward flatter minima; DSGD-AC achieves higher test accuracy and flatter solutions than both standard DSGD and centralized SGD across evaluated tasks.

Conclusion: Consensus errors can serve as a useful implicit regularizer in decentralized learning, prompting a shift in design principles toward algorithms that exploit, rather than suppress, controlled consensus dynamics to improve generalization.

Abstract: Decentralized training is often regarded as inferior to centralized training because the consensus errors between workers are thought to undermine convergence and generalization, even with homogeneous data distributions. This work challenges this view by introducing decentralized SGD with Adaptive Consensus (DSGD-AC), which intentionally preserves non-vanishing consensus errors through a time-dependent scaling mechanism. We prove that these errors are not random noise but systematically align with the dominant Hessian subspace, acting as structured perturbations that guide optimization toward flatter minima. Across image classification and machine translation benchmarks, DSGD-AC consistently surpasses both standard DSGD and centralized SGD in test accuracy and solution flatness. Together, these results establish consensus errors as a useful implicit regularizer and open a new perspective on the design of decentralized learning algorithms.

</details>


### [254] [Manifold-Constrained Energy-Based Transition Models for Offline Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02900)
*Zeyu Fang,Zuyuan Zhang,Mahdi Imani,Tian Lan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Manifold-constrained energy-based transitions (MC-ETM) stabilize offline RL under distribution shift by learning a latent next-state manifold and using diffusion-based hard negatives to sharpen the energy landscape, enabling conservative rollouts and improved performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline RL suffers from distribution shift: policy improvement drives rollouts into underrepresented regions, where model error causes severe value overestimation. A robust transition model and reliable uncertainty signal are needed to limit over-optimistic updates.

Method: Train conditional energy-based transition models with a manifold projection–diffusion negative sampler to learn a latent manifold of next states. Generate near-manifold hard negatives by perturbing latent codes and performing Langevin dynamics in latent space with the energy model. Use the energy as a reliability signal to truncate rollouts when minimum energy over sampled next states exceeds a threshold and apply pessimistic penalties based on Q-value dispersion across energy-guided samples. Formalize via a hybrid pessimistic MDP and derive a bound separating in-support evaluation error from truncation risk.

Result: Empirically, MC-ETM improves multi-step dynamics fidelity and yields higher normalized returns on standard offline control benchmarks, particularly under irregular dynamics and sparse data coverage.

Conclusion: MC-ETM provides a principled, conservative framework for offline RL that tightens the energy landscape around dataset support and delivers more reliable policy improvement under distribution shift; future work could explore scalability, alternative sampling schedules, and comparisons to other uncertainty-aware approaches.

Abstract: Model-based offline reinforcement learning is brittle under distribution shift: policy improvement drives rollouts into state--action regions weakly supported by the dataset, where compounding model error yields severe value overestimation. We propose Manifold-Constrained Energy-based Transition Models (MC-ETM), which train conditional energy-based transition models using a manifold projection--diffusion negative sampler. MC-ETM learns a latent manifold of next states and generates near-manifold hard negatives by perturbing latent codes and running Langevin dynamics in latent space with the learned conditional energy, sharpening the energy landscape around the dataset support and improving sensitivity to subtle out-of-distribution deviations. For policy optimization, the learned energy provides a single reliability signal: rollouts are truncated when the minimum energy over sampled next states exceeds a threshold, and Bellman backups are stabilized via pessimistic penalties based on Q-value-level dispersion across energy-guided samples. We formalize MC-ETM through a hybrid pessimistic MDP formulation and derive a conservative performance bound separating in-support evaluation error from truncation risk. Empirically, MC-ETM improves multi-step dynamics fidelity and yields higher normalized returns on standard offline control benchmarks, particularly under irregular dynamics and sparse data coverage.

</details>


### [255] [Spatiotemporal Decision Transformer for Traffic Coordination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02903)
*Haoran Su,Yandong Sun,Hanxiao Deng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MADT: Multi-Agent Decision Transformer for traffic signal control; treats multi-agent coordination as sequence modeling; uses graph attention, temporal Transformer, return-to-go; offline learning with potential online fine-tuning; achieves 5-6% travel-time reduction on grid and real networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses sample-inefficiency and coordination challenges in multi-agent RL for traffic control; leverages offline data and sequence modeling to improve scalability and coordination.

Method: Extend Decision Transformer to multi-agent setting using (i) graph attention to model spatial dependencies among intersections, (ii) temporal transformer encoder to capture traffic dynamics, (iii) return-to-go conditioning to specify target performance; supports offline learning; architecture facilitates online fine-tuning.

Result: In experiments on synthetic grid networks and real-world scenarios, MADT achieves state-of-the-art performance, reducing average travel time by 5-6% relative to strongest baseline and demonstrates superior coordination among adjacent intersections.

Conclusion: MADT proves that multi-agent sequence modeling with graph and temporal transformers plus return-to-go conditioning is effective for traffic signal control; offline learning from historical data is viable with potential online adaptation; future work includes ablations, scalability, transferability, and real-time deployment considerations.

Abstract: Traffic signal control is a critical challenge in urban transportation, requiring coordination among multiple intersections to optimize network-wide traffic flow. While reinforcement learning has shown promise for adaptive signal control, existing methods struggle with multi-agent coordination and sample efficiency. We introduce MADT (Multi-Agent Decision Transformer), a novel approach that reformulates multi-agent traffic signal control as a sequence modeling problem. MADT extends the Decision Transformer paradigm to multi-agent settings by incorporating: (1) a graph attention mechanism for modeling spatial dependencies between intersections, (2) a|temporal transformer encoder for capturing traffic dynamics, and (3) return-to-go conditioning for target performance specification. Our approach enables offline learning from historical traffic data, with architecture design that facilitates potential online fine-tuning. Experiments on synthetic grid networks and real-world traffic scenarios demonstrate that MADT achieves state-of-the-art performance, reducing average travel time by 5-6% compared to the strongest baseline while exhibiting superior coordination among adjacent intersections.

</details>


### [256] [A Random Matrix Theory Perspective on the Consistency of Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02908)
*Binxu Wang,Jacob Zavatone-Veth,Cengiz Pehlevan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A random-matrix-theory framework explains cross-split consistency in diffusion models trained on non-overlapping data subsets. Shared Gaussian statistics largely determine outputs; a self-consistent noise renormalization κ explains shrinkage toward the data mean. Fluctuations hinge on anisotropy, input inhomogeneity, and dataset size, with fractional-matrix-power tools enabling trajectory analysis. Validated on UNet and DiT in non-memorization regime, establishing a reproducibility baseline by linking data spectra to output stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand why diffusion models trained on disjoint data splits produce similar outputs and quantify how finite data shapes denoisers and sampling maps. Provide a principled baseline for reproducibility in diffusion training.

Method: Develop a random-matrix-theory (RMT) framework in a linear diffusion setting to quantify expectation and variability of the learned denoiser and sampling map. Derive a self-consistent renormalization κ(σ^2) of the noise level, and variance formulas capturing anisotropy, inhomogeneity, and dataset-size scaling. Extend deterministic-equivalence tools to fractional matrix powers to analyze full sampling trajectories. Validate predictions on UNet and DiT in the non-memorization regime.

Result: The theory sharply predicts diffusion-model behavior: sampling variability renormalizes noise and pulls samples toward the data mean; cross-split disagreements arise from eigenmode anisotropy, input inhomogeneity, and dataset size scaling. Fractional powers enable trajectory analysis. Empirical validation on UNet and DiT aligns with predictions, outlining where splits diverge and providing a principled reproducibility baseline.

Conclusion: Links spectral properties of data to the stability of generative outputs in diffusion models and offers a principled reproducibility baseline for diffusion-training experiments, with implications for model design and evaluation when data splits are non-overlapping.

Abstract: Diffusion models trained on different, non-overlapping subsets of a dataset often produce strikingly similar outputs when given the same noise seed. We trace this consistency to a simple linear effect: the shared Gaussian statistics across splits already predict much of the generated images. To formalize this, we develop a random matrix theory (RMT) framework that quantifies how finite datasets shape the expectation and variance of the learned denoiser and sampling map in the linear setting. For expectations, sampling variability acts as a renormalization of the noise level through a self-consistent relation $σ^2 \mapsto κ(σ^2)$, explaining why limited data overshrink low-variance directions and pull samples toward the dataset mean. For fluctuations, our variance formulas reveal three key factors behind cross-split disagreement: \textit{anisotropy} across eigenmodes, \textit{inhomogeneity} across inputs, and overall scaling with dataset size. Extending deterministic-equivalence tools to fractional matrix powers further allows us to analyze entire sampling trajectories. The theory sharply predicts the behavior of linear diffusion models, and we validate its predictions on UNet and DiT architectures in their non-memorization regime, identifying where and how samples deviates across training data split. This provides a principled baseline for reproducibility in diffusion training, linking spectral properties of data to the stability of generative outputs.

</details>


### [257] [Notes on the Reward Representation of Posterior Updates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02912)
*Pedro A. Ortega*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KL-regularized soft updates are Bayesian posteriors within a fixed model, yielding a sharp identifiability: updates reveal relative incentives but not absolute rewards; coherence across conditioning orders adds a constraint.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify when decision-making-as-inference can be treated as literal Bayesian updating under KL regularization, and what such updates reveal about incentives and rewards in control/RL.

Method: Analyze a single fixed probabilistic model where a KL-regularized update equals a Bayesian posterior. Interpret the update as an information channel carrying evidence, derive identifiability results showing posterior updates fix relative incentives while leaving absolute rewards ambiguous up to context-specific baselines, and impose a coherence constraint via a reusable continuation value across different conditioning orders.

Result: Posterior updates determine context-dependent incentive signals (relative rewards) but do not uniquely set absolute rewards; absolute rewards are identifiable only up to baselines. Adding a coherence constraint by requiring the same continuation value across update directions links reward descriptions across conditioning orders.

Conclusion: Provides a sharp identification result about what KL-regularized updates reveal in a fixed-model Bayesian setting and highlights fundamental ambiguity in absolute rewards, suggesting extra structure is needed to pin down global reward scales.

Abstract: Many ideas in modern control and reinforcement learning treat decision-making as inference: start from a baseline distribution and update it when a signal arrives. We ask when this can be made literal rather than metaphorical. We study the special case where a KL-regularized soft update is exactly a Bayesian posterior inside a single fixed probabilistic model, so the update variable is a genuine channel through which information is transmitted. In this regime, behavioral change is driven only by evidence carried by that channel: the update must be explainable as an evidence reweighing of the baseline. This yields a sharp identification result: posterior updates determine the relative, context-dependent incentive signal that shifts behavior, but they do not uniquely determine absolute rewards, which remain ambiguous up to context-specific baselines. Requiring one reusable continuation value across different update directions adds a further coherence constraint linking the reward descriptions associated with different conditioning orders.

</details>


### [258] [Weighted Temporal Decay Loss for Learning Wearable PPG Data with Sparse Clinical Labels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02917)
*Yunsung Chung,Keum San Chun,Migyeong Gwak,Han Feng,Yingshuo Liu,Chanho Lim,Viswam Nathan,Nassir Marrouche,Sharanya Arcot Desai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Biomarker-aware temporal weighting for PPG-based health prediction to address label sparsity; simple linear decay with regularization improves performance and yields interpretable temporal sensitivity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical labels for biosignals are sparse and temporally distant from the recording, making supervision weak; a decay-weighted loss can better align PPG segments with ground-truth labels.

Method: Train with a biomarker-specific decay of sample weights based on time gap between PPG segment and label; compare four decay families; apply a regularizer to avoid trivial solutions; evaluate on smartwatch PPG from 450 participants across 10 biomarkers in a subject-wise setting.

Result: Improved AUPRC: 0.715 (biomarker-aware) vs 0.674 (fine-tuned self-supervised baseline) and 0.626 (Random Forest). Among decay families, linear decay was most robust. Learned decay rates provide interpretable view of how quickly PPG evidence becomes stale.

Conclusion: A simple, interpretable decay-based weighting scheme can address label sparsity in wearable PPG health monitoring, improving accuracy and offering insight into temporal sensitivity per biomarker.

Abstract: Advances in wearable computing and AI have increased interest in leveraging PPG for health monitoring over the past decade. One of the biggest challenges in developing health algorithms based on such biosignals is the sparsity of clinical labels, which makes biosignals temporally distant from lab draws less reliable for supervision. To address this problem, we introduce a simple training strategy that learns a biomarker-specific decay of sample weight over the time gap between a segment and its ground truth label and uses this weight in the loss with a regularizer to prevent trivial solutions. On smartwatch PPG from 450 participants across 10 biomarkers, the approach improves over baselines. In the subject-wise setting, the proposed approach averages 0.715 AUPRC, compared to 0.674 for a fine-tuned self-supervised baseline and 0.626 for a feature-based Random Forest. A comparison of four decay families shows that a simple linear decay function is most robust on average. Beyond accuracy, the learned decay rates summarize how quickly each biomarker's PPG evidence becomes stale, providing an interpretable view of temporal sensitivity.

</details>


### [259] [A Reproducible Framework for Bias-Resistant Machine Learning on Small-Sample Neuroimaging Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02920)
*Jagan Mohan Reddy Dwarampudi,Jennifer L Purks,Joshua Wong,Renjie Hu,Tania Banerjee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A bias-resistant, reproducible ML framework for small-sample neuroimaging combining domain-informed features, nested CV, and calibrated thresholds, achieving ~0.66 balanced accuracy on high-dimensional MRI data with an interpretable feature subset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve reproducibility and generalization in data-limited neuroimaging ML by avoiding reuse of folds for model selection and performance estimation and by integrating interpretability with unbiased evaluation.

Method: Domain-informed feature engineering; nested cross-validation; calibrated decision-threshold optimization; importance-guided ranking to select a compact, interpretable feature subset; applied to high-dimensional structural MRI predicting DBS cognitive outcomes.

Result: Nested-CV balanced accuracy 0.660 ± 0.068 on the test, with a compact interpretable subset; demonstrates unbiased evaluation and potential for generalizable ML in data-limited biomedical domains.

Conclusion: Combines interpretability with unbiased evaluation to provide a generalizable computational blueprint for reliable ML in data-limited biomedicine.

Abstract: We introduce a reproducible, bias-resistant machine learning framework that integrates domain-informed feature engineering, nested cross-validation, and calibrated decision-threshold optimization for small-sample neuroimaging data. Conventional cross-validation frameworks that reuse the same folds for both model selection and performance estimation yield optimistically biased results, limiting reproducibility and generalization. Demonstrated on a high-dimensional structural MRI dataset of deep brain stimulation cognitive outcomes, the framework achieved a nested-CV balanced accuracy of 0.660\,$\pm$\,0.068 using a compact, interpretable subset selected via importance-guided ranking. By combining interpretability and unbiased evaluation, this work provides a generalizable computational blueprint for reliable machine learning in data-limited biomedical domains.

</details>


### [260] [How Does the Lagrangian Guide Safe Reinforcement Learning through Diffusion Models?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02924)
*Xiaoyuan Cheng,Wenxuan Yuan,Boyang Li,Yuanchao Xu,Yiming Yang,Hao Liang,Bei Peng,Robert Loftin,Zhuo Sun,Yukun Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Augmented Lagrangian-Guided Diffusion (ALGD) for off-policy safe reinforcement learning, stabilizing diffusion-based policy learning by using an augmented Lagrangian to locally convexify the energy landscape; claims theoretical justification and strong empirical performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based RL enables multimodal policies but current work focuses on offline reward maximization with limited safety guarantees in online/off-policy settings; there is instability in primal-dual optimization when using the Lagrangian as an energy in diffusion dynamics.

Method: Reinterpret the Lagrangian as an energy guiding denoising dynamics in diffusion-based RL. Identify instability due to non-convex Lagrangian landscape. Introduce augmented Lagrangian to locally convexify the energy, stabilizing both policy generation and training without changing the optimal policy distribution.

Result: The augmented approach yields a stabilized off-policy safe RL method with theoretical grounding and empirical demonstration of strong, stable performance across diverse environments.

Conclusion: ALGD provides a theoretically sound and practically effective framework for stable, off-policy safe diffusion-based RL by leveraging augmented Lagrangian to regularize the energy landscape guiding diffusion dynamics.

Abstract: Diffusion policy sampling enables reinforcement learning (RL) to represent multimodal action distributions beyond suboptimal unimodal Gaussian policies. However, existing diffusion-based RL methods primarily focus on offline settings for reward maximization, with limited consideration of safety in online settings. To address this gap, we propose Augmented Lagrangian-Guided Diffusion (ALGD), a novel algorithm for off-policy safe RL. By revisiting optimization theory and energy-based model, we show that the instability of primal-dual methods arises from the non-convex Lagrangian landscape. In diffusion-based safe RL, the Lagrangian can be interpreted as an energy function guiding the denoising dynamics. Counterintuitively, direct usage destabilizes both policy generation and training. ALGD resolves this issue by introducing an augmented Lagrangian that locally convexifies the energy landscape, yielding a stabilized policy generation and training process without altering the distribution of the optimal policy. Theoretical analysis and extensive experiments demonstrate that ALGD is both theoretically grounded and empirically effective, achieving strong and stable performance across diverse environments.

</details>


### [261] [Refining Decision Boundaries In Anomaly Detection Using Similarity Search Within the Feature Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02925)
*Sidahmed Benabderrahmane,Petko Valtchev,James Cheney,Talal Rahwan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SDA2E: a sparse dual-adversarial attention autoencoder with similarity-guided active learning for imbalanced anomaly detection; achieves strong ranking and labeling efficiency across 52 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Detect rare anomalies in highly imbalanced datasets (e.g., APTs); conventional active learning underutilizes geometric structure; need compact discriminative representations and effective query strategies.

Method: Propose SDA2E autoencoder with sparse, dual adversarial, attention-based encoding; three similarity-guided AL strategies: normal-like expansion, anomaly-like prioritization, hybrid; introduce SIM_NM1 for sparse binary embeddings; extensive evaluation on 52 imbalanced datasets including DARPA scenarios; compare with 15 baselines.

Result: Consistent superior ranking (nDCG up to 1.0); labeling effort reduced up to 80%; results statistically significant.

Conclusion: Framework is robust, efficient, and well-validated for anomaly detection in cybersecurity contexts such as APTs.

Abstract: Detecting rare and diverse anomalies in highly imbalanced datasets-such as Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) in cybersecurity-remains a fundamental challenge for machine learning systems. Active learning offers a promising direction by strategically querying an oracle to minimize labeling effort, yet conventional approaches often fail to exploit the intrinsic geometric structure of the feature space for model refinement. In this paper, we introduce SDA2E, a Sparse Dual Adversarial Attention-based AutoEncoder designed to learn compact and discriminative latent representations from imbalanced, high-dimensional data. We further propose a similarity-guided active learning framework that integrates three novel strategies to refine decision boundaries efficiently: mormal-like expansion, which enriches the training set with points similar to labeled normals to improve reconstruction fidelity; anomaly-like prioritization, which boosts ranking accuracy by focusing on points resembling known anomalies; and a hybrid strategy that combines both for balanced model refinement and ranking. A key component of our framework is a new similarity measure, Normalized Matching 1s (SIM_NM1), tailored for sparse binary embeddings. We evaluate SDA2E extensively across 52 imbalanced datasets, including multiple DARPA Transparent Computing scenarios, and benchmark it against 15 state-of-the-art anomaly detection methods. Results demonstrate that SDA2E consistently achieves superior ranking performance (nDCG up to 1.0 in several cases) while reducing the required labeled data by up to 80% compared to passive training. Statistical tests confirm the significance of these improvements. Our work establishes a robust, efficient, and statistically validated framework for anomaly detection that is particularly suited to cybersecurity applications such as APT detection.

</details>


### [262] [Distance Marching for Generative Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02928)
*Zimo Wang,Ishit Mehta,Haolin Lu,Chung-En Sun,Ge Yan,Tsui-Wei Weng,Tzu-Mao Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Distance Marching, a time-unconditional generative modeling approach that uses distance-field-inspired losses and two principled inference methods to focus denoising on nearer targets, improving FID on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet and enabling effective class-conditional generation with less sampling steps; distance prediction aids early stopping and OOD detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Time-unconditional generative models face ambiguous supervision because the same noisy input can correspond to multiple noise levels without time conditioning, which can misdirect denoising and hinder learning. A principled framework is needed to steer learning toward the data manifold.

Method: Propose Distance Marching with two principled inference methods. Design losses that emphasize closer targets to steer denoising directions toward the data manifold. Apply across architectures. Evaluate on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet for unconditional generation and on class-conditional ImageNet for conditional cases. Compare to recent time-unconditional baselines and flow matching; achieve faster sampling with fewer steps.

Result: Consistent FID improvements: +13.5% on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet over time-unconditional baselines. In class-conditional ImageNet, surpasses flow matching with their losses and inference methods, achieving lower FID than flow matching's final performance using 60% of sampling steps, and 13.6% lower FID on average across backbone sizes. Distance prediction also aids early stopping and OOD detection.

Conclusion: Distance field modeling offers a principled lens for generative modeling and can guide denoising directions toward the data manifold, enabling stronger performance and potential practical benefits (early stopping, OOD detection).

Abstract: Time-unconditional generative models learn time-independent denoising vector fields. But without time conditioning, the same noisy input may correspond to multiple noise levels and different denoising directions, which interferes with the supervision signal. Inspired by distance field modeling, we propose Distance Marching, a new time-unconditional approach with two principled inference methods. Crucially, we design losses that focus on closer targets. This yields denoising directions better directed toward the data manifold. Across architectures, Distance Marching consistently improves FID by 13.5% on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet over recent time-unconditional baselines. For class-conditional ImageNet generation, despite removing time input, Distance Marching surpasses flow matching using our losses and inference methods. It achieves lower FID than flow matching's final performance using 60% of the sampling steps and 13.6% lower FID on average across backbone sizes. Moreover, our distance prediction is also helpful for early stopping during sampling and for OOD detection. We hope distance field modeling can serve as a principled lens for generative modeling.

</details>


### [263] [RPG-AE: Neuro-Symbolic Graph Autoencoders with Rare Pattern Mining for Provenance-Based Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02929)
*Asif Tauhid,Sidahmed Benabderrahmane,Mohamad Altrabulsi,Ahamed Foisal,Talal Rahwan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unified neuro-symbolic framework combining Graph Autoencoder with rare-pattern mining for provenance-based anomaly detection; outperforms baseline GAE and is competitive with ensembles on DARPA TC data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: APT-like threats are long-term, stealthy, and easily blend with normal behavior; need robust, interpretable anomaly detection leveraging relational structure.

Method: Construct process behavioral graphs via kNN on feature similarity; train Graph Autoencoder to learn normal graph structure; detect anomalies via reconstruction deviations; apply rare-pattern mining to boost scores for rare co-occurrences; evaluate on DARPA Transparent Computing datasets.

Result: Rare-pattern boosting yields substantial gains in anomaly ranking over baseline GAE; unified model outperforms individual context detectors and is competitive with ensemble methods.

Conclusion: Coupling graph representation learning with pattern mining improves effectiveness and interpretability in provenance-based security anomaly detection.

Abstract: Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) are sophisticated, long-term cyberattacks that are difficult to detect because they operate stealthily and often blend into normal system behavior. This paper presents a neuro-symbolic anomaly detection framework that combines a Graph Autoencoder (GAE) with rare pattern mining to identify APT-like activities in system-level provenance data. Our approach first constructs a process behavioral graph using k-Nearest Neighbors based on feature similarity, then learns normal relational structure using a Graph Autoencoder. Anomaly candidates are identified through deviations between observed and reconstructed graph structure. To further improve detection, we integrate an rare pattern mining module that discovers infrequent behavioral co-occurrences and uses them to boost anomaly scores for processes exhibiting rare signatures. We evaluate the proposed method on the DARPA Transparent Computing datasets and show that rare-pattern boosting yields substantial gains in anomaly ranking quality over the baseline GAE. Compared with existing unsupervised approaches on the same benchmark, our single unified model consistently outperforms individual context-based detectors and achieves performance competitive with ensemble aggregation methods that require multiple separate detectors. These results highlight the value of coupling graph-based representation learning with classical pattern mining to improve both effectiveness and interpretability in provenance-based security anomaly detection.

</details>


### [264] [Rare Event Early Detection: A Dataset of Sepsis Onset for Critically Ill Trauma Patients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02930)
*Yin Jin,Tucker R. Stewart,Deyi Zhou,Chhavi Gupta,Arjita Nema,Scott C. Brakenridge,Grant E. O'Keefe,Juhua Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Public, standardized post-trauma sepsis onset dataset from MIMIC-III; frames daily rare-event detection in ICU workflow; baseline experiments indicate need for further method development; benchmark and code provided.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sepsis imposes high burden and current public datasets largely treat ICU patients as a uniform group, neglecting post-trauma inflammatory overlap with sepsis. A targeted post-trauma sepsis dataset enables development of early detection methods specifically for trauma patients.

Method: Extracted and relabeled data using standardized post-trauma clinical facts from MIMIC-III; validated the labeling; framed onset detection as a daily rare-event problem within ICU workflow; conducted comprehensive experiments to establish a benchmark.

Result: Released a publicly available dataset for post-trauma sepsis onset, with a general benchmark based on extensive experiments; results indicate the need for further method development; data code is provided at the referenced GitHub repository.

Conclusion: A specialized post-trauma sepsis onset dataset facilitates focused early-detection research, enabling targeted methodological advances and establishing a benchmark for future work.

Abstract: Sepsis is a major public health concern due to its high morbidity, mortality, and cost. Its clinical outcome can be substantially improved through early detection and timely intervention. By leveraging publicly available datasets, machine learning (ML) has driven advances in both research and clinical practice. However, existing public datasets consider ICU patients (Intensive Care Unit) as a uniform group and neglect the potential challenges presented by critically ill trauma patients in whom injury-related inflammation and organ dysfunction can overlap with the clinical features of sepsis. We propose that a targeted identification of post-traumatic sepsis is necessary in order to develop methods for early detection. Therefore, we introduce a publicly available standardized post-trauma sepsis onset dataset extracted, relabeled using standardized post-trauma clinical facts, and validated from MIMIC-III. Furthermore, we frame early detection of post-trauma sepsis onset according to clinical workflow in ICUs in a daily basis resulting in a new rare event detection problem. We then establish a general benchmark through comprehensive experiments, which shows the necessity of further advancements using this new dataset. The data code is available at https://github.com/ML4UWHealth/SepsisOnset_TraumaCohort.git.

</details>


### [265] [3D-Learning: Diffusion-Augmented Distributionally Robust Decision-Focused Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02943)
*Jiaqi Wen,Lei Fan,Jianyi Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A diffusion-augmented distributionally robust, decision-focused learning framework (3D-Learning) for Predict-then-Optimize pipelines that robustly handles out-of-distribution shifts by searching worst-case distributions within a diffusion model, outperforming standard DRO and data augmentation in LLM resource provisioning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PTO predictors are vulnerable to OOD samples, causing large decision errors. There is a need for decision-focused robustness that accounts for distributional shifts beyond standard training data.

Method: Introduce Distributionally Robust Decision-Focused Learning (DR-DFL) to optimize decision performance under worst-case distribution. Replace classical DRO with Diffusion-Augmented Distributionally Robust Decision-Focused Learning (3D-Learning), which searches for the worst-case distribution in the parameterized diffusion model space, ensuring consistency with real data while exploring a rich distributional family.

Result: Empirical evaluation on an LL M resource provisioning task shows that 3D-Learning achieves superior OOD generalization and outperforms existing DRO and data augmentation methods.

Conclusion: Diffusion-based worst-case distribution search provides a powerful, data-consistent mechanism to balance average performance and worst-case risk in PTO, with potential applicability to a broad class of decision-focused learning problems.

Abstract: Predict-then-Optimize (PTO) pipelines are widely employed in computing and networked systems, where Machine Learning (ML) models are used to predict critical contextual information for downstream decision-making tasks such as cloud LLM serving, data center demand response, and edge workload scheduling. However, these ML predictors are often vulnerable to out-of-distribution (OOD) samples at test time, leading to significant decision performance degradation due to large prediction errors. To address the generalization challenges under OOD conditions, we present the framework of Distributionally Robust Decision-Focused Learning (DR-DFL), which trains ML models to optimize decision performance under the worst-case distribution. Instead of relying on classical Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO) techniques, we propose Diffusion-Augmented Distributionally Robust Decision-Focused Learning (3D-Learning), which searches for the worst-case distribution within the parameterized space of a diffusion model. By leveraging the powerful distribution modeling capabilities of diffusion models, 3D-Learning identifies worst-case distributions that remain consistent with real data, achieving a favorable balance between average and worst-case scenarios. Empirical results on an LLM resource provisioning task demonstrate that 3D-Learning outperforms existing DRO and Data Augmentation methods in OOD generalization performance.

</details>


### [266] [Variational Sparse Paired Autoencoders (vsPAIR) for Inverse Problems and Uncertainty Quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02948)
*Jack Michael Solomon,Rishi Leburu,Matthias Chung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: vsPAIR is a variational sparse paired autoencoder for inverse problems that links a latent map from observed data to quantities of interest, enabling fast, uncertainty-aware, interpretable reconstructions with learned sparsity; it introduces a hard-concrete spike-and-slab relaxation and a beta hyperprior, and is demonstrated on blind inpainting and CT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inverse problems require uncertainty quantification and interpretability alongside accurate reconstructions. Standard VAEs may not provide structured, sparse latent factors tied to quantities of interest. A paired, sparse, variational framework can yield interpretable uncertainty by anchoring QoI representations to clean data and concentrating information into identifiable factors.

Method: The approach uses two VAEs: one encoding observations and another encoding quantities of interest, connected via a learned latent mapping. Variational training yields uncertainty estimates. Sparsity is induced through a hard-concrete spike-and-slab relaxation for differentiable training and a beta hyperprior to adapt sparsity levels. The architecture is validated on blind inpainting and computed tomography (CT).

Result: The vsPAIR architecture demonstrates capability as an inverse problem solver and provides interpretable, structured uncertainty estimates. The experiments illustrate that pairing and sparsity yield interpretable factors and uncertainty, with the proposed relaxations enabling feasible training.

Conclusion: vsPAIR offers a principled framework for fast, uncertainty-aware inverse problem solving with interpretable, sparse latent structure. The proposed training relaxations (spike-and-slab and beta hyperprior) contribute to trainability and adaptive sparsity, and the method shows promise across imaging tasks such as inpainting and CT.

Abstract: Inverse problems are fundamental to many scientific and engineering disciplines; they arise when one seeks to reconstruct hidden, underlying quantities from noisy measurements. Many applications demand not just point estimates but interpretable uncertainty. Providing fast inference alongside uncertainty estimates remains challenging yet desirable in numerous applications.
  We propose the Variational Sparse Paired Autoencoder (vsPAIR) to address this challenge. The architecture pairs a standard VAE encoding observations with a sparse VAE encoding quantities of interest, connected through a learned latent mapping. The variational structure enables uncertainty estimation, the paired architecture encourages interpretability by anchoring QoI representations to clean data, and sparse encodings provide structure by concentrating information into identifiable factors rather than diffusing across all dimensions. We also propose modifications to existing sparse VAE methods: a hard-concrete spike-and-slab relaxation for differentiable training and a beta hyperprior for adaptive sparsity levels. To validate the effectiveness of our proposed architecture, we conduct experiments on blind inpainting and computed tomography, demonstrating that vsPAIR is a capable inverse problem solver that can provide interpretable and structured uncertainty estimates.

</details>


### [267] [Quant VideoGen: Auto-Regressive Long Video Generation via 2-Bit KV-Cache Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02958)
*Haocheng Xi,Shuo Yang,Yilong Zhao,Muyang Li,Han Cai,Xingyang Li,Yujun Lin,Zhuoyang Zhang,Jintao Zhang,Xiuyu Li,Zhiying Xu,Jun Wu,Chenfeng Xu,Ion Stoica,Song Han,Kurt Keutzer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QVG reduces KV cache memory for autoregressive video diffusion by up to 7x using training-free quantization with semantic smoothing and progressive residual quantization, achieving a favorable quality–memory trade-off with marginal latency cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV cache memory bottleneck in autoregressive video diffusion impedes deployment and long-horizon consistency; need memory-efficient generation without retraining.

Method: Quant VideoGen applies Semantic Aware Smoothing to generate low-magnitude residuals and Progressive Residual Quantization (coarse-to-fine stages) for training-free KV cache quantization.

Result: On LongCat Video, HY WorldPlay, Self Forcing benchmarks, achieves Pareto improvement: up to 7× memory reduction, <4% end-to-end latency overhead, and superior generation quality vs baselines.

Conclusion: Quant VideoGen effectively alleviates KV cache bottlenecks, enabling more scalable deployment of autoregressive video diffusion with preserved or improved quality.

Abstract: Despite rapid progress in autoregressive video diffusion, an emerging system algorithm bottleneck limits both deployability and generation capability: KV cache memory. In autoregressive video generation models, the KV cache grows with generation history and quickly dominates GPU memory, often exceeding 30 GB, preventing deployment on widely available hardware. More critically, constrained KV cache budgets restrict the effective working memory, directly degrading long horizon consistency in identity, layout, and motion. To address this challenge, we present Quant VideoGen (QVG), a training free KV cache quantization framework for autoregressive video diffusion models. QVG leverages video spatiotemporal redundancy through Semantic Aware Smoothing, producing low magnitude, quantization friendly residuals. It further introduces Progressive Residual Quantization, a coarse to fine multi stage scheme that reduces quantization error while enabling a smooth quality memory trade off. Across LongCat Video, HY WorldPlay, and Self Forcing benchmarks, QVG establishes a new Pareto frontier between quality and memory efficiency, reducing KV cache memory by up to 7.0 times with less than 4% end to end latency overhead while consistently outperforming existing baselines in generation quality.

</details>


### [268] [Human-Centric Traffic Signal Control for Equity: A Multi-Agent Action Branching Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02959)
*Xiaocai Zhang,Neema Nassir,Lok Sang Chan,Milad Haghani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multi-agent, action-branching, discrete-control framework (MA2B-DDQN) for traffic signal coordination that prioritizes traveler equity by decomposing per-intersection local actions and a global duration action, with a pedestrian/vehicle/transit-aware reward; validated across seven Melbourne scenarios showing reduced impacted travelers and robust performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of vehicle-centric DRL methods and the high-dimensional discrete action space in corridor traffic signal control, while explicitly optimizing traveler-level equity.

Method: Introduce MA2B-DDQN with action-branching discrete control: (i) local per-intersection actions allocating green time between the next two phases, (ii) a single global action setting total duration of those phases. Train with a human-centric reward that penalizes delayed travelers (pedestrians, vehicles, transit). Evaluate on seven Melbourne scenarios, comparing to DRL baselines and baselines.

Result: Significant reduction in the number of impacted travelers across seven realistic scenarios; improved robustness with low variance across settings; outperforms existing DRL and baseline methods.

Conclusion: A scalable, fair traffic signal control framework that effectively coordinates multi-intersection corridors while prioritizing traveler equity, adaptable to various urban contexts.

Abstract: Coordinating traffic signals along multimodal corridors is challenging because many multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (DRL) approaches remain vehicle-centric and struggle with high-dimensional discrete action spaces. We propose MA2B-DDQN, a human-centric multi-agent action-branching double Deep Q-Network (DQN) framework that explicitly optimizes traveler-level equity. Our key contribution is an action-branching discrete control formulation that decomposes corridor control into (i) local, per-intersection actions that allocate green time between the next two phases and (ii) a single global action that selects the total duration of those phases. This decomposition enables scalable coordination under discrete control while reducing the effective complexity of joint decision-making. We also design a human-centric reward that penalizes the number of delayed individuals in the corridor, accounting for pedestrians, vehicle occupants, and transit passengers. Extensive evaluations across seven realistic traffic scenarios in Melbourne, Australia, demonstrate that our approach significantly reduces the number of impacted travelers, outperforming existing DRL and baseline methods. Experiments confirm the robustness of our model, showing minimal variance across diverse settings. This framework not only advocates for a fairer traffic signal system but also provides a scalable solution adaptable to varied urban traffic conditions.

</details>


### [269] [Q-ShiftDP: A Differentially Private Parameter-Shift Rule for Quantum Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02962)
*Hoang M. Ngo,Nhat Hoang-Xuan,Quan Nguyen,Nguyen Do,Incheol Shin,My T. Thai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Q-ShiftDP, a DP mechanism tailored to quantum machine learning that uses the parameter-shift rule to tighten gradient sensitivity; by blending calibrated Gaussian noise with intrinsic quantum noise, it achieves better privacy-utility trade-offs than classical DP methods, with empirical benchmark results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Privacy-preserving quantum machine learning is challenging: classical DP-SGD cannot exploit the unique properties of quantum gradient estimation (boundedness and stochasticity) and may yield suboptimal privacy-utility trade-offs. A mechanism tailored to quantum gradients can tighten sensitivity analyses and reduce noise requirements.

Method: Propose Differentially Private Parameter-Shift Rule (Q-ShiftDP). Leverage the parameter-shift rule for gradient estimation to establish tighter sensitivity bounds due to inherent boundedness and stochasticity of quantum gradients. Add carefully calibrated Gaussian noise and incorporate intrinsic quantum noise to achieve formal privacy guarantees and utility bounds.

Result: The approach provides formal privacy and utility guarantees and, empirically on benchmark datasets, demonstrates that Q-ShiftDP consistently outperforms classical DP methods in QML.

Conclusion: Q-ShiftDP demonstrates that tailoring DP mechanisms to quantum gradient properties yields a better privacy-utility balance in quantum machine learning. The combination of calibrated Gaussian noise and intrinsic quantum noise enhances privacy guarantees and utility, motivating further development of quantum-specific DP methods.

Abstract: Quantum Machine Learning (QML) promises significant computational advantages, but preserving training data privacy remains challenging. Classical approaches like differentially private stochastic gradient descent (DP-SGD) add noise to gradients but fail to exploit the unique properties of quantum gradient estimation. In this work, we introduce the Differentially Private Parameter-Shift Rule (Q-ShiftDP), the first privacy mechanism tailored to QML. By leveraging the inherent boundedness and stochasticity of quantum gradients computed via the parameter-shift rule, Q-ShiftDP enables tighter sensitivity analysis and reduces noise requirements. We combine carefully calibrated Gaussian noise with intrinsic quantum noise to provide formal privacy and utility guarantees, and show that harnessing quantum noise further improves the privacy-utility trade-off. Experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that Q-ShiftDP consistently outperforms classical DP methods in QML.

</details>


### [270] [Co2PO: Coordinated Constrained Policy Optimization for Multi-Agent RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02970)
*Shrenik Patel,Christine Truong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Co2PO is a communication-augmented MARL framework that uses a shared blackboard and a hazard predictor to enable proactive, risk-aware coordination for safety-constrained optimization, outperforming reactive baselines while achieving cost-compliant policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Constrained MARL must balance exploration with safety. Existing Lagrangian/centralized-critic methods react to violations and are often overly conservative, hindering exploration and performance.

Method: Introduce Co2PO with a shared blackboard for positional intent and yield signals, governed by a learned hazard predictor that forecasts violations over a long horizon. Integrate forecasts into a constrained optimization objective, enabling risk-triggered communication, adaptive gating, and shared memory for coordination.

Result: Empirical tests show higher returns than leading constrained baselines and convergence to cost-compliant policies at deployment. Ablation studies confirm the necessity of risk-triggered communication, adaptive gating, and shared memory.

Conclusion: Proactive, communication-driven coordination can resolve the exploration–safety tension in constrained MARL, delivering better performance without sacrificing safety, and the shared-memory, hazard-predictor approach is key to anticipating collective hazards.

Abstract: Constrained multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) faces a fundamental tension between exploration and safety-constrained optimization. Existing leading approaches, such as Lagrangian methods, typically rely on global penalties or centralized critics that react to violations after they occur, often suppressing exploration and leading to over-conservatism. We propose Co2PO, a novel MARL communication-augmented framework that enables coordination-driven safety through selective, risk-aware communication. Co2PO introduces a shared blackboard architecture for broadcasting positional intent and yield signals, governed by a learned hazard predictor that proactively forecasts potential violations over an extended temporal horizon. By integrating these forecasts into a constrained optimization objective, Co2PO allows agents to anticipate and navigate collective hazards without the performance trade-offs inherent in traditional reactive constraints. We evaluate Co2PO across a suite of complex multi-agent safety benchmarks, where it achieves higher returns compared to leading constrained baselines while converging to cost-compliant policies at deployment. Ablation studies further validate the necessity of risk-triggered communication, adaptive gating, and shared memory components.

</details>


### [271] [Why Some Models Resist Unlearning: A Linear Stability Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02986)
*Wei-Kai Chang,Rajiv Khanna*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper analyzes machine unlearning through asymptotic linear stability and data coherence, establishing theoretical forget/remember thresholds linked to data geometry and memorization, with two-layer ReLU CNN experiments and random matrix theory support; empirical validation aligns with predicted boundary.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a principled theoretical understanding of when and why unlearning works, connecting optimization dynamics with data geometry and memorization properties.
