Method: Develop an asymptotic linear stability framework using data coherence (cross-sample alignment of loss directions) decomposed into retain/forget/between axes; derive stability thresholds; analyze a two-layer ReLU CNN under a signal-plus-noise model; apply random matrix theory; perform empirical verification via Hessian tests, CNN heatmaps, and analysis of batching/mixing/data-model alignment.

Result: Derives tight thresholds separating convergence from divergence in unlearning, shows that lower SNR (weaker memorization) reduces coherence and facilitates forgetting, while high SNR resists unlearning; empirical measures (Hessian tests, heatmaps) agree with predicted boundary; establishes a principled link between memorization, coherence, and unlearning.

Conclusion: Provides the first principled account of the tradeoffs between memorization, coherence, and unlearning; connects data properties to forgettability and offers a framework for predicting unlearning behavior across batching, mixing, and model/data alignment.

Abstract: Machine unlearning, the ability to erase the effect of specific training samples without retraining from scratch, is critical for privacy, regulation, and efficiency. However, most progress in unlearning has been empirical, with little theoretical understanding of when and why unlearning works. We tackle this gap by framing unlearning through the lens of asymptotic linear stability to capture the interaction between optimization dynamics and data geometry. The key quantity in our analysis is data coherence which is the cross sample alignment of loss surface directions near the optimum. We decompose coherence along three axes: within the retain set, within the forget set, and between them, and prove tight stability thresholds that separate convergence from divergence. To further link data properties to forgettability, we study a two layer ReLU CNN under a signal plus noise model and show that stronger memorization makes forgetting easier: when the signal to noise ratio (SNR) is lower, cross sample alignment is weaker, reducing coherence and making unlearning easier; conversely, high SNR, highly aligned models resist unlearning. For empirical verification, we show that Hessian tests and CNN heatmaps align closely with the predicted boundary, mapping the stability frontier of gradient based unlearning as a function of batching, mixing, and data/model alignment. Our analysis is grounded in random matrix theory tools and provides the first principled account of the trade offs between memorization, coherence, and unlearning.

</details>


### [272] [NLI:Non-uniform Linear Interpolation Approximation of Nonlinear Operations for Efficient LLMs Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02988)
*Jiangyong Yu,Xiaomeng Han,Xing Hu,Chen Xu,Zhe Jiang,Dawei Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Non-uniform Linear Interpolation (NLI) provides a calibration-free, DP-optimal framework to approximate nonlinear functions (SiLU, RMSNorm, Softmax) in LLMs, enabling a hardware-friendly, plug-and-play nonlinear unit with minimal accuracy loss and ~4x throughput gains over state-of-the-art designs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs suffer from large memory footprints and high compute costs, especially in nonlinear layers (e.g., SiLU, RMSNorm, Softmax). Existing compression/acceleration focus on linear layers; a general, hardware-friendly method to approximate nonlinearities without calibration is needed to reduce cost while preserving accuracy.

Method: Recasts cutpoint selection for nonlinear function approximation as a dynamic programming problem, achieving globally minimal interpolation error in O(M·N^2) time via Bellman’s optimality principle. Develops a universal nonlinear computation unit as a plug-and-play module, with a hardware-oriented design validated by experiments.

Result: The NLI framework achieves integration into LLMs with almost no loss in accuracy. Hardware experiments show more than 4× improvement in computational efficiency compared to state-of-the-art designs.

Conclusion: NLI offers a practical, calibration-free, DP-optimal approach to approximate nonlinearities in neural networks, delivering substantial hardware efficiency gains and broad applicability to nonlinear layers across deep models.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable performance across a wide range of tasks, but their deployment is often constrained by substantial memory footprints and computational costs. While prior work has achieved significant progress in compressing and accelerating linear layers, nonlinear layers-such as SiLU, RMSNorm, and Softmax-still heavily depend on high-precision floating-point operations. In this paper, we propose a calibration-free, dynamic-programming-optimal, and hardware-friendly framework called Non-uniform Linear Interpolation (NLI). NLI is capable of efficiently approximating a variety of nonlinear functions, enabling seamless integration into LLMs and other deep neural networks with almost no loss in accuracy. NLI ingeniously recasts cutpoint selection as a dynamic-programming problem, achieving the globally minimal interpolation error in O(MxN2) time via Bellman's optimality principle. Based on the NLI algorithm, we also design and implement a plug-and-play universal nonlinear computation unit. Hardware experiments demonstrate that the NLI Engine achieves more than 4x improvement in computational efficiency compared to the state-of-the-art designs.

</details>


### [273] [SAFE-KD: Risk-Controlled Early-Exit Distillation for Vision Backbones](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03043)
*Salim Khazem*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAFE-KD is a universal multi-exit wrapper for vision backbones that uses Distillation and conformal risk control to enable safe early exits, delivering calibrated per-exit thresholds with finite-sample guarantees and improved accuracy/compute trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable efficient, reliable early-exit inference with theoretical risk guarantees across modern vision models.

Method: Attach lightweight exit heads at intermediate depths; distill a strong teacher into all exits via Decoupled Knowledge Distillation (DKD); enforce deep-to-shallow consistency among exits; calibrate per-exit stopping thresholds on a held-out set using conformal risk control to bound selective misclassification risk under exchangeability.

Result: Across multiple datasets and architectures, SAFE-KD yields better accuracy-vs-compute trade-offs, stronger calibration, and robustness under corruption, with finite-sample risk guarantees.

Conclusion: SAFE-KD provides a universal, principled framework for early exiting in vision models, combining hierarchical distillation with conformal risk control to deliver reliable, efficient inference.

Abstract: Early-exit networks reduce inference cost by allowing ``easy'' inputs to stop early, but practical deployment hinges on knowing \emph{when} early exit is safe. We introduce SAFE-KD, a universal multi-exit wrapper for modern vision backbones that couples hierarchical distillation with \emph{conformal risk control}. SAFE-KD attaches lightweight exit heads at intermediate depths, distills a strong teacher into all exits via Decoupled Knowledge Distillation (DKD), and enforces deep-to-shallow consistency between exits. At inference, we calibrate per-exit stopping thresholds on a held-out set using conformal risk control (CRC) to guarantee a user-specified \emph{selective} misclassification risk (among the samples that exit early) under exchangeability. Across multiple datasets and architectures, SAFE-KD yields improved accuracy compute trade-offs, stronger calibration, and robust performance under corruption while providing finite-sample risk guarantees.

</details>


### [274] [Learning to Repair Lean Proofs from Compiler Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02990)
*Evan Wang,Simon Chess,Daniel Lee,Siyuan Ge,Ajit Mallavarapu,Vasily Ilin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: APRIL introduces a large supervised dataset for Lean proof repair that pairs erroneous proofs with compiler feedback, corrected proofs, and natural-language explanations; a finetuned 4B-model on APRIL improves single-shot repair and surpasses open-source baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of supervision for understanding and repairing Leanproof failures due to dataset bias toward correct proofs; enable models to diagnose and fix proofs using compiler feedback.

Method: Systematically generate proof failures with Lean compiler feedback, create aligned targets for corrected proofs and natural-language explanations, assemble 260k annotated tuples (APRIL), and train/fine-tune language models; evaluate in a single-shot repair setting.

Result: Training on APRIL substantially improves repair accuracy and feedback-conditioned reasoning; a finetuned 4B-parameter model outperforms the strongest open-source baseline in single-shot repair.

Conclusion: Diagnostic-conditioned supervision complements feedback-using provers and the APRIL dataset provides a public resource for learning to diagnose and repair Lean proofs via compiler feedback.

Abstract: As neural theorem provers become increasingly agentic, the ability to interpret and act on compiler feedback is critical. However, existing Lean datasets consist almost exclusively of correct proofs, offering little supervision for understanding and repairing failures. We study Lean proof repair as a supervised learning problem: given an erroneous proof and compiler feedback, predict both a corrected proof and a natural-language diagnosis grounded in the same feedback. We introduce APRIL (Automated Proof Repair in Lean), a dataset of 260,000 supervised tuples pairing systematically generated proof failures with compiler diagnostics and aligned repair and explanation targets. Training language models on APRIL substantially improves repair accuracy and feedback-conditioned reasoning; in our single-shot repair evaluation setting, a finetuned 4B-parameter model outperforms the strongest open-source baseline. We view diagnostic-conditioned supervision as a complementary training signal for feedback-using provers. Our dataset is available at \href{https://huggingface.co/datasets/uw-math-ai/APRIL}{this link}.

</details>


### [275] [Neural Predictor-Corrector: Solving Homotopy Problems with Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03086)
*Jiayao Mai,Bangyan Liao,Zhenjun Zhao,Yingping Zeng,Haoang Li,Javier Civera,Tailin Wu,Yi Zhou,Peidong Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unified neural predictor-corrector (NPC) for homotopy-based solvers that replaces handcrafted step heuristics with learned policies via reinforcement learning and amortized offline training, achieving better efficiency and stability across unseen instances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address reliance on hand-crafted heuristics in predictor-corrector solvers across diverse homotopy problems; enable a single, generalizable framework that can learn effective stepping policies and termination criteria.

Method: Unify multiple homotopy problems under a single neural framework; design Neural Predictor-Corrector (NPC); cast policy selection as sequential decision-making; apply reinforcement learning to discover efficient strategies; implement amortized offline training for a class of problems to enable fast online inference on new instances.

Result: NPC generalizes to unseen problem instances and consistently outperforms classical and specialized baselines in efficiency while maintaining superior stability across tasks.

Conclusion: Unifying homotopy methods within a neural framework and learning policies for predictor-corrector steps offers a robust, generalizable approach for solving challenging problems across domains.

Abstract: The Homotopy paradigm, a general principle for solving challenging problems, appears across diverse domains such as robust optimization, global optimization, polynomial root-finding, and sampling. Practical solvers for these problems typically follow a predictor-corrector (PC) structure, but rely on hand-crafted heuristics for step sizes and iteration termination, which are often suboptimal and task-specific. To address this, we unify these problems under a single framework, which enables the design of a general neural solver. Building on this unified view, we propose Neural Predictor-Corrector (NPC), which replaces hand-crafted heuristics with automatically learned policies. NPC formulates policy selection as a sequential decision-making problem and leverages reinforcement learning to automatically discover efficient strategies. To further enhance generalization, we introduce an amortized training mechanism, enabling one-time offline training for a class of problems and efficient online inference on new instances. Experiments on four representative homotopy problems demonstrate that our method generalizes effectively to unseen instances. It consistently outperforms classical and specialized baselines in efficiency while demonstrating superior stability across tasks, highlighting the value of unifying homotopy methods into a single neural framework.

</details>


### [276] [Adaptive Batch Sizes Using Non-Euclidean Gradient Noise Scales for Stochastic Sign and Spectral Descent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03001)
*Hiroki Naganuma,Shagun Gupta,Youssef Briki,Ioannis Mitliagkas,Irina Rish,Parameswaran Raman,Hao-Jun Michael Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Non-Euclidean gradient-noise scales (aligned with dual norms) for signSGD and specSGD enable adaptive batch sizing that preserves validation performance while cutting training steps by up to 66% on a 160M-parameter Llama model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend the gradient-noise-scale (GNS) framework beyond Euclidean SGD to non-Euclidean optimizers (e.g., signSGD/SpecSGD) to improve hardware utilization via adaptive batch sizes without sacrificing convergence.

Method: Derive gradient-noise scales consistent with the dual-norm geometry of signSGD (l_infty) and specSGD (S_infty); develop an efficient variance-estimation procedure using local mini-batch gradients across ranks in distributed data-parallel setups; validate with adaptive batch schedules on a large model.

Result: Adaptive, non-Euclidean GNS-based batch sizing achieves validation loss comparable to constant-batch baselines while reducing training steps by up to 66% for Signum and Muon on a 160M-parameter Llama model.

Conclusion: Non-Euclidean GNS enables principled adaptive batching for non-Euclidean optimizers, preserving accuracy while improving hardware efficiency; the proposed variance estimator makes practical deployment feasible and suggests broad applicability to other norm-based optimizers.

Abstract: To maximize hardware utilization, modern machine learning systems typically employ large constant or manually tuned batch size schedules, relying on heuristics that are brittle and costly to tune. Existing adaptive strategies based on gradient noise scale (GNS) offer a principled alternative. However, their assumption of SGD's Euclidean geometry creates a fundamental mismatch with popular optimizers based on generalized norms, such as signSGD / Signum ($\ell_\infty$) and stochastic spectral descent (specSGD) / Muon ($\mathcal{S}_\infty$). In this work, we derive gradient noise scales for signSGD and specSGD that naturally emerge from the geometry of their respective dual norms. To practically estimate these non-Euclidean metrics, we propose an efficient variance estimation procedure that leverages the local mini-batch gradients on different ranks in distributed data-parallel systems. Our experiments demonstrate that adaptive batch size strategies using non-Euclidean GNS enable us to match the validation loss of constant-batch baselines while reducing training steps by up to 66% for Signum and Muon on a 160 million parameter Llama model.

</details>


### [277] [Spectral Evolution Search: Efficient Inference-Time Scaling for Reward-Aligned Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03208)
*Jinyan Ye,Zhongjie Duan,Zhiwen Li,Cen Chen,Daoyuan Chen,Yaliang Li,Yingda Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SES is a gradient-free evolutionary search over a low-frequency subspace of initial noise to align visual generative models at inference time, exploiting a spectral bias that dampens high-frequency perturbations; it yields better quality-cost trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inference-time alignment of generative models with downstream objectives traditionally relies on high-dimensional noise optimization, which is inefficient. A spectral bias suggests that model sensitivity to perturbations is stronger for low frequencies and attenuates for high frequencies.

Method: SES performs gradient-free evolutionary search confined to a low-frequency subspace of the initial noise, guided by a Spectral Scaling Prediction derived from perturbation propagation dynamics, enabling efficient optimization without updating model parameters.

Result: Empirically, SES improves the Pareto frontier for generation quality vs. computational cost and consistently outperforms strong baselines under equivalent budgets.

Conclusion: SES provides a plug-and-play, spectrally informed strategy for inference-time noise optimization that achieves higher efficiency by focusing on impactful low-frequency perturbations.

Abstract: Inference-time scaling offers a versatile paradigm for aligning visual generative models with downstream objectives without parameter updates. However, existing approaches that optimize the high-dimensional initial noise suffer from severe inefficiency, as many search directions exert negligible influence on the final generation. We show that this inefficiency is closely related to a spectral bias in generative dynamics: model sensitivity to initial perturbations diminishes rapidly as frequency increases. Building on this insight, we propose Spectral Evolution Search (SES), a plug-and-play framework for initial noise optimization that executes gradient-free evolutionary search within a low-frequency subspace. Theoretically, we derive the Spectral Scaling Prediction from perturbation propagation dynamics, which explains the systematic differences in the impact of perturbations across frequencies. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SES significantly advances the Pareto frontier of generation quality versus computational cost, consistently outperforming strong baselines under equivalent budgets.

</details>


### [278] [Causal Graph Spatial-Temporal Autoencoder for Reliable and Interpretable Process Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03004)
*Xiangrui Zhang,Chunyue Song,Wei Dai,Zheng Zhang,Kaihua Gao,Furong Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A causal graph spatial-temporal autoencoder (CGSTAE) for industrial process monitoring that fuses SSAM-based correlation learning with a three-step causal graph structure discovery, plus a GCLSTM-based encoder-decoder for time-series reconstruction, enabling effective fault detection via feature and residual statistics; validated on the Tennessee Eastman process and a real-world air separation process.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Increase reliability and interpretability of industrial process monitoring by extracting invariant causal structure from dynamic correlations and providing accurate, explainable fault detection.

Method: 1) Spatial self-attention-based correlation graph learning (SSAM) to capture dynamic relationships among variables. 2) A novel three-step causal graph structure learning algorithm (reverse-causal-invariance perspective) to derive an invariant causal graph from varying correlations. 3) A spatial-temporal encoder-decoder built with graph convolutional LSTM (GCLSTM) units operating in a sequence-to-sequence framework to reconstruct time-series data. 4) Monitoring via two statistics computed in feature space and residual space for fault detection and interpretation.

Result: The CGSTAE framework was validated on the Tennessee Eastman process and a real-world air separation process, demonstrating effective process monitoring and fault detection through the proposed statistics in both feature and residual spaces.

Conclusion: CGSTAE provides a unified, interpretable, and effective approach for industrial process monitoring by learning dynamic correlations, extracting invariant causal structure, and accurately reconstructing temporal data for reliable fault detection in real industrial settings.

Abstract: To improve the reliability and interpretability of industrial process monitoring, this article proposes a Causal Graph Spatial-Temporal Autoencoder (CGSTAE). The network architecture of CGSTAE combines two components: a correlation graph structure learning module based on spatial self-attention mechanism (SSAM) and a spatial-temporal encoder-decoder module utilizing graph convolutional long-short term memory (GCLSTM). The SSAM learns correlation graphs by capturing dynamic relationships between variables, while a novel three-step causal graph structure learning algorithm is introduced to derive a causal graph from these correlation graphs. The algorithm leverages a reverse perspective of causal invariance principle to uncover the invariant causal graph from varying correlations. The spatial-temporal encoder-decoder, built with GCLSTM units, reconstructs time-series process data within a sequence-to-sequence framework. The proposed CGSTAE enables effective process monitoring and fault detection through two statistics in the feature space and residual space. Finally, we validate the effectiveness of CGSTAE in process monitoring through the Tennessee Eastman process and a real-world air separation process.

</details>


### [279] [R1-SyntheticVL: Is Synthetic Data from Generative Models Ready for Multimodal Large Language Model?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03300)
*Jingyi Zhang,Tianyi Lin,Huanjin Yao,Xiang Lan,Shunyu Liu,Jiaxing Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CADS introduces Collective Adversarial Data Synthesis for multimodal LLMs, using CAD-Generate and CAD-Judge with Adversarial Context Optimization to autonomously produce MMSynthetic-20K data and train R1-SyntheticVL, achieving superior benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data scarcity and quality for training multimodal LLMs on real-world tasks by generating high-quality, diverse, and challenging multimodal data via collective intelligence and adversarial optimization.

Method: Two cyclic phases CAD-Generate (collective generation of diverse data) and CAD-Judge (quality assessment). Adversarial Context Optimization to craft challenging contexts. Construction of MMSynthetic-20K dataset and training of R1-SyntheticVL.

Result: Model achieves superior performance on multiple benchmarks, demonstrating the effectiveness of autonomous data synthesis for MLLMs.

Conclusion: CADS provides a general, autonomous data-synthesis framework for improving multimodal learning, with potential applicability beyond the MMSynthetic-20K setting.

Abstract: In this work, we aim to develop effective data synthesis techniques that autonomously synthesize multimodal training data for enhancing MLLMs in solving complex real-world tasks. To this end, we propose Collective Adversarial Data Synthesis (CADS), a novel and general approach to synthesize high-quality, diverse and challenging multimodal data for MLLMs. The core idea of CADS is to leverage collective intelligence to ensure high-quality and diverse generation, while exploring adversarial learning to synthesize challenging samples for effectively driving model improvement. Specifically, CADS operates with two cyclic phases, i.e., Collective Adversarial Data Generation (CAD-Generate) and Collective Adversarial Data Judgment (CAD-Judge). CAD-Generate leverages collective knowledge to jointly generate new and diverse multimodal data, while CAD-Judge collaboratively assesses the quality of synthesized data. In addition, CADS introduces an Adversarial Context Optimization mechanism to optimize the generation context to encourage challenging and high-value data generation. With CADS, we construct MMSynthetic-20K and train our model R1-SyntheticVL, which demonstrates superior performance on various benchmarks.

</details>


### [280] [From Zero to Hero: Advancing Zero-Shot Foundation Models for Tabular Outlier Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03018)
*Xueying Ding,Haomin Wen,Simon Klütterman,Leman Akoglu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces OUTFORMER, a foundation-model-based outlier detector trained on synthetic labeled data, enabling zero-shot, in-context inference for new tasks and achieving state-of-the-art on AdBench and two new large OD benchmarks (~1.5k datasets).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the lack of labeled outliers and the difficulty of hyperparameter tuning in outlier detection by leveraging foundation models and synthetic priors to enable plug-and-play zero-shot deployment.

Method: Pretrain on synthetic labeled datasets using a mixture of synthetic priors and self-evolving curriculum training. During inference, the model uses the new task's training data as in-context input to predict test labels without requiring labeled outliers.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on AdBench and two newly introduced large-scale OD benchmarks, while maintaining fast inference across >1,500 datasets.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effective in-context learning for outlier detection, enabling zero-shot deployment and reducing task-specific model selection, with strong initial scalability evidence; requires further validation on diverse real-world distributions and robustness analyses.

Abstract: Outlier detection (OD) is widely used in practice; but its effective deployment on new tasks is hindered by lack of labeled outliers, which makes algorithm and hyperparameter selection notoriously hard. Foundation models (FMs) have transformed ML, and OD is no exception: Shen et. al. (2025) introduced FoMo-0D, the first FM for OD, achieving remarkable performance against numerous baselines. This work introduces OUTFORMER, which advances FoMo-0D with (1) a mixture of synthetic priors and (2) self-evolving curriculum training. OUTFORMER is pretrained solely on synthetic labeled datasets and infers test labels of a new task by using its training data as in-context input. Inference is fast and zero-shot, requiring merely forward pass and no labeled outliers. Thanks to in-context learning, it requires zero additional work-no OD model training or bespoke model selection-enabling truly plug-and-play deployment. OUTFORMER achieves state-of-the-art performance on the prominent AdBench, as well as two new large-scale OD benchmarks that we introduce, comprising over 1,500 datasets, while maintaining speedy inference.

</details>


### [281] [FedKRSO: Communication and Memory Efficient Federated Fine-Tuning of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03019)
*Guohao Yang,Tongle Wu,Yuanxiong Guo,Ying Sun,Yanmin Gong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedKRSO enables communication- and memory-efficient federated fine-tuning of LLMs by restricting updates to a server-generated set of random low-dimensional subspaces and transmitting only update accumulators, achieving near full-FL performance with reduced overhead; supported by convergence analysis and GLUE experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated learning for LLM fine-tuning faces high communication costs and resource constraints. While PEFT reduces overhead, it often sacrifices performance relative to full fine-tuning. There is a need for methods that approach full transfer performance in FL while staying efficient.

Method: Introduce FedKRSO: Clients perform updates only within a shared random low-dimension subspace set generated by the server. Instead of sending full parameter updates, clients transmit update accumulators projected onto these subspaces. The server aggregates these accumulators to update the global model and disseminates updates to clients. The paper provides rigorous convergence analysis under general FL settings and validates performance on GLUE across various FL scenarios.

Result: Empirical results show FedKRSO achieves superior performance with low communication and memory overhead, closely matching federated full fine-tuning (FFT) performance on the GLUE benchmark, across diverse FL setups.

Conclusion: FedKRSO makes federated fine-tuning of LLMs practical on resource-constrained devices by attaining near-FFT accuracy with substantially reduced communication and memory costs, supported by convergence theory and GLUE-based experiments.

Abstract: Fine-tuning is essential to adapt general-purpose large language models (LLMs) to domain-specific tasks. As a privacy-preserving framework to leverage decentralized data for collaborative model training, Federated Learning (FL) is gaining popularity in LLM fine-tuning, but remains challenging due to the high cost of transmitting full model parameters and computing full gradients on resource-constrained clients. While Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods are widely used in FL to reduce communication and memory costs, they often sacrifice model performance compared to FFT. This paper proposes FedKRSO (Federated $K$-Seed Random Subspace Optimization), a novel method that enables communication and memory efficient FFT of LLMs in federated settings. In FedKRSO, clients update the model within a shared set of random low-dimension subspaces generated by the server to save memory usage. Furthermore, instead of transmitting full model parameters in each FL round, clients send only the model update accumulators along the subspaces to the server, enabling efficient global model aggregation and dissemination. By using these strategies, FedKRSO can substantially reduce communication and memory overhead while overcoming the performance limitations of PEFT, closely approximating the performance of federated FFT. The convergence properties of FedKRSO are analyzed rigorously under general FL settings. Extensive experiments on the GLUE benchmark across diverse FL scenarios demonstrate that FedKRSO achieves both superior performance and low communication and memory overhead, paving the way towards on federated LLM fine-tuning at the resource-constrained edge.

</details>


### [282] [Consistency Deep Equilibrium Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03024)
*Junchao Lin,Zenan Ling,Jingwen Xu,Robert C. Qiu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Consistency Deep Equilibrium Models (C-DEQ) accelerate DEQ inference by consistency distillation: train intermediate states along an ODE trajectory to map directly to the fixed point, enabling few-step, flexible inference while maintaining teacher performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DEQs enable infinite-depth modeling with constant memory but incur high inference latency due to iterative fixed-point solvers; reducing iterations without sacrificing accuracy is desirable for practical use.

Method: Model the DEQ fixed-point iteration as evolution along a fixed ODE trajectory. Train C-DEQs to consistently map intermediate states to the fixed point (the teacher), via distillation, allowing few-step inference and multi-step evaluation with performance-compute trade-offs.

Result: Empirically, C-DEQs achieve consistent 2-20x accuracy improvements over implicit DEQs under the same few-step inference budget across diverse tasks.

Conclusion: C-DEQ offers a flexible, memory-efficient DEQ acceleration approach, enabling fast inference with accuracy close to the teacher and adjustable compute through multi-step evaluation.

Abstract: Deep Equilibrium Models (DEQs) have emerged as a powerful paradigm in deep learning, offering the ability to model infinite-depth networks with constant memory usage. However, DEQs incur significant inference latency due to the iterative nature of fixed-point solvers. In this work, we introduce the Consistency Deep Equilibrium Model (C-DEQ), a novel framework that leverages consistency distillation to accelerate DEQ inference. We cast the DEQ iterative inference process as evolution along a fixed ODE trajectory toward the equilibrium. Along this trajectory, we train C-DEQs to consistently map intermediate states directly to the fixed point, enabling few-step inference while preserving the performance of the teacher DEQ. At the same time, it facilitates multi-step evaluation to flexibly trade computation for performance gains. Extensive experiments across various domain tasks demonstrate that C-DEQs achieves consistent 2-20$\times$ accuracy improvements over implicit DEQs under the same few-step inference budget.

</details>


### [283] [Scaling Continual Learning with Bi-Level Routing Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03473)
*Meng Lou,Yunxiang Fu,Yizhou Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CaRE introduces a scalable continual learner with BR-MoE, enabling effective long-sequence CIL via bi-level routing that activates task-specific routers and experts across layers, plus a challenging long-task evaluation protocol; claims state-of-the-art on sequences up to 100–300 tasks; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address stability-plasticity and representation learning challenges in class-incremental learning over hundreds of tasks, where existing methods degrade in long sequences and lack scalable routing.

Method: Bi-Level Routing Mixture-of-Experts (BR-MoE) with two-stage routing: (1) router selection identifies relevant task-specific routers; (2) expert routing activates/aggregates experts to inject discriminative and comprehensive representations into intermediate layers; operates within a pre-trained model; supports long task sequences; includes new evaluation protocol.

Result: CaRE achieves leading performance across datasets and CIL settings, especially in long sequences (100–300 tasks), outperforming baselines by a large margin.

Conclusion: CaRE is a pioneering scalable CIL approach, establishing the feasibility of long-sequence continual learning with BR-MoE; code will be released.

Abstract: Continual learning, especially class-incremental learning (CIL), on the basis of a pre-trained model (PTM) has garnered substantial research interest in recent years. However, how to effectively learn both discriminative and comprehensive feature representations while maintaining stability and plasticity over very long task sequences remains an open problem. We propose CaRE, a scalable {C}ontinual Le{a}rner with efficient Bi-Level {R}outing Mixture-of-{E}xperts (BR-MoE). The core idea of BR-MoE is a bi-level routing mechanism: a router selection stage that dynamically activates relevant task-specific routers, followed by an expert routing phase that dynamically activates and aggregates experts, aiming to inject discriminative and comprehensive representations into every intermediate network layer. On the other hand, we introduce a challenging evaluation protocol for comprehensively assessing CIL methods across very long task sequences spanning hundreds of tasks. Extensive experiments show that CaRE demonstrates leading performance across a variety of datasets and task settings, including commonly used CIL datasets with classical CIL settings (e.g., 5-20 tasks). To the best of our knowledge, CaRE is the first continual learner that scales to very long task sequences (ranging from 100 to over 300 non-overlapping tasks), while outperforming all baselines by a large margin on such task sequences. Code will be publicly released at https://github.com/LMMMEng/CaRE.git.

</details>


### [284] [Clarify Before You Draw: Proactive Agents for Robust Text-to-CAD Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03045)
*Bo Yuan,Zelin Zhao,Petr Molodyk,Bin Hu,Yongxin Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ProCAD introduces a proactive, agentic framework for text-to-CadQuery that uses a clarifying agent to resolve under-specified prompts before code generation, improving robustness and reducing invalid outputs with low interaction overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-to-CAD prompts are often under-specified or inconsistent, causing missing dimensions and conflicts; existing models often hallucinate when prompts are ambiguous.

Method: A two-agent system: a clarifying agent audits prompts and asks targeted clarifications to yield a self-consistent spec, and a CAD coding agent translates the spec into CadQuery code. The coding agent is fine-tuned on a curated text-to-CadQuery dataset; the clarifying agent is trained with agentic SFT on clarification trajectories.

Result: Proactive clarification markedly improves robustness to ambiguous prompts, with a large performance gain over frontier models (e.g., mean Chamfer distance ↓79.9%, invalidity ratio ↓ from 4.8% to 0.9%). Public release of code and datasets.

Conclusion: Proactively resolving specification issues before synthesis enhances text-to-CAD reliability while keeping interaction overhead low, indicating the value of agentic clarification for structured code generation and potential applicability to other domains.

Abstract: Large language models have recently enabled text-to-CAD systems that synthesize parametric CAD programs (e.g., CadQuery) from natural language prompts. In practice, however, geometric descriptions can be under-specified or internally inconsistent: critical dimensions may be missing and constraints may conflict. Existing fine-tuned models tend to reactively follow user instructions and hallucinate dimensions when the text is ambiguous. To address this, we propose a proactive agentic framework for text-to-CadQuery generation, named ProCAD, that resolves specification issues before code synthesis. Our framework pairs a proactive clarifying agent, which audits the prompt and asks targeted clarification questions only when necessary to produce a self-consistent specification, with a CAD coding agent that translates the specification into an executable CadQuery program. We fine-tune the coding agent on a curated high-quality text-to-CadQuery dataset and train the clarifying agent via agentic SFT on clarification trajectories. Experiments show that proactive clarification significantly improves robustness to ambiguous prompts while keeping interaction overhead low. ProCAD outperforms frontier closed-source models, including Claude Sonnet 4.5, reducing the mean Chamfer distance by 79.9 percent and lowering the invalidity ratio from 4.8 percent to 0.9 percent. Our code and datasets will be made publicly available.

</details>


### [285] [Robust Representation Learning in Masked Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03531)
*Anika Shrivastava,Renu Rameshan,Samar Agnihotri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MAE pretraining yields robust, class-aware representations; depth increases class-separability; MAEs maintain early, global attention; two metrics quantify robustness under degradations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explain why MAEs achieve strong downstream classification and robustness, by analyzing representation structure and attention patterns across layers.

Method: Perform layer-wise analysis of token embeddings during pretraining/fine-tuning, compare with ViTs, examine class-subspace organization; define and compute two sensitivity indicators: directional alignment between clean and perturbed embeddings, and head-wise retention of active features under degradations; evaluate under blur/occlusion.

Result: Embeddings from different classes lie in subspaces that become increasingly separable with depth; MAE exhibits early and persistent global attention across encoder layers; the two indicators quantify robustness and correlate with robust performance.

Conclusion: MAE fosters robust, class-aware latent representations with characteristic attention dynamics; the proposed metrics provide quantitative insight into robustness and can guide model design.

Abstract: Masked Autoencoders (MAEs) achieve impressive performance in image classification tasks, yet the internal representations they learn remain less understood. This work started as an attempt to understand the strong downstream classification performance of MAE. In this process we discover that representations learned with the pretraining and fine-tuning, are quite robust - demonstrating a good classification performance in the presence of degradations, such as blur and occlusions. Through layer-wise analysis of token embeddings, we show that pretrained MAE progressively constructs its latent space in a class-aware manner across network depth: embeddings from different classes lie in subspaces that become increasingly separable. We further observe that MAE exhibits early and persistent global attention across encoder layers, in contrast to standard Vision Transformers (ViTs). To quantify feature robustness, we introduce two sensitivity indicators: directional alignment between clean and perturbed embeddings, and head-wise retention of active features under degradations. These studies help establish the robust classification performance of MAEs.

</details>


### [286] [CoBA-RL: Capability-Oriented Budget Allocation for Reinforcement Learning in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03048)
*Zhiyuan Yao,Yi-Kai Zhang,Yuxin Chen,Yueqing Sun,Zishan Xu,Yu Yang,Tianhao Hu,Qi Gu,Hui Su,Xunliang Cai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive rollout budgeting for RL-based LLM training using a Capability-Oriented Value function and heap-based scheduling to allocate compute to high-value samples, improving generalization while reducing compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL for LLM reasoning often uses uniform rollout budgets (e.g., GRPO), leading to inefficiency. Existing adaptive methods rely on instance-level metrics (e.g., pass rates) and fail to capture the model's dynamic learning state.

Method: Introduce CoBA-RL. It defines a Capability-Oriented Value function that maps tasks to potential training gains and uses a heap-based greedy strategy to allocate and calibrate the rollout budget toward samples with high training value, adapting as the model evolves.

Result: Empirical results show improved exploration-exploitation balance and consistent generalization gains across multiple challenging benchmarks, indicating effective post-training efficiency and robustness to budget changes.

Conclusion: Quantifying sample training value and optimizing budget allocation are pivotal for advancing LLM post-training efficiency.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a key approach for enhancing LLM reasoning. However, standard frameworks like Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) typically employ a uniform rollout budget, leading to resource inefficiency. Moreover, existing adaptive methods often rely on instance-level metrics, such as task pass rates, failing to capture the model's dynamic learning state. To address these limitations, we propose CoBA-RL, a reinforcement learning algorithm designed to adaptively allocate rollout budgets based on the model's evolving capability. Specifically, CoBA-RL utilizes a Capability-Oriented Value function to map tasks to their potential training gains and employs a heap-based greedy strategy to efficiently self-calibrate the distribution of computational resources to samples with high training value. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach effectively orchestrates the trade-off between exploration and exploitation, delivering consistent generalization improvements across multiple challenging benchmarks. These findings underscore that quantifying sample training value and optimizing budget allocation are pivotal for advancing LLM post-training efficiency.

</details>


### [287] [Fedcompass: Federated Clustered and Periodic Aggregation Framework for Hybrid Classical-Quantum Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03052)
*Yueheng Wang,Xing He,Zinuo Cai,Rui Zhang,Ruhui Ma,Yuan Liu,Rajkumar Buyya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FEDCOMPASS improves performance and stability of hybrid classical-quantum federated learning under non-IID data by using layered aggregation: spectral clustering groups clients by class distribution for cluster-wise classical feature extractor aggregation, and circular mean aggregation with adaptive optimization stabilizes quantum parameter updates; evaluated on three benchmarks with up to 10.22% accuracy gain and better convergence vs six baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-IID data widely degrades performance in hybrid classical-quantum federated learning; there is a need for robust, scalable aggregation that preserves utility of both classical feature extractors and quantum parameters.

Method: 1) Layered aggregation framework. 2) Spectral clustering to group clients by class distribution similarity for cluster-wise aggregation of classical feature extractors. 3) For quantum parameters, apply circular mean aggregation coupled with adaptive optimization to ensure stable global updates.

Result: Experiments on three benchmark datasets show up to 10.22 percentage point improvement in test accuracy and improved convergence stability under non-IID settings, outperforming six strong federated learning baselines.

Conclusion: FEDCOMPASS effectively mitigates non-IID challenges in hybrid classical-quantum federated learning, yielding higher accuracy and more stable convergence across datasets.

Abstract: Federated learning enables collaborative model training across decentralized clients under privacy constraints. Quantum computing offers potential for alleviating computational and communication burdens in federated learning, yet hybrid classical-quantum federated learning remains susceptible to performance degradation under non-IID data. To address this,we propose FEDCOMPASS, a layered aggregation framework for hybrid classical-quantum federated learning. FEDCOMPASS employs spectral clustering to group clients by class distribution similarity and performs cluster-wise aggregation for classical feature extractors. For quantum parameters, it uses circular mean aggregation combined with adaptive optimization to ensure stable global updates. Experiments on three benchmark datasets show that FEDCOMPASS improves test accuracy by up to 10.22% and enhances convergence stability under non-IID settings, outperforming six strong federated learning baselines.

</details>


### [288] [Shortcut Features as Top Eigenfunctions of NTK: A Linear Neural Network Case and More](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03066)
*Jinwoo Lim,Suhyun Kim,Soo-Mook Moon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NTK-based analysis shows shortcut learning in linear nets arises from spectral properties: shortcut features are NTK eigenfunctions with large eigenvalues under imbalanced cluster data; these features dominate outputs and persist under training, even with margin control; findings extend empirically to simple deep nets (two-layer ReLU, ResNet-18).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain why neural networks latch onto non-generalizable, spurious features (shortcuts) when training data are imbalanced or clustered, by leveraging the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) framework to analyze feature selection in the kernel regime.

Method: Analyze NTK eigenfunctions for linear networks to identify when a feature becomes a shortcut. Show that features with large NTK eigenvalues (under imbalanced clusters) dominate learning and remain influential due to cluster variance. Examine margin constraints to assess whether max-margin bias is the sole driver. Extend the analysis empirically to a two-layer fully-connected ReLU network and ResNet-18.

Result: Shortcut features correspond to NTK eigenfunctions with large eigenvalues under data imbalance; such features dominate the learned function and retain influence due to intra-cluster variance, even after training and margin control. The max-margin bias is not the only mechanism for shortcut learning. The phenomena are empirically observed to extend to modestly more complex architectures (two-layer ReLU network and ResNet-18).

Conclusion: The NTK spectral properties of the data distribution underpin shortcut learning: high-eigenvalue NTK directions aligned with imbalanced/clustered features steer learning and persist despite training. Addressing shortcuts may require shaping the NTK spectrum or data distribution rather than relying solely on margin-based regularization. Empirical support extends beyond linear nets to certain shallow/deeper architectures.

Abstract: One of the chronic problems of deep-learning models is shortcut learning. In a case where the majority of training data are dominated by a certain feature, neural networks prefer to learn such a feature even if the feature is not generalizable outside the training set. Based on the framework of Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK), we analyzed the case of linear neural networks to derive some important properties of shortcut learning. We defined a feature of a neural network as an eigenfunction of NTK. Then, we found that shortcut features correspond to features with larger eigenvalues when the shortcuts stem from the imbalanced number of samples in the clustered distribution. We also showed that the features with larger eigenvalues still have a large influence on the neural network output even after training, due to data variances in the clusters. Such a preference for certain features remains even when a margin of a neural network output is controlled, which shows that the max-margin bias is not the only major reason for shortcut learning. These properties of linear neural networks are empirically extended for more complex neural networks as a two-layer fully-connected ReLU network and a ResNet-18.

</details>


### [289] [FlashSinkhorn: IO-Aware Entropic Optimal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03067)
*Felix X. -F. Ye,Xingjie Li,An Yu,Ming-Ching Chang,Linsong Chu,Davis Wertheimer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlashSinkhorn: an IO-aware entropic OT solver that rewrites stabilized log-Sinkhorn updates as row-wise LogSumExp on biased dot-product scores, enabling fused, streaming Triton kernels with on-chip SRAM. Achieves large speedups and linear-memory behavior for EOT, with open-source release.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome high memory traffic and limited kernel fusion in existing OT solvers (dense tensorization with quadratic IO, or online backends with tiled maps) and enable scalable, fast EOT on GPUs for large point-cloud or OT-based tasks.

Method: Rewrite stabilized log-domain Sinkhorn updates as row-wise LogSumExp reductions over biased dot-product scores; implement fused Triton kernels with on-chip SRAM tiling (FlashAttention-style fusion) to stream tiles and update dual potentials in a single pass; provide streaming kernels for transport enabling first- and second-order optimization.

Result: On A100 GPUs, up to 32x forward-pass and 161x end-to-end speedups over state-of-the-art online baselines on point-cloud OT; improved scalability for OT-based downstream tasks; publicly released open-source implementation.

Conclusion: FlashSinkhorn delivers an IO-efficient, fused, streaming EOT solver for squared Euclidean cost, reducing HBM IO while preserving linear memory, enabling scalable optimization and practical deployment; accompanied by open-source code.

Abstract: Entropic optimal transport (EOT) via Sinkhorn iterations is widely used in modern machine learning, yet GPU solvers remain inefficient at scale. Tensorized implementations suffer quadratic HBM traffic from dense $n\times m$ interactions, while existing online backends avoid storing dense matrices but still rely on generic tiled map-reduce reduction kernels with limited fusion. We present \textbf{FlashSinkhorn}, an IO-aware EOT solver for squared Euclidean cost that rewrites stabilized log-domain Sinkhorn updates as row-wise LogSumExp reductions of biased dot-product scores, the same normalization as transformer attention. This enables FlashAttention-style fusion and tiling: fused Triton kernels stream tiles through on-chip SRAM and update dual potentials in a single pass, substantially reducing HBM IO per iteration while retaining linear-memory operations. We further provide streaming kernels for transport application, enabling scalable first- and second-order optimization. On A100 GPUs, FlashSinkhorn achieves up to $32\times$ forward-pass and $161\times$ end-to-end speedups over state-of-the-art online baselines on point-cloud OT, improves scalability on OT-based downstream tasks. For reproducibility, we release an open-source implementation at https://github.com/ot-triton-lab/ot_triton.

</details>


### [290] [Geometry-Preserving Neural Architectures on Manifolds with Boundary](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03082)
*Karthik Elamvazhuthi,Shiba Biswal,Kian Rosenblum,Arushi Katyal,Tianli Qu,Grady Ma,Rishi Sonthalia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Geometry-aware neural networks that interleave manifold-geometric updates across layers; universal approximation for constrained neural ODEs; learnable projections via heat-kernel limits; validated on S^2, SO(3), and S^{d-1} manifolds with analytic and learned projections.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Preserve geometric structure in learning for manifold-valued data and constrained dynamics; unify projection-based and exponential-map updates as discretizations of projected dynamical systems on manifolds.

Method: Propose a unified class of architectures that interleave projection layers with intrinsic exponential-map updates (discretizations of projected dynamical systems on manifolds). Prove universal approximation for constrained neural ODEs and analyze architectures enforcing geometry only at the output. When the constraint set is unknown, learn projections via small-time heat-kernel limits (diffusion/flow-matching as data-based projections). Experimental validation on dynamics over S^2 and SO(3), and diffusion on S^{d-1}-valued features.

Result: Universal approximation results for constrained neural ODEs; a separate universal property for output-only geometry enforcement; data-driven projections via heat-kernel limits enable effective diffusion/flow-matching; exact feasibility for analytic geometric updates; strong performance for learned projections.

Conclusion: Geometry-aware architectures can preserve manifold constraints across layers, with both interleaved and output-only designs endowed with universal approximation properties. Learning projections via heat-kernel limits offers a practical, data-based approach, validated on manifold-valued tasks (S^2, SO(3), S^{d-1}).

Abstract: Preserving geometric structure is important in learning. We propose a unified class of geometry-aware architectures that interleave geometric updates between layers, where both projection layers and intrinsic exponential map updates arise as discretizations of projected dynamical systems on manifolds (with or without boundary). Within this framework, we establish universal approximation results for constrained neural ODEs. We also analyze architectures that enforce geometry only at the output, proving a separate universal approximation property that enables direct comparison to interleaved designs. When the constraint set is unknown, we learn projections via small-time heat-kernel limits, showing diffusion/flow-matching can be used as data-based projections. Experiments on dynamics over S^2 and SO(3), and diffusion on S^{d-1}-valued features demonstrate exact feasibility for analytic updates and strong performance for learned projections

</details>


### [291] [TextME: Bridging Unseen Modalities Through Text Descriptions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03098)
*Soyeon Hong,Jinchan Kim,Jaegook You,Seungtaek Choi,Suha Kwak,Hyunsouk Cho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TextME enables expanding modalities using only text descriptions by projecting diverse modalities into a shared LLM embedding space, enabling zero-shot cross-modal transfer and cross-modal retrieval without paired data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Paired multi-modal datasets are costly or infeasible in many domains; there is a need for modality expansion without supervision to leverage existing encoders across modalities.

Method: Project diverse modalities into a unified anchor in LLM embedding space using TextME; exploit the geometric structure of pretrained contrastive encoders to enable zero-shot transfer with only text descriptions and no paired supervision; demonstrate cross-modal retrieval between modality pairs not explicitly aligned.

Result: Empirical evidence that modality gaps are consistent across image, video, audio, 3D, X-ray, and molecular domains; text-only training preserves substantial performance of pretrained encoders; emergent cross-modal retrieval (e.g., audio-to-image, 3D-to-image) without explicit pairing during training.

Conclusion: Text-only training is a practical and scalable alternative to paired supervision for modality expansion, enabling cross-modal transfer and retrieval across diverse modalities.

Abstract: Expanding multimodal representations to novel modalities is constrained by reliance on large-scale paired datasets (e.g., text-image, text-audio, text-3D, text-molecule), which are costly and often infeasible in domains requiring expert annotation such as medical imaging and molecular analysis. We introduce TextME, the first text-only modality expansion framework, to the best of our knowledge, projecting diverse modalities into LLM embedding space as a unified anchor. Our approach exploits the geometric structure of pretrained contrastive encoders to enable zero-shot cross-modal transfer using only text descriptions, without paired supervision. We empirically validate that such consistent modality gaps exist across image, video, audio, 3D, X-ray, and molecular domains, demonstrating that text-only training can preserve substantial performance of pretrained encoders. We further show that our framework enables emergent cross-modal retrieval between modality pairs not explicitly aligned during training (e.g., audio-to-image, 3D-to-image). These results establish text-only training as a practical alternative to paired supervision for modality expansion.

</details>


### [292] [Consensus Group Relative Policy Optimization for Text Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03102)
*Yuki Ichihara,Yuu Jinnai,Kaito Ariu,Eiji Uchibe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: C-GRPO distills Minimum Bayes Risk (MBR) decoding into training by framing the consensus utility as a group-relative objective within GRPO, enabling MBR-like performance without gold references or explicit preference data, thereby amortizing inference-time costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Strong decoding methods rely on sample-and-rerank with consensus-based utilities, which is computationally expensive at inference. Prior amortization approaches depend on gold labels or curated preferences. The work seeks to remove this data bottleneck while preserving MBR-like gains.

Method: Introduce Consensus Group Relative Policy Optimization (C-GRPO): a group-relative objective within GRPO that distills MBR decoding into training. It uses only a utility function and policy samples, avoiding gold references or explicit preference labels.

Result: Under ideal conditions, the objective is directionally aligned with the gradient of the expected-utility objective behind MBR decoding, providing a convergence guarantee. Empirical results on WMT 2024 machine translation and XSum summarization show C-GRPO achieves performance comparable to MBR decoding without inference-time overhead and outperforms reference-free baselines.

Conclusion: C-GRPO offers an effective, data-light way to approximate MBR decoding during training, enabling similar decoding quality with lower inference cost. It demonstrates promise across MT and summarization benchmarks, with potential applicability to other text-generation tasks.

Abstract: Many strong decoding methods for text generation follow a sample-and-rerank paradigm: they draw multiple candidates, score each under a utility (reward) function using consensus across samples, and return the best one. Although effective, these methods incur high computational costs during inference due to repeated sampling and scoring. Prior attempts to amortize inference-time computation typically rely on gold references, teacher labels, or curated preference data, increasing dataset construction effort and the demand for high-fidelity reward models. We propose Consensus Group Relative Policy Optimization (C-GRPO), which distills Minimum Bayes Risk (MBR) decoding into training by formulating the consensus utility as a group-relative objective within GRPO. C-GRPO requires only a utility function and policy samples, without gold references or explicit preference labels. Under ideal conditions, we show that the objective function of C-GRPO is directionally aligned with the gradient of the expected-utility objective underlying MBR decoding, leading to a convergence guarantee. Experiments on machine translation (WMT 2024) and text summarization (XSum) demonstrate that C-GRPO successfully achieves performance comparable to MBR decoding without the associated inference-time overhead, while outperforming reference-free baseline methods.

</details>


### [293] [Function-Space Empirical Bayes Regularisation with Large Vision-Language Model Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03119)
*Pengcheng Hao,Huaze Tang,Ercan Engin Kuruoglu,Wenbo Ding*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes VLM-FS-EB, a function-space empirical Bayes framework that uses vision-language models to generate semantically meaningful context points and embeddings, yielding expressive priors in function space for Bayesian deep learning; shows improved predictive performance and uncertainty, especially for OOD and data-scarce settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GP priors used in many function-space Bayesian methods are often insufficiently expressive for high-dimensional neural networks. There is a need for more expressive, semantically informed priors in function space. Leveraging large vision-language models can supply meaningful context and embeddings to construct such priors.

Method: Introduce VLM-FS-EB. Generate synthetic context points via large vision-language models; extract embeddings from these points using the same or related VLMs; build expressive functional priors in function space; apply an empirical Bayes regularisation framework during training and evaluation; compare against standard baselines across predictive accuracy and uncertainty metrics.

Result: Experimental results show consistent improvements in predictive performance and more reliable uncertainty estimates, with strong gains in out-of-distribution detection and in data-scarce regimes compared to baselines.

Conclusion: VLM-FS-EB demonstrates that function-space priors informed by semantic context from vision-language models can substantially improve Bayesian deep learning, particularly for OOD robustness and data-scarce settings, without sacrificing calibration or scalability.

Abstract: Bayesian deep learning (BDL) provides a principled framework for reliable uncertainty quantification by combining deep neural networks with Bayesian inference. A central challenge in BDL lies in the design of informative prior distributions that scale effectively to high-dimensional data. Recent functional variational inference (VI) approaches address this issue by imposing priors directly in function space; however, most existing methods rely on Gaussian process (GP) priors, whose expressiveness and generalisation capabilities become limited in high-dimensional regimes. In this work, we propose VLM-FS-EB, a novel function-space empirical Bayes regularisation framework, leveraging large vision-language models (VLMs) to generates semantically meaningful context points. These synthetic samples are then used VLMs for embeddings to construct expressive functional priors. Furthermore, the proposed method is evaluated against various baselines, and experimental results demonstrate that our method consistently improves predictive performance and yields more reliable uncertainty estimates, particularly in out-of-distribution (OOD) detection tasks and data-scarce regimes.

</details>


### [294] [Quantized Evolution Strategies: High-precision Fine-tuning of Quantized LLMs at Low-precision Cost](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03120)
*Yinggan Xu,Risto Miikkulainen,Xin Qiu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QES enables direct fine-tuning of quantized LLMs in the quantized parameter space using full-parameter ES with accumulated error feedback and seed replay, outperforming zeroth-order methods on arithmetic tasks and enabling fully quantized models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PTQ reduces memory/compute but fixes weights, hindering fine-tuning. Backprop-based RL is incompatible with quantized, non-differentiable parameters; ES offers a gradient-free alternative but can suffer from vanishing or noisy gradients. There is a need for a robust, low-memory method to fine-tune quantized models directly.

Method: Propose Quantized Evolution Strategies (QES) that operates in quantized space. Two key innovations: (1) accumulated error feedback to preserve informative gradient signals despite quantization, and (2) stateless seed replay to minimize memory usage, making it comparable to low-precision inference. Evaluation against state-of-the-art zeroth-order fine-tuning on arithmetic reasoning tasks; code released.

Result: QES significantly outperforms the leading zeroth-order fine-tuning method on arithmetic reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: Direct fine-tuning in quantized space is feasible with QES, enabling scaling of LLMs entirely in the quantized domain; public code available for replication.

Abstract: Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) is essential for deploying Large Language Models (LLMs) on memory-constrained devices, yet it renders models static and difficult to fine-tune. Standard fine-tuning paradigms, including Reinforcement Learning (RL), fundamentally rely on backpropagation and high-precision weights to compute gradients. Thus they cannot be used on quantized models, where the parameter space is discrete and non-differentiable. While Evolution Strategies (ES) offer a backpropagation-free alternative, optimization of the quantized parameters can still fail due to vanishing or inaccurate gradient. This paper introduces Quantized Evolution Strategies (QES), an optimization paradigm that performs full-parameter fine-tuning directly in the quantized space. QES is based on two innovations: (1) it integrates accumulated error feedback to preserve high-precision gradient signals, and (2) it utilizes a stateless seed replay to reduce memory usage to low-precision inference levels. QES significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art zeroth-order fine-tuning method on arithmetic reasoning tasks, making direct fine-tuning for quantized models possible. It therefore opens up the possibility for scaling up LLMs entirely in the quantized space. The source code is available at https://github.com/dibbla/Quantized-Evolution-Strategies .

</details>


### [295] [Contrastive Concept-Tree Search for LLM-Assisted Algorithm Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03132)
*Timothee Leleu,Sudeera Gunathilaka,Federico Ghimenti,Surya Ganguli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Contrastive Concept-Tree Search (CCTS) leverages a hierarchical concept representation extracted from LLM-generated programs to guide search via a likelihood-ratio reweighting of parents, improving efficiency and interpretability in LLM-assisted algorithm discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To exploit the LLM's internal space more effectively by using explicit concept hierarchies to bias search away from misleading concepts, addressing inefficiencies of purely fitness-based baselines.

Method: Extract hierarchical concepts from generated programs; train a contrastive model on high- vs low-performing solutions; reweight parent choices using a likelihood-ratio to bias search toward useful concept combinations; provide explicit concept trees rather than LLM lineage.

Result: Improved search efficiency over fitness-based baselines; produced interpretable, task-specific concept trees on Erdős-type combinatorics problems; gains mainly from learning which concepts to avoid; validated in a controlled synthetic environment that mirrors LLM dynamics.

Conclusion: Explicit contrastive concept guidance can enhance LLM-assisted discovery by focusing searches on productive concepts and avoiding misleading ones, yielding interpretable hierarchies and better performance; approach generalizes to other domains.

Abstract: Large language Model (LLM)-assisted algorithm discovery is an iterative, black-box optimization process over programs to approximatively solve a target task, where an LLM proposes candidate programs and an external evaluator provides task feedback. Despite intense recent research on the topic and promising results, how can the LLM internal representation of the space of possible programs be maximally exploited to improve performance is an open question. Here, we introduce Contrastive Concept-Tree Search (CCTS), which extracts a hierarchical concept representation from the generated programs and learns a contrastive concept model that guides parent selection. By reweighting parents using a likelihood-ratio score between high- and low-performing solutions, CCTS biases search toward useful concept combinations and away from misleading ones, providing guidance through an explicit concept hierarchy rather than the algorithm lineage constructed by the LLM. We show that CCTS improves search efficiency over fitness-based baselines and produces interpretable, task-specific concept trees across a benchmark of open Erdős-type combinatorics problems. Our analysis indicates that the gains are driven largely by learning which concepts to avoid. We further validate these findings in a controlled synthetic algorithm-discovery environment, which reproduces qualitatively the search dynamics observed with the LLMs.

</details>


### [296] [Enhanced Parcel Arrival Forecasting for Logistic Hubs: An Ensemble Deep Learning Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03135)
*Xinyue Pan,Yujia Xu,Benoit Montreuil*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A deep-learning ensemble framework using historical arrival patterns and real-time parcel updates to forecast hub workloads, outperforming traditional methods and single-model DL baselines in a city-case study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Growing e-commerce demand drives the need for timely parcel delivery and more efficient, predictable logistics hubs; improving forecast accuracy supports planning and resource management.

Method: Proposes a novel deep learning–based ensemble framework that combines historical arrival patterns with real-time parcel status updates to predict upcoming hub workloads, enabling short-term forecasts and longer-horizon accuracy improvements.

Result: Empirical evaluation in a major city's parcel logistics case study shows the ensemble method outperforms traditional forecasting techniques and standalone deep learning models.

Conclusion: The approach has strong potential to enhance operational efficiency in logistics hubs and warrants broader adoption and further development for strategic planning and resource optimization.

Abstract: The rapid expansion of online shopping has increased the demand for timely parcel delivery, compelling logistics service providers to enhance the efficiency, agility, and predictability of their hub networks. In order to solve the problem, we propose a novel deep learning-based ensemble framework that leverages historical arrival patterns and real-time parcel status updates to forecast upcoming workloads at logistic hubs. This approach not only facilitates the generation of short-term forecasts, but also improves the accuracy of future hub workload predictions for more strategic planning and resource management. Empirical tests of the algorithm, conducted through a case study of a major city's parcel logistics, demonstrate the ensemble method's superiority over both traditional forecasting techniques and standalone deep learning models. Our findings highlight the significant potential of this method to improve operational efficiency in logistics hubs and advocate for its broader adoption.

</details>


### [297] [SATORIS-N: Spectral Analysis based Traffic Observation Recovery via Informed Subspaces and Nuclear-norm minimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03138)
*Sampad Mohanty,Bhaskar Krishnamachari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SATORIS-N: a subspace-aware SDP method to impute partially observed traffic-density by leveraging slowly-evolving singular subspaces, outperforming standard matrix completion under high occlusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traffic-density matrices across days show low-rank structure and stable singular-subspace directions. Conventional completion methods struggle with extensive missing data; exploiting subspace priors can improve reconstruction quality for intelligent-vehicle applications.

Method: Convex SDP formulation of nuclear norm that explicitly incorporates prior singular-subspace information to enforce low-rankness and subspace alignment. Also explores a lightweight implicit subspace-alignment strategy by concatenating matrices from consecutive days to encourage alignment of singular directions.

Result: The explicit SDP approach yields a single global optimum and is markedly more robust than baseline methods at high missing rates, outperforming SoftImpute, IterativeSVD, statistical methods, and deep-learning baselines on Beijing and Shanghai datasets; the implicit strategy provides modest gains at low missingness.

Conclusion: The framework generalizes to spatiotemporal settings where singular subspaces evolve slowly over time and is applicable to V2X contexts (cooperative perception, predictive routing, V2I) where reliable imputation under sensor dropouts is critical.

Abstract: Traffic-density matrices from different days exhibit both low rank and stable correlations in their singular-vector subspaces. Leveraging this, we introduce SATORIS-N, a framework for imputing partially observed traffic-density by informed subspace priors from neighboring days. Our contribution is a subspace-aware semidefinite programming (SDP)} formulation of nuclear norm that explicitly informs the reconstruction with prior singular-subspace information. This convex formulation jointly enforces low rank and subspace alignment, providing a single global optimum and substantially improving accuracy under medium and high occlusion. We also study a lightweight implicit subspace-alignment} strategy in which matrices from consecutive days are concatenated to encourage alignment of spatial or temporal singular directions. Although this heuristic offers modest gains when missing rates are low, the explicit SDP approach is markedly more robust when large fractions of entries are missing. Across two real-world datasets (Beijing and Shanghai), SATORIS-N consistently outperforms standard matrix-completion methods such as SoftImpute, IterativeSVD, statistical, and even deep learning baselines at high occlusion levels. The framework generalizes to other spatiotemporal settings in which singular subspaces evolve slowly over time. In the context of intelligent vehicles and vehicle-to-everything (V2X) systems, accurate traffic-density
  reconstruction enables critical applications including cooperative perception, predictive routing, and vehicle-to-infrastructure (V2I) communication optimization. When infrastructure sensors or vehicle-reported observations are incomplete - due to communication dropouts, sensor occlusions, or sparse connected vehicle penetration-reliable imputation becomes essential for safe and efficient autonomous navigation.

</details>


### [298] [Self-Hinting Language Models Enhance Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03143)
*Baohao Liao,Hanze Dong,Xinxing Xu,Christof Monz,Jiang Bian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAGE introduces privileged self-hints to alleviate reward-sparsity collapse in Group Relative Policy Optimization by diversifying rollout trajectories during training without changing the terminal reward, and using a no-hint policy at test time. It yields consistent gains across multiple benchmarks and LLMs, with an accessible codebase.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO suffers from sparse terminal rewards where many rollouts receive identical rewards, causing relative advantages to vanish and hindering learning. There is a need to increase within-group diversity of trajectories to preserve learning signals and enable effective credit assignment.

Method: For each prompt x, sample a compact hint h (e.g., a plan or decomposition) and generate a solution τ conditioned on (x, h). The terminal reward R(x, τ) remains unchanged; hints reshape the rollout distribution to diversify within-group outcomes under finite sampling. Training is on-policy with privileged supervision; at test time, use h = ∅ (no hints) and deploy the no-hint policy. Sampling diverse self-hints acts as an adaptive curriculum, potentially more responsive to learner bottlenecks than fixed hints from a static policy or external model.

Result: Empirical evaluation over 6 benchmarks with 3 LLMs shows consistent improvements over GRPO: +2.0 on Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, +1.2 on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, and +1.3 on Qwen3-4B-Instruct (average gains). The authors provide code at the linked repository.

Conclusion: SAGE effectively mitigates reward-sparsity collapse in GRPO by injecting privileged hints during training to diversify rollout distributions without altering the objective. It yields robust, test-time no-hint performance gains and offers an adaptive curriculum that better aligns with learners’ bottlenecks.

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has recently emerged as a practical recipe for aligning large language models with verifiable objectives. However, under sparse terminal rewards, GRPO often stalls because rollouts within a group frequently receive identical rewards, causing relative advantages to collapse and updates to vanish. We propose self-hint aligned GRPO with privileged supervision (SAGE), an on-policy reinforcement learning framework that injects privileged hints during training to reshape the rollout distribution under the same terminal verifier reward. For each prompt $x$, the model samples a compact hint $h$ (e.g., a plan or decomposition) and then generates a solution $τ$ conditioned on $(x,h)$. Crucially, the task reward $R(x,τ)$ is unchanged; hints only increase within-group outcome diversity under finite sampling, preventing GRPO advantages from collapsing under sparse rewards. At test time, we set $h=\varnothing$ and deploy the no-hint policy without any privileged information.
