Moreover, sampling diverse self-hints serves as an adaptive curriculum that tracks the learner's bottlenecks more effectively than fixed hints from an initial policy or a stronger external model. Experiments over 6 benchmarks with 3 LLMs show that SAGE consistently outperforms GRPO, on average +2.0 on Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, +1.2 on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and +1.3 on Qwen3-4B-Instruct. The code is available at https://github.com/BaohaoLiao/SAGE.

</details>


### [299] [What Makes a Good Example? Modeling Exemplar Selection with Neural Network Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03144)
*Fanxiao Wani Qiu,Oscar Leong,Alexander LaTourrette*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Humans prefer exemplar sets that balance representativeness and diversity; transformer features best match human teaching judgments; dataset distillation can model teaching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decode the cognitive strategy humans use to select exemplars for teaching and map it to machine learning subset selection.

Method: Embed novel visual categories along a morph continuum using pretrained vision models. Compare exemplar selection strategies emphasizing prototypicality, joint representativeness, and diversity. Have adults select 1–3 exemplars to teach a learner. Evaluate model–human alignment across transformer- and CNN-based representations.

Result: Strategies based on joint representativeness, especially with diversity, best captured human judgments. Pure prototypicality or pure diversity alone performed worse. Transformer representations aligned more closely with human behavior than CNNs.

Conclusion: Joint representativeness–based subset selection is a robust principle for teaching with exemplars. Transformer features provide a cognitive-aligned representation. Findings support dataset distillation approaches as computational models for teaching and improving exemplar-based ML systems.

Abstract: Teaching requires distilling a rich category distribution into a small set of informative exemplars. Although prior work shows that humans consider both representativeness and diversity when teaching, the computational principles underlying these tradeoffs remain unclear. We address this gap by modeling human exemplar selection using neural network feature representations and principled subset selection criteria. Novel visual categories were embedded along a one-dimensional morph continuum using pretrained vision models, and selection strategies varied in their emphasis on prototypicality, joint representativeness, and diversity. Adult participants selected one to three exemplars to teach a learner. Model-human comparisons revealed that strategies based on joint representativeness, or its combination with diversity, best captured human judgments, whereas purely prototypical or diversity-based strategies performed worse. Moreover, transformer-based representations consistently aligned more closely with human behavior than convolutional networks. These results highlight the potential utility of dataset distillation methods in machine learning as computational models for teaching.

</details>


### [300] [MemCast: Memory-Driven Time Series Forecasting with Experience-Conditioned Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03164)
*Xiaoyu Tao,Mingyue Cheng,Ze Guo,Shuo Yu,Yaguo Liu,Qi Liu,Shijin Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MemCast reframes time series forecasting as an experience-conditioned reasoning task by learning a hierarchical memory from training data. It summarizes predictions into historical patterns, distills inference trajectories into reasoning wisdom, and extracts temporal features into general laws, which guide inference and reflective iteration. A dynamic confidence adaptation updates memory entries to enable continual evolution without test leakage. Empirical results show consistent improvements over baselines; code is released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of explicit experience accumulation and continual evolution in LLM-based forecasters for time series forecasting, enabling better adaptation and decision-making through memory-driven reasoning.

Method: Construct a hierarchical memory from training experiences: (1) summarize prediction results into historical patterns, (2) distill inference trajectories into reasoning wisdom, (3) induce extracted temporal features into general laws. During inference, use historical patterns to guide reasoning, reasoning wisdom to select favorable trajectories, and general laws as criteria for reflective iteration. Implement dynamic confidence adaptation to update memory entries without leaking test set distribution, enabling continual evolution.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple datasets show MemCast consistently outperforms previous methods, validating the effectiveness of the approach.

Conclusion: MemCast provides a memory-guided, continual-evolution framework for TSF that leverages experience-conditioned reasoning to improve forecasting accuracy and adaptability; code availability facilitates reproducibility.

Abstract: Time series forecasting (TSF) plays a critical role in decision-making for many real-world applications. Recently, LLM-based forecasters have made promising advancements. Despite their effectiveness, existing methods often lack explicit experience accumulation and continual evolution. In this work, we propose MemCast, a learning-to-memory framework that reformulates TSF as an experience-conditioned reasoning task. Specifically, we learn experience from the training set and organize it into a hierarchical memory. This is achieved by summarizing prediction results into historical patterns, distilling inference trajectories into reasoning wisdom, and inducing extracted temporal features into general laws. Furthermore, during inference, we leverage historical patterns to guide the reasoning process and utilize reasoning wisdom to select better trajectories, while general laws serve as criteria for reflective iteration. Additionally, to enable continual evolution, we design a dynamic confidence adaptation strategy that updates the confidence of individual entries without leaking the test set distribution. Extensive experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that MemCast consistently outperforms previous methods, validating the effectiveness of our approach. Our code is available at https://github.com/Xiaoyu-Tao/MemCast-TS.

</details>


### [301] [StepScorer: Accelerating Reinforcement Learning with Step-wise Scoring and Psychological Regret Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03171)
*Zhe Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PRM introduces per-step regret signals to reinforcement learning, turning sparse rewards into dense feedback. It yields about 36% faster convergence than PPO on Lunar Lander and shows promise for continuous control and delayed-feedback tasks, by modeling human-like counterfactual regret signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse and delayed rewards impede efficient RL learning. A dense, interpretable feedback signal grounded in counterfactual reasoning can accelerate policy improvement and enable rapid adaptation in real-world tasks.

Method: PRM computes a per-step regret r_t as the difference between the expected value of the optimal action and the value of the action actually taken in state s_t. This regret signal serves as a dense feedback signal added to the learning objective, transforming sparse terminal rewards into step-wise guidance. The approach is evaluated by integrating the regret signal with PPO in benchmark environments (e.g., Lunar Lander).

Result: PRM achieves stable performance and converges approximately 36% faster than standard PPO on Lunar Lander. It is particularly effective in continuous-control tasks and environments with delayed feedback, suggesting broad applicability to robotics, finance, and adaptive education.

Conclusion: By formalizing counterfactual, human-inspired regret as a computable signal, PRM bridges behavioral economics and RL, offering faster policy adaptation in scenarios with sparse or delayed rewards and enabling broader real-world deployment.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning algorithms often suffer from slow convergence due to sparse reward signals, particularly in complex environments where feedback is delayed or infrequent. This paper introduces the Psychological Regret Model (PRM), a novel approach that accelerates learning by incorporating regret-based feedback signals after each decision step. Rather than waiting for terminal rewards, PRM computes a regret signal based on the difference between the expected value of the optimal action and the value of the action taken in each state. This transforms sparse rewards into dense feedback signals through a step-wise scoring framework, enabling faster convergence. We demonstrate that PRM achieves stable performance approximately 36\% faster than traditional Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) in benchmark environments such as Lunar Lander. Our results indicate that PRM is particularly effective in continuous control tasks and environments with delayed feedback, making it suitable for real-world applications such as robotics, finance, and adaptive education where rapid policy adaptation is critical. The approach formalizes human-inspired counterfactual thinking as a computable regret signal, bridging behavioral economics and reinforcement learning.

</details>


### [302] [Adversarial construction as a potential solution to the experiment design problem in large task spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03172)
*Prakhar Godara,Frederick Callaway,Marcelo G. Mattar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adversarial construction identifies high-impact tasks in a large HMM-generated task space, enabling efficient exploration beyond random sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of a robust, task-general theory of human behavior and the impracticality of exhaustively exploring task spaces, the paper seeks a principled method to discover tasks that provoke qualitatively novel behavior.

Method: Define the task space as binary sequence prediction tasks generated by HMMs; apply an adversarial construction approach to iteratively search for task instances that maximize a measure of qualitative novelty in behavior.

Result: Empirical results show adversarial construction outperforms random environment sampling in eliciting novel behaviors; demonstrates potential as a principled design tool in high-dimensional task spaces.

Conclusion: Adversarial construction is a promising general-purpose strategy for task discovery and experimental design, potentially enabling a task-general theory of behavior across complex environments.

Abstract: Despite decades of work, we still lack a robust, task-general theory of human behavior even in the simplest domains. In this paper we tackle the generality problem head-on, by aiming to develop a unified model for all tasks embedded in a task-space. In particular we consider the space of binary sequence prediction tasks where the observations are generated by the space parameterized by hidden Markov models (HMM). As the space of tasks is large, experimental exploration of the entire space is infeasible. To solve this problem we propose the adversarial construction approach, which helps identify tasks that are most likely to elicit a qualitatively novel behavior. Our results suggest that adversarial construction significantly outperforms random sampling of environments and therefore could be used as a proxy for optimal experimental design in high-dimensional task spaces.

</details>


### [303] [Probe-then-Commit Multi-Objective Bandits: Theoretical Benefits of Limited Multi-Arm Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03175)
*Ming Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PtC-P-UCB: an online multi-armed bandit algorithm for probe-then-commit resource selection with Pareto frontier optimization. It interleaves limited probing of q>1 candidates with committing to one, achieving Pareto-frontier expansion and improved regret bounds, bridging bandits (q=1) and full-information (q=K). Extends to multi-modal probing with variance-adaptive guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The abstract targets online resource selection (multi-radio access and edge offloading) where only limited probing of candidate arms is possible. It addresses learning Pareto-optimal performance across multiple objectives (throughput, latency, energy, reliability) and fills theory gaps between classical bandits and full-information settings by developing a probe-then-commit algorithm with frontier-aware probing.

Method: Propose PtC-P-UCB, which selects q probes by approximately maximizing a frontier-coverage potential (hypervolume-inspired) and commits to the arm with a marginal hypervolume gain. The analysis yields dominated-hypervolume frontier error ~ O~(K_P d / sqrt(qT)) and scalarized regret ~ O~(L_φ d sqrt((K/q)T)); extended to multi-modal probing with variance-adaptive bounds.

Result: The algorithm achieves a 1/sqrt(q) acceleration in learning efficiency for Pareto-frontier expansion and regret, with bounds that explicitly depend on the Pareto-frontier size K_P, dimension d, horizon T, and scalarizer φ. Multi-modal probing yields variance-adaptive extensions.

Conclusion: PtC-P-UCB provides a principled, theoretically grounded approach to online resource selection with limited probing, bridging extremes (q=1 vs q=K) and enabling efficient multi-objective optimization. The multi-modal extension broadens applicability to richer feedback channels.

Abstract: We study an online resource-selection problem motivated by multi-radio access selection and mobile edge computing offloading. In each round, an agent chooses among $K$ candidate links/servers (arms) whose performance is a stochastic $d$-dimensional vector (e.g., throughput, latency, energy, reliability). The key interaction is \emph{probe-then-commit (PtC)}: the agent may probe up to $q>1$ candidates via control-plane measurements to observe their vector outcomes, but must execute exactly one candidate in the data plane. This limited multi-arm feedback regime strictly interpolates between classical bandits ($q=1$) and full-information experts ($q=K$), yet existing multi-objective learning theory largely focuses on these extremes. We develop \textsc{PtC-P-UCB}, an optimistic probe-then-commit algorithm whose technical core is frontier-aware probing under uncertainty in a Pareto mode, e.g., it selects the $q$ probes by approximately maximizing a hypervolume-inspired frontier-coverage potential and commits by marginal hypervolume gain to directly expand the attained Pareto region. We prove a dominated-hypervolume frontier error of $\tilde{O} (K_P d/\sqrt{qT})$, where $K_P$ is the Pareto-frontier size and $T$ is the horizon, and scalarized regret $\tilde{O} (L_φd\sqrt{(K/q)T})$, where $φ$ is the scalarizer. These quantify a transparent $1/\sqrt{q}$ acceleration from limited probing. We further extend to \emph{multi-modal probing}: each probe returns $M$ modalities (e.g., CSI, queue, compute telemetry), and uncertainty fusion yields variance-adaptive versions of the above bounds via an effective noise scale.

</details>


### [304] [DynSplit-KV: Dynamic Semantic Splitting for KVCache Compression in Efficient Long-Context LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03184)
*Jiancai Ye,Jun Liu,Qingchen Li,Tianlang Zhao,Hanbin Zhang,Jiayi Pan,Ningyi Xu,Guohao Dai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DynSplit-KV dynamically identifies semantic delimiters to split KVCache blocks and maps variable-length blocks to a fixed-length format, yielding higher accuracy and lower inference overhead in long-context LLM inference compared to rigid splitting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KVCache compression is needed to mitigate memory and latency bottlenecks in long-context LLM inference; rigid splitting with fixed intervals or delimiters misaligns semantics, causing substantial accuracy loss and overhead.

Method: Introduce dynamic importance-aware delimiter selection to preserve semantic integrity during splitting, and a uniform mapping strategy to convert variable-length semantic blocks into fixed-length blocks for efficient processing.

Result: Dynamic delimiter selection improves accuracy by 49.9%; uniform mapping reduces inference overhead by 4.9x; overall, DynSplit-KV achieves 2.2x speedup over FlashAttention and 2.6x peak memory reduction in long-context scenarios.

Conclusion: Dynamic, semantics-aware splitting is essential for effective KVCache compression. DynSplit-KV demonstrates significant accuracy and efficiency gains by adaptively selecting delimiters and standardizing block length, outperforming rigid-splitting baselines.

Abstract: Although Key-Value (KV) Cache is essential for efficient large language models (LLMs) inference, its growing memory footprint in long-context scenarios poses a significant bottleneck, making KVCache compression crucial. Current compression methods rely on rigid splitting strategies, such as fixed intervals or pre-defined delimiters. We observe that rigid splitting suffers from significant accuracy degradation (ranging from 5.5% to 55.1%) across different scenarios, owing to the scenario-dependent nature of the semantic boundaries. This highlights the necessity of dynamic semantic splitting to match semantics. To achieve this, we face two challenges. (1) Improper delimiter selection misaligns semantics with the KVCache, resulting in 28.6% accuracy loss. (2) Variable-length blocks after splitting introduce over 73.1% additional inference overhead. To address the above challenges, we propose DynSplit-KV, a KVCache compression method that dynamically identifies delimiters for splitting. We propose: (1) a dynamic importance-aware delimiter selection strategy, improving accuracy by 49.9%. (2) A uniform mapping strategy that transforms variable-length semantic blocks into a fixed-length format, reducing inference overhead by 4.9x. Experiments show that DynSplit-KV achieves the highest accuracy, 2.2x speedup compared with FlashAttention and 2.6x peak memory reduction in long-context scenarios.

</details>


### [305] [Prompt Augmentation Scales up GRPO Training on Mathematical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03190)
*Wenquan Lu,Hai Huang,Randall Balestriero*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Prompt augmentation with GRPO mitigates entropy collapse during reinforcement learning for LLM math reasoning, enabling longer horizon training and achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple math benchmarks; code and models released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Entropy collapse in reinforcement post-training causes decreasing policy entropy, training instability, and limited exploration; fixed reasoning prompts reduce diversity and hinder further gains.

Method: Introduce prompt augmentation to generate reasoning traces under diverse templates/formats during GRPO-based training; operate without KL regularization to sustain longer training; evaluate on standard math benchmarks using Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on MATH Level 3-5 benchmarks with 44.5 per-benchmark accuracy and 51.3 per-question accuracy across AIME24, AMC, MATH500, Minerva, OlympiadBench; demonstrates stable longer training horizons.

Conclusion: Prompt augmentation effectively mitigates entropy collapse and enables continued policy improvement in reinforcement learning for LLM reasoning; promising approach for wider reasoning tasks, with code and checkpoints available.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning algorithms such as group-relative policy optimization (GRPO) have demonstrated strong potential for improving the mathematical reasoning capabilities of large language models. However, prior work has consistently observed an entropy collapse phenomenon during reinforcement post-training, characterized by a monotonic decrease in policy entropy that ultimately leads to training instability and collapse. As a result, most existing approaches restrict training to short horizons (typically 5-20 epochs), limiting sustained exploration and hindering further policy improvement. In addition, nearly all prior work relies on a single, fixed reasoning prompt or template during training. In this work, we introduce prompt augmentation, a training strategy that instructs the model to generate reasoning traces under diverse templates and formats, thereby increasing rollout diversity. We show that, without a KL regularization term, prompt augmentation enables stable scaling of training duration under a fixed dataset and allows the model to tolerate low-entropy regimes without premature collapse. Empirically, a Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B model trained with prompt augmentation on the MATH Level 3-5 dataset achieves state-of-the-art performance, reaching 44.5 per-benchmark accuracy and 51.3 per-question accuracy on standard mathematical reasoning benchmarks, including AIME24, AMC, MATH500, Minerva, and OlympiadBench. The code and model checkpoints are available at https://github.com/wenquanlu/prompt-augmentation-GRPO.

</details>


### [306] [Reinforcement Learning with Promising Tokens for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03195)
*Jing-Cheng Pang,Liang Lu,Xian Tang,Kun Jiang,Sijie Wu,Kai Zhang,Xubin Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLPT narrows the RL action space to a dynamically identified set of promising tokens using semantic priors, decoupling decision-making from token generation to improve stability and sample efficiency in LLM RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The full vocabulary as actions makes RL over LLMs large and noisy; valid reasoning paths are hypothesized to lie in a low-rank subspace, so restricting the action space should reduce variance and improve learning.

Method: Identify a dynamic subset of promising tokens via the base model's semantic priors and mask the action space to this subset; decouple strategic decision-making from token generation; apply RL algorithms (GRPO, DAPO) across model sizes (4B/8B) and tasks.

Result: Empirical results show reduced gradient variance, stabilized training, and improved sample efficiency; RLPT outperforms standard RL baselines and integrates across multiple model sizes and RL algorithms on math, coding, and telecom reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: RLPT effectively mitigates the action-space issue in RL for LLMs, providing better training stability and sample efficiency and compatibility with existing RL frameworks.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a key paradigm for aligning and optimizing large language models (LLMs). Standard approaches treat the LLM as the policy and apply RL directly over the full vocabulary space. However, this formulation includes the massive tail of contextually irrelevant tokens in the action space, which could distract the policy from focusing on decision-making among the truly reasonable tokens. In this work, we verify that valid reasoning paths could inherently concentrate within a low-rank subspace. Based on this insight, we introduce Reinforcement Learning with Promising Tokens (RLPT), a framework that mitigates the action space issue by decoupling strategic decision-making from token generation. Specifically, RLPT leverages the semantic priors of the base model to identify a dynamic set of \emph{promising tokens} and constrains policy optimization exclusively to this refined subset via masking. Theoretical analysis and empirical results demonstrate that RLPT effectively reduces gradient variance, stabilizes the training process, and improves sample efficiency. Experiment results on math, coding, and telecom reasoning show that RLPT outperforms standard RL baselines and integrates effectively across various model sizes (4B and 8B) and RL algorithms (GRPO and DAPO).

</details>


### [307] [From Scalar Rewards to Potential Trends: Shaping Potential Landscapes for Model-Based Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03201)
*Yao-Hui Li,Zeyu Wang,Xin Li,Wei Pang,Yingfang Yuan,Zhengkun Chen,Boya Zhang,Riashat Islam,Alex Lamb,Yonggang Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SLOPE reframes reward modeling in model-based RL from scalar regression to optimistic potential estimation, boosting exploration and performance in sparse reward settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard scalar reward regression yields flat gradients in sparse tasks, hindering planning. A robust signal is needed to guide trajectory optimization.

Method: Use optimistic distributional regression to estimate high-confidence upper bounds on returns, producing a shaped potential landscape rather than a scalar reward; this elevates rare successes and yields non-flat gradients for planning.

Result: Empirical evaluation across 30+ tasks, 5 benchmarks shows SLOPE outperforms leading baselines across fully sparse, semi-sparse, and dense reward regimes.

Conclusion: SLOPE provides a general, effective approach to reward shaping in MBRL, improving sample efficiency and exploration across reward sparsity levels.

Abstract: Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) achieves high sample efficiency by simulating future trajectories with learned dynamics and reward models. However, its effectiveness is severely compromised in sparse reward settings. The core limitation lies in the standard paradigm of regressing ground-truth scalar rewards: in sparse environments, this yields a flat, gradient-free landscape that fails to provide directional guidance for planning. To address this challenge, we propose Shaping Landscapes with Optimistic Potential Estimates (SLOPE), a novel framework that shifts reward modeling from predicting scalars to constructing informative potential landscapes. SLOPE employs optimistic distributional regression to estimate high-confidence upper bounds, which amplifies rare success signals and ensures sufficient exploration gradients. Evaluations on 30+ tasks across 5 benchmarks demonstrate that SLOPE consistently outperforms leading baselines in fully sparse, semi-sparse, and dense rewards.

</details>


### [308] [Sparsity is Combinatorial Depth: Quantifying MoE Expressivity via Tropical Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03204)
*Ye Su,Huayi Tang,Zixuan Gong,Yong Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First tropical-geometry analysis of MoE, linking Top-k routing to the k-th elementary symmetric tropical polynomial; input regions form the Normal Fan of a hypersimplex, giving capacity scaling with C(N,k). Introduces Effective Capacity under the Manifold Hypothesis and Combinatorial Resilience, unifying discrete and continuous geometry to justify conditional computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between heuristic efficiency claims for MoE and a geometric explanation of its expressivity and capacity, especially under data manifolds where dense networks may fail.

Method: Apply tropical geometry to MoE; establish an algebraic isomorphism between Top-k routing and the k-th elementary symmetric tropical polynomial; interpret routing as partitioning input space via the Normal Fan of a hypersimplex; define Effective Capacity and analyze combinatorial properties (transversality of routing cones).

Result: Shows sparsity acts as combinatorial depth, enabling capacity scaling by binomial coefficient C(N,k); MoE maintains high expressivity on low-dimensional data due to combinatorial resilience, unlike dense nets which can suffer capacity collapse; provides a theoretical bridge between discrete hypersimplex geometry and continuous neural functions.

Conclusion: The framework unifies discrete hypersimplex geometry with neural function geometry, offering rigorous justification for why conditional computation via MoE can outperform in topology-driven expressivity; supports the view that geometric/topological properties underpin MoE success.

Abstract: While Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures define the state-of-the-art, their theoretical success is often attributed to heuristic efficiency rather than geometric expressivity. In this work, we present the first analysis of MoE through the lens of tropical geometry, establishing that the Top-$k$ routing mechanism is algebraically isomorphic to the $k$-th elementary symmetric tropical polynomial. This isomorphism partitions the input space into the Normal Fan of a Hypersimplex, revealing that \textbf{sparsity is combinatorial depth} which scales geometric capacity by the binomial coefficient $\binom{N}{k}$. Moving beyond ambient bounds, we introduce the concept of \textit{Effective Capacity} under the Manifold Hypothesis. We prove that while dense networks suffer from capacity collapse on low-dimensional data, MoE architectures exhibit \textit{Combinatorial Resilience}, maintaining high expressivity via the transversality of routing cones. In this study, our framework unifies the discrete geometry of the Hypersimplex with the continuous geometry of neural functions, offering a rigorous theoretical justification for the topological supremacy of conditional computation.

</details>


### [309] [Lookahead Sample Reward Guidance for Test-Time Scaling of Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03211)
*Yeongmin Kim,Donghyeok Shin,Byeonghu Na,Minsang Park,Richard Lee Kim,Il-Chul Moon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LiDAR sampling is an efficient test-time diffusion-sampling method that computes expected future reward (EFR) using marginal samples from a pre-trained model, detaching neural backprop paths to enable closed-form guidance. With lookahead sampling and a high-quality final solver, it achieves strong human-aligned performance with far fewer samples, matching state-of-the-art gradient guidance (SDXL) at ~9.5× faster speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gradient-guided diffusion sampling, which aims to steer samples toward higher human-aligned rewards, relies on Taylor approximations of the expected future reward (EFR) and requires backpropagation at each step, making it computationally expensive. There is a need for a backprop-free, efficient method that preserves alignment quality.

Method: Compute EFR at any timestep x_t using only marginal samples from a pre-trained diffusion model, detaching the neural dependency to allow closed-form guidance (no neural backprop). Introduce lookahead sampling to collect marginal samples efficiently. For final sample generation, apply an accurate solver to guide particles toward high-reward lookahead samples, forming the LiDAR sampling scheme.

Result: LiDAR achieves substantial performance gains with as few as three samples using a 3-step lookahead solver, with performance improving as lookahead accuracy and sample count increase. It matches GenEval performance of the latest SDXL gradient-guidance method while delivering about a 9.5× speedup.

Conclusion: LiDAR sampling offers a fast, backprop-free alternative to gradient-based guidance for diffusion models, delivering near-parity with state-of-the-art methods at a fraction of the computational cost and with clear scalability via more samples and more accurate lookahead.

Abstract: Diffusion models have demonstrated strong generative performance; however, generated samples often fail to fully align with human intent. This paper studies a test-time scaling method that enables sampling from regions with higher human-aligned reward values. Existing gradient guidance methods approximate the expected future reward (EFR) at an intermediate particle $\mathbf{x}_t$ using a Taylor approximation, but this approximation at each time step incurs high computational cost due to sequential neural backpropagation. We show that the EFR at any $\mathbf{x}_t$ can be computed using only marginal samples from a pre-trained diffusion model. The proposed EFR formulation detaches the neural dependency between $\mathbf{x}_t$ and the EFR, enabling closed-form guidance computation without neural backpropagation. To further improve efficiency, we introduce lookahead sampling to collect marginal samples. For final sample generation, we use an accurate solver that guides particles toward high-reward lookahead samples. We refer to this sampling scheme as LiDAR sampling. LiDAR achieves substantial performance improvements using only three samples with a 3-step lookahead solver, exhibiting steep performance gains as lookahead accuracy and sample count increase; notably, it reaches the same GenEval performance as the latest gradient guidance method for SDXL with a 9.5x speedup.

</details>


### [310] [Topology Matters: A Cautionary Case Study of Graph SSL on Neuro-Inspired Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03217)
*May Kristine Jonson Carlon,Su Myat Noe,Haojiong Wang,Yasuo Kuniyoshi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hierarchical SSL for connectome-like data fails due to misaligned invariance objectives with topology; topology-aware heuristics outperform SSL; highlights need for topology-preserving SSL objectives in neuro-AI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address multi-scale brain organization by learning node-, edge-, and graph-level embeddings; assess the suitability of SSL on topology-rich connectome data.

Method: Four-stage evaluation using a controllable synthetic benchmark that mimics connectome topology; train a hierarchical SSL model (node/edge/graph embeddings) and compare to topology-aware heuristics; ablate to diagnose objective mismatch.

Result: Invariance-based SSL is fundamentally misaligned and catastrophically outperformed by topology-aware heuristics; ablations confirm an objective mismatch between SSL invariance goals and community/modularity structure.

Conclusion: Generic graph SSL is ill-suited for connectome-like data; study advocates topology-aware SSL objectives that reward preservation of structure (modularity, motifs) for neuro-AI research.

Abstract: Understanding how local interactions give rise to global brain organization requires models that can represent information across multiple scales. We introduce a hierarchical self-supervised learning (SSL) framework that jointly learns node-, edge-, and graph-level embeddings, inspired by multimodal neuroimaging. We construct a controllable synthetic benchmark mimicking the topological properties of connectomes. Our four-stage evaluation protocol reveals a critical failure: the invariance-based SSL model is fundamentally misaligned with the benchmark's topological properties and is catastrophically outperformed by classical, topology-aware heuristics. Ablations confirm an objective mismatch: SSL objectives designed to be invariant to topological perturbations learn to ignore the very community structure that classical methods exploit. Our results expose a fundamental pitfall in applying generic graph SSL to connectome-like data. We present this framework as a cautionary case study, highlighting the need for new, topology-aware SSL objectives for neuro-AI research that explicitly reward the preservation of structure (e.g., modularity or motifs).

</details>


### [311] [BayeSQP: Bayesian Optimization through Sequential Quadratic Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03232)
*Paul Brunzema,Sebastian Trimpe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BayeSQP combines Bayesian optimization with sequential quadratic programming by using second-order GP surrogates for objective and constraints to model values, gradients, and Hessians from zero-order data, solving a local SOC subproblem under uncertainty and selecting the next point via a constrained Thompson sampling line search; it shows improvements in high-dimensional settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address black-box optimization by integrating classical SQP structure with Bayesian uncertainty modeling. Existing BO methods often rely on zeroth-order information and may not exploit gradient/Hessian information; BayeSQP aims to obtain gradient/Hessian estimates from a GP and use them in a tractable, uncertainty-aware local subproblem to improve sample efficiency and convergence in high-dimensional problems.

Method: Use second-order Gaussian process surrogates for both objective and constraints to jointly model function values, gradients, and Hessians from only function evaluations. At each iteration, construct a local subproblem using the GP posterior to obtain a search direction. The subproblem explicitly incorporates uncertainty in function and derivative estimates, yielding a tractable second-order cone program (SOCP) for high-probability improvements under model uncertainty. A one-dimensional line search via constrained Thompson sampling selects the next evaluation point.

Result: Empirical results indicate BayeSQP outperforms state-of-the-art methods in specific high-dimensional settings.

Conclusion: BayeSQP provides a principled and flexible framework that bridges classical optimization (SQP) with modern black-box optimization (Bayesian methods), offering uncertainty-aware, gradient/Hessian-informed subproblems for efficient high-dimensional optimization.

Abstract: We introduce BayeSQP, a novel algorithm for general black-box optimization that merges the structure of sequential quadratic programming with concepts from Bayesian optimization. BayeSQP employs second-order Gaussian process surrogates for both the objective and constraints to jointly model the function values, gradients, and Hessian from only zero-order information. At each iteration, a local subproblem is constructed using the GP posterior estimates and solved to obtain a search direction. Crucially, the formulation of the subproblem explicitly incorporates uncertainty in both the function and derivative estimates, resulting in a tractable second-order cone program for high probability improvements under model uncertainty. A subsequent one-dimensional line search via constrained Thompson sampling selects the next evaluation point. Empirical results show thatBayeSQP outperforms state-of-the-art methods in specific high-dimensional settings. Our algorithm offers a principled and flexible framework that bridges classical optimization techniques with modern approaches to black-box optimization.

</details>


### [312] [Merging Beyond: Streaming LLM Updates via Activation-Guided Rotations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03237)
*Yuxuan Yao,Haonan Sheng,Qingsong Lv,Han Wu,Shuqi Liu,Zehua Liu,Zengyan Liu,Jiahui Gao,Haochen Tan,Xiaojin Fu,Haoli Bai,Hing Cheung So,Zhijiang Guo,Linqi Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Streaming Merging and Activation-guided Rotation-aware Merging (ARM) to iteratively merge models by treating merging coefficients as learning rates and aligning updates along activation-derived subspaces; claims to surpass fully converged SFT using only early checkpoints, across 1.7B–14B models and tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for efficient, controllable, and scalable model adaptation beyond post-hoc merging and simple interpolation, leveraging the optimization dynamics of supervised fine-tuning (SFT).

Method: Proposes Streaming Merging as an iterative optimization framework. ARM derives rotation vectors from activation subspaces and treats merging coefficients as learning rates to steer parameter updates along data-driven trajectories, aligning semantic subspaces rather than performing linear interpolation, using only early SFT checkpoints.

Result: Empirically, ARM surpasses fully converged SFT checkpoints across model sizes from 1.7B to 14B and domains like math and code, indicating improved adaptation efficiency and effectiveness.

Conclusion: ARM offers a scalable, lightweight approach for efficient model adaptation that can transcend converged checkpoints; Streaming Merging provides a new paradigm for updating LLMs by integrating optimization dynamics into the merging process.

Abstract: The escalating scale of Large Language Models (LLMs) necessitates efficient adaptation techniques. Model merging has gained prominence for its efficiency and controllability. However, existing merging techniques typically serve as post-hoc refinements or focus on mitigating task interference, often failing to capture the dynamic optimization benefits of supervised fine-tuning (SFT). In this work, we propose Streaming Merging, an innovative model updating paradigm that conceptualizes merging as an iterative optimization process. Central to this paradigm is \textbf{ARM} (\textbf{A}ctivation-guided \textbf{R}otation-aware \textbf{M}erging), a strategy designed to approximate gradient descent dynamics. By treating merging coefficients as learning rates and deriving rotation vectors from activation subspaces, ARM effectively steers parameter updates along data-driven trajectories. Unlike conventional linear interpolation, ARM aligns semantic subspaces to preserve the geometric structure of high-dimensional parameter evolution. Remarkably, ARM requires only early SFT checkpoints and, through iterative merging, surpasses the fully converged SFT model. Experimental results across model scales (1.7B to 14B) and diverse domains (e.g., math, code) demonstrate that ARM can transcend converged checkpoints. Extensive experiments show that ARM provides a scalable and lightweight framework for efficient model adaptation.

</details>


### [313] [GraDE: A Graph Diffusion Estimator for Frequent Subgraph Discovery in Neural Architectures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03257)
*Yikang Yang,Zhengxin Yang,Minghao Luo,Luzhou Peng,Hongxiao Li,Wanling Gao,Lei Wang,Jianfeng Zhan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraDE introduces a diffusion-based estimator for frequent subgraph mining in neural architectures, achieving scalable discovery with strong ranking performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The problem combines the need to find frequent subgraphs in large neural-architecture graphs with two traditional extremes: exact enumeration (accurate but intractable for large subgraphs) and sampling (scalable but loses discovery power).

Method: Develop Graph Diffusion Estimator (GraDE), a diffusion-guided search framework that uses graph diffusion models to learn a distribution over subgraphs and score their typicality, enabling efficient identification of frequent patterns and scalable discovery of large-scale motifs.

Result: Empirical results show up to 114% improvement in ranking accuracy over sampling baselines and discovery of large-scale patterns with up to 30× higher median frequency compared to sampling-based methods.

Conclusion: First integration of graph diffusion models for frequent subgraph identification in neural architectures; GraDE provides both computational feasibility and strong discovery capability, suggesting broad applicability to scalable motif mining and design-space exploration; future work could explore extensions to other graph domains and efficiency optimizations.

Abstract: Finding frequently occurring subgraph patterns or network motifs in neural architectures is crucial for optimizing efficiency, accelerating design, and uncovering structural insights. However, as the subgraph size increases, enumeration-based methods are perfectly accurate but computationally prohibitive, while sampling-based methods are computationally tractable but suffer from a severe decline in discovery capability. To address these challenges, this paper proposes GraDE, a diffusion-guided search framework that ensures both computational feasibility and discovery capability. The key innovation is the Graph Diffusion Estimator (GraDE), which is the first to introduce graph diffusion models to identify frequent subgraphs by scoring their typicality within the learned distribution. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that the estimator achieves superior ranking accuracy, with up to 114\% improvement compared to sampling-based baselines. Benefiting from this, the proposed framework successfully discovers large-scale frequent patterns, achieving up to 30$\times$ higher median frequency than sampling-based methods.

</details>


### [314] [Beyond Suffixes: Token Position in GCG Adversarial Attacks on Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03265)
*Hicham Eddoubi,Umar Faruk Abdullahi,Fadi Hassan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adversarial token placement in GCG jailbreak attacks (prefix vs suffix) significantly affects attack success, revealing a blind spot in safety evaluations for LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety alignment robustness against jailbreaks is under-studied with respect to token placement; existing evaluations focus on suffix-based prompts, potentially missing a critical attack axis.

Method: Analyze Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) jailbreak attacks as a case study; compare optimization for prefix vs suffix adversarial tokens and assess impact of varying token positions during evaluation.

Result: Findings show that both prefix-optimizing attacks and varying adversarial token positions materially influence attack success rates, indicating that token position is a key factor in jailbreak effectiveness.

Conclusion: Evaluation protocols for adversarial robustness in LLMs should systematically account for the position of adversarial tokens; current safety benchmarks are incomplete without this axis.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have seen widespread adoption across multiple domains, creating an urgent need for robust safety alignment mechanisms. However, robustness remains challenging due to jailbreak attacks that bypass alignment via adversarial prompts. In this work, we focus on the prevalent Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) attack and identify a previously underexplored attack axis in jailbreak attacks typically framed as suffix-based: the placement of adversarial tokens within the prompt. Using GCG as a case study, we show that both optimizing attacks to generate prefixes instead of suffixes and varying adversarial token position during evaluation substantially influence attack success rates. Our findings highlight a critical blind spot in current safety evaluations and underline the need to account for the position of adversarial tokens in the adversarial robustness evaluation of LLMs.

</details>


### [315] [Unveiling Covert Toxicity in Multimodal Data via Toxicity Association Graphs: A Graph-Based Metric and Interpretable Detection Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03268)
*Guanzong Wu,Zihao Zhu,Siwei Lyu,Baoyuan Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Toxicity Association Graphs (TAGs) to detect covert toxicity in multimodal data, introducing the Multimodal Toxicity Covertness (MTC) metric and a Covert Toxic Dataset for evaluation; claims improved performance and strong interpretability across covertness regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Toxic content can be encoded in cross-modal interactions where individual modalities appear benign; there is a need for a quantifiable, interpretable measure of hidden toxicity and a benchmark to evaluate it.

Method: Build Toxicity Association Graphs to capture semantic links between innocuous entities and latent toxic implications; define MTC to quantify concealment in multimodal expressions; create Covert Toxic Dataset to benchmark high-covertness instances; produce auditable, interpretable detection outcomes.

Result: Experimental results reportedly outperform existing methods across low- and high-covertness regimes while maintaining interpretability and transparency; the dataset provides a rigorous testbed for evaluating both the metric and the framework.

Conclusion: Advances explainable multimodal toxicity detection and establishes foundation for context-aware, interpretable safety systems; pressure-tested by a dedicated covert toxicity benchmark; suggests future work on broader contexts and improvements in interpretability.

Abstract: Detecting toxicity in multimodal data remains a significant challenge, as harmful meanings often lurk beneath seemingly benign individual modalities: only emerging when modalities are combined and semantic associations are activated. To address this, we propose a novel detection framework based on Toxicity Association Graphs (TAGs), which systematically model semantic associations between innocuous entities and latent toxic implications. Leveraging TAGs, we introduce the first quantifiable metric for hidden toxicity, the Multimodal Toxicity Covertness (MTC), which measures the degree of concealment in toxic multimodal expressions. By integrating our detection framework with the MTC metric, our approach enables precise identification of covert toxicity while preserving full interpretability of the decision-making process, significantly enhancing transparency in multimodal toxicity detection. To validate our method, we construct the Covert Toxic Dataset, the first benchmark specifically designed to capture high-covertness toxic multimodal instances. This dataset encodes nuanced cross-modal associations and serves as a rigorous testbed for evaluating both the proposed metric and detection framework. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach outperforms existing methods across both low- and high-covertness toxicity regimes, while delivering clear, interpretable, and auditable detection outcomes. Together, our contributions advance the state of the art in explainable multimodal toxicity detection and lay the foundation for future context-aware and interpretable approaches. Content Warning: This paper contains examples of toxic multimodal content that may be offensive or disturbing to some readers. Reader discretion is advised.

</details>


### [316] [BlockRR: A Unified Framework of RR-type Algorithms for Label Differential Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03277)
*Haixia Liu,Yi Ding*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BlockRR is a unified randomized-response framework for label differential privacy that subsumes existing RR-type mechanisms, provides ε-label DP guarantees, and uses a weight-matrix partitioning scheme to leverage label priors; under composition, multiple BlockRRs remain ε-label DP; empirically, it improves the balance between test accuracy and per-class accuracy under high/moderate privacy (ε ≤ 3) on imbalanced CIFAR-10 variants, and defaults to standard RR with no extra loss under low privacy (ε ≥ 4).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address fragmentation in RR-type mechanisms for label DP by offering a single, theoretically grounded framework that can adapt to label priors and composition, while delivering practical utility on imbalanced data.

Method: Introduce BlockRR as a generalized RR-type mechanism, prove ε-label DP, design a partitioning method based on a weight matrix derived from label priors, and apply the parallel composition principle. Evaluate on CIFAR-10 variants with varying class imbalance.

Result: BlockRR satisfies ε-label DP. The weight-matrix partitioning preserves DP under composition. Empirically, in high/moderate privacy (ε ≤ 3), BlockRR achieves a better trade-off between test accuracy and per-class accuracy than baselines. In low privacy (ε ≥ 4), BlockRR reduces to standard RR without additional performance loss.

Conclusion: BlockRR provides a unified, principled, and practical RR-type framework for label DP, simplifying analysis while delivering favorable utility under stricter privacy budgets and gracefully degrading to standard RR when privacy requirements are looser.

Abstract: In this paper, we introduce BlockRR, a novel and unified randomized-response mechanism for label differential privacy. This framework generalizes existed RR-type mechanisms as special cases under specific parameter settings, which eliminates the need for separate, case-by-case analysis. Theoretically, we prove that BlockRR satisfies $ε$-label DP. We also design a partition method for BlockRR based on a weight matrix derived from label prior information; the parallel composition principle ensures that the composition of two such mechanisms remains $ε$-label DP. Empirically, we evaluate BlockRR on two variants of CIFAR-10 with varying degrees of class imbalance. Results show that in the high-privacy and moderate-privacy regimes ($ε\leq 3.0$), our propsed method gets a better balance between test accuaracy and the average of per-class accuracy. In the low-privacy regime ($ε\geq 4.0$), all methods reduce BlockRR to standard RR without additional performance loss.

</details>


### [317] [Universal Approximation of Continuous Functionals on Compact Subsets via Linear Measurements and Scalar Nonlinearities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03290)
*Andrey Krylov,Maksim Penkin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Universal approximation of continuous functionals on compact sets of Hilbert-space products by a two-stage architecture: finite linear measurements followed by nonlinear scalarities; extends to Banach-valued maps with finite-rank approximations; justifies the common measure-then-nonlinearity paradigm in operator learning and imaging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalize the universal approximation phenomenon from functions on finite-dimensional domains to functionals on infinite-dimensional input spaces, and to provide a principled justification for neural-like architectures that first sample linearly and then apply nonlinearities.

Method: Show that any continuous functional on compact subsets of products of Hilbert spaces can be uniformly approximated by structures that compute finitely many continuous linear measurements of the input and then combine these measurements through continuous scalar nonlinearities; extend to maps with values in a Banach space to obtain finite-rank approximations.

Result: Proves a universal approximation result for such functionals using a finite-measurement, nonlinear-combination scheme; yields finite-rank approximations for Banach-space-valued maps; provides a compact-set justification for the design pattern used in operator learning and imaging.

Conclusion: Supports and legitimizes the prevalent 'measure, apply scalar nonlinearities, then combine' design pattern in operator learning and imaging under a compact-set framework, and broadens it to finite-rank approximations in Banach-valued settings.

Abstract: We study universal approximation of continuous functionals on compact subsets of products of Hilbert spaces. We prove that any such functional can be uniformly approximated by models that first take finitely many continuous linear measurements of the inputs and then combine these measurements through continuous scalar nonlinearities. We also extend the approximation principle to maps with values in a Banach space, yielding finite-rank approximations. These results provide a compact-set justification for the common ``measure, apply scalar nonlinearities, then combine'' design pattern used in operator learning and imaging.

</details>


### [318] [Anomaly Detection via Mean Shift Density Enhancement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03293)
*Pritam Kar,Rahul Bordoloi,Olaf Wolkenhauer,Saptarshi Bej*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Mean Shift Density Enhancement (MSDE) is a fully unsupervised anomaly detector that scores anomalies by cumulative displacement during density-driven mean-shift; using adaptive density weights from a UMAP-based neighborhood, it yields robust, state-of-the-art performance across anomaly types and noise levels on ADBench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unsupervised anomaly detectors often generalize poorly across anomaly types and noisy data; there is a need for a robust framework that does not rely on fixed structural assumptions.

Method: MSDE uses a weighted mean-shift procedure with sample-specific density weights derived from a UMAP fuzzy neighborhood; anomalies shift toward density modes while normal samples remain stable; anomaly score is total displacement over a few iterations.

Result: On ADBench (46 real-world datasets, 4 anomaly generation mechanisms, 6 noise levels) MSDE outperforms 13 baselines, achieving strong, balanced performance in AUC-ROC, AUC-PR, and Precision@n across noise levels and anomaly types.

Conclusion: Displacement-based scoring via adaptive density-aware mean-shift offers a robust alternative to existing unsupervised anomaly detection methods.

Abstract: Unsupervised anomaly detection stands as an important problem in machine learning, with applications in financial fraud prevention, network security and medical diagnostics. Existing unsupervised anomaly detection algorithms rarely perform well across different anomaly types, often excelling only under specific structural assumptions. This lack of robustness also becomes particularly evident under noisy settings. We propose Mean Shift Density Enhancement (MSDE), a fully unsupervised framework that detects anomalies through their geometric response to density-driven manifold evolution. MSDE is based on the principle that normal samples, being well supported by local density, remain stable under iterative density enhancement, whereas anomalous samples undergo large cumulative displacements as they are attracted toward nearby density modes. To operationalize this idea, MSDE employs a weighted mean-shift procedure with adaptive, sample-specific density weights derived from a UMAP-based fuzzy neighborhood graph. Anomaly scores are defined by the total displacement accumulated across a small number of mean-shift iterations. We evaluate MSDE on the ADBench benchmark, comprising forty six real-world tabular datasets, four realistic anomaly generation mechanisms, and six noise levels. Compared to 13 established unsupervised baselines, MSDE achieves consistently strong, balanced and robust performance for AUC-ROC, AUC-PR, and Precision@n, at several noise levels and on average over several types of anomalies. These results demonstrate that displacement-based scoring provides a robust alternative to the existing state-of-the-art for unsupervised anomaly detection.

</details>


### [319] [Lipschitz Multiscale Deep Equilibrium Models: A Theoretically Guaranteed and Accelerated Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03297)
*Naoki Sato,Hideaki Iiduka*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A Lipschitz multiscale DEQ is proposed that guarantees fixed-point convergence for both forward and backward passes, enabling up to 4.75x speed-ups on CIFAR-10 with only a minor drop in accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DEQs offer memory-efficient, infinitely deep representations but lack convergence guarantees and are computationally expensive due to repeated fixed-point iterations; guaranteeing convergence could greatly reduce computation.

Method: Introduce Lipschitz multiscale DEQ architecture with hyperparameter-based guarantees of fixed-point convergence for both passes; leverage a multiscale design and constrained Lipschitz properties to enable stable fixed-point iterations and faster computation; evaluate on image classification (CIFAR-10).

Result: The approach yields up to 4.75× speed-up in training/inference on CIFAR-10, with only a minor decrease in accuracy, demonstrating practical convergence guarantees for DEQs.

Conclusion: Convergence guarantees for DEQs can be achieved via hyperparameter tuning in a Lipschitz multiscale architecture, delivering substantial computational speed-ups with limited accuracy loss.

Abstract: Deep equilibrium models (DEQs) achieve infinitely deep network representations without stacking layers by exploring fixed points of layer transformations in neural networks. Such models constitute an innovative approach that achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art methods in many large-scale numerical experiments, despite requiring significantly less memory. However, DEQs face the challenge of requiring vastly more computational time for training and inference than conventional methods, as they repeatedly perform fixed-point iterations with no convergence guarantee upon each input. Therefore, this study explored an approach to improve fixed-point convergence and consequently reduce computational time by restructuring the model architecture to guarantee fixed-point convergence. Our proposed approach for image classification, Lipschitz multiscale DEQ, has theoretically guaranteed fixed-point convergence for both forward and backward passes by hyperparameter adjustment, achieving up to a 4.75$\times$ speed-up in numerical experiments on CIFAR-10 at the cost of a minor drop in accuracy.

</details>


### [320] [Periodic Regularized Q-Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03301)
*Hyukjun Yang,Han-Dong Lim,Donghwan Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PRQ introduces periodic regularized Q-learning by regularizing the projection operator, yielding a contraction in regularized projected value iteration (RP-VI) and extending to a sample-based algorithm with finite-time convergence guarantees under linear function approximation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of convergence guarantees for Q-learning under linear function approximation by enforcing contraction through regularized projections.

Method: Define a regularized projection operator, develop regularized projected value iteration (RP-VI), prove contraction, and extend to a stochastic (sample-based) PRQ algorithm with finite-time analysis.

Result: RP-VI is a contraction; PRQ achieves finite-time convergence under linear function approximation with theoretical guarantees.

Conclusion: Regularizing the projection operator provides stable convergence for Q-learning with function approximation, offering a rigorously analyzed PRQ algorithm with finite-time guarantees.

Abstract: In reinforcement learning (RL), Q-learning is a fundamental algorithm whose convergence is guaranteed in the tabular setting. However, this convergence guarantee does not hold under linear function approximation. To overcome this limitation, a significant line of research has introduced regularization techniques to ensure stable convergence under function approximation. In this work, we propose a new algorithm, periodic regularized Q-learning (PRQ). We first introduce regularization at the level of the projection operator and explicitly construct a regularized projected value iteration (RP-VI), subsequently extending it to a sample-based RL algorithm. By appropriately regularizing the projection operator, the resulting projected value iteration becomes a contraction. By extending this regularized projection into the stochastic setting, we establish the PRQ algorithm and provide a rigorous theoretical analysis that proves finite-time convergence guarantees for PRQ under linear function approximation.

</details>


### [321] [medR: Reward Engineering for Clinical Offline Reinforcement Learning via Tri-Drive Potential Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03305)
*Qianyi Xu,Gousia Habib,Feng Wu,Yanrui Du,Zhihui Chen,Swapnil Mishra,Dilruk Perera,Mengling Feng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Automates offline reward design for clinical RL via LLMs, using a potential-based reward with survival, confidence, and competence; introduces metrics to evaluate reward structures before deployment; aims to improve policy performance and generalization across diseases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle the reward engineering bottleneck in offline reinforcement learning for dynamic treatment regimes, which currently relies on manual heuristics that generalize poorly across diverse pathologies.

Method: Proposes a pipeline that uses Large Language Models to design and verify reward functions formulated as potential functions comprising three components: survival, confidence, and competence. Introduces quantitative metrics to evaluate and select the best reward structure prior to deployment, enabling disease-specific customization through domain knowledge.

Result: Framework formulation with automated reward design and offline evaluation; empirical results are not described in the abstract, but the approach is claimed to enhance policy performance and generalization.

Conclusion: LLM-informed automated reward design can reduce manual engineering burden, enable disease-specific customization, and improve RL policy performance in clinical offline settings through validated evaluation metrics.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) offers a powerful framework for optimizing dynamic treatment regimes (DTRs). However, clinical RL is fundamentally bottlenecked by reward engineering: the challenge of defining signals that safely and effectively guide policy learning in complex, sparse offline environments. Existing approaches often rely on manual heuristics that fail to generalize across diverse pathologies. To address this, we propose an automated pipeline leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) for offline reward design and verification. We formulate the reward function using potential functions consisted of three core components: survival, confidence, and competence. We further introduce quantitative metrics to rigorously evaluate and select the optimal reward structure prior to deployment. By integrating LLM-driven domain knowledge, our framework automates the design of reward functions for specific diseases while significantly enhancing the performance of the resulting policies.

</details>


### [322] [Entropy-Gated Selective Policy Optimization:Token-Level Gradient Allocation for Hybrid Training of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03309)
*Yuelin Hu,Zhengxue Cheng,Wei Liu,Li Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Entropy Gated Selective Policy Optimization (EGSPO) extends sample-level RLHF with token-level entropy gating to allocate PPO updates selectively; yields improvements in AIME and MATH with modest overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce variance, preserve knowledge, and promote exploration in RLHF for LLMs focusing on mathematical reasoning; address limitations of pure sample-level mixing.

Method: Three-stage: Stage 1 SFT on expert data; Stage 2 RL rollouts with per-token predictive entropy; Stage 3 entropy-gated gradient allocation for PPO: high-entropy tokens get full PPO updates; low-entropy tokens get attenuated PPO updates; both branches use advantage A_t.

Result: Consistent improvements: +3.8% on AIME, +2.9% on MATH vs CHORD phi; ~3.4% extra compute.

Conclusion: Entropy-gated, token-level modulation improves math reasoning in RLHF with manageable compute cost; demonstrates entropy estimates can guide exploration vs exploitation while preserving knowledge.

Abstract: Hybrid training methods for large language models combine supervised fine tuning (SFT) on expert demonstrations with reinforcement learning (RL) on model rollouts, typically at the sample level. We propose Entropy Gated Selective Policy Optimization (EGSPO), a three stage framework that extends sample level mixing with token level gradient modulation.
  Stage 1, SFT expert learning, establishes a reliable warm up policy using expert demonstrations with a pure SFT loss. Stage 2, RL rollout generation, samples trajectories from the current policy and computes per token predictive entropy. Stage 3, the EGSPO mechanism, applies entropy gated gradient allocation: a predictive entropy module routes high entropy tokens to full PPO updates to encourage exploration, and low entropy tokens to attenuated PPO updates to reduce variance and preserve knowledge. Critically, both branches incorporate the advantage function A_t, ensuring that incorrect trajectories receive consistent negative learning signals and preventing reinforcement of confident errors.
  EGSPO achieves consistent improvements on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, with gains of 3.8 percent on AIME and 2.9 percent on MATH over the CHORD phi baseline, while incurring only 3.4 percent additional computational overhead.

</details>


### [323] [Information-Theoretic Multi-Model Fusion for Target-Oriented Adaptive Sampling in Materials Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03319)
*Yixuan Zhang,Zhiyuan Li,Weijia He,Mian Dai,Chen Shen,Teng Long,Hongbin Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A targeted, information-theoretic adaptive sampling framework for high-dimensional, limited-budget design optimization; reframes optimization as trajectory discovery to concentrate search on target-relevant directions, achieving efficient convergence across diverse tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In high-dimensional, heterogeneous design spaces with costly measurements, full response-surface models are inefficient; need reliable progress toward target properties under budget constraints.

Method: Maintain low-entropy information state; dimension-aware information budgeting; adaptive bootstrapped distillation over a heterogeneous surrogate reservoir; structure-aware candidate manifold analysis; Kalman-inspired multi-model fusion to blend exploitation and exploration.

Result: Under a unified protocol without dataset-specific tuning, achieves improved sample efficiency and reliability across 14 materials design tasks (candidate pools 600 to 4e6; features 10 to 1e3), usually locating top regions within 100 evaluations. Additional 20D synthetic benchmarks (Ackley, Rastrigin, Schwefel) demonstrate robustness to rugged/multimodal landscapes.

Conclusion: The framework enhances sample efficiency and reliability for target-oriented discovery in high-dimensional settings, with demonstrated robustness across diverse landscapes; potential for broader deployment in accelerated materials discovery and other domains, though computational cost and scaling deserve further study.

Abstract: Target-oriented discovery under limited evaluation budgets requires making reliable progress in high-dimensional, heterogeneous design spaces where each new measurement is costly, whether experimental or high-fidelity simulation. We present an information-theoretic framework for target-oriented adaptive sampling that reframes optimization as trajectory discovery: instead of approximating the full response surface, the method maintains and refines a low-entropy information state that concentrates search on target-relevant directions. The approach couples data, model beliefs, and physics/structure priors through dimension-aware information budgeting, adaptive bootstrapped distillation over a heterogeneous surrogate reservoir, and structure-aware candidate manifold analysis with Kalman-inspired multi-model fusion to balance consensus-driven exploitation and disagreement-driven exploration. Evaluated under a single unified protocol without dataset-specific tuning, the framework improves sample efficiency and reliability across 14 single- and multi-objective materials design tasks spanning candidate pools from $600$ to $4 \times 10^6$ and feature dimensions from $10$ to $10^3$, typically reaching top-performing regions within 100 evaluations. Complementary 20-dimensional synthetic benchmarks (Ackley, Rastrigin, Schwefel) further demonstrate robustness to rugged and multimodal landscapes.

</details>


### [324] [From Inexact Gradients to Byzantine Robustness: Acceleration and Optimization under Similarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03329)
*Renaud Gaucher,Aymeric Dieuleveut,Hadrien Hendrikx*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Formulates Byzantine-robust distributed optimization as an inexact-gradient problem, enabling optimal asymptotic error with GD and two acceleration schemes (Nesterov-like and Similarity-based) that significantly reduce communication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the vulnerability of standard federated learning to Byzantine failures and the ad-hoc analyses of robust aggregations; seek a unified, scalable framework to enable accelerated and efficient robust optimization.

Method: Cast the problem as general optimization with inexact gradient oracles (including additive and multiplicative errors); prove that GD on top of robust aggregations achieves optimal asymptotic error; develop two acceleration schemes: (1) a Nesterov-type accelerated method derived from inexact-gradient results, and (2) Optimization under Similarity using an auxiliary loss approximating the global loss; analyze theoretical convergence and empirically validate reduced communication complexity.

Result: Demonstrates, both theoretically and empirically, that robust GD achieves optimal asymptotic error under Byzantine settings; the proposed acceleration schemes drastically cut communication complexity compared with prior methods while preserving convergence guarantees.

Conclusion: Robust distributed optimization can be recast as inexact-gradient optimization, enabling accelerated and communication-efficient algorithms. The two proposed schemes offer faster convergence (Nesterov-like) and reduced communication (Similarity-based), paving the way for more scalable Byzantine-robust FL methods.

Abstract: Standard federated learning algorithms are vulnerable to adversarial nodes, a.k.a. Byzantine failures. To solve this issue, robust distributed learning algorithms have been developed, which typically replace parameter averaging by robust aggregations. While generic conditions on these aggregations exist to guarantee the convergence of (Stochastic) Gradient Descent (SGD), the analyses remain rather ad-hoc. This hinders the development of more complex robust algorithms, such as accelerated ones. In this work, we show that Byzantine-robust distributed optimization can, under standard generic assumptions, be cast as a general optimization with inexact gradient oracles (with both additive and multiplicative error terms), an active field of research.
  This allows for instance to directly show that GD on top of standard robust aggregation procedures obtains optimal asymptotic error in the Byzantine setting. Going further, we propose two optimization schemes to speed up the convergence. The first one is a Nesterov-type accelerated scheme whose proof directly derives from accelerated inexact gradient results applied to our formulation. The second one hinges on Optimization under Similarity, in which the server leverages an auxiliary loss function that approximates the global loss. Both approaches allow to drastically reduce the communication complexity compared to previous methods, as we show theoretically and empirically.

</details>


### [325] [Bayesian Conformal Prediction as a Decision Risk Problem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03331)
*Fanyi Wu,Veronika Lohmanova,Samuel Kaski,Michele Caprio*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bayesian conformal prediction (BCP) uses Bayesian posterior predictive densities as non-conformity scores within a split conformal framework to minimize expected prediction set size, achieving valid coverage under misspecification with competitive set sizes and reduced variability, and improved empirical coverage in sparse regression with a misspecified prior (81% vs 49%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address robustness of predictive sets to model misspecification and distribution shift while preserving coverage; reduce variability in set size; improve coverage in sparse regression.

Method: Compute non-conformity scores from Bayesian posterior predictive densities; apply Bayesian quadrature to estimate expected prediction set size; operate within split conformal framework; evaluate on regression and classification including ImageNet-A; contrast with split conformal prediction and Bayesian credible intervals.

Result: Valid coverage guarantees in split conformal setting; empirical coverage under misspecification close to nominal; prediction sets comparable in size to split conformal; lower run-to-run variability; in sparse regression with nominal 80% coverage, empirical coverage 81%, while Bayesian credible intervals under-cover at 49%.

Conclusion: BCP offers robust, efficient predictive sets under distribution shift and mispecified priors, matching sizes of traditional conformal methods while improving stability and coverage in challenging settings.

Abstract: Bayesian posterior predictive densities as non-conformity scores and Bayesian quadrature are used to estimate and minimise the expected prediction set size. Operating within a split conformal framework, BCP provides valid coverage guarantees and demonstrates reliable empirical coverage under model misspecification. Across regression and classification tasks, including distribution-shifted settings such as ImageNet-A, BCP yields prediction sets of comparable size to split conformal prediction, while exhibiting substantially lower run-to-run variability in set size. In sparse regression with nominal coverage of 80 percent, BCP achieves 81 percent empirical coverage under a misspecified prior, whereas Bayesian credible intervals under-cover at 49 percent.

</details>


### [326] [Robustness as an Emergent Property of Task Performance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03344)
*Shir Ashury-Tahan,Ariel Gera,Elron Bandel,Michal Shmueli-Scheuer,Leshem Choshen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Robustness appears to be an emergent property that scales with task competence rather than an inherent model property; as models saturate on a task, robustness to perturbations emerges correspondingly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether robustness is an independent capability or a byproduct of task-specific competence, given conflicting views on how to improve robustness for real-world tasks.

Method: Empirical analysis across multiple models, diverse datasets, and configurations (e.g., paraphrases, temperature variations). Measures task performance and robustness to perturbations, then analyzes correlation and the extent to which robustness is determined by the task rather than the model.

Result: Strong positive correlation between task performance and robustness across models and datasets. Robustness is primarily driven by task-specific competence, not intrinsic model-level properties. Robustness appears to emerge as performance improves.

Conclusion: As new tasks saturate, robustness on these tasks is likely to emerge naturally. For researchers, measuring or improving robustness may be less critical than achieving task performance. For practitioners, reliability on easier tasks is expected, while harder tasks may still pose reliability challenges.

Abstract: Robustness is often regarded as a critical future challenge for real-world applications, where stability is essential. However, as models often learn tasks in a similar order, we hypothesize that easier tasks will be easier regardless of how they are presented to the model. Indeed, in this paper, we show that as models approach high performance on a task, robustness is effectively achieved. Through an empirical analysis of multiple models across diverse datasets and configurations (e.g., paraphrases, different temperatures), we find a strong positive correlation. Moreover, we find that robustness is primarily driven by task-specific competence rather than inherent model-level properties, challenging current approaches that treat robustness as an independent capability. Thus, from a high-level perspective, we may expect that as new tasks saturate, model robustness on these tasks will emerge accordingly. For researchers, this implies that explicit efforts to measure and improve robustness may warrant reduced emphasis, as such robustness is likely to develop alongside performance gains.
