Motivation: To efficiently train sparse neural networks by exploiting inherent sparsity and reducing computation, while ensuring convergence guarantees through a multilevel optimization setting.

Method: Integrates sparsity-inducing linearized Bregman iterations with adaptive freezing of the network structure, alternating phases of static and dynamic sparsity pattern updates; embeds the process in a multilevel optimization framework to provide convergence guarantees.

Result: Empirically demonstrates highly sparse and accurate models on standard benchmarks; theoretical FLOP count relative to SGD drops from 38% (standard Bregman iterations) to 6% with the proposed method while maintaining test accuracy.

Conclusion: The approach delivers convergence-guaranteed dynamic sparse training with substantial computational savings and preserved accuracy, highlighting the practical viability of adaptive sparsity patterns in combination with Bregman-inspired updates.

Abstract: We introduce a dynamic sparse training algorithm based on linearized Bregman iterations / mirror descent that exploits the naturally incurred sparsity by alternating between periods of static and dynamic sparsity pattern updates. The key idea is to combine sparsity-inducing Bregman iterations with adaptive freezing of the network structure to enable efficient exploration of the sparse parameter space while maintaining sparsity. We provide convergence guaranties by embedding our method in a multilevel optimization framework. Furthermore, we empirically show that our algorithm can produce highly sparse and accurate models on standard benchmarks. We also show that the theoretical number of FLOPs compared to SGD training can be reduced from 38% for standard Bregman iterations to 6% for our method while maintaining test accuracy.

</details>


### [356] [MatGPTQ: Accurate and Efficient Post-Training Matryoshka Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03537)
*Maximilian Kleinegger,Elvir Crnčević,Dan Alistarh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A post-training, multi-precision, one-shot quantization method for Matryoshka-style models (MatGPTQ) that delivers a single checkpoint capable of slicing into multiple bit-widths with maintained accuracy and improved efficiency, addressing QAT dependence and lack of open-source tooling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of the original MatQuant (expensive quantization-aware training, need for fast PTQ, lack of open-source support, and kernel limitations), enabling practical, single-checkpoint, multi-precision deployment.

Method: Introduce Post-Training Matryoshka Quantization (MatGPTQ) a PTQ pipeline that optimizes a single parent model for multiple target precisions in one pass using a small calibration set. Casts Matryoshka quantization as a multi-precision objective with bit-slicing and cross-bit error compensation, includes a budget-aware search for heterogeneous per-layer bit-widths, and provides efficient kernels for slicing and mixed-precision execution.

Result: Achieves high-bit accuracy while delivering substantial gains at low bit-widths across standard LLMs and benchmarks; establishes a new state-of-the-art for Matryoshka-style PTQ; enables single-checkpoint, multi-precision deployment; code released at the given repository.

Conclusion: MatGPTQ makes Matryoshka-style quantization practical and open-source, enabling flexible, memory- and latency-aware multi-precision inference from a single checkpoint.

Abstract: Matryoshka Quantization (MatQuant) is a recent quantization approach showing that a single integer-quantized model can be served across multiple precisions, by slicing the most significant bits (MSB) at inference time. This enables a single checkpoint to cover a wide range of memory and latency budgets, but renders quantization much more challenging. In particular, the initial MatQuant relies on expensive quantization-aware training (QAT) variants, rather than fast one-shot post training quantization (PTQ), and lacks open-source and kernel support. We address all of these limitations by introducing Post-Training Matryoshka Quantization (MatGPTQ), a new PTQ pipeline that produces a single parent model jointly optimized for multiple target precisions in one-shot, based on a small calibration set. MatGPTQ casts Matryoshka quantization as a multi-precision objective with bit-slicing and cross-bit error compensation, resulting in an algorithm that produces a multi-bit-width, "sliceable" model in a single pass. We also incorporate a new budget-aware search for heterogeneous per-layer bit-witdhs and provide efficient kernels that implement slicing and mixed-precision execution. Across standard LLMs and benchmarks, MatGPTQ preserves high-bit accuracy while substantially improving performance at low-bit-witdh settings. Overall, we establish a new state of the art for Matryoshka-style post-training quantization and make single-checkpoint, multi-precision deployment open and practical. Code is available at https://github.com/IST-DASLab/MatGPTQ.

</details>


### [357] [Equilibrium Propagation for Non-Conservative Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03670)
*Antonino Emanuele Scurria,Dimitri Vanden Abeele,Bortolo Matteo Mognetti,Serge Massar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extend Equilibrium Propagation (EP) to nonconservative systems by modifying learning dynamics with a term tied to non-reciprocal interactions, enabling exact gradient computation; augmented variational formulation; MNIST experiments show improved speed and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address EP's limitation to energy-based (conservative) systems and enable exact gradient learning for nonreciprocal dynamics, including feedforward networks.

Method: Introduce a learning-phase term proportional to the non-reciprocal part of the interaction to recover the exact gradient; provide a variational formulation using an augmented state-space energy function; demonstrate generality to arbitrary nonconservative systems.

Result: MNIST experiments show better performance and faster learning than previous proposals.

Conclusion: EP can be generalized to nonconservative dynamics with exact gradient learning; the variational/augmented formulation provides a principled basis and practical algorithm.

Abstract: Equilibrium Propagation (EP) is a physics-inspired learning algorithm that uses stationary states of a dynamical system both for inference and learning. In its original formulation it is limited to conservative systems, $\textit{i.e.}$ to dynamics which derive from an energy function. Given their importance in applications, it is important to extend EP to nonconservative systems, $\textit{i.e.}$ systems with non-reciprocal interactions. Previous attempts to generalize EP to such systems failed to compute the exact gradient of the cost function. Here we propose a framework that extends EP to arbitrary nonconservative systems, including feedforward networks. We keep the key property of equilibrium propagation, namely the use of stationary states both for inference and learning. However, we modify the dynamics in the learning phase by a term proportional to the non-reciprocal part of the interaction so as to obtain the exact gradient of the cost function. This algorithm can also be derived using a variational formulation that generates the learning dynamics through an energy function defined over an augmented state space. Numerical experiments using the MNIST database show that this algorithm achieves better performance and learns faster than previous proposals.

</details>


### [358] [How to Train Your Resistive Network: Generalized Equilibrium Propagation and Analytical Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03546)
*Jonathan Lin,Aman Desai,Frank Barrows,Francesco Caravelli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Exact gradient computation for Kirchhoff-based analog networks; Generalized Equilibrium Propagation framework; enables local, energy-efficient learning in resistor networks with output-layer readout only and subset resistance updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the energy inefficiency of digital ML hardware by enabling accurate training of analog, locality-constrained systems using physically plausible learning rules.

Method: Graph-theoretic and analytical framework leveraging Kirchhoff's laws to compute gradients exactly; introduces Generalized Equilibrium Propagation (GEP) as a unifying framework for Hebbian-like local learning (including Equilibrium Propagation and Coupled Learning); numerical simulations on resistor networks.

Result: Demonstrates training of resistor networks without full-field replica or full-network readout; updates can be restricted to output layer or a subset of resistances with only modest performance degradation.

Conclusion: The analytical gradient approach and GEP provide a viable pathway for energy-efficient, locally constrained training of analog hardware, with practical flexibility in what parameters are updated.

Abstract: Machine learning is a powerful method of extracting meaning from data; unfortunately, current digital hardware is extremely energy-intensive. There is interest in an alternative analog computing implementation that could match the performance of traditional machine learning while being significantly more energy-efficient. However, it remains unclear how to train such analog computing systems while adhering to locality constraints imposed by the physical (as opposed to digital) nature of these systems. Local learning algorithms such as Equilibrium Propagation and Coupled Learning have been proposed to address this issue. In this paper, we develop an algorithm to exactly calculate gradients using a graph theoretic and analytical framework for Kirchhoff's laws. We also introduce Generalized Equilibrium Propagation, a framework encompassing a broad class of Hebbian learning algorithms, including Coupled Learning and Equilibrium Propagation, and show how our algorithm compares. We demonstrate our algorithm using numerical simulations and show that we can train resistor networks without the need for a replica or readout over all resistors, only at the output layer. We also show that under the analytical gradient approach, it is possible to update only a subset of the resistance values without a strong degradation in performance.

</details>


### [359] [ContraLog: Log File Anomaly Detection with Contrastive Learning and Masked Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03678)
*Simon Dietz,Kai Klede,An Nguyen,Bjoern M Eskofier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ContraLog reframes log anomaly detection by predicting continuous embeddings of log messages without parsers, using joint message and sequence encoders trained with masked LM and contrastive learning; achieves strong results on HDFS, BGL, Thunderbird; embeddings themselves are anomaly-predictive even without sequence context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome reliance on log parsers that discard semantic content and to leverage rich embedding information for anomaly detection.

Method: Two-tower architecture: a message encoder for per-message embeddings and a sequence encoder for temporal dependencies. Training uses masked language modeling plus contrastive learning to predict masked embeddings from context; parser-free, self-supervised.

Result: Empirical evaluation on HDFS, BGL, Thunderbird shows effective anomaly detection in complex datasets; message embeddings carry meaningful information and show anomaly predictiveness even without sequence context.

Conclusion: Embedding-level prediction is a viable approach for log anomaly detection and may generalize to other event sequences.

Abstract: Log files record computational events that reflect system state and behavior, making them a primary source of operational insights in modern computer systems. Automated anomaly detection on logs is therefore critical, yet most established methods rely on log parsers that collapse messages into discrete templates, discarding variable values and semantic content. We propose ContraLog, a parser-free and self-supervised method that reframes log anomaly detection as predicting continuous message embeddings rather than discrete template IDs. ContraLog combines a message encoder that produces rich embeddings for individual log messages with a sequence encoder to model temporal dependencies within sequences. The model is trained with a combination of masked language modeling and contrastive learning to predict masked message embeddings based on the surrounding context. Experiments on the HDFS, BGL, and Thunderbird benchmark datasets empirically demonstrate effectiveness on complex datasets with diverse log messages. Additionally, we find that message embeddings generated by ContraLog carry meaningful information and are predictive of anomalies even without sequence context. These results highlight embedding-level prediction as an approach for log anomaly detection, with potential applicability to other event sequences.

</details>


### [360] [Universal One-third Time Scaling in Learning Peaked Distributions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03685)
*Yizhou Liu,Ziming Liu,Cengiz Pehlevan,Jeff Gore*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Softmax and cross-entropy inherently cause power-law convergence in LLM training, yielding a universal 1/3 exponent for loss scaling with time, explaining neural scaling and suggesting efficiency gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain the origin of slow, power-law loss convergence observed during training of large language models and connect it to fundamental components of the loss (softmax plus cross-entropy).

Method: Systematic analysis of toy models and empirical evaluation on LLMs, focusing on peaked next-token distributions and the interaction of softmax with cross-entropy.

Result: Power-law vanishing of losses and gradients arises intrinsically from softmax+cross-entropy; universal time-scaling exponent of 1/3; provides a mechanistic account of neural scaling and identifies a bottleneck in training efficiency.

Conclusion: Offers a mechanistic explanation for neural scaling and points to new directions to improve LLM training efficiency by mitigating this intrinsic bottleneck or rethinking the loss formulation.

Abstract: Training large language models (LLMs) is computationally expensive, partly because the loss exhibits slow power-law convergence whose origin remains debatable. Through systematic analysis of toy models and empirical evaluation of LLMs, we show that this behavior can arise intrinsically from the use of softmax and cross-entropy. When learning peaked probability distributions, e.g., next-token distributions, these components yield power-law vanishing losses and gradients, creating a fundamental optimization bottleneck. This ultimately leads to power-law time scaling of the loss with a universal exponent of $1/3$. Our results provide a mechanistic explanation for observed neural scaling and suggest new directions for improving LLM training efficiency.

</details>


### [361] [NPCNet: Navigator-Driven Pseudo Text for Deep Clustering of Early Sepsis Phenotyping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03562)
*Pi-Ju Tsai,Charkkri Limbud,Kuan-Fu Chen,Yi-Ju Tseng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NPCNet is a deep clustering method with a target navigator that uses temporal EHRs to discover clinically meaningful sepsis phenotypes (α, β, γ, δ) with distinct SOFA trajectories, enabling better-aligned treatment decisions such as early vasopressor use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sepsis is heterogeneous; existing clustering often ignores clinical relevance, hindering identification of phenotypes with meaningful differences in trajectory and treatment response.

Method: Proposes NPCNet, a deep clustering network with a target navigator that integrates temporal Electronic Health Records to align phenotypes with clinical significance, identifying four phenotypes (α, β, γ, δ) with divergent SOFA trajectories and evaluating treatment effects.

Result: NPCNet differentiates patients who are likely to improve (α) from those at risk of deterioration (δ) despite similar early severity; phenotypes α, β, and δ may benefit from early vasopressor administration; overall, NPCNet enhances precision treatment by uncovering clinically distinct phenotypes.

Conclusion: NPCNet provides a clinically meaningful sepsis phenotyping framework that links trajectory-based phenotypes to potential treatment strategies, supporting precision medicine in sepsis care.

Abstract: Sepsis is a heterogeneous syndrome. Identifying clinically distinct phenotypes may enable more precise treatment strategies. In recent years, many researchers have applied clustering algorithms to sepsis patients. However, the clustering process rarely incorporates clinical relevance, potentially limiting to reflect clinically distinct phenotypes. We propose NPCNet, a novel deep clustering network with a target navigator that integrates temporal Electronic Health Records (EHRs) to better align sepsis phenotypes with clinical significance. We identify four sepsis phenotypes ($α$, $β$, $γ$, and $δ$) with divergence in SOFA trajectories. Notably, while $α$ and $δ$ phenotypes both show severe conditions in the early stage, NPCNet effectively differentiates patients who are likely to improve ($α$) from those at risk of deterioration ($δ$). Furthermore, through the treatment effect analysis, we discover that $α$, $β$, and $δ$ phenotypes may benefit from early vasopressor administration. The results show that NPCNet enhances precision treatment strategies by uncovering clinically distinct phenotypes.

</details>


### [362] [QuAIL: Quality-Aware Inertial Learning for Robust Training under Data Corruption](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03686)
*Mattia Sabella,Alberto Archetti,Pietro Pinoli,Matteo Matteucci,Cinzia Cappiello*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QuAIL introduces a learnable feature-modulation layer with a quality-aware proximal regularizer to robustify tabular models against column-level corruption without data repair, improving performance across many datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world tabular data often suffers non-uniform, feature-dependent corruption. Instance-level quality annotations are rare; leveraging column-level reliability priors can guide robust learning without costly data cleaning.

Method: Add a learnable feature-modulation layer to models and constrain its updates via a quality-dependent proximal regularizer, enabling selective feature adaptation based on reliability. No explicit data repair or sample-level reweighting.

Result: Empirical evaluation on 50 classification and regression datasets shows QuAIL consistently improves average performance over neural baselines under both random and value-dependent corruption, with strong robustness in low-data and systematically biased settings.

Conclusion: Incorporating feature reliability priors directly into optimization dynamics is a practical, effective approach for resilient tabular learning, reducing the need for data repair while improving robustness across diverse corruptions.

Abstract: Tabular machine learning systems are frequently trained on data affected by non-uniform corruption, including noisy measurements, missing entries, and feature-specific biases. In practice, these defects are often documented only through column-level reliability indicators rather than instance-wise quality annotations, limiting the applicability of many robustness and cleaning techniques. We present QuAIL, a quality-informed training mechanism that incorporates feature reliability priors directly into the learning process. QuAIL augments existing models with a learnable feature-modulation layer whose updates are selectively constrained by a quality-dependent proximal regularizer, thereby inducing controlled adaptation across features of varying trustworthiness. This stabilizes optimization under structured corruption without explicit data repair or sample-level reweighting. Empirical evaluation across 50 classification and regression datasets demonstrates that QuAIL consistently improves average performance over neural baselines under both random and value-dependent corruption, with especially robust behavior in low-data and systematically biased settings. These results suggest that incorporating feature reliability information directly into optimization dynamics is a practical and effective approach for resilient tabular learning.

</details>


### [363] [CoGenCast: A Coupled Autoregressive-Flow Generative Framework for Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03564)
*Yaguo Liu,Mingyue Cheng,Daoyu Wang,Xiaoyu Tao,Qi Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoGenCast is a hybrid time-series forecasting framework that marries pretrained decoder-only LLMs with a flow-matching mechanism. It reconfigures LLMs into an encoder-decoder backbone by altering attention, enabling bidirectional context encoding and causal generation, and uses flow-matching to model continuous temporal dynamics conditioned on the generated representation, supporting multimodal and cross-domain forecasting with improved accuracy across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Time-series forecasting requires both semantic understanding of contextual conditions and accurate stochastic modeling of continuous dynamics. Existing approaches typically specialize in either semantics (autoregressive LLMs) or continuous generation (diffusion-like models) and fail to capture both aspects simultaneously.

Method: Repurpose pre-trained decoder-only LLMs into a native forecasting encoder-decoder by modifying attention topology to enable bidirectional context encoding and causal representation generation. Integrate a flow-matching mechanism to model temporal evolution conditioned on the autoregressively generated representations, enabling continuous stochastic dynamics. Design supports multimodal forecasting and cross-domain unified training.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks show that CoGenCast consistently outperforms previous baselines.

Conclusion: A hybrid framework that couples LLM-based semantic context modeling with flow-based continuous dynamics improves time-series forecasting, with the added benefits of multimodal and cross-domain applicability; code is released.

Abstract: Time series forecasting can be viewed as a generative problem that requires both semantic understanding over contextual conditions and stochastic modeling of continuous temporal dynamics. Existing approaches typically rely on either autoregressive large language models (LLMs) for semantic context modeling or diffusion-like models for continuous probabilistic generation. However, neither method alone can adequately model both aspects simultaneously. In this work, we propose CoGenCast, a hybrid generative framework that couples pre-trained LLMs with flow-matching mechanism for effective time series forecasting. Specifically, we reconfigure pre-trained decoder-only LLMs into a native forecasting encoder-decoder backbone by modifying only the attention topology, enabling bidirectional context encoding and causal representation generation. Building on this, a flow-matching mechanism is further integrated to model temporal evolution, capturing continuous stochastic dynamics conditioned on the autoregressively generated representation. Notably, CoGenCast naturally supports multimodal forecasting and cross-domain unified training. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks show that CoGenCast consistently outperforms previous compared baselines. Code is available at https://github.com/liuyaguo/_CoGenCast.

</details>


### [364] [LLM-Inspired Pretrain-Then-Finetune for Small-Data, Large-Scale Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03690)
*Zishi Zhang,Jinhui Han,Ming Hu,Yijie Peng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pretrain-then-finetune Transformer using domain-informed synthetic data plus real observations to solve small-data, large-scale operational decisions; provides nonasymptotic guarantees and an economies-of-scale for fine-tuning; requires tailored architecture, not a generic LLM application.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address operations management problems with many simultaneous decisions when per-instance data are scarce or noisy, by leveraging cross-task structure and high-capacity models. Extend the successful pretrain-finetune paradigm (as with LLMs) to decision environments with domain knowledge encoded in synthetic data.

Method: Design problem-specific Transformer architecture; pretrain on large-scale synthetic data that encodes managerial knowledge and structural features; fine-tune on real observations; develop a nonasymptotic error analysis showing how pretraining and fine-tuning contribute to performance, with an economies-of-scale effect: transfer learning becomes more effective as the number of instances grows.

Result: Theoretical guarantees validating the approach; performance is governed by the better of pretraining or fine-tuning; finetuning exhibits economies-of-scale, improving transfer learning as instance count increases; the method effectively extracts cross-task structure and is not an off-the-shelf LLM application.

Conclusion: A principled, tailored pretrain-then-finetune pipeline for decision problems that injects domain knowledge via synthetic data and adapts to real data, supported by nonasymptotic guarantees and an economies-of-scale advantage for fine-tuning.

Abstract: We consider small-data, large-scale decision problems in which a firm must make many operational decisions simultaneously (e.g., across a large product portfolio) while observing only a few, potentially noisy, data points per instance. Inspired by the success of large language models (LLMs), we propose a pretrain-then-finetune approach built on a designed Transformer model to address this challenge. The model is first pretrained on large-scale, domain-informed synthetic data that encode managerial knowledge and structural features of the decision environment, and is then fine-tuned on real observations. This new pipeline offers two complementary advantages: pretraining injects domain knowledge into the learning process and enables the training of high-capacity models using abundant synthetic data, while finetuning adapts the pretrained model to the operational environment and improves alignment with the true data-generating regime. While we have leveraged the Transformer's state-of-the-art representational capacity, particularly its attention mechanism, to efficiently extract cross-task structure, our approach is not an off-the-shelf application. Instead, it relies on problem-specific architectural design and a tailored training procedure to match the decision setting. Theoretically, we develop the first comprehensive error analysis regarding Transformer learning in relevant contexts, establishing nonasymptotic guarantees that validate the method's effectiveness. Critically, our analysis reveals how pretraining and fine-tuning jointly determine performance, with the dominant contribution governed by whichever is more favorable. In particular, finetuning exhibits an economies-of-scale effect, whereby transfer learning becomes increasingly effective as the number of instances grows.

</details>


### [365] [Riemannian Neural Optimal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03566)
*Alessandro Micheli,Yueqi Cao,Anthea Monod,Samir Bhatt*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Continuous, geometry-aware neural OT on manifolds (RNOT) avoids discretization and achieves sub-exponential dimension scaling, outperforming discretization-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neural OT approaches on manifolds rely on discretization, suffering the curse of dimensionality; extending OT to high-dimensional manifolds requires methods that respect manifold geometry while remaining scalable.

Method: Introduce Riemannian Neural OT (RNOT) maps: continuous neural-network parameterizations of OT maps on manifolds that do not discretize the domain and encode geometry by construction.

Result: Theoretical guarantee: RNOT maps approximate Riemannian OT maps with sub-exponential complexity in dimension under mild regularity. Empirical results on synthetic/real data show improved scalability and competitive performance relative to discretization-based baselines.

Conclusion: RNOT provides a scalable, geometry-consistent neural OT framework for manifolds, mitigating the curse of dimensionality and enabling practical high-dimensional manifold OT.

Abstract: Computational optimal transport (OT) offers a principled framework for generative modeling. Neural OT methods, which use neural networks to learn an OT map (or potential) from data in an amortized way, can be evaluated out of sample after training, but existing approaches are tailored to Euclidean geometry. Extending neural OT to high-dimensional Riemannian manifolds remains an open challenge. In this paper, we prove that any method for OT on manifolds that produces discrete approximations of transport maps necessarily suffers from the curse of dimensionality: achieving a fixed accuracy requires a number of parameters that grows exponentially with the manifold dimension. Motivated by this limitation, we introduce Riemannian Neural OT (RNOT) maps, which are continuous neural-network parameterizations of OT maps on manifolds that avoid discretization and incorporate geometric structure by construction. Under mild regularity assumptions, we prove that RNOT maps approximate Riemannian OT maps with sub-exponential complexity in the dimension. Experiments on synthetic and real datasets demonstrate improved scalability and competitive performance relative to discretization-based baselines.

</details>


### [366] [Anytime Pretraining: Horizon-Free Learning-Rate Schedules with Weight Averaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03702)
*Alexandru Meterez,Pranav Ajit Nair,Depen Morwani,Cengiz Pehlevan,Sham Kakade*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Anytime learning schedules exist for overparameterized linear regression; weight averaging (model merging) enables minimax convergence with horizon-free, polynomially decaying steps; empirically, weight averaging with simple schedules matches cosine LR in final loss for 150M–300M parameter LLM pretraining across 1–32x Chinchilla scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill the gap for horizon-free training in continual/open-ended LLM pretraining by establishing theoretical guarantees for anytime schedules and clarifying the role of weight averaging in achieving minimax rates.

Method: Theoretical analysis of overparameterized linear regression to prove existence of anytime schedules and the central role of weight averaging; deriving the decay rate from source and capacity conditions. Empirical evaluation on 150M and 300M parameter language models trained across various scales, comparing constant LR with weight averaging and 1/sqrt(t) with weight averaging against cosine LR.

Result: Proves the existence of horizon-free anytime schedules and shows weight averaging achieves minimax convergence rates; decay is polynomial in time with rate determined by problem conditions. Empirically, anytime schedules with weight averaging attain final losses comparable to cosine schedules across the entire training horizon.

Conclusion: Weight averaging combined with horizon-free step sizes is a practical, effective alternative to cosine learning rate schedules for large language model pretraining, offering a simple approach to anytime training.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly trained in continual or open-ended settings, where the total training horizon is not known in advance. Despite this, most existing pretraining recipes are not anytime: they rely on horizon-dependent learning rate schedules and extensive tuning under a fixed compute budget. In this work, we provide a theoretical analysis demonstrating the existence of anytime learning schedules for overparameterized linear regression, and we highlight the central role of weight averaging - also known as model merging - in achieving the minimax convergence rates of stochastic gradient descent. We show that these anytime schedules polynomially decay with time, with the decay rate determined by the source and capacity conditions of the problem. Empirically, we evaluate 150M and 300M parameter language models trained at 1-32x Chinchilla scale, comparing constant learning rates with weight averaging and $1/\sqrt{t}$ schedules with weight averaging against a well-tuned cosine schedule. Across the full training range, the anytime schedules achieve comparable final loss to cosine decay. Taken together, our results suggest that weight averaging combined with simple, horizon-free step sizes offers a practical and effective anytime alternative to cosine learning rate schedules for large language model pretraining.

</details>


### [367] [Asymmetric Hierarchical Anchoring for Audio-Visual Joint Representation: Resolving Information Allocation Ambiguity for Robust Cross-Modal Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03570)
*Bixing Wu,Yuhong Zhao,Zongli Ye,Jiachen Lian,Xiangyu Yue,Gopala Anumanchipalli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Asymmetric Hierarchical Anchoring (AHA) improves cross-modal generalization by enforcing directional, hierarchical semantic anchoring using audio RVQ and a GRL-based decoupler with Local Sliding Alignment; yields state-of-the-art cross-modal transfer on AVE/AVVP and shows better disentanglement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Symmetric cross-modal frameworks suffer information allocation ambiguity and semantic leakage between modalities due to lack of inductive bias; a directional, structured approach is needed to align semantics across modalities while preserving modality-specific purity.

Method: Introduce Asymmetric Hierarchical Anchoring (AHA) with a shared hierarchical semantic anchor. Utilize audio Residual Vector Quantization (RVQ) to induce hierarchical discrete representations guiding video feature distillation into a common semantic space. Replace mutual information estimators with a GRL-based adversarial decoupler to suppress modality-specific leakage. Add Local Sliding Alignment (LSA) for fine-grained temporal alignment across modalities.

Result: Extensive experiments on AVE and AVVP show AHA consistently outperforms symmetric baselines in cross-modal transfer. Additional analysis on talking-face disentanglement indicates improved semantic consistency and disentanglement, supporting broader applicability of the framework.

Conclusion: AHA provides a robust directional framework for CMG, reducing leakage and improving cross-modal transfer through hierarchical anchoring, RVQ-guided distillation, adversarial decoupling, and temporal alignment.

Abstract: Audio-visual joint representation learning under Cross-Modal Generalization (CMG) aims to transfer knowledge from a labeled source modality to an unlabeled target modality through a unified discrete representation space. Existing symmetric frameworks often suffer from information allocation ambiguity, where the absence of structural inductive bias leads to semantic-specific leakage across modalities. We propose Asymmetric Hierarchical Anchoring (AHA), which enforces directional information allocation by designating a structured semantic anchor within a shared hierarchy. In our instantiation, we exploit the hierarchical discrete representations induced by audio Residual Vector Quantization (RVQ) to guide video feature distillation into a shared semantic space. To ensure representational purity, we replace fragile mutual information estimators with a GRL-based adversarial decoupler that explicitly suppresses semantic leakage in modality-specific branches, and introduce Local Sliding Alignment (LSA) to encourage fine-grained temporal alignment across modalities. Extensive experiments on AVE and AVVP benchmarks demonstrate that AHA consistently outperforms symmetric baselines in cross-modal transfer. Additional analyses on talking-face disentanglement experiment further validate that the learned representations exhibit improved semantic consistency and disentanglement, indicating the broader applicability of the proposed framework.

</details>


### [368] [Decision-oriented benchmarking to transform AI weather forecast access: Application to the Indian monsoon](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03767)
*Rajat Masiwal,Colin Aitken,Adam Marchakitus,Mayank Gupta,Katherine Kowal,Hamid A. Pahlavan,Tyler Yang,Y. Qiang Sun,Michael Kremer,Amir Jina,William R. Boos,Pedram Hassanzadeh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A decision-oriented benchmarking framework for AI-based weather prediction (AIWP), demonstrated on Indian monsoon onset forecasting, linking meteorology, AI, and social science with potential large-scale policy impact.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AIWP evaluations emphasize meteorological metrics and computational efficiency, but neglect decision-relevant, operational needs of vulnerable populations. A framework integrating meteorology, AI, and social sciences is needed to translate predictive skill into actionable risk management.

Method: Proposes a decision-oriented benchmarking framework and applies it to the Indian monsoon problem. Uses both deterministic and probabilistic metrics to assess regional onset index predictions out-of-sample. Demonstrates an agriculturally relevant, regionally-leveraged forecast with a long lead time; informs a government initiative to issue forecasts to 38 million farmers in 2025.

Result: AIWP models skillfully predict a regionally relevant onset index weeks in advance when evaluated out-of-sample. The framework supported a policy deployment to deliver AI-based monsoon onset forecasts to 38 million farmers in India, capturing a notable weeks-long pause in monsoon progression.

Conclusion: A decision-oriented benchmarking framework is a critical component for harnessing AIWP models to help vulnerable populations adapt to weather shocks under climate variability and change, enabling scalable, governance-relevant use of AI-driven forecasts.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence weather prediction (AIWP) models now often outperform traditional physics-based models on common metrics while requiring orders-of-magnitude less computing resources and time. Open-access AIWP models thus hold promise as transformational tools for helping low- and middle-income populations make decisions in the face of high-impact weather shocks. Yet, current approaches to evaluating AIWP models focus mainly on aggregated meteorological metrics without considering local stakeholders' needs in decision-oriented, operational frameworks. Here, we introduce such a framework that connects meteorology, AI, and social sciences. As an example, we apply it to the 150-year-old problem of Indian monsoon forecasting, focusing on benefits to rain-fed agriculture, which is highly susceptible to climate change. AIWP models skillfully predict an agriculturally relevant onset index at regional scales weeks in advance when evaluated out-of-sample using deterministic and probabilistic metrics. This framework informed a government-led effort in 2025 to send 38 million Indian farmers AI-based monsoon onset forecasts, which captured an unusual weeks-long pause in monsoon progression. This decision-oriented benchmarking framework provides a key component of a blueprint for harnessing the power of AIWP models to help large vulnerable populations adapt to weather shocks in the face of climate variability and change.

</details>


### [369] [Optimization and Generation in Aerodynamics Inverse Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03582)
*Huaguan Chen,Ning Lin,Luxi Chen,Rui Zhang,Wenbing Huang,Chongxuan Li,Hao Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified inverse-design framework that links optimization and guided generation via optimal design point and distribution, introducing a new cost-predictor loss, density-gradient optimization to preserve plausible geometries, and an efficient high-dimensional covariance estimator. Validated on 2D and 3D aerodynamic benchmarks with simulations and physical prototypes, showing improvements in optimization and guided generation, with supporting offline RL results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inverse design with physics-based objectives is hard due to the coupling of high-dimensional geometry and expensive simulations. Existing methods struggle to achieve efficient optimization while maintaining plausible designs; a unified theory connecting optimization and generative design can improve performance and practicality.

Method: (1) Recast inverse design into two canonical solutions: optimal design point (optimization) and optimal design distribution (guided generation). (2) Propose a new training loss for cost predictors (used to forecast objective values) and a density-gradient optimization algorithm that improves objectives while preserving plausible shapes. (3) Unified view of existing training-free guided generation methods. (4) Develop a time- and memory-efficient algorithm to approximate high-dimensional conditional covariance to address limitations of current methods. (5) Empirical validation on controlled 2D tasks and high-fidelity 3D aerodynamic benchmarks (car and aircraft) using OpenFOAM and 3D-printed prototypes; offline RL experiments.

Result: The approach yields consistent gains in both optimization and guided generation across 2D and 3D aerodynamic tasks. The covariance-estimation method scales to high dimensions and enables better approximation of conditional covariances in guided generation. Experimental validation via OpenFOAM simulations and wind-tunnel-like 3D-printed prototypes corroborates practical improvements in drag-reduction objectives. Offline RL results further support the method's generality.

Conclusion: A cohesive framework that unifies optimization and guided generation for inverse design with physics-based objectives. It introduces scalable loss functions and density-gradient optimization, plus an efficient covariance-estimator to improve high-dimensional conditional relationships, demonstrated across simulations and physical experiments in aerodynamics with broader implication for data-driven design and offline reinforcement learning.

Abstract: Inverse design with physics-based objectives is challenging because it couples high-dimensional geometry with expensive simulations, as exemplified by aerodynamic shape optimization for drag reduction. We revisit inverse design through two canonical solutions, the optimal design point and the optimal design distribution, and relate them to optimization and guided generation. Building on this view, we propose a new training loss for cost predictors and a density-gradient optimization method that improves objectives while preserving plausible shapes. We further unify existing training-free guided generation methods. To address their inability to approximate conditional covariance in high dimensions, we develop a time- and memory-efficient algorithm for approximate covariance estimation. Experiments on a controlled 2D study and high-fidelity 3D aerodynamic benchmarks (car and aircraft), validated by OpenFOAM simulations and miniature wind-tunnel tests with 3D-printed prototypes, demonstrate consistent gains in both optimization and guided generation. Additional offline RL results further support the generality of our approach.

</details>


### [370] [UniGeM: Unifying Data Mixing and Selection via Geometric Exploration and Mining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03772)
*Changhao Wang,Yunfei Yu,Xinhao Yao,Jiaolong Yang,Riccardo Cantoro,Chaobo Li,Qing Cui,Jun Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UniGeM unifies data mixing and selection for LLM data curation as a manifold approximation problem, using a two-stage hierarchical process (Macro-Exploration and Micro-Mining) without training proxies or external datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data quality is the main bottleneck in scaling LLMs; decoupled mixing and selection can disrupt code corpora and integrity, and reliance on proxy models or external references is undesirable.

Method: A hierarchical framework: Macro-Exploration learns mixing weights via stability-based clustering; Micro-Mining filters high-quality instances by their geometric distribution to preserve logical consistency; operates without training proxies or external datasets.

Result: Validated on 8B and 16B MoE models trained on 100B tokens; achieves ~2.0x data efficiency over a random baseline and outperforms state-of-the-art methods in reasoning-heavy tasks and multilingual generalization.

Conclusion: Data curation can be effectively formulated as a manifold approximation problem; UniGeM offers data-efficient, robust curation without proxy models, with demonstrated gains in reasoning and multilingual capabilities.

Abstract: The scaling of Large Language Models (LLMs) is increasingly limited by data quality. Most methods handle data mixing and sample selection separately, which can break the structure in code corpora. We introduce \textbf{UniGeM}, a framework that unifies mixing and selection by treating data curation as a \textit{manifold approximation} problem without training proxy models or relying on external reference datasets. UniGeM operates hierarchically: \textbf{Macro-Exploration} learns mixing weights with stability-based clustering; \textbf{Micro-Mining} filters high-quality instances by their geometric distribution to ensure logical consistency. Validated by training 8B and 16B MoE models on 100B tokens, UniGeM achieves \textbf{2.0$\times$ data efficiency} over a random baseline and further improves overall performance compared to SOTA methods in reasoning-heavy evaluations and multilingual generalization.

</details>


### [371] [Reward Redistribution for CVaR MDPs using a Bellman Operator on L-infinity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03778)
*Aneri Muni,Vincent Taboga,Esther Derman,Pierre-Luc Bacon,Erick Delage*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Augmentation-based static CVaR for MDPs yields a contracting Bellman operator over all bounded value functions, enabling risk-averse value iteration and Q-learning with discretized augmented states, with convergence guarantees and discretization error bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static CVaR lacks a recursive Bellman structure and standard augmentation often causes sparse rewards and degenerate fixed points; a formulation with dense per-step rewards and global contraction is desired to apply dynamic programming and RL methods.

Method: Introduce an augmented state approach for CVaR, discretize the augmentation, derive a Bellman operator with dense per-step rewards, prove contraction on the space of bounded value functions, develop risk-averse value iteration and model-free Q-learning using discretized augmented states, and establish convergence and discretization error bounds.

Result: Proposed algorithms converge and learn CVaR-sensitive policies; empirical results show effective trade-offs between performance and safety via CVaR-awareness.

Conclusion: The augmentation-based CVaR formulation overcomes issues of sparse rewards and non-contractive dynamics in static CVaR RL, enabling tractable, convergent risk-averse algorithms with guarantees and practical CVaR-aware behavior.

Abstract: Tail-end risk measures such as static conditional value-at-risk (CVaR) are used in safety-critical applications to prevent rare, yet catastrophic events. Unlike risk-neutral objectives, the static CVaR of the return depends on entire trajectories without admitting a recursive Bellman decomposition in the underlying Markov decision process. A classical resolution relies on state augmentation with a continuous variable. However, unless restricted to a specialized class of admissible value functions, this formulation induces sparse rewards and degenerate fixed points. In this work, we propose a novel formulation of the static CVaR objective based on augmentation. Our alternative approach leads to a Bellman operator with: (1) dense per-step rewards; (2) contracting properties on the full space of bounded value functions. Building on this theoretical foundation, we develop risk-averse value iteration and model-free Q-learning algorithms that rely on discretized augmented states. We further provide convergence guarantees and approximation error bounds due to discretization. Empirical results demonstrate that our algorithms successfully learn CVaR-sensitive policies and achieve effective performance-safety trade-offs.

</details>


### [372] [SAGE-5GC: Security-Aware Guidelines for Evaluating Anomaly Detection in the 5G Core Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03596)
*Cristian Manca,Christian Scano,Giorgio Piras,Fabio Brau,Maura Pintor,Battista Biggio*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Security-aware evaluation of ML-based anomaly detectors for 5G Core networks (SAGE-5GC) that assesses robustness under adversarial, non-IID, and realistic conditions using a genetic-algorithm-based attacker within firewall of controllable features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional anomaly detector evaluations assume IID data and static attackers, which do not reflect real 5G core environments. There is a need for methodologies that evaluate detectors under realistic deployment, including adaptive adversaries that preserve malicious functionality.

Method: Introduce SAGE-5GC guidelines for evaluating anomaly detectors in 5G Core networks. Use a realistic 5G Core dataset to train multiple detectors and assess baseline against PFCP-based control-plane attacks. Extend evaluation to adversarial settings by perturbing attacker-controllable features within operational constraints. Analyze model sensitivity to perturbations and attacker robustness. Develop a GA-based optimization strategy that operates solely on attacker-controllable features without prior knowledge of the detector.

Result: Adversarially crafted attacks significantly degrade detecting performance, demonstrating vulnerability of detectors under adaptive adversaries and the necessity for security-aware evaluation.

Conclusion: Robust, security-aware evaluation methodologies are essential for anomaly detection in 5G networks deployed in real-world environments; SAGE-5GC provides practical guidelines and a framework to stress-test detectors against adaptive threats.

Abstract: Machine learning-based anomaly detection systems are increasingly being adopted in 5G Core networks to monitor complex, high-volume traffic. However, most existing approaches are evaluated under strong assumptions that rarely hold in operational environments, notably the availability of independent and identically distributed (IID) data and the absence of adaptive attackers.In this work, we study the problem of detecting 5G attacks \textit{in the wild}, focusing on realistic deployment settings. We propose a set of Security-Aware Guidelines for Evaluating anomaly detectors in 5G Core Network (SAGE-5GC), driven by domain knowledge and consideration of potential adversarial threats. Using a realistic 5G Core dataset, we first train several anomaly detectors and assess their baseline performance against standard 5GC control-plane cyberattacks targeting PFCP-based network services.We then extend the evaluation to adversarial settings, where an attacker tries to manipulate the observable features of the network traffic to evade detection, under the constraint that the intended functionality of the malicious traffic is preserved. Starting from a selected set of controllable features, we analyze model sensitivity and adversarial robustness through randomized perturbations. Finally, we introduce a practical optimization strategy based on genetic algorithms that operates exclusively on attacker-controllable features and does not require prior knowledge of the underlying detection model. Our experimental results show that adversarially crafted attacks can substantially degrade detection performance, underscoring the need for robust, security-aware evaluation methodologies for anomaly detection in 5G networks deployed in the wild.

</details>


### [373] [Efficient Estimation of Kernel Surrogate Models for Task Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03783)
*Zhenshuo Zhang,Minxuan Duan,Hongyang R. Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Kernel surrogate models are proposed for task attribution to capture nonlinear second-order interactions between training tasks, offering improved correlation with leave-one-out truth and better downstream task selection compared to linear surrogates and influence-function baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify how individual training tasks influence a target task (task attribution) in multi-task training of large AI models. Leave-one-out retraining is infeasible; linear surrogates miss nonlinear interactions (synergy/antagonism). A unified framework and better predictive models are needed to understand and optimize task weighting.

Method: (1) Develop a unified task weighting framework linking task attribution methods; (2) establish a second-order connection between linear surrogate models and influence functions; (3) introduce kernel surrogate models to capture nonlinear task interactions; (4) devise a gradient-based estimation procedure using first-order approximations of pretrained models to efficiently learn the kernel surrogate without retraining.

Result: Kernel surrogates achieve ~25% higher correlation with leave-one-out ground truth than linear surrogates and influence-function baselines. In downstream task selection, they yield ~40% improved demonstration selection in in-context learning and multi-objective RL benchmarks. Relative estimation error via the proposed gradient-based method is <2% without retraining.

Conclusion: Kernel surrogate models more accurately capture second-order task interactions, enabling better task attribution and more effective task selection across domains, with substantial empirical gains over linear and influence-function baselines.

Abstract: Modern AI agents such as large language models are trained on diverse tasks -- translation, code generation, mathematical reasoning, and text prediction -- simultaneously. A key question is to quantify how each individual training task influences performance on a target task, a problem we refer to as task attribution. The direct approach, leave-one-out retraining, measures the effect of removing each task, but is computationally infeasible at scale. An alternative approach that builds surrogate models to predict a target task's performance for any subset of training tasks has emerged in recent literature. Prior work focuses on linear surrogate models, which capture first-order relationships, but miss nonlinear interactions such as synergy, antagonism, or XOR-type effects. In this paper, we first consider a unified task weighting framework for analyzing task attribution methods, and show a new connection between linear surrogate models and influence functions through a second-order analysis. Then, we introduce kernel surrogate models, which more effectively represent second-order task interactions. To efficiently learn the kernel surrogate, we develop a gradient-based estimation procedure that leverages a first-order approximation of pretrained models; empirically, this yields accurate estimates with less than $2\%$ relative error without repeated retraining. Experiments across multiple domains -- including math reasoning in transformers, in-context learning, and multi-objective reinforcement learning -- demonstrate the effectiveness of kernel surrogate models. They achieve a $25\%$ higher correlation with the leave-one-out ground truth than linear surrogates and influence-function baselines. When used for downstream task selection, kernel surrogate models yield a $40\%$ improvement in demonstration selection for in-context learning and multi-objective reinforcement learning benchmarks.

</details>


### [374] [Explanations Leak: Membership Inference with Differential Privacy and Active Learning Defense](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03611)
*Fatima Ezzeddine,Osama Zammar,Silvia Giordano,Omran Ayoub*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Examines how counterfactual explanations (CFs) in MLaaS can amplify membership inference attacks (MIAs) and proposes a defense by combining differential privacy (DP) with active learning (AL), followed by an empirical study of privacy-utility-explanation trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As MLaaS expose explanations via APIs, CFs may leak information about training data or model behavior, increasing vulnerability to MIAs. The work addresses the gap in understanding this threat and how to mitigate it without sacrificing utility or explainability.

Method: 1) Systematically analyze how exposing CFs through query-based APIs enables more effective shadow-based MIAs. 2) Propose a defense framework combining Differential Privacy (DP) with Active Learning (AL) to reduce memorization and limit data exposure. 3) Extensive empirical evaluation to characterize the privacy leakage, predictive performance, and explanation quality trade-off.

Result: Counterfactual explanations via API exposure strengthen shadow-based MIAs. The proposed DP+AL defense reduces memorization and data exposure, but introduces a three-way trade-off among privacy, accuracy, and explanation quality. The results underscore the need to balance transparency, utility, and privacy in explainable MLaaS deployments.

Conclusion: Transparent MLaaS must carefully manage privacy risks associated with explanations. DP+AL is a promising direction to defend against CF-induced privacy leakage while preserving usefulness and interpretability; further work is needed to optimize trade-offs and guide real-world deployment.

Abstract: Counterfactual explanations (CFs) are increasingly integrated into Machine Learning as a Service (MLaaS) systems to improve transparency; however, ML models deployed via APIs are already vulnerable to privacy attacks such as membership inference and model extraction, and the impact of explanations on this threat landscape remains insufficiently understood. In this work, we focus on the problem of how CFs expand the attack surface of MLaaS by strengthening membership inference attacks (MIAs), and on the need to design defense mechanisms that mitigate this emerging risk without undermining utility and explainability. First, we systematically analyze how exposing CFs through query-based APIs enables more effective shadow-based MIAs. Second, we propose a defense framework that integrates Differential Privacy (DP) with Active Learning (AL) to jointly reduce memorization and limit effective training data exposure. Finally, we conduct an extensive empirical evaluation to characterize the three-way trade-off between privacy leakage, predictive performance, and explanation quality. Our findings highlight the need to carefully balance transparency, utility, and privacy in the responsible deployment of explainable MLaaS systems.

</details>


### [375] [Quantization-Aware Regularizers for Deep Neural Networks Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03614)
*Dario Malchiodi,Mattia Ferraretto,Marco Frasca*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes per-layer regularization to cluster weights during training and embeds quantization parameters into backpropagation, enabling quantization-aware training to reduce accuracy loss while preserving compression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the mismatch between large, over-parameterized networks and resource-constrained deployment; standard post-training quantization can hurt accuracy, so integrating quantization into learning aims to preserve performance.

Method: Introduce per-layer regularization terms that drive weights to form clusters and treat quantization representatives as trainable network parameters embedded in the optimization loop, effectively incorporating quantization into backpropagation.

Result: On CIFAR-10 with AlexNet and VGG16, the approach demonstrates effective quantization-aware training, reducing accuracy loss and maintaining compression potential.

Conclusion: Quantization-aware training via embedded quantization parameters and cluster-promoting regularization is feasible and beneficial, marking a first step toward integrating quantization directly into the learning process.

Abstract: Deep Neural Networks reached state-of-the-art performance across numerous domains, but this progress has come at the cost of increasingly large and over-parameterized models, posing serious challenges for deployment on resource-constrained devices. As a result, model compression has become essential, and -- among compression techniques -- weight quantization is largely used and particularly effective, yet it typically introduces a non-negligible accuracy drop. However, it is usually applied to already trained models, without influencing how the parameter space is explored during the learning phase. In contrast, we introduce per-layer regularization terms that drive weights to naturally form clusters during training, integrating quantization awareness directly into the optimization process. This reduces the accuracy loss typically associated with quantization methods while preserving their compression potential. Furthermore, in our framework quantization representatives become network parameters, marking, to the best of our knowledge, the first approach to embed quantization parameters directly into the backpropagation procedure. Experiments on CIFAR-10 with AlexNet and VGG16 models confirm the effectiveness of the proposed strategy.

</details>


### [376] [Bridging Online and Offline RL: Contextual Bandit Learning for Multi-Turn Code Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03806)
*Ziru Chen,Dongdong Chen,Ruinan Jin,Yingbin Liang,Yujia Xie,Huan Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cobalt is a contextual bandit framework that uses offline trajectories to train LLMs for one-step, context-conditioned code generation, blending online and offline RL to improve multi-turn code generation while mitigating reward hacking; achieves gains over online RL baselines on LiveCodeBench and improves 8B models; released code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL for LLMs on real-world tasks can outperform offline RL but is expensive and unstable. Multi-turn code generation can be treated as a one-step recoverable MDP, enabling a hybrid online/offline learning approach with reduced training cost.

Method: Collect trajectories with a reference LLM and split them into partial trajectories that serve as contextual prompts. During online learning, train the LLM to complete each partial prompt via single-step code generation (contextual bandit). Augment training with perturbed trajectories to reduce in-context reward hacking.

Result: Cobalt outperforms two online RL baselines (GRPO and VeRPO) and yields substantial absolute improvements in Pass@1 for R1-Distill 8B and Qwen3 8B on LiveCodeBench (up to 9.0 and 6.2 points). The authors also provide code and data.

Conclusion: Cobalt is a promising solution for iterative decision-making tasks such as multi-turn code generation, effectively blending online and offline RL; open-source resources available.

Abstract: Recently, there have been significant research interests in training large language models (LLMs) with reinforcement learning (RL) on real-world tasks, such as multi-turn code generation. While online RL tends to perform better than offline RL, its higher training cost and instability hinders wide adoption. In this paper, we build on the observation that multi-turn code generation can be formulated as a one-step recoverable Markov decision process and propose contextual bandit learning with offline trajectories (Cobalt), a new method that combines the benefits of online and offline RL. Cobalt first collects code generation trajectories using a reference LLM and divides them into partial trajectories as contextual prompts. Then, during online bandit learning, the LLM is trained to complete each partial trajectory prompt through single-step code generation. Cobalt outperforms two multi-turn online RL baselines based on GRPO and VeRPO, and substantially improves R1-Distill 8B and Qwen3 8B by up to 9.0 and 6.2 absolute Pass@1 scores on LiveCodeBench. Also, we analyze LLMs' in-context reward hacking behaviors and augment Cobalt training with perturbed trajectories to mitigate this issue. Overall, our results demonstrate Cobalt as a promising solution for iterative decision-making tasks like multi-turn code generation. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/OSU-NLP-Group/cobalt.

</details>


### [377] [Ultra Fast PDE Solving via Physics Guided Few-step Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03627)
*Cindy Xiangrui Kong,Yueqi Wang,Haoyang Zheng,Weijian Luo,Guang Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A diffusion-model-based PDE solver is distilled into a few-step generator with physics-guided distillation to speed up inference and improve physical consistency, achieving orders-of-magnitude faster inference and significantly reduced PDE error on five benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address high sampling costs and weak physical consistency in diffusion-based PDE solvers by incorporating explicit physics constraints and distilling to a compact generator for fast, physics-aware PDE solving.

Method: Propose Phys-Instruct: a physics-guided distillation framework that compresses a pre-trained diffusion PDE solver into a few-step generator by aligning generator and prior diffusion distributions; inject PDE knowledge through a distillation guidance term to enforce physics constraints; grounded by a theoretical training objective with tractable gradients.

Result: On five PDE benchmarks, achieves orders-of-magnitude faster inference and reduces PDE error by more than 8x compared to state-of-the-art diffusion baselines; the unconditional student serves as a compact prior enabling efficient, physically consistent downstream conditional inference.

Conclusion: Phys-Instruct introduces a novel, effective, and efficient physics-constrained, diffusion-based PDE solver with rapid sampling and improved physical fidelity, suitable for broad downstream tasks.

Abstract: Diffusion-based models have demonstrated impressive accuracy and generalization in solving partial differential equations (PDEs). However, they still face significant limitations, such as high sampling costs and insufficient physical consistency, stemming from their many-step iterative sampling mechanism and lack of explicit physics constraints. To address these issues, we propose Phys-Instruct, a novel physics-guided distillation framework which not only (1) compresses a pre-trained diffusion PDE solver into a few-step generator via matching generator and prior diffusion distributions to enable rapid sampling, but also (2) enhances the physics consistency by explicitly injecting PDE knowledge through a PDE distillation guidance. Physic-Instruct is built upon a solid theoretical foundation, leading to a practical physics-constrained training objective that admits tractable gradients. Across five PDE benchmarks, Phys-Instruct achieves orders-of-magnitude faster inference while reducing PDE error by more than 8 times compared to state-of-the-art diffusion baselines. Moreover, the resulting unconditional student model functions as a compact prior, enabling efficient and physically consistent inference for various downstream conditional tasks. Our results indicate that Phys-Instruct is a novel, effective, and efficient framework for ultra-fast PDE solving powered by deep generative models.

</details>


### [378] [Enhancing Imbalanced Node Classification via Curriculum-Guided Feature Learning and Three-Stage Attention Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03808)
*Abdul Joseph Fofanah,Lian Wen,David Chen,Shaoyang Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes CL3AN-GNN, a curriculum-guided three-stage attention network to address imbalanced node classification in GNNs, showing improved accuracy, F1, and AUC across eight OGB datasets with faster convergence and interpretable curriculum stages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imbalanced labels in graphs cause biased learning; need mechanisms to stabilize early learning and improve minority-class performance; leverage curriculum learning and staged attention to separate simple vs complex patterns.

Method: Three-stage attention framework Engage/Enact/Embed; start with simple features (local 1-hop patterns, low-degree node attributes, class-separable pairs from initial GCN/GAT); Enact handles multi-step, cross-type edges, and minority-frontier nodes with adjustable attention; Embed performs iterative message passing with curriculum-weighted losses; evaluate on eight OGB datasets; analyze gradient stability and attention curves.

Result: Consistent improvements across all eight datasets in accuracy, F1-score, and AUC relative to state-of-the-art; faster convergence and better generalization on imbalanced graphs; clear step-wise interpretability via gradient stability and attention correlations.

Conclusion: Provides a theoretically grounded curriculum learning framework for GNNs with practical effectiveness against imbalanced graphs; demonstrates robustness across graph domains and offers insights via convergence and attention analysis.

Abstract: Imbalanced node classification in graph neural networks (GNNs) happens when some labels are much more common than others, which causes the model to learn unfairly and perform badly on the less common classes. To solve this problem, we propose a Curriculum-Guided Feature Learning and Three-Stage Attention Network (CL3AN-GNN), a learning network that uses a three-step attention system (Engage, Enact, Embed) similar to how humans learn. The model begins by engaging with structurally simpler features, defined as (1) local neighbourhood patterns (1-hop), (2) low-degree node attributes, and (3) class-separable node pairs identified via initial graph convolutional networks and graph attention networks (GCN and GAT) embeddings. This foundation enables stable early learning despite label skew. The Enact stage then addresses complicated aspects: (1) connections that require multiple steps, (2) edges that connect different types of nodes, and (3) nodes at the edges of minority classes by using adjustable attention weights. Finally, Embed consolidates these features via iterative message passing and curriculum-aligned loss weighting. We evaluate CL3AN-GNN on eight Open Graph Benchmark datasets spanning social, biological, and citation networks. Experiments show consistent improvements across all datasets in accuracy, F1-score, and AUC over recent state-of-the-art methods. The model's step-by-step method works well with different types of graph datasets, showing quicker results than training everything at once, better performance on new, imbalanced graphs, and clear explanations of each step using gradient stability and attention correlation learning curves. This work provides both a theoretically grounded framework for curriculum learning in GNNs and practical evidence of its effectiveness against imbalances, validated through metrics, convergence speeds, and generalisation tests.

</details>


### [379] [CTTVAE: Latent Space Structuring for Conditional Tabular Data Generation on Imbalanced Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03641)
*Milosh Devic,Jordan Gierschendorf,David Garson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CTTVAE+TBS combines a conditional Transformer-based tabular VAE with a class-aware triplet loss and a training-by-sampling strategy to improve minority-class synthetic data. It yields superior downstream utility for rare classes across six benchmarks while maintaining fidelity and offering privacy-friendly sampling, with ablations confirming both components' contributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address severe class imbalance in tabular data by generating synthetic samples that are representative and useful for downstream learning, particularly for minority (rare) groups.

Method: CTTVAE = Conditional Transformer-based Tabular Variational Autoencoder. Two complementary mechanisms: (i) a class-aware triplet margin loss to restructure the latent space for sharper intra-class compactness and inter-class separation; (ii) a training-by-sampling strategy that adaptively increases exposure to underrepresented groups. Together, CTTVAE+TBS forms a conditional data generation framework.

Result: Across six real-world benchmarks, CTTVAE+TBS achieves the strongest downstream utility on minority classes, often surpassing models trained on the original imbalanced data, while maintaining competitive fidelity and bridging gaps for interpolation-based and deep generative methods. Ablation studies show both latent structuring and targeted sampling contribute to gains.

Conclusion: Prioritizes downstream performance in rare categories, providing a robust, interpretable solution for conditional tabular data generation with applicability to healthcare, fraud detection, and predictive maintenance where small gains in minority cases are critical.

Abstract: Generating synthetic tabular data under severe class imbalance is essential for domains where rare but high-impact events drive decision-making. However, most generative models either overlook minority groups or fail to produce samples that are useful for downstream learning. We introduce CTTVAE, a Conditional Transformer-based Tabular Variational Autoencoder equipped with two complementary mechanisms: (i) a class-aware triplet margin loss that restructures the latent space for sharper intra-class compactness and inter-class separation, and (ii) a training-by-sampling strategy that adaptively increases exposure to underrepresented groups. Together, these components form CTTVAE+TBS, a framework that consistently yields more representative and utility-aligned samples without destabilizing training. Across six real-world benchmarks, CTTVAE+TBS achieves the strongest downstream utility on minority classes, often surpassing models trained on the original imbalanced data while maintaining competitive fidelity and bridging the gap for privacy for interpolation-based sampling methods and deep generative methods. Ablation studies further confirm that both latent structuring and targeted sampling contribute to these gains. By explicitly prioritizing downstream performance in rare categories, CTTVAE+TBS provides a robust and interpretable solution for conditional tabular data generation, with direct applicability to industries such as healthcare, fraud detection, and predictive maintenance where even small gains in minority cases can be critical.

</details>


### [380] [Reinforcement Fine-Tuning for History-Aware Dense Retriever in RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03645)
*Yicheng Zhang,Zhen Qin,Zhaomin Wu,Wenqi Zhang,Shuiguang Deng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RL-based retriever optimization for RAG via stochastic retrieval and an MDP formulation, with retrieval-history to reduce state aliasing; yields consistent improvements across pipelines, datasets, and retriever scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the objective mismatch between retriever training and RAG performance; overcome two RL-related challenges—deterministic retrieval incompatible with RL and state aliasing from query-only retrieval in multi-hop reasoning—by enabling stochastic retrieval and a Markov decision process formulation with history-aware state.

Method: Replace deterministic retrieval with stochastic sampling; formulate RAG as a Markov decision process and optimize the retriever via reinforcement learning; incorporate retrieval history into the state at each retrieval step to mitigate state aliasing.

Result: Extensive experiments across diverse RAG pipelines, datasets, and retriever scales show consistent improvements in RAG performance.

Conclusion: An RL-based retriever optimization framework that uses stochastic retrieval and retrieval-history-aware state effectively aligns retriever objectives with RAG goals, improves performance across varied pipelines and scales, and mitigates state aliasing in multi-hop settings.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) enables large language models (LLMs) to produce evidence-based responses, and its performance hinges on the matching between the retriever and LLMs. Retriever optimization has emerged as an efficient alternative to fine-tuning LLMs. However, existing solutions suffer from objective mismatch between retriever optimization and the goal of RAG pipeline. Reinforcement learning (RL) provides a promising solution to address this limitation, yet applying RL to retriever optimization introduces two fundamental challenges: 1) the deterministic retrieval is incompatible with RL formulations, and 2) state aliasing arises from query-only retrieval in multi-hop reasoning. To address these challenges, we replace deterministic retrieval with stochastic sampling and formulate RAG as a Markov decision process, making retriever optimizable by RL. Further, we incorporate retrieval history into the state at each retrieval step to mitigate state aliasing. Extensive experiments across diverse RAG pipelines, datasets, and retriever scales demonstrate consistent improvements of our approach in RAG performance.

</details>


### [381] [PLATE: Plasticity-Tunable Efficient Adapters for Geometry-Aware Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03846)
*Romain Cosentino*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PLATE is a no-data-for-old-tasks continual learning method for pretrained models that exploits geometric redundancy by learning a structured low-rank update ΔW = B A Q^T, with B and Q derived from frozen pretrained weights and only A trained, enabling a tunable plasticity-retention trade-off.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adapting foundation models in practice is hampered by the unavailability of old-task data and old-pretraining distributions; there is substantial geometric redundancy in pretrained networks that can be leveraged to protect old behavior while learning new tasks.

Method: Construct update subspaces from pretrained weights: compute B and Q once from the pretrained weights and keep them frozen; train only the matrix A for the new task, with ΔW = B A Q^T. This creates a structured, low-rank, plasticity-constrained update that restricts changes to a subspace aligned with pretraining directions, improving retention.

Result: The method enables continual learning without access to past data, offering explicit control over the plasticity-retention trade-off. Reported benefits include reduced functional drift on old data and improved worst-case retention guarantees; code for PLATE is provided (GitHub link).

Conclusion: PLATE provides a data-efficient continual learning framework for foundation models by exploiting pretrained geometric redundancy through a frozen, structured update, allowing explicit plasticity control and avoiding access to old-task data.
