Result: Agents achieve limited rediscovery success (<50 F1), exhibit high variance across runs, and show recurring failure modes in experimental design, execution, and evidence-based reasoning.

Conclusion: FIRE-Bench offers a rigorous diagnostic framework to measure progress toward reliable agent-driven scientific discovery, highlighting the substantial remaining challenges in full-cycle scientific research by autonomous agents.

Abstract: Autonomous agents powered by large language models (LLMs) promise to accelerate scientific discovery end-to-end, but rigorously evaluating their capacity for verifiable discovery remains a central challenge. Existing benchmarks face a trade-off: they either heavily rely on LLM-as-judge evaluations of automatically generated research outputs or optimize convenient yet isolated performance metrics that provide coarse proxies for scientific insight. To address this gap, we introduce FIRE-Bench (Full-cycle Insight Rediscovery Evaluation), a benchmark that evaluates agents through the rediscovery of established findings from recent, high-impact machine learning research. Agents are given only a high-level research question extracted from a published, verified study and must autonomously explore ideas, design experiments, implement code, execute their plans, and derive conclusions supported by empirical evidence. We evaluate a range of state-of-the-art agents with frontier LLMs backbones like gpt-5 on FIRE-Bench. Our results show that full-cycle scientific research remains challenging for current agent systems: even the strongest agents achieve limited rediscovery success (<50 F1), exhibit high variance across runs, and display recurring failure modes in experimental design, execution, and evidence-based reasoning. FIRE-Bench provides a rigorous and diagnostic framework for measuring progress toward reliable agent-driven scientific discovery.

</details>


### [410] [Reasoning about Reasoning: BAPO Bounds on Chain-of-Thought Token Complexity in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02909)
*Kiran Tomlinson,Tobias Schnabel,Adith Swaminathan,Jennifer Neville*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Establishes linear (Ω(n)) lower bounds on the number of reasoning tokens required for CoT in three BAPO-hard tasks (binary majority, triplet matching, graph reachability), provides matching/near upper bounds, and empirical evidence showing near-linear scaling and failure under tight reasoning budgets, highlighting fundamental inference-time bottlenecks for CoT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify how the amount of reasoning computed during inference must scale with input size, thereby revealing fundamental limits of chain-of-thought approaches and guiding efficient model and budget design.

Method: Extend the bounded attention prefix oracle (BAPO) model to formalize information flow in LLMs; prove Ω(n) lower bounds for three canonical tasks; construct explicit upper bounds; conduct experiments with frontier reasoning models to observe token scaling.

Result: Proved Ω(n) lower bounds with matching or near-matching upper bounds for the three tasks; empirical results show approximately linear growth of required reasoning tokens with input size and failures when the reasoning budget is too small.

Conclusion: Identifies a fundamental bottleneck in inference-time compute for chain-of-thought and provides a principled framework to analyze and optimize the required reasoning length.

Abstract: Inference-time scaling via chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning is a major driver of state-of-the-art LLM performance, but it comes with substantial latency and compute costs. We address a fundamental theoretical question: how many reasoning tokens are required to solve a problem as input size grows? By extending the bounded attention prefix oracle (BAPO) model--an abstraction of LLMs that quantifies the information flow required to solve a task--we prove lower bounds on the CoT tokens required for three canonical BAPO-hard tasks: binary majority, triplet matching, and graph reachability. We show that each requires $Ω(n)$ reasoning tokens when the input size is $n$. We complement these results with matching or near-matching upper bounds via explicit constructions. Finally, our experiments with frontier reasoning models show approximately linear reasoning token scaling on these tasks and failures when constrained to smaller reasoning budgets, consistent with our theoretical lower bounds. Together, our results identify fundamental bottlenecks in inference-time compute through CoT and offer a principled tool for analyzing optimal reasoning length.

</details>


### [411] [DeltaEvolve: Accelerating Scientific Discovery through Momentum-Driven Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02919)
*Jiachen Jiang,Tianyu Ding,Zhihui Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DeltaEvolve: an EM-based, LLM-driven evolutionary framework that uses semantic deltas instead of full-code histories, enabling momentum-guided updates and progressive disclosure to reduce tokens while improving discovery across domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Full-code histories in prior approaches (e.g., AlphaEvolve) are context-inefficient and provide weak, hard-to-interpret guidance for evolution; semantic guidance and transferability of components can improve efficiency and performance.

Method: Formally treats agents as an Expectation-Maximization process: E-step uses the language model to sample candidate programs; M-step updates the control context based on evaluation feedback. DeltaEvolve replaces full-code snapshots with semantic delta representations of how and why modifications affect performance. Programs are decomposable; the semantic delta captures transferable components. A multi-level database and progressive disclosure system organize deltas to reduce input tokens. Empirical evaluation across diverse scientific tasks.

Result: DeltaEvolve achieves better solution quality with less token consumption than full-code-based evolutionary agents across tasks in multiple scientific domains.

Conclusion: Semantic delta representations and progressive disclosure improve the efficiency and effectiveness of LLM-driven evolutionary search, offering a scalable approach for automated science discovery.

Abstract: LLM-driven evolutionary systems have shown promise for automated science discovery, yet existing approaches such as AlphaEvolve rely on full-code histories that are context-inefficient and potentially provide weak evolutionary guidance. In this work, we first formalize the evolutionary agents as a general Expectation-Maximization framework, where the language model samples candidate programs (E-step) and the system updates the control context based on evaluation feedback (M-step). Under this view, constructing context via full-code snapshots constitutes a suboptimal M-step, as redundant implement details dilutes core algorithmic ideas, making it difficult to provide clear inspirations for evolution. To address this, we propose DeltaEvolve, a momentum-driven evolutionary framework that replaces full-code history with structured semantic delta capturing how and why modifications between successive nodes affect performance. As programs are often decomposable, semantic delta usually contains many effective components which are transferable and more informative to drive improvement. By organizing semantic delta through multi-level database and progressive disclosure mechanism, input tokens are further reduced. Empirical evaluations on tasks across diverse scientific domains show that our framework can discover better solution with less token consumption over full-code-based evolutionary agents.

</details>


### [412] [UAT-LITE: Inference-Time Uncertainty-Aware Attention for Pretrained Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02952)
*Elias Hossain,Shubhashis Roy Dipta,Subash Neupane,Rajib Rana,Ravid Shwartz-Ziv,Ivan Garibay,Niloofar Yousefi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: UAT-LITE introduces inference-time Bayesian dropout to quantify token-level epistemic uncertainty and modulate self-attention, improving calibration and robustness with minimal cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Calibrated NLP predictions are essential for reliable, high-stakes deployment. Post-hoc calibration and heavy ensembles have limitations in altering computation or incurring large training/storage costs. The work seeks a lightweight, inference-time uncertainty mechanism that affects model reasoning.

Method: Uses Monte Carlo dropout at inference to estimate token-level epistemic uncertainty from stochastic forward passes and modulate self-attention during contextualization without changing pretrained weights or training objectives. Includes a layerwise variance decomposition to diagnose uncertainty accumulation across transformer depth. Evaluated on SQuAD 2.0, MNLI, and SST-2.

Result: Achieves ~20% reduction in Expected Calibration Error on average compared to a fine-tuned BERT-base baseline, while preserving task accuracy; improves selective prediction and robustness under distribution shift.

Conclusion: Inference-time, approximate Bayesian modulation of self-attention via MC dropout is effective for improving calibration and reliability in pretrained transformers, with diagnostic value from depth-wise variance decomposition and minimal training overhead.

Abstract: Neural NLP models are often miscalibrated, assigning high confidence to incorrect predictions, which undermines selective prediction and high-stakes deployment. Post-hoc calibration methods adjust output probabilities but leave internal computation unchanged, while ensemble and Bayesian approaches improve uncertainty at substantial training or storage cost. We propose UAT-LITE, an inference-time framework that makes self-attention uncertainty-aware using approximate Bayesian inference via Monte Carlo dropout in pretrained transformer classifiers. Token-level epistemic uncertainty is estimated from stochastic forward passes and used to modulate self-attention during contextualization, without modifying pretrained weights or training objectives. We additionally introduce a layerwise variance decomposition to diagnose how predictive uncertainty accumulates across transformer depth. Across the SQuAD 2.0 answerability, MNLI, and SST-2, UAT-LITE reduces Expected Calibration Error by approximately 20% on average relative to a fine-tuned BERT-base baseline while preserving task accuracy, and improves selective prediction and robustness under distribution shift.

</details>


### [413] [Generative Engine Optimization: A VLM and Agent Framework for Pinterest Acquisition Growth](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02961)
*Faye Zhang,Qianyu Cheng,Jasmine Wan,Vishwakarma Singh,Jinfeng Rao,Kofi Boakye*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Pinterest GEO: a reverse-search framework using VLMs and trend mining to align image content with user search intent, enabling semantically coherent collections and authority-aware linking; yields 20% organic traffic growth and multi-million MAU growth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Shifts in search: generative search reduces reliance on traditional SEO signals; visual platforms risk disintermediation; need to expose semantic depth and discovery signals for billions of assets.

Method: Fine-tune vision-language models to predict actual user search queries; augment with agents mining real-time internet trends; build semantically coherent collection pages via multimodal embeddings; deploy hybrid VLM and two-tower ANN for authority-aware interlinking across billions of assets.

Result: Scales across billions of images and tens of millions of collections; 20% organic traffic growth; multi-million MAU growth; demonstrates viability of GEO for visual platforms.

Conclusion: Presents a principled path for visual platforms to succeed in the generative search era by aligning indexing with user intent, leveraging trend-aware VLMs and robust interlinking; impacts design of search interfaces and ranking signals.

Abstract: Large Language Models are fundamentally reshaping content discovery through AI-native search systems such as ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude. Unlike traditional search engines that match keywords to documents, these systems infer user intent, synthesize multimodal evidence, and generate contextual answers directly on the search page, introducing a paradigm shift from Search Engine Optimization (SEO) to Generative Engine Optimization (GEO). For visual content platforms hosting billions of assets, this poses an acute challenge: individual images lack the semantic depth and authority signals that generative search prioritizes, risking disintermediation as user needs are satisfied in-place without site visits.
  We present Pinterest GEO, a production-scale framework that pioneers reverse search design: rather than generating generic image captions describing what content is, we fine-tune Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to predict what users would actually search for, augmented this with AI agents that mine real-time internet trends to capture emerging search demand. These VLM-generated queries then drive construction of semantically coherent Collection Pages via multimodal embeddings, creating indexable aggregations optimized for generative retrieval. Finally, we employ hybrid VLM and two-tower ANN architectures to build authority-aware interlinking structures that propagate signals across billions of visual assets. Deployed at scale across billions of images and tens of millions of collections, GEO delivers 20\% organic traffic growth contributing to multi-million monthly active user (MAU) growth, demonstrating a principled pathway for visual platforms to thrive in the generative search era.

</details>


### [414] [Structuring Value Representations via Geometric Coherence in Markov Decision Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02978)
*Zuyuan Zhang,Zeyu Fang,Tian Lan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GCR-RL introduces a poset-based value-function learning framework that refines a sequence of super-posets to enforce geometric coherence; it provides Q-learning and actor-critic algorithms with convergence guarantees and shows improved sample efficiency and stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Leverage geometric and order-theoretic structure to stabilize and speed up reinforcement learning by recasting value estimation as learning ordered relationships within a evolving poset framework.

Method: Construct a sequence of super-poset refinements by progressively refining posets and extracting additional order relations from temporal-difference signals; implement two algorithms (Q-learning and actor-critic) to realize these refinements efficiently; analyze their theoretical convergence properties.

Result: Empirical evaluation demonstrates significant gains in sample efficiency and stable performance across tasks compared to strong baselines.

Conclusion: A principled poset-based, geometry-aware framework for RL (GCR-RL) that achieves stable, data-efficient learning and offers convergence guarantees, highlighting the potential of order-theoretic approaches in RL.

Abstract: Geometric properties can be leveraged to stabilize and speed reinforcement learning. Existing examples include encoding symmetry structure, geometry-aware data augmentation, and enforcing structural restrictions. In this paper, we take a novel view of RL through the lens of order theory and recast value function estimates into learning a desired poset (partially ordered set). We propose \emph{GCR-RL} (Geometric Coherence Regularized Reinforcement Learning) that computes a sequence of super-poset refinements -- by refining posets in previous steps and learning additional order relationships from temporal difference signals -- thus ensuring geometric coherence across the sequence of posets underpinning the learned value functions. Two novel algorithms by Q-learning and by actor--critic are developed to efficiently realize these super-poset refinements. Their theoretical properties and convergence rates are analyzed. We empirically evaluate GCR-RL in a range of tasks and demonstrate significant improvements in sample efficiency and stable performance over strong baselines.

</details>


### [415] [Are LLMs Biased Like Humans? Causal Reasoning as a Function of Prior Knowledge, Irrelevant Information, and Reasoning Budget](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02983)
*Hanna M. Dettki,Charley M. Wu,Bob Rehder*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs show mostly rule-like causal reasoning across collider tasks; a small interpretable model can compress their judgments; humans detect latent factors not considered by LLMs; CoT helps robustness; LLMs differ from human collider biases, suggesting complementary use with caveats on intrinsic uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether LLMs perform normative causal computation, rely on heuristics, or rely on brittle pattern matching in causal judgment tasks; and to assess robustness and biases relative to humans.

Method: Benchmark 20+ LLMs against matched human baseline on 11 causal-judgment tasks framed as collider structures (C1 -> E <- C2). Evaluate robustness to semantic abstraction and prompt overloading; compare LLMs to a small interpretable model; examine presence of chain-of-thought (CoT) effects on robustness and biases.

Result: A small interpretable model accounts for much of LLMs’ causal judgments; most LLMs rely on rule-like strategies rather than human-like collider biases (weak explaining away, Markov violations). CoT increases robustness for many models under perturbations. Humans consider unmentioned latent factors, leading to different probability judgments than LLMs.

Conclusion: LLMs can complement human reasoning where explicit biases are undesirable, but their reliance on rule-like strategies may fail under intrinsic uncertainty; highlights need to characterize LLM reasoning strategies for safe deployment.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in domains where causal reasoning matters, yet it remains unclear whether their judgments reflect normative causal computation, human-like shortcuts, or brittle pattern matching. We benchmark 20+ LLMs against a matched human baseline on 11 causal judgment tasks formalized by a collider structure ($C_1 \!\rightarrow\! E\! \leftarrow \!C_2$). We find that a small interpretable model compresses LLMs' causal judgments well and that most LLMs exhibit more rule-like reasoning strategies than humans who seem to account for unmentioned latent factors in their probability judgments. Furthermore, most LLMs do not mirror the characteristic human collider biases of weak explaining away and Markov violations. We probe LLMs' causal judgment robustness under (i) semantic abstraction and (ii) prompt overloading (injecting irrelevant text), and find that chain-of-thought (CoT) increases robustness for many LLMs. Together, this divergence suggests LLMs can complement humans when known biases are undesirable, but their rule-like reasoning may break down when uncertainty is intrinsic -- highlighting the need to characterize LLM reasoning strategies for safe, effective deployment.

</details>


### [416] [Large Language Models Can Take False First Steps at Inference-time Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02991)
*Haijiang Yan,Jian-Qiao Zhu,Adam Sanborn*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a Bayesian account that explains why LLMs' planning appears short-sighted at inference, showing that self-generated context shifts planning strength; two experiments connect generative context to planning behavior and bias reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reconcile the gap between training-time planning capabilities and inference-time behavior by grounding planning in the evolving generative context, accounting for differences between natural language and the LLMs' internal language.

Method: Propose a Bayesian framework for planning grounded in accumulating self-generated context. Conduct two controlled experiments: (1) a random-generation task where human prompts constrain planning and self-generated context accumulates to strengthen planning; (2) a Gaussian-sampling task where conditioning on self-generated sequences reduces initial bias.

Result: Findings show a planning-shift driven by accumulated self-generated context; planning strength increases with context accumulation; conditioning on self-generated sequences reduces initial bias.

Conclusion: The work provides a theoretical explanation and empirical evidence that LLMs plan ahead during inference in a context-driven manner, linking internal generative state to observable planning behavior.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have been shown to acquire sequence-level planning abilities during training, yet their planning behavior exhibited at inference time often appears short-sighted and inconsistent with these capabilities. We propose a Bayesian account for this gap by grounding planning behavior in the evolving generative context: given the subtle differences between natural language and the language internalized by LLMs, accumulated self-generated context drives a planning-shift during inference and thereby creates the appearance of compromised planning behavior. We further validate the proposed model through two controlled experiments: a random-generation task demonstrating constrained planning under human prompts and increasing planning strength as self-generated context accumulates, and a Gaussian-sampling task showing reduced initial bias when conditioning on self-generated sequences. These findings provide a theoretical explanation along with empirical evidence for characterizing how LLMs plan ahead during inference.

</details>


### [417] [Agent Alpha: Tree Search Unifying Generation, Exploration and Evaluation for Computer-Use Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02995)
*Sizhe Tang,Rongqian Chen,Tian Lan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agent Alpha uses step-level MCTS with alpha-UCT for GUI agents, enabling regressive planning, active modeling of the planning space, and optimized search via comparison-driven evaluation and diversity constraints; achieves ~77% OSWorld success, outperforming trajectory-level baselines under the same compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of regressive ability and recoverability in trajectory-level sampling; enable reuse of partial successes and early misstep recovery; unify generation, exploration, and evaluation in a single planning loop.

Method: Integrate alpha-UCT guided search into the GUI interaction loop; apply step-level MCTS to model/exploit planning space structures; use comparison-driven evaluation to mitigate absolute scoring biases; enforce diversity-constrained expansion to maintain a compact informative search space; analyze regret bound of alpha-UCT.

Result: On OSWorld, Agent Alpha achieves state-of-the-art ~77% success rate, significantly outperforming trajectory-level baselines with equivalent compute.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effectiveness of step-level, regret-aware planning with alpha-UCT for GUI agents; enables deliberate planning, pruning, and prefix reuse; offers theoretical (regret) and empirical validation; potential for broad applicability in planning-based agents.

Abstract: While scaling test-time compute through trajectory-level sampling has significantly improved Graphical User Interface (GUI) agents, the lack of regressive ability prevents the reuse of partial successes and the recovery from early missteps. In this paper, we introduce Agent Alpha, a unified framework that synergizes generation, exploration, and evaluation through step-level Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS). It enables active modeling or exploiting structures of the planning space. By integrating alpha-UCT guided search into the interaction loop, Agent Alpha enables deliberate planning, facilitating early pruning of suboptimal branches and efficient prefix reuse. We also employ comparison-driven evaluation to mitigate absolute scoring biases and diversity-constrained expansion to maintain a compact, informative search space. Regret bound of alpha-UCT is analyzed. On the OSWorld benchmark, Agent Alpha achieves a state-of-the-art success rate of $\sim 77\%$, significantly outperforming trajectory-level baselines under equivalent compute.

</details>


### [418] [Methods and Open Problems in Differentiable Social Choice: Learning Mechanisms, Decisions, and Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03003)
*Zhiyu An,Wan Du*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Survey and framework for differentiable social choice, treating voting rules, mechanisms, and aggregation as differentiable models learned from data; shows classical axioms and impossibility results reappearing as optimization objectives, constraints, and trade-offs; identifies 36 open problems guiding future work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As ML systems increasingly perform social choice in auctions, budgeting, governance, and model alignment, there is a need for normative scrutiny and a unified, data-driven framework that integrates economics, machine learning, and democratic theory.

Method: Comprehensive literature review across auctions, voting, budgeting, liquid democracy, decentralized aggregation, and inverse mechanism learning; synthesize classical axioms/impossibility results with modern differentiable optimization; map theoretical results to practical architectures and learning objectives; enumerate open questions.

Result: Proposes a cohesive differentiable social choice framework that recasts axioms and impossibility results as optimization objectives/constraints; demonstrates cross-domain connections and provides a structured research agenda.

Conclusion: Defines 36 open problems that outline a new research agenda at the intersection of machine learning, economics, and democratic theory, aimed at advancing differentiable social choice.

Abstract: Social choice is no longer a peripheral concern of political theory or economics-it has become a foundational component of modern machine learning systems. From auctions and resource allocation to federated learning, participatory governance, and the alignment of large language models, machine learning pipelines increasingly aggregate heterogeneous preferences, incentives, and judgments into collective decisions. In effect, many contemporary machine learning systems already implement social choice mechanisms, often implicitly and without explicit normative scrutiny.
  This Review surveys differentiable social choice: an emerging paradigm that formulates voting rules, mechanisms, and aggregation procedures as learnable, differentiable models optimized from data. We synthesize work across auctions, voting, budgeting, liquid democracy, decentralized aggregation, and inverse mechanism learning, showing how classical axioms and impossibility results reappear as objectives, constraints, and optimization trade-offs. We conclude by identifying 36 open problems defining a new research agenda at the intersection of machine learning, economics, and democratic theory.

</details>


### [419] [Distilling LLM Reasoning into Graph of Concept Predictors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03006)
*Ziyang Yu,Liang Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GCP adds a reasoning-aware, DAG-based distillation framework that mirrors the teacher's decision process with modular predictors, boosting sample efficiency, interpretability, and training stability under limited labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce inference cost for LLMs in discriminative tasks while preserving intermediate reasoning signals and enabling diagnostics of where errors arise and what reasoning is missing.

Method: Model teacher reasoning as a directed acyclic graph (DAG) and mirror it with modular concept predictors in the student. Employ a graph-aware acquisition strategy to target uncertainty/disagreement at critical reasoning nodes. Use targeted sub-module retraining to attribute downstream loss to specific predictors and update only the most influential modules. Evaluate on eight NLP classification benchmarks; release code.

Result: Improved performance over baselines under limited annotation budgets; more interpretable and controllable training dynamics; demonstrated sample-efficiency gains across eight NLP benchmarks.

Conclusion: Graph of Concept Predictors (GCP) is an effective reasoning-aware active distillation framework that externalizes reasoning signals, improves sample efficiency, and enhances interpretability and training stability for LLM-based discriminative tasks.

Abstract: Deploying Large Language Models (LLMs) for discriminative workloads is often limited by inference latency, compute, and API costs at scale. Active distillation reduces these costs by querying an LLM oracle to train compact discriminative students, but most pipelines distill only final labels, discarding intermediate reasoning signals and offering limited diagnostics of what reasoning is missing and where errors arise. We propose Graph of Concept Predictors (GCP), a reasoning-aware active distillation framework that externalizes the teacher's decision process as a directed acyclic graph and mirrors it with modular concept predictors in the student. GCP enhances sample efficiency through a graph-aware acquisition strategy that targets uncertainty and disagreement at critical reasoning nodes. Additionally, it improves training stability and efficiency by performing targeted sub-module retraining, which attributes downstream loss to specific concept predictors and updates only the most influential modules. Experiments on eight NLP classification benchmarks demonstrate that GCP enhances performance under limited annotation budgets while yielding more interpretable and controllable training dynamics. Code is available at: https://github.com/Ziyang-Yu/GCP.

</details>


### [420] [STAR: Similarity-guided Teacher-Assisted Refinement for Super-Tiny Function Calling Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03022)
*Jiliang Ni,Jiachen Pu,Zhongyi Yang,Jingfeng Luo,Conggang Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces STAR, a two-pronged framework to distill LLM capabilities into super-tiny models for function calling, achieving SOTA results among models under 1B parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable smaller models to inherit LLM capabilities for function calling, addressing overfitting, instability, poor rewards, and integration challenges in existing pipelines.

Method: Two innovations: (1) Constrained Knowledge Distillation (CKD): an objective using top-k forward KL with penalties for confidently incorrect predictions to stabilize training while preserving exploration for RL; (2) Similarity-guided RL (Sim-RL): a similarity-based reward that yields a fine-grained, continuous signal reflecting the closeness of generated outputs to ground truth; integrated in a cohesive curriculum.

Result: Empirical evaluation on challenging benchmarks shows STAR achieves state-of-the-art performance for models of their size; a 0.6B STAR model is the best open-model under 1B, beating several larger open models.

Conclusion: STAR demonstrates a practical pipeline to distill LLM capabilities into super-tiny models, enabling powerful, accessible, and efficient AI agents for function calling tasks.

Abstract: The proliferation of Large Language Models (LLMs) in function calling is pivotal for creating advanced AI agents, yet their large scale hinders widespread adoption, necessitating transferring their capabilities into smaller ones. However, existing paradigms are often plagued by overfitting, training instability, ineffective binary rewards for multi-solution tasks, and the difficulty of synergizing techniques. We introduce STAR: Similarity-guided Teacher-Assisted Refinement, a novel holistic framework that effectively transfers LLMs' capabilities to super-tiny models. STAR consists of two core technical innovations: (1) Constrained Knowledge Distillation (CKD), a training objective that augments top-k forward KL divergence to suppress confidently incorrect predictions, ensuring training stability while preserving exploration capacity for downstream RL. STAR holistically synergizes these strategies within a cohesive training curriculum, enabling super-tiny models to achieve exceptional performance on complex function calling tasks; (2) Similarity-guided RL (Sim-RL), a RL mechanism that introduces a fine-grained, similarity-based reward. This provides a robust, continuous, and rich signal for better policy optimization by evaluating the similarity between generated outputs and the ground truth. Extensive experiments on challenging and renowned benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our method. Our STAR models establish SOTA in their size classes, significantly outperforming baselines. Remarkably, our 0.6B STAR model achieves the best performance among all open models under 1B, surpassing even several well-known open models at a larger scale. STAR demonstrates a training framework that distills capabilities of LLMs into super-tiny models, paving the way for powerful, accessible, and efficient AI agents.

</details>


### [421] [RC-GRPO: Reward-Conditioned Group Relative Policy Optimization for Multi-Turn Tool Calling Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03025)
*Haitian Zhong,Jixiu Zhai,Lei Song,Jiang Bian,Qiang Liu,Tieniu Tan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Reward-Conditioned Group Relative Policy Optimization (RC-GRPO) uses discrete reward tokens to steer exploration in multi-turn LLM tool calling, addressing sparse rewards and within-group flatness in GRPO. It fine-tunes a Reward-Conditioned Trajectory Policy (RCTP) with injected high/low reward tokens and samples diverse reward tokens during GRPO to boost within-group diversity and advantage gains, achieving strong results on BFCLv4 and surpassing some closed-source models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SFT + GRPO approaches struggle when within-group reward variation is low, leading to uninformative advantages and vanishing updates in group-normalized policy optimization for multi-turn tool calling with sparse rewards.

Method: 1) Train a Reward-Conditioned Trajectory Policy (RCTP) on trajectories augmented with reward goal tokens (e.g., <|high_reward|>, <|low_reward|>). 2) During RL, sample diverse reward tokens within each GRPO group and condition rollouts on the sampled token to induce within-group diversity and improve advantage gains.

Result: On the BFCLv4 multi-turn benchmark, RC-GRPO yields consistent performance improvements over baselines, and the Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct model with RC-GRPO even surpasses all closed-source API models.

Conclusion: Reward-conditioned prompts enable controllable exploration and improved within-group learning in multi-turn LLM tool calling, making RC-GRPO a promising approach for sparse-reward RL in dialogue-heavy tasks.

Abstract: Multi-turn tool calling is challenging for Large Language Models (LLMs) because rewards are sparse and exploration is expensive. A common recipe, SFT followed by GRPO, can stall when within-group reward variation is low (e.g., more rollouts in a group receive the all 0 or all 1 reward), making the group-normalized advantage uninformative and yielding vanishing updates. To address this problem, we propose RC-GRPO (Reward-Conditioned Group Relative Policy Optimization), which treats exploration as a controllable steering problem via discrete reward tokens. We first fine-tune a Reward-Conditioned Trajectory Policy (RCTP) on mixed-quality trajectories with reward goal special tokens (e.g., <|high_reward|>, <|low_reward|>) injected into the prompts, enabling the model to learn how to generate distinct quality trajectories on demand. Then during RL, we sample diverse reward tokens within each GRPO group and condition rollouts on the sampled token to improve within-group diversity, improving advantage gains. On the Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard v4 (BFCLv4) multi-turn benchmark, our method yields consistently improved performance than baselines, and the performance on Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct even surpasses all closed-source API models.

</details>


### [422] [Visual Reasoning over Time Series via Multi-Agent System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03026)
*Weilin Ruan,Yuxuan Liang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MAS4TS introduces a tool-driven multi-agent system for general time-series tasks, integrating visual reasoning over plots with latent trajectory reconstruction via an Analyzer-Reasoner-Executor framework, achieving state-of-the-art performance with strong generalization and efficient inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current time-series methods and pretrained large-model approaches struggle to incorporate intuitive visual reasoning and generalize across diverse tasks with adaptive tool usage.

Method: MAS4TS deploys three specialized agents (Analyzer, Reasoner, Executor) with shared memory and gated communication, plus a router that selects task-specific tool chains. Visual reasoning over time-series plots is performed using a Vision-Language Model to extract temporal structure, followed by latent-space trajectory reconstruction.

Result: Extensive benchmarks show MAS4TS achieves state-of-the-art performance on a broad set of time-series tasks, with strong generalization and efficient inference.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates effective integration of visual reasoning, tool-driven coordination, and latent reconstruction, enabling generalizable and efficient time-series analysis across tasks.

Abstract: Time series analysis underpins many real-world applications, yet existing time-series-specific methods and pretrained large-model-based approaches remain limited in integrating intuitive visual reasoning and generalizing across tasks with adaptive tool usage. To address these limitations, we propose MAS4TS, a tool-driven multi-agent system for general time series tasks, built upon an Analyzer-Reasoner-Executor paradigm that integrates agent communication, visual reasoning, and latent reconstruction within a unified framework. MAS4TS first performs visual reasoning over time series plots with structured priors using a Vision-Language Model to extract temporal structures, and subsequently reconstructs predictive trajectories in latent space. Three specialized agents coordinate via shared memory and gated communication, while a router selects task-specific tool chains for execution. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that MAS4TS achieves state-of-the-art performance across a wide range of time series tasks, while exhibiting strong generalization and efficient inference.

</details>


### [423] [KANFIS A Neuro-Symbolic Framework for Interpretable and Uncertainty-Aware Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03034)
*Binbin Yong,Haoran Pei,Jun Shen,Haoran Li,Qingguo Zhou,Zhao Su*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: KANFIS is a compact neuro-symbolic inference system that uses additive function decomposition to avoid exponential rule growth in high dimensions, supports Type-1 and Interval Type-2 fuzzy logic, uses sparse masking for compact, interpretable rule sets, and achieves competitive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the exponential rule explosion and structural complexity of conventional ANFIS due to product-based inference in high-dimensional spaces, while preserving interpretability and uncertainty modeling.

Method: Introduce Kolmogorov-Arnold Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System (KANFIS) with additive aggregation; linear scaling of parameters and rule complexity with input dimensionality; sparse masking to generate compact rule sets; compatibility with Type-1 and Interval Type-2 fuzzy logic; interpretable rule semantics and transparent inference.

Result: Empirical results show KANFIS achieves competitive performance against representative neural and neuro-fuzzy baselines.

Conclusion: KANFIS provides a scalable, interpretable neuro-fuzzy framework that handles uncertainty via IT2 logic and maintains transparency with additive decomposition, addressing the structural limitations of ANFIS.

Abstract: Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System (ANFIS) was designed to combine the learning capabilities of neural network with the reasoning transparency of fuzzy logic. However, conventional ANFIS architectures suffer from structural complexity, where the product-based inference mechanism causes an exponential explosion of rules in high-dimensional spaces. We herein propose the Kolmogorov-Arnold Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System (KANFIS), a compact neuro-symbolic architecture that unifies fuzzy reasoning with additive function decomposition. KANFIS employs an additive aggregation mechanism, under which both model parameters and rule complexity scale linearly with input dimensionality rather than exponentially. Furthermore, KANFIS is compatible with both Type-1 (T1) and Interval Type-2 (IT2) fuzzy logic systems, enabling explicit modeling of uncertainty and ambiguity in fuzzy representations. By using sparse masking mechanisms, KANFIS generates compact and structured rule sets, resulting in an intrinsically interpretable model with clear rule semantics and transparent inference processes. Empirical results demonstrate that KANFIS achieves competitive performance against representative neural and neuro-fuzzy baselines.

</details>


### [424] [MAS-ProVe: Understanding the Process Verification of Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03053)
*Vishal Venkataramani,Haizhou Shi,Zixuan Ke,Austin Xu,Xiaoxiao He,Yingbo Zhou,Semih Yavuz,Hao Wang,Shafiq Joty*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MAS-ProVe systematically evaluates process verification for multi-agent systems across multiple paradigms and granularities; results show limited and highly variable benefits of process verification, with LLMs acting as judges often outperforming reward-based methods; context-length trade-offs and a small gap between LLM-as-judge and single-agent performance; open challenges remain; code is provided.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the uncertain effectiveness and high variance of process verification in MAS and determine whether verifying intermediate steps can reliably guide MAS coordination.

Method: An empirical study spanning three verification paradigms (LLM-as-a-Judge, reward models, process reward models), two granularity levels (agent-level, iteration-level), five verifiers, four context-management strategies, six diverse MAS frameworks, and multiple reasoning benchmarks.

Result: Process-level verification does not consistently improve MAS performance and shows high variance. LLM-as-a-Judge generally outperforms reward-based approaches, with trained judges beating general-purpose LLMs. There is a small performance gap between LLMs as judges and as single agents, and a context-length–performance trade-off in verification.

Conclusion: Process verification for MAS remains an open challenge requiring advances beyond current paradigms; the authors provide code for reproducibility.

Abstract: Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) built on Large Language Models (LLMs) often exhibit high variance in their reasoning trajectories. Process verification, which evaluates intermediate steps in trajectories, has shown promise in general reasoning settings, and has been suggested as a potential tool for guiding coordination of MAS; however, its actual effectiveness in MAS remains unclear. To fill this gap, we present MAS-ProVe, a systematic empirical study of process verification for multi-agent systems (MAS). Our study spans three verification paradigms (LLM-as-a-Judge, reward models, and process reward models), evaluated across two levels of verification granularity (agent-level and iteration-level). We further examine five representative verifiers and four context management strategies, and conduct experiments over six diverse MAS frameworks on multiple reasoning benchmarks. We find that process-level verification does not consistently improve performance and frequently exhibits high variance, highlighting the difficulty of reliably evaluating partial multi-agent trajectories. Among the methods studied, LLM-as-a-Judge generally outperforms reward-based approaches, with trained judges surpassing general-purpose LLMs. We further observe a small performance gap between LLMs acting as judges and as single agents, and identify a context-length-performance trade-off in verification. Overall, our results suggest that effective and robust process verification for MAS remains an open challenge, requiring further advances beyond current paradigms. Code is available at https://github.com/Wang-ML-Lab/MAS-ProVe.

</details>


### [425] [De-conflating Preference and Qualification: Constrained Dual-Perspective Reasoning for Job Recommendation with Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03097)
*Bryce Kan,Wei Yang,Emily Nguyen,Ganghui Yi,Bowen Yi,Chenxiao Yu,Yan Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: JobRec decouples candidate preference and employer qualification in professional matching using a dual-perspective LLM framework with constrained optimization, achieving better performance and controllability, aided by synthetic data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional LLM-based job recommendation often conflates subjective candidate preferences with objective employer qualifications, yielding confounded supervision due to recruitment-funnel censoring and reducing policy controllability. Decoupling these decision dimensions can enable more interpretable and controllable recommendations.

Method: Introduce Unified Semantic Alignment Schema to map candidate and job attributes into structured semantic layers. Employ a Two-Stage Cooperative Training Strategy to learn decoupled experts that separately infer preference (candidate-side) and qualification (employer-side). Add a Lagrangian-based Policy Alignment module to optimize recommendations under explicit eligibility constraints. Address data scarcity with a synthetic dataset refined by domain experts.

Result: Empirical evaluations show JobRec consistently outperforms strong baselines and offers improved controllability for strategy-aware professional matching.

Conclusion: Decoupled, constrained reasoning in job recommendation improves performance and controllability; synthetic expert-refined data helps mitigate data scarcity and supports effective policy-aligned recommendations.

Abstract: Professional job recommendation involves a complex bipartite matching process that must reconcile a candidate's subjective preference with an employer's objective qualification. While Large Language Models (LLMs) are well-suited for modeling the rich semantics of resumes and job descriptions, existing paradigms often collapse these two decision dimensions into a single interaction signal, yielding confounded supervision under recruitment-funnel censoring and limiting policy controllability. To address these challenges, We propose JobRec, a generative job recommendation framework for de-conflating preference and qualification via constrained dual-perspective reasoning. JobRec introduces a Unified Semantic Alignment Schema that aligns candidate and job attributes into structured semantic layers, and a Two-Stage Cooperative Training Strategy that learns decoupled experts to separately infer preference and qualification. Building on these experts, a Lagrangian-based Policy Alignment module optimizes recommendations under explicit eligibility requirements, enabling controllable trade-offs. To mitigate data scarcity, we construct a synthetic dataset refined by experts. Experiments show that JobRec consistently outperforms strong baselines and provides improved controllability for strategy-aware professional matching.

</details>


### [426] [Risky-Bench: Probing Agentic Safety Risks under Real-World Deployment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03100)
*Jingnan Zheng,Yanzhen Luo,Jingjun Xu,Bingnan Liu,Yuxin Chen,Chenhang Cui,Gelei Deng,Chaochao Lu,Xiang Wang,An Zhang,Tat-Seng Chua*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Risky-Bench is a domain-agnostic framework for evaluating the safety of LLM-based agents in real-world, long‑horizon settings by defining context-aware safety rubrics and testing across a structured safety space under varied threat models; it reveals substantial safety risks in life‑assist agents and is extensible to other deployments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safety evaluations are often risk-specific to particular agent setups, overlook long-horizon interactive dynamics, and lack transferability across configurations. There is a need for an evaluation paradigm that covers broader safety risks under real-world deployment conditions.

Method: Develop a domain-agnostic safety framework that derives context-aware safety rubrics outlining a defined safety space. Evaluate agent safety by executing realistic tasks within this space under different threat assumptions, enabling systematic assessment across scenarios. Demonstrated in life-assist agents, with an extensible pipeline designed for other deployment settings.

Result: Exposure of substantial safety risks in state-of-the-art life-assist agents under realistic execution conditions; the framework functions as a structured, extensible evaluation pipeline.

Conclusion: Risky-Bench offers an extensible methodology for agent safety assessment that is not tied to a single domain and can be adapted to diverse deployment settings, enabling systematic, real-world risk evaluation.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as agents that operate in real-world environments, introducing safety risks beyond linguistic harm. Existing agent safety evaluations rely on risk-oriented tasks tailored to specific agent settings, resulting in limited coverage of safety risk space and failing to assess agent safety behavior during long-horizon, interactive task execution in complex real-world deployments. Moreover, their specialization to particular agent settings limits adaptability across diverse agent configurations. To address these limitations, we propose Risky-Bench, a framework that enables systematic agent safety evaluation grounded in real-world deployment. Risky-Bench organizes evaluation around domain-agnostic safety principles to derive context-aware safety rubrics that delineate safety space, and systematically evaluates safety risks across this space through realistic task execution under varying threat assumptions. When applied to life-assist agent settings, Risky-Bench uncovers substantial safety risks in state-of-the-art agents under realistic execution conditions. Moreover, as a well-structured evaluation pipeline, Risky-Bench is not confined to life-assist scenarios and can be adapted to other deployment settings to construct environment-specific safety evaluations, providing an extensible methodology for agent safety assessment.

</details>


### [427] [Understanding Multi-Agent LLM Frameworks: A Unified Benchmark and Experimental Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03128)
*Abdelghny Orogat,Ana Rostam,Essam Mansour*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Architectural design choices in multi-agent LLM frameworks cause order-of-magnitude differences in latency, accuracy, and coordination; a standardized, framework-level evaluation (MAFBench) is needed and developed, along with an architectural taxonomy, to guide design and framework selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a gap in understanding how framework-level architectures influence performance in multi-agent LLM systems. Existing benchmarks target individual capabilities and lack standardization across frameworks, making it hard to isolate architectural effects.

Method: Propose an architectural taxonomy to systematically compare multi-agent LLM frameworks. Develop MAFBench, a unified evaluation suite that aggregates existing benchmarks under a standardized execution pipeline. Conduct controlled empirical studies across multiple widely used frameworks using MAFBench.

Result: Findings show framework-level design choices can increase latency by >100x, reduce planning accuracy by up to 30%, and drop coordination success from >90% to <30%. Translate results into concrete architectural design principles and guidance for framework selection; outline future research directions.

Conclusion: Framework-level architecture substantially affects performance; MAFBench enables standardized framework-level evaluation and guides design and framework selection, with significant implications for building scalable, efficient multi-agent LLM systems.

Abstract: Multi-agent LLM frameworks are widely used to accelerate the development of agent systems powered by large language models (LLMs). These frameworks impose distinct architectural structures that govern how agents interact, store information, and coordinate tasks. However, their impact on system performance remains poorly understood. This gap is critical, as architectural choices alone can induce order-of-magnitude differences in latency and throughput, as well as substantial variation in accuracy and scalability. Addressing this challenge requires (i) jointly evaluating multiple capabilities, such as orchestration overhead, memory behavior, planning, specialization, and coordination, and (ii) conducting these evaluations under controlled, framework-level conditions to isolate architectural effects. Existing benchmarks focus on individual capabilities and lack standardized framework-level evaluation. We address these limitations by (i) introducing an architectural taxonomy for systematically comparing multi-agent LLM frameworks along fundamental dimensions, and (ii) developing MAFBench, a unified evaluation suite that integrates existing benchmarks under a standardized execution pipeline. Using MAFBench, we conduct a controlled empirical study across several widely used frameworks. Our results show that framework-level design choices alone can increase latency by over 100x, reduce planning accuracy by up to 30%, and lower coordination success from above 90% to below 30%. Finally, we translate our findings into concrete architectural design principles and framework selection guidance, and outline promising future research directions.

</details>


### [428] [General Agents Contain World Models, even under Partial Observability and Stochasticity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03146)
*Santiago Cifuentes*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Extends the prior result to stochastic agents in partially observable environments, showing they still contain a model of the world; even less powerful agents do.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify whether agents can avoid learning their environment through randomness; understand the minimal assumptions under which an internal world model exists.

Method: Theoretical extension of the framework to stochastic agents in partially observable settings; formal proofs showing approximate environmental reconstruction is possible via querying, despite randomness; weakening the notion of generality.

Result: Shows stochastic, partially observable agents possess an internal model of the environment enabling approximate reconstruction; even less powerful agents suffice to contain a world model.

Conclusion: Randomization cannot circumvent environment learning; broader implications for agent design and analysis, indicating environment knowledge is a fundamental property under these conditions; extends the scope of previous results.

Abstract: Deciding whether an agent possesses a model of its surrounding world is a fundamental step toward understanding its capabilities and limitations. In [10], it was shown that, within a particular framework, every almost optimal and general agent necessarily contains sufficient knowledge of its environment to allow an approximate reconstruction of it by querying the agent as a black box. This result relied on the assumptions that the agent is deterministic and that the environment is fully observable.
  In this work, we remove both assumptions by extending the theorem to stochastic agents operating in partially observable environments. Fundamentally, this shows that stochastic agents cannot avoid learning their environment through the usage of randomization. We also strengthen the result by weakening the notion of generality, proving that less powerful agents already contain a model of the world in which they operate.

</details>


### [429] [Enhancing Foundation VLM Robustness to Missing Modality: Scalable Diffusion for Bi-directional Feature Restoration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03151)
*Wei Dai,Haoyu Wang,Honghao Chang,Lijun He,Fan Li,Jian Sun,Haixia Bi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Plug-in diffusion-based missing modality restoration for Vision-Language Models with Dynamic Modality Gating and Cross-Modal Mutual Learning, achieving strong zero-shot performance across missing modalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs degrade when modalities are missing; current prompt-based and imputation-based methods either hinder generalization or introduce semantically irrelevant noise.

Method: Use an enhanced diffusion model as a mid-stage training module. Dynamic Modality Gating adaptively leverages conditional features to steer semantically consistent generation. Cross-Modal Mutual Learning aligns the semantic spaces of dual encoders bidirectionally.

Result: Zero-shot benchmarks show the method outperforms baselines; extensive ablations confirm robustness, scalability, and reliability across varying missing rates and environments.

Conclusion: Proposes a general, scalable restoration strategy for missing modalities in VLMs; preserves semantics and generalization; code and models will be publicly available.

Abstract: Vision Language Models (VLMs) typically assume complete modality input during inference. However, their effectiveness drops sharply when certain modalities are unavailable or incomplete. Current research primarily faces two dilemmas: Prompt-based methods struggle to restore missing yet indispensable features and impair generalization of VLMs. Imputation-based approaches, lacking effective guidance, are prone to generating semantically irrelevant noise. Restoring precise semantics while sustaining VLM generalization remains challenging. Therefore, we propose a general missing modality restoration strategy in this paper. We introduce an enhanced diffusion model as a pluggable mid-stage training module to effectively restore missing features. Our strategy introduces two key innovations: (I) Dynamic Modality Gating, which adaptively leverages conditional features to steer the generation of semantically consistent features; (II) Cross-Modal Mutual Learning mechanism, which bridges the semantic spaces of dual encoders to achieve bidirectional alignment. Zero-shot evaluations across benchmark datasets demonstrate that our approach outperforms existing baseline methods. Extensive experiments and ablation studies confirm our model as a robust and scalable extension for VLMs in missing modality scenarios, ensuring reliability across diverse missing rates and environments. Our code and models will be publicly available.

</details>


### [430] [VALUEFLOW: Toward Pluralistic and Steerable Value-based Alignment in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03160)
*Woojin Kim,Sieun Hyeon,Jusang Oh,Jaeyoung Do*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VALUEFLOW is a unified framework for extracting, evaluating, and steering value intensity in LLMs, combining HIVES, VIDB, and anchor-based evaluation to enable calibrated, multi-value alignment at scale; reveals steerability asymmetries across theories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address gaps in value-alignment: hierarchical value structure, calibrated intensity in evaluation, and controllable steerability of LLMs across multiple value theories.

Method: Introduce three components: HIVES hierarchical embedding space; VIDB large-scale value-labeled texts with intensity estimates via ranking; anchor-based evaluator ranking outputs against VIDB to produce intensity scores.

Result: Large-scale study across ten models and four value theories; identify asymmetries in steerability and composition laws; demonstrate scalable infrastructure.

Conclusion: VALUEFLOW advances pluralistic alignment by providing end-to-end extraction, evaluation, and calibrated steering; enabling principled, scalable control of value intensity in LLMs.

Abstract: Aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) with the diverse spectrum of human values remains a central challenge: preference-based methods often fail to capture deeper motivational principles. Value-based approaches offer a more principled path, yet three gaps persist: extraction often ignores hierarchical structure, evaluation detects presence but not calibrated intensity, and the steerability of LLMs at controlled intensities remains insufficiently understood. To address these limitations, we introduce VALUEFLOW, the first unified framework that spans extraction, evaluation, and steering with calibrated intensity control. The framework integrates three components: (i) HIVES, a hierarchical value embedding space that captures intra- and cross-theory value structure; (ii) the Value Intensity DataBase (VIDB), a large-scale resource of value-labeled texts with intensity estimates derived from ranking-based aggregation; and (iii) an anchor-based evaluator that produces consistent intensity scores for model outputs by ranking them against VIDB panels. Using VALUEFLOW, we conduct a comprehensive large-scale study across ten models and four value theories, identifying asymmetries in steerability and composition laws for multi-value control. This paper establishes a scalable infrastructure for evaluating and controlling value intensity, advancing pluralistic alignment of LLMs.

</details>


### [431] [Beyond Quantity: Trajectory Diversity Scaling for Code Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03219)
*Guhong Chen,Chenghao Sun,Cheng Fu,Qiyao Wang,Zhihong Huang,Chaopeng Wei,Guangxu Chen,Feiteng Fang,Ahmadreza Argha,Bing Zhao,Xander Xu,Qi Han,Hamid Alinejad-Rokny,Qiang Qu,Binhua Li,Shiwen Ni,Min Yang,Hu Wei,Yongbin Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TDScaling uses trajectory diversity scaling to synthesize data for code agents, outperforming quantity scaling under a fixed budget; introduces four components and shows improvements on tool-use and coding tasks; dataset and code to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current scaling strategies rely on increasing data volume, but quality and diversity of synthetic data are limited, restricting generalization of MCP-based code agents. There is an early bottleneck in trajectory data utilization; diversity-focused synthesis aims to cover long-tail scenarios and coherent multi-agent trajectories.

Method: Four innovations: (1) Business Cluster mechanism capturing real-service logical dependencies; (2) blueprint-driven multi-agent paradigm enforcing trajectory coherence; (3) adaptive evolution using Domain Entropy, Reasoning Mode Entropy, and Cumulative Action Complexity to steer synthesis toward long-tail scenarios and prevent mode collapse; (4) sandboxed code tool mitigating catastrophic forgetting of intrinsic coding capabilities.

Result: Empirical results on general tool-use benchmarks (BFCL, tau^2-Bench) and code agent tasks (RebenchT, CodeCI, BIRD) show TDScaling improves both tool-use generalization and inherent coding proficiency; under a fixed budget, diversity-led scaling yields larger gains than quantity-led; plan to release full codebase and a synthesized dataset with 30,000+ tool clusters.

Conclusion: Trajectory diversity scaling is an effective data synthesis paradigm for code agents, delivering better generalization and coding ability under fixed budgets; the proposed components enable realistic, coherent trajectories and long-tail coverage. A full release of code and dataset is planned.

Abstract: As code large language models (LLMs) evolve into tool-interactive agents via the Model Context Protocol (MCP), their generalization is increasingly limited by low-quality synthetic data and the diminishing returns of quantity scaling. Moreover, quantity-centric scaling exhibits an early bottleneck that underutilizes trajectory data. We propose TDScaling, a Trajectory Diversity Scaling-based data synthesis framework for code agents that scales performance through diversity rather than raw volume. Under a fixed training budget, increasing trajectory diversity yields larger gains than adding more trajectories, improving the performance-cost trade-off for agent training. TDScaling integrates four innovations: (1) a Business Cluster mechanism that captures real-service logical dependencies; (2) a blueprint-driven multi-agent paradigm that enforces trajectory coherence; (3) an adaptive evolution mechanism that steers synthesis toward long-tail scenarios using Domain Entropy, Reasoning Mode Entropy, and Cumulative Action Complexity to prevent mode collapse; and (4) a sandboxed code tool that mitigates catastrophic forgetting of intrinsic coding capabilities. Experiments on general tool-use benchmarks (BFCL, tau^2-Bench) and code agent tasks (RebenchT, CodeCI, BIRD) demonstrate a win-win outcome: TDScaling improves both tool-use generalization and inherent coding proficiency. We plan to release the full codebase and the synthesized dataset (including 30,000+ tool clusters) upon publication.

</details>


### [432] [TAME: A Trustworthy Test-Time Evolution of Agent Memory with Systematic Benchmarking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03224)
*Yu Cheng,Jiuan Zhou,Yongkang Hu,Yihang Chen,Huichi Zhou,Mingang Chen,Zhizhong Zhang,Kun Shao,Yuan Xie,Zhaoxia Yin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Dual-memory evolutionary framework (TAME) mitigates agent memory misevolution by separating executor and evaluator memories, using a closed-loop process to improve task performance and safety, validated on Trust-Memevo benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the vulnerability of safety alignment during test-time memory evolution, where agent memory changes can degrade trustworthiness even during benign task evolution.

Method: Introduce TAME with two memory streams: executor memory (drives task performance by distilling generalizable methodologies) and evaluator memory (refines safety and task-utility judgments from historical feedback). Operates via a closed-loop pipeline: memory filtering, draft generation, trustworthy refinement, execution, and dual-track memory updating.

Result: Trust-Memevo experiments show that naïve memory evolution leads to trustworthiness decline; TAME mitigates this misevolution and yields joint improvements in trustworthiness and task performance across domains and settings.

Conclusion: A dual-memory, closed-loop framework can preserve trustworthiness during memory evolution while maintaining or enhancing task utility, offering a practical path toward safer AGI development.

Abstract: Test-time evolution of agent memory serves as a pivotal paradigm for achieving AGI by bolstering complex reasoning through experience accumulation. However, even during benign task evolution, agent safety alignment remains vulnerable-a phenomenon known as Agent Memory Misevolution. To evaluate this phenomenon, we construct the Trust-Memevo benchmark to assess multi-dimensional trustworthiness during benign task evolution, revealing an overall decline in trustworthiness across various task domains and evaluation settings. To address this issue, we propose TAME, a dual-memory evolutionary framework that separately evolves executor memory to improve task performance by distilling generalizable methodologies, and evaluator memory to refine assessments of both safety and task utility based on historical feedback. Through a closed loop of memory filtering, draft generation, trustworthy refinement, execution, and dual-track memory updating, TAME preserves trustworthiness without sacrificing utility. Experiments demonstrate that TAME mitigates misevolution, achieving a joint improvement in both trustworthiness and task performance.

</details>


### [433] [The Necessity of a Unified Framework for LLM-Based Agent Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03238)
*Pengyu Zhu,Li Sun,Philip S. Yu,Sen Su*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a standardized, unified evaluation framework for agent benchmarks to mitigate confounds from prompts, tool configurations, and environment, enabling fairer and more reproducible assessment of LLM-based agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agent benchmarks are heavily confounded by extraneous factors (system prompts, tool configurations, environmental dynamics); evaluations are fragmented and researcher-specific, hindering attribution of gains to the model; lack of standardized data leads to untraceable errors, non-reproducibility, unfairness, and opacity.

Method: Introduces a conceptual proposal aimed at standardizing agent evaluation (a framework for standard components and protocols), though the abstract does not provide detailed implementation.

Result: The contribution is a theoretical/proposal-level framework without reported empirical results in the abstract.

Conclusion: A unified evaluation framework is essential for rigorous advancement of agent evaluation, addressing standardization to reduce confounds, improve reproducibility, and increase transparency.

Abstract: With the advent of Large Language Models (LLMs), general-purpose agents have seen fundamental advancements. However, evaluating these agents presents unique challenges that distinguish them from static QA benchmarks. We observe that current agent benchmarks are heavily confounded by extraneous factors, including system prompts, toolset configurations, and environmental dynamics. Existing evaluations often rely on fragmented, researcher-specific frameworks where the prompt engineering for reasoning and tool usage varies significantly, making it difficult to attribute performance gains to the model itself. Additionally, the lack of standardized environmental data leads to untraceable errors and non-reproducible results. This lack of standardization introduces substantial unfairness and opacity into the field. We propose that a unified evaluation framework is essential for the rigorous advancement of agent evaluation. To this end, we introduce a proposal aimed at standardizing agent evaluation.

</details>


### [434] [Accordion-Thinking: Self-Regulated Step Summaries for Efficient and Readable LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03249)
*Zhicheng Yang,Zhijiang Guo,Yinya Huang,Yongxin Wang,Wenlei Shi,Yiwei Wang,Xiaodan Liang,Jing Tang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Framework (Accordion-Thinking) enables LLMs to self-compress long reasoning via dynamic summarization (Fold mode), reducing token history without harming accuracy. RL incentives push the model to compress more efficiently; results show the Fold-Unfold accuracy gap closes over training, achieving ~3x throughput on 48GB GPUs with human-readable summaries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome linear KV-cache growth and quadratic attention limits inherent in long chain-of-thought reasoning, enabling scalable, efficient reasoning with large models.

Method: An end-to-end framework where the model periodically summarizes its intermediate thoughts (Fold inference) and discards history to reduce token dependency. Dynamic summarization controls granularity; reinforcement learning incentivizes compression and efficiency during training.

Result: The accuracy gap between Fold and Unfold narrows and eventually vanishes with training. The model learns to encode essential reasoning signals into compact summaries, enabling effective compression. Achieves ~3x throughput on a 48GB GPU while maintaining accuracy; provides structured, human-readable step summaries.

Conclusion: Learned self-compression allows efficient long-horizon reasoning with minimal token overhead, preserving solution quality and enabling scalable inference for complex tasks.

Abstract: Scaling test-time compute via long Chain-ofThought unlocks remarkable gains in reasoning capabilities, yet it faces practical limits due to the linear growth of KV cache and quadratic attention complexity. In this paper, we introduce Accordion-Thinking, an end-to-end framework where LLMs learn to self-regulate the granularity of the reasoning steps through dynamic summarization. This mechanism enables a Fold inference mode, where the model periodically summarizes its thought process and discards former thoughts to reduce dependency on historical tokens. We apply reinforcement learning to incentivize this capability further, uncovering a critical insight: the accuracy gap between the highly efficient Fold mode and the exhaustive Unfold mode progressively narrows and eventually vanishes over the course of training. This phenomenon demonstrates that the model learns to encode essential reasoning information into compact summaries, achieving effective compression of the reasoning context. Our Accordion-Thinker demonstrates that with learned self-compression, LLMs can tackle complex reasoning tasks with minimal dependency token overhead without compromising solution quality, and it achieves a 3x throughput while maintaining accuracy on a 48GB GPU memory configuration, while the structured step summaries provide a human-readable account of the reasoning process.

</details>


### [435] [LPS-Bench: Benchmarking Safety Awareness of Computer-Use Agents in Long-Horizon Planning under Benign and Adversarial Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03255)
*Tianyu Chen,Chujia Hu,Ge Gao,Dongrui Liu,Xia Hu,Wenjie Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LPS-Bench benchmarks long-horizon planning safety for MCP-based CUAs across 65 scenarios (7 domains, 9 risk types), using a data-generating pipeline and an LLM judge to measure planning-time safety along trajectories; reveals safety gaps, offers mitigation strategies, and opensource release.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address planning-time safety risks in long-horizon tasks for computer-use agents (CUAs) that interact with real systems, which existing benchmarks miss by focusing on short-horizon or GUI tasks and execution-time errors.

Method: A multi-agent automated data-generation pipeline creates 65 scenarios spanning 7 task domains and 9 risk types. Safety is assessed with an LLM-as-a-judge protocol that evaluates the planning trajectory of MCP-based CUAs under benign and adversarial interactions.

Result: Experiments show substantial safety-deficiency in current CUAs during long-horizon planning. The authors analyze risks, propose mitigation strategies, and demonstrate improved safety considerations. Code is open-sourced.

Conclusion: Establishes a new benchmark for planning-time safety of MCP-based CUAs in long-horizon tasks, highlights critical gaps in current systems, and provides practical mitigations and a path forward for safer automated tool use.

Abstract: Computer-use agents (CUAs) that interact with real computer systems can perform automated tasks but face critical safety risks. Ambiguous instructions may trigger harmful actions, and adversarial users can manipulate tool execution to achieve malicious goals. Existing benchmarks mostly focus on short-horizon or GUI-based tasks, evaluating on execution-time errors but overlooking the ability to anticipate planning-time risks. To fill this gap, we present LPS-Bench, a benchmark that evaluates the planning-time safety awareness of MCP-based CUAs under long-horizon tasks, covering both benign and adversarial interactions across 65 scenarios of 7 task domains and 9 risk types. We introduce a multi-agent automated pipeline for scalable data generation and adopt an LLM-as-a-judge evaluation protocol to assess safety awareness through the planning trajectory. Experiments reveal substantial deficiencies in existing CUAs' ability to maintain safe behavior. We further analyze the risks and propose mitigation strategies to improve long-horizon planning safety in MCP-based CUA systems. We open-source our code at https://github.com/tychenn/LPS-Bench.

</details>


### [436] [CSR-Bench: A Benchmark for Evaluating the Cross-modal Safety and Reliability of MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03263)
*Yuxuan Liu,Yuntian Shi,Kun Wang,Haoting Shen,Kun Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CSR-Bench is a cross-modal reliability benchmark for multimodal LLMs that tests four stress patterns (Safety, Over-rejection, Bias, Hallucination) across 61 granular types. It requires integrated image-text interpretation and uses text-only controls to diagnose modality-induced shifts; evaluation of 16 state-of-the-art MLLMs reveals cross-modal alignment gaps, weak safety awareness, language-dominant interference, and performance degradation from text-only to multimodal inputs, with a trade-off between reducing over-rejection and maintaining safe, non-discriminatory behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the tendency of multimodal LLM safety to be driven by unimodal shortcuts rather than genuine cross-modal understanding. The work aims to quantify cross-modal reliability and diagnose modality-induced behavior shifts in safety-related tasks.

Method: Introduce CSR-Bench with four stress-testing interaction patterns spanning Safety, Over-rejection, Bias, and Hallucination, covering 61 fine-grained types. Each instance requires integrated image-text interpretation, and paired text-only controls diagnose modality-induced shifts. The authors evaluate 16 state-of-the-art MLLMs.

Result: The study finds systematic cross-modal alignment gaps. Models exhibit weak safety awareness, strong language dominance under interference, and consistent performance degradation when moving from text-only controls to multimodal inputs. There is a trade-off between reducing over-rejection and maintaining safe, non-discriminatory behavior, suggesting some safety gains may stem from refusal-oriented heuristics rather than robust intent understanding.

Conclusion: Current MLLMs show notable cross-modal safety and reliability gaps. Improvements should focus on enhancing genuine joint understanding rather than relying on refusal heuristics, and future work should validate safety behavior across diverse, integrated multimodal scenarios.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) enable interaction over both text and images, but their safety behavior can be driven by unimodal shortcuts instead of true joint intent understanding. We introduce CSR-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating cross-modal reliability through four stress-testing interaction patterns spanning Safety, Over-rejection, Bias, and Hallucination, covering 61 fine-grained types. Each instance is constructed to require integrated image-text interpretation, and we additionally provide paired text-only controls to diagnose modality-induced behavior shifts. We evaluate 16 state-of-the-art MLLMs and observe systematic cross-modal alignment gaps.
