Models show weak safety awareness, strong language dominance under interference, and consistent performance degradation from text-only controls to multimodal inputs. We also observe a clear trade-off between reducing over-rejection and maintaining safe, non-discriminatory behavior, suggesting that some apparent safety gains may come from refusal-oriented heuristics rather than robust intent understanding. WARNING: This paper contains unsafe contents.

</details>


### [437] [Agentic Proposing: Enhancing Large Language Model Reasoning via Compositional Skill Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03279)
*Zhengbo Jiao,Shaobo Wang,Zifan Zhang,Xuan Ren,Wei Wang,Bing Zhao,Hu Wei,Linfeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agentic Proposing uses a goal-driven, modular reasoning agent to synthesize high-quality, verifiable training data, achieving strong cross-domain performance and state-of-the-art results with far fewer trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle the trade-off in data synthesis between problem complexity and structural validity, and between difficulty and solvability, by generating scalable, high-quality synthetic signals to replace large human-curated datasets.

Method: Model problem synthesis as a goal-driven sequential decision process where an agent selects and composes modular reasoning skills; employ internal reflection and tool-use; implement Agentic-Proposer-4B trained with Multi-Granularity Policy Optimization (MGPO) to produce high-precision training trajectories across mathematics, coding, and science.

Result: Downstream solvers trained on agent-synthesized data outperform leading baselines and generalize across domains; a 30B solver trained on 11,000 trajectories achieves 91.6% on AIME25, rivaling frontier models like GPT-5, indicating that high-quality synthetic data can substitute for large human-curated datasets.

Conclusion: Agentic Proposing with MGPO enables scalable generation of high-quality, verifiable training data that supports strong cross-domain generalization and competitive performance with relatively small synthetic datasets.

Abstract: Advancing complex reasoning in large language models relies on high-quality, verifiable datasets, yet human annotation remains cost-prohibitive and difficult to scale. Current synthesis paradigms often face a recurring trade-off: maintaining structural validity typically restricts problem complexity, while relaxing constraints to increase difficulty frequently leads to inconsistent or unsolvable instances. To address this, we propose Agentic Proposing, a framework that models problem synthesis as a goal-driven sequential decision process where a specialized agent dynamically selects and composes modular reasoning skills. Through an iterative workflow of internal reflection and tool-use, we develop the Agentic-Proposer-4B using Multi-Granularity Policy Optimization (MGPO) to generate high-precision, verifiable training trajectories across mathematics, coding, and science. Empirical results demonstrate that downstream solvers trained on agent-synthesized data significantly outperform leading baselines and exhibit robust cross-domain generalization. Notably, a 30B solver trained on only 11,000 synthesized trajectories achieves a state-of-the-art 91.6% accuracy on AIME25, rivaling frontier-scale proprietary models such as GPT-5 and proving that a small volume of high-quality synthetic signals can effectively substitute for massive human-curated datasets.

</details>


### [438] [MeetBench-XL: Calibrated Multi-Dimensional Evaluation and Learned Dual-Policy Agents for Real-Time Meetings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03285)
*Yuelin Hu,Jun Xu,Bingcong Lu,Zhengxue Cheng,Hongwei Hu,Ronghua Wu,Li Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MeetAll: a bilingual multimodal enterprise meeting corpus (231 meetings, ~140 hours) with an enterprise-grounded question-injection protocol; MeetBench XL: a human-aligned, multi-dimension evaluation suite (fidelity, intent, efficiency, clarity, completeness); MeetMaster XL: a dual-policy agent routing between fast/slow reasoning and tool use (retrieval, cross-meeting aggregation, web search) with a lightweight classifier for low-overhead routing, showing gains over commercial systems; resources available at GitHub.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enterprise meeting AI needs go beyond simple QA: queries arise organically, require long temporal contexts, cross-stakeholder perspectives, tool augmentation, and privacy/latency constraints. Current benchmarks fail to capture real-world workflows; there is a need for grounded datasets and evaluation grounded in enterprise tasks.

Method: 1) Data: MeetAll—231 enterprise meetings (140 hours), bilingual and multimodal; questions injected via an enterprise-informed protocol, validated by domain experts and human discriminability studies; protocol grounded in cognitive load, temporal context span, domain expertise, and actionable task execution. 2) Evaluation: MeetBench XL—multi-dimensional protocol aligned with human judgement: factual fidelity, intent alignment, response efficiency, structural clarity, completeness. 3) Modeling: MeetMaster XL—a dual-policy agent jointly optimizes routing between fast/slow reasoning and tool invocation (retrieval, cross-meeting aggregation, web search). Includes a lightweight classifier for routing with low overhead; aims for better latency-quality tradeoffs. 4) Evaluation: ablations, robustness tests, real-world deployment case study; comparison to commercial systems.

Result: Communication shows consistent gains over commercial baselines, with ablations and robustness validated; the dual-policy approach plus tool augmentation yields improved latency-quality tradeoffs and effective cross-meeting analytics; real-world deployment supports practicality.

Conclusion: A grounded enterprise-meeting benchmark suite (MeetAll + MeetBench XL) and a dual-policy, tool-augmented agent (MeetMaster XL) address enterprise-specific AI workflow needs, enabling improved factual fidelity, intent alignment, and efficiency in real-world scenarios; resources are provided for reproducibility.

Abstract: Enterprise meeting environments require AI assistants that handle diverse operational tasks, from rapid fact checking during live discussions to cross meeting analysis for strategic planning, under strict latency, cost, and privacy constraints. Existing meeting benchmarks mainly focus on simplified question answering and fail to reflect real world enterprise workflows, where queries arise organically from multi stakeholder collaboration, span long temporal contexts, and require tool augmented reasoning.
  We address this gap through a grounded dataset and a learned agent framework. First, we introduce MeetAll, a bilingual and multimodal corpus derived from 231 enterprise meetings totaling 140 hours. Questions are injected using an enterprise informed protocol validated by domain expert review and human discriminability studies. Unlike purely synthetic benchmarks, this protocol is grounded in four enterprise critical dimensions: cognitive load, temporal context span, domain expertise, and actionable task execution, calibrated through interviews with stakeholders across finance, healthcare, and technology sectors.
  Second, we propose MeetBench XL, a multi dimensional evaluation protocol aligned with human judgment that measures factual fidelity, intent alignment, response efficiency, structural clarity, and completeness. Third, we present MeetMaster XL, a learned dual policy agent that jointly optimizes query routing between fast and slow reasoning paths and tool invocation, including retrieval, cross meeting aggregation, and web search. A lightweight classifier enables accurate routing with minimal overhead, achieving a superior quality latency tradeoff over single model baselines. Experiments against commercial systems show consistent gains, supported by ablations, robustness tests, and a real world deployment case study.Resources: https://github.com/huyuelin/MeetBench.

</details>


### [439] [Rejecting Arguments Based on Doubt in Structured Bipolar Argumentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03286)
*Michael A. Müller,Srdjan Vesic,Bruno Yun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces structured bipolar argumentation frameworks (SBAFs) where arguments are sentences with attack and support relations; presents semantics that allow not forcing acceptance of defended arguments and include language extensions for sentence-level acceptability, placing these semantics between admissible and complete; demonstrates how deductive support semantics arise as a special case and how the approach clarifies when abstract argumentation is warranted.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivated by two often-ignored ideas: (i) agents may rationally reject arguments based on doubt, so not all defendable arguments must be accepted, and (ii) it can be clearer to reason about which sentences an agent accepts rather than which arguments; existing abstract frameworks emphasize complete/acceptance of arguments and may overlook sentence-level acceptability.

Method: Define SBAFs with sentences as arguments and both attack and support relations; develop two-semantic features: (1) non-forcing of acceptance of defended arguments (unlike completeness); (2) language extensions to specify acceptable sentence sets alongside argument extensions; formalize semantics that yield sets of arguments and sets of sentences; relate to existing abstract argumentation by showing when to ignore support and that deductive support semantics is a special case.

Result: A coherent semantic framework that sits between admissible and complete semantics, enabling both argument-level and sentence-level extensions; demonstrates that deductive support semantics can be recovered; provides a new perspective on interpretations of argumentation and a way to bridge with existing approaches.

Conclusion: SBAFs offer a middle ground between admissible and complete semantics, enabling sentence-level acceptability and providing conditions under which abstract argumentation is warranted; the framework unifies and generalizes existing approaches and opens avenues for further exploration of linguistic and structured aspects in computational argumentation.

Abstract: This paper develops a new approach to computational argumentation that is informed by philosophical and linguistic views. Namely, it takes into account two ideas that have received little attention in the literature on computational argumentation: First, an agent may rationally reject an argument based on mere doubt, thus not all arguments they could defend must be accepted; and, second, that it is sometimes more natural to think in terms of which individual sentences or claims an agent accepts in a debate, rather than which arguments. In order to incorporate these two ideas into a computational approach, we first define the notion of structured bipolar argumentation frameworks (SBAFs), where arguments consist of sentences and we have both an attack and a support relation between them. Then, we provide semantics for SBAFs with two features: (1) Unlike with completeness-based semantics, our semantics do not force agents to accept all defended arguments. (2) In addition to argument extensions, which give acceptable sets of arguments, we also provide semantics for language extensions that specify acceptable sets of sentences. These semantics represent reasonable positions an agent might have in a debate. Our semantics lie between the admissible and complete semantics of abstract argumentation. Further, our approach can be used to provide a new perspective on existing approaches. For instance, we can specify the conditions under which an agent can ignore support between arguments (i.e. under which the use of abstract argumentation is warranted) and we show that deductive support semantics is a special case of our approach.

</details>


### [440] [Memora: A Harmonic Memory Representation Balancing Abstraction and Specificity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03315)
*Menglin Xia,Xuchao Zhang,Shantanu Dixit,Paramaguru Harimurugan,Rujia Wang,Victor Ruhle,Robert Sim,Chetan Bansal,Saravan Rajmohan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Memora introduces a harmonic memory framework that balances abstraction and detail, using abstractions, cue anchors, and a retrieval policy to enable scalable, context-aware recall; it subsumes standard RAG and KG memory as special cases and achieves state-of-the-art results on LoCoMo and LongMemEval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agent memory must grow with information while supporting efficient, context-aware retrieval; abstraction is needed for scalability but can obscure fine-grained details necessary for reasoning, requiring a balance.

Method: Memora organizes memory with primary abstractions that index concrete memory values and consolidate related updates into unified memory entries, plus cue anchors that broaden retrieval across memory and link related memories; a retrieval policy actively exploits these connections to retrieve beyond direct semantic similarity; theoretical analysis shows RAG and KG-based memory systems as special cases of the framework.

Result: The framework unifies existing memory paradigms (RAG and KG) as special cases and demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on LoCoMo and LongMemEval, with improved retrieval relevance and reasoning as memory scales.

Conclusion: Memora provides a scalable, well-connected memory representation that preserves detail through abstraction, enabling more effective retrieval and reasoning and encompassing existing approaches as special cases.

Abstract: Agent memory systems must accommodate continuously growing information while supporting efficient, context-aware retrieval for downstream tasks. Abstraction is essential for scaling agent memory, yet it often comes at the cost of specificity, obscuring the fine-grained details required for effective reasoning. We introduce Memora, a harmonic memory representation that structurally balances abstraction and specificity. Memora organizes information via its primary abstractions that index concrete memory values and consolidate related updates into unified memory entries, while cue anchors expand retrieval access across diverse aspects of the memory and connect related memories. Building on this structure, we employ a retrieval policy that actively exploits these memory connections to retrieve relevant information beyond direct semantic similarity. Theoretically, we show that standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Knowledge Graph (KG)-based memory systems emerge as special cases of our framework. Empirically, Memora establishes a new state-of-the-art on the LoCoMo and LongMemEval benchmarks, demonstrating better retrieval relevance and reasoning effectiveness as memory scales.

</details>


### [441] [MentalSeek-Dx: Towards Progressive Hypothetico-Deductive Reasoning for Real-world Psychiatric Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03340)
*Xiao Sun,Yuming Yang,Junnan Zhu,Jiang Zhong,Xinyu Zhou,Kaiwen Wei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: First disorder-level psychiatric diagnosis benchmark in real-world settings; 712 de-identified EHRs, 76 disorders, 18 LLMs show parity with coarse categories but fail at disorder-level; introduces MentalSeek-Dx, a 14B-parameter model trained with supervised trajectory construction and curriculum RL to achieve SOTA on MentalDx Bench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between ecologically valid clinical assessment and existing benchmarks that measure coarse diagnostic categories; promote clinically grounded reasoning in LLMs for psychiatric diagnosis.

Method: Create MentalDx Bench: 712 de-identified EHRs annotated by board-certified psychiatrists under ICD-11; covers 76 disorders across 16 categories. Evaluate 18 LLMs; identify paradigm misalignment between pattern recognition and clinical hypothetico-deductive reasoning. Develop MentalSeek-Dx, a medical-specialized LLM trained to internalize clinical reasoning via supervised trajectory construction and curriculum-based reinforcement learning.

Result: MentalSeek-Dx achieves state-of-the-art performance on MentalDx Bench with 14B parameters, outperforming larger models and highlighting the value of trajectory-based clinical reasoning for disorder-level psychiatric diagnosis.

Conclusion: A clinically grounded framework with supervised trajectory construction and curriculum RL can bridge the gap between pattern-based LLMs and clinically valid, disorder-level psychiatric diagnosis, enabling reliable, efficient diagnosis with a compact model.

Abstract: Mental health disorders represent a burgeoning global public health challenge. While Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated potential in psychiatric assessment, their clinical utility is severely constrained by benchmarks that lack ecological validity and fine-grained diagnostic supervision. To bridge this gap, we introduce \textbf{MentalDx Bench}, the first benchmark dedicated to disorder-level psychiatric diagnosis within real-world clinical settings. Comprising 712 de-identified electronic health records annotated by board-certified psychiatrists under ICD-11 guidelines, the benchmark covers 76 disorders across 16 diagnostic categories. Evaluation of 18 LLMs reveals a critical \textit{paradigm misalignment}: strong performance at coarse diagnostic categorization contrasts with systematic failure at disorder-level diagnosis, underscoring a gap between pattern-based modeling and clinical hypothetico-deductive reasoning. In response, we propose \textbf{MentalSeek-Dx}, a medical-specialized LLM trained to internalize this clinical reasoning process through supervised trajectory construction and curriculum-based reinforcement learning. Experiments on MentalDx Bench demonstrate that MentalSeek-Dx achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance with only 14B parameters, establishing a clinically grounded framework for reliable psychiatric diagnosis.

</details>


### [442] [CRL-VLA: Continual Vision-Language-Action Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03445)
*Qixin Zeng,Shuo Zhang,Hongyin Zhang,Renjie Wang,Han Zhao,Libang Zhao,Runze Li,Donglin Wang,Chao Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CRL-VLA introduces a continual post-training framework for Vision-Language-Action models in open-world reinforcement learning, featuring a theoretical stability-plasticity bound and an asymmetric regulation strategy via a dual-critic with Goal-Conditioned Value Formulation (GCVF). It achieves improved anti-forgetting and forward adaptation on LIBERO by anchoring semantics with a frozen critic and enabling adaptation with a trainable estimator.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In open-world embodied robotics, agents must retain old skills while rapidly acquiring new ones. Continual reinforcement learning (CRL) promises lifelong adaptability for VLA models, but achieving stability and plasticity simultaneously remains challenging. A theoretical bound helps understand trade-offs and guides algorithm design.

Method: Propose CRL-VLA: a dual-critic architecture with a frozen (semantic-anchor) critic and a trainable estimator under a Goal-Conditioned Value Formulation (GCVF). Introduce asymmetric regulation by constraining the magnitude of advantages on prior tasks while allowing growth on new tasks. Derive a unified performance bound linking stability-plasticity to goal-conditioned advantage scaled by policy divergence, and validate on LIBERO benchmark.

Result: Empirical results show CRL-VLA better balances stability and plasticity, achieving stronger anti-forgetting and improved forward adaptation compared to baselines on LIBERO.

Conclusion: CRL-VLA provides a principled approach to continual post-training for VLA models by tying performance to a task-goal advantage metric and enforcing asymmetric constraints across tasks, yielding improved lifelong learning in robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Lifelong learning is critical for embodied agents in open-world environments, where reinforcement learning fine-tuning has emerged as an important paradigm to enable Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models to master dexterous manipulation through environmental interaction. Thus, Continual Reinforcement Learning (CRL) is a promising pathway for deploying VLA models in lifelong robotic scenarios, yet balancing stability (retaining old skills) and plasticity (learning new ones) remains a formidable challenge for existing methods. We introduce CRL-VLA, a framework for continual post-training of VLA models with rigorous theoretical bounds. We derive a unified performance bound linking the stability-plasticity trade-off to goal-conditioned advantage magnitude, scaled by policy divergence. CRL-VLA resolves this dilemma via asymmetric regulation: constraining advantage magnitudes on prior tasks while enabling controlled growth on new tasks. This is realized through a simple but effective dual-critic architecture with novel Goal-Conditioned Value Formulation (GCVF), where a frozen critic anchors semantic consistency and a trainable estimator drives adaptation. Experiments on the LIBERO benchmark demonstrate that CRL-VLA effectively harmonizes these conflicting objectives, outperforming baselines in both anti-forgetting and forward adaptation.

</details>


### [443] [Building Interpretable Models for Moral Decision-Making](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03351)
*Mayank Goel,Aritra Das,Paras Chopra*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A small 2-layer transformer on trolley-style moral dilemmas attains 77% accuracy on Moral Machine data and is amenable to detailed interpretability analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how neural networks perform moral reasoning and where biases emerge during computation.

Method: Build a compact 2-layer transformer with embeddings that encode who is affected, how many are involved, and the outcome; train on Moral Machine data; apply multiple interpretability techniques to map moral reasoning to distinct network stages.

Result: Achieves 77% accuracy; model remains small enough for in-depth analysis; biases localize to distinct computational stages as revealed by interpretability analyses.

Conclusion: Small, interpretable models can illuminate how machine moral judgments arise, showing stage-specific biases and providing a tractable framework for studying machine morality.

Abstract: We build a custom transformer model to study how neural networks make moral decisions on trolley-style dilemmas. The model processes structured scenarios using embeddings that encode who is affected, how many people, and which outcome they belong to. Our 2-layer architecture achieves 77% accuracy on Moral Machine data while remaining small enough for detailed analysis. We use different interpretability techniques to uncover how moral reasoning distributes across the network, demonstrating that biases localize to distinct computational stages among other findings.

</details>


### [444] [GFlowPO: Generative Flow Network as a Language Model Prompt Optimizer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03358)
*Junmo Cho,Suhan Kim,Sangjune An,Minsu Kim,Dong Bok Lee,Heejun Lee,Sung Ju Hwang,Hae Beom Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GFlowPO introduces a probabilistic, sample-efficient prompt optimization framework that casts prompt search as posterior inference with a meta-prompt prior, using an off-policy GFlowNet to train a prompt-LM and a Dynamic Memory Update to focus search on high-reward prompts. It achieves superior results on multiple NLP tasks compared with discrete prompt baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the combinatorial prompt space and sparse rewards in LM prompting; existing RL-based methods are often on-policy and data-inefficient due to fixed meta-prompts.

Method: Two-stage approach: (1) train a lightweight prompt-LM using an off-policy Generative Flow Network objective with replay-based policy to reuse past evaluations; (2) apply Dynamic Memory Update (DMU) to update the meta-prompt by integrating diverse prompts from a replay buffer and top-performing ones from a small priority queue, thus concentrating search on high-reward regions.

Result: Empirically, GFlowPO yields consistent improvements over recent discrete prompt optimization baselines across few-shot text classification, instruction induction, and QA tasks.

Conclusion: Framing prompt search as posterior inference with GFlowNet and DMU yields sample-efficient, scalable prompt optimization with strong cross-task performance; the approach reduces reliance on on-policy updates and enhances exploration-exploitation balance.

Abstract: Finding effective prompts for language models (LMs) is critical yet notoriously difficult: the prompt space is combinatorially large, rewards are sparse due to expensive target-LM evaluation. Yet, existing RL-based prompt optimizers often rely on on-policy updates and a meta-prompt sampled from a fixed distribution, leading to poor sample efficiency. We propose GFlowPO, a probabilistic prompt optimization framework that casts prompt search as a posterior inference problem over latent prompts regularized by a meta-prompted reference-LM prior. In the first step, we fine-tune a lightweight prompt-LM with an off-policy Generative Flow Network (GFlowNet) objective, using a replay-based training policy that reuses past prompt evaluations to enable sample-efficient exploration. In the second step, we introduce Dynamic Memory Update (DMU), a training-free mechanism that updates the meta-prompt by injecting both (i) diverse prompts from a replay buffer and (ii) top-performing prompts from a small priority queue, thereby progressively concentrating the search process on high-reward regions. Across few-shot text classification, instruction induction benchmarks, and question answering tasks, GFlowPO consistently outperforms recent discrete prompt optimization baselines.

</details>


### [445] [Risk Awareness Injection: Calibrating Vision-Language Models for Safety without Compromising Utility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03402)
*Mengxuan Wang,Yuxin Chen,Gang Xu,Tao He,Hongjie Jiang,Ming Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RAI is a training-free safety calibration that boosts VLM risk signals by modulating high-risk visual tokens via an Unsafe Prototype Subspace, restoring LLМ-like unsafe-content detection without hurting cross-modal reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs dilute or obscure risk cues from unsafe visual content; existing safety methods are costly or degrade utility. A lightweight, training-free approach that reclaims LLM-like risk recognition in VLMs is highly desirable.

Method: Build an Unsafe Prototype Subspace from language embeddings and apply targeted modulation to selected high-risk visual tokens to amplify safety-critical signals in the cross-modal feature space, preserving original token semantics.

Result: Across multiple jailbreak and utility benchmarks, RAI substantially reduces attack success while maintaining task performance.

Conclusion: RAI offers a lightweight, training-free mechanism to recalibrate VLM safety, restoring LLM-like risk perception in the visual modality with minimal utility loss; efficacy hinges on correct identification of high-risk visual tokens and the robustness of the prototype subspace.

Abstract: Vision language models (VLMs) extend the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) to cross-modal settings, yet remain highly vulnerable to multimodal jailbreak attacks. Existing defenses predominantly rely on safety fine-tuning or aggressive token manipulations, incurring substantial training costs or significantly degrading utility. Recent research shows that LLMs inherently recognize unsafe content in text, and the incorporation of visual inputs in VLMs frequently dilutes risk-related signals. Motivated by this, we propose Risk Awareness Injection (RAI), a lightweight and training-free framework for safety calibration that restores LLM-like risk recognition by amplifying unsafe signals in VLMs. Specifically, RAI constructs an Unsafe Prototype Subspace from language embeddings and performs targeted modulation on selected high-risk visual tokens, explicitly activating safety-critical signals within the cross-modal feature space. This modulation restores the model's LLM-like ability to detect unsafe content from visual inputs, while preserving the semantic integrity of original tokens for cross-modal reasoning. Extensive experiments across multiple jailbreak and utility benchmarks demonstrate that RAI substantially reduces attack success rate without compromising task performance.

</details>


### [446] [Feasible strategies for conflict resolution within intuitionistic fuzzy preference-based conflict situations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03403)
*Guangming Lang,Mingchuan Shang,Mengjun Hu,Jie Zhou,Feng Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces intuitionistic fuzzy preference-based conflict (IFPFC) to capture nuanced attitudes toward issue pairs; develops measures and three-way conflict analysis models for triads (pairs, agents, issues); uses relative loss functions to set thresholds; proposes adjustment mechanism-based feasible strategies and an algorithm, illustrated by an example.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical preference-based conflict models rely on only three qualitative relations (preference, converse, indifference), which inadequately capture the subtleties of conflict; there is a need for finer granularity using intuitionistic fuzzy sets to model degrees of acceptance, rejection, and hesitation in attitudes toward issue pairs.

Method: Define intuitionistic fuzzy preference-based conflict situation; derive conflict measures; construct three-way analysis models for different triads (trisecting issue pairs, agents, and issues); introduce relative loss functions to determine thresholds; develop adjustment mechanism–based feasible strategies and an algorithm to construct them.

Result: The framework enables finer-grained conflict assessment and actionable strategies; an illustrative example demonstrates the model’s validity and effectiveness.

Conclusion: The intuitionistic fuzzy framework enhances precision in conflict modeling, enabling better thresholding and strategy construction; the approach is applicable to triadic analyses of agent pairs, agents, and issues.

Abstract: In three-way conflict analysis, preference-based conflict situations characterize agents' attitudes towards issues by formally modeling their preferences over pairs of issues. However, existing preference-based conflict models rely exclusively on three qualitative relations, namely, preference, converse, and indifference, to describe agents' attitudes towards issue pairs, which significantly limits their capacity in capturing the essence of conflict. To overcome this limitation, we introduce the concept of an intuitionistic fuzzy preference-based conflict situation that captures agents' attitudes towards issue pairs with finer granularity than that afforded by classical preference-based models. Afterwards, we develop intuitionistic fuzzy preference-based conflict measures within this framework, and construct three-way conflict analysis models for trisecting the set of agent pairs, the agent set, and the issue set. Additionally, relative loss functions built on the proposed conflict functions are employed to calculate thresholds for three-way conflict analysis. Finally, we present adjustment mechanism-based feasible strategies that simultaneously account for both adjustment magnitudes and conflict degrees, together with an algorithm for constructing such feasible strategies, and provide an illustrative example to demonstrate the validity and effectiveness of the proposed model.

</details>


### [447] [DiscoverLLM: From Executing Intents to Discovering Them](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03429)
*Tae Soo Kim,Yoonjoo Lee,Jaesang Yu,John Joon Young Chung,Juho Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DiscoverLLM presents a general framework for training LLMs to help users form and discover their intents by using a hierarchical user-state model that guides exploration when intents are unclear and convergence when they crystallize. It demonstrates cross-domain gains in task performance and conversation efficiency, with a human study showing improved satisfaction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Users often arrive with ill-formed or evolving intents; traditional clarifications fail when users have not yet formed concrete goals. There is a need for an interactive paradigm where the model collaborates with users to surface latent intents through exploration rather than static questions.

Method: Propose DiscoverLLM with a novel hierarchical user simulator that models cognitive states as a sequence of progressively concrete intents. The degree of concretization serves as a reward signal for training the LLM to adaptively diverge (explore options) when intents are unclear and converge (refine/execute) when intents crystallize. Evaluation across interactive benchmarks in creative writing, technical writing, and SVG drawing; includes a 75-participant human study.

Result: The framework achieves over 10% higher task performance and up to 40% shorter conversations in proposed benchmarks. In a user study with 75 participants, DiscoverLLM improved conversation satisfaction and efficiency compared to baselines.

Conclusion: A generalizable, exploration–convergence collaboration paradigm enables LLMs to assist users in forming and discovering their intents, improving efficiency and satisfaction across domains. The hierarchical intent model and reward signal provide a scalable mechanism for training models to surface latent goals.

Abstract: To handle ambiguous and open-ended requests, Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly trained to interact with users to surface intents they have not yet expressed (e.g., ask clarification questions). However, users are often ambiguous because they have not yet formed their intents: they must observe and explore outcomes to discover what they want. Simply asking "what kind of tone do you want?" fails when users themselves do not know. We introduce DiscoverLLM, a novel and generalizable framework that trains LLMs to help users form and discover their intents. Central to our approach is a novel user simulator that models cognitive state with a hierarchy of intents that progressively concretize as the model surfaces relevant options -- where the degree of concretization serves as a reward signal that models can be trained to optimize. Resulting models learn to collaborate with users by adaptively diverging (i.e., explore options) when intents are unclear, and converging (i.e., refine and implement) when intents concretize. Across proposed interactive benchmarks in creative writing, technical writing, and SVG drawing, DiscoverLLM achieves over 10% higher task performance while reducing conversation length by up to 40%. In a user study with 75 human participants, DiscoverLLM improved conversation satisfaction and efficiency compared to baselines.

</details>


### [448] [Ontology-to-tools compilation for executable semantic constraint enforcement in LLM agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03439)
*Xiaochi Zhou,Patrick Bulter,Changxuan Yang,Simon D. Rihm,Thitikarn Angkanaporn,Jethro Akroyd,Sebastian Mosbach,Markus Kraft*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Ontology-to-tools compilation embeds executable ontological semantics into LLM-driven knowledge graph work, using The World Avatar (TWA) and the Model Context Protocol to enforce semantic constraints during generation; demonstrated on metal-organic polyhedra synthesis literature, reducing manual schema and prompt engineering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge large language models with formal domain knowledge by translating ontologies into executable tools that constrain generation and knowledge construction at generation time rather than post-hoc validation.

Method: Extend TWA's semantic agent framework with the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and ontology-derived tools; implement an agent-based workflow that translates ontologies into executable tools and iteratively extracts, validates, and repairs structured knowledge from unstructured text; validate via a metal-organic polyhedra synthesis case study.

Result: Executable ontological semantics guide LLM behavior, enabling semantic constraints during knowledge extraction and repair and reducing manual schema/prompt engineering; demonstrates a general paradigm for embedding formal knowledge into generative systems.

Conclusion: Ontology-to-tools compilation is a general, viable paradigm for coupling LLMs with formal domain knowledge, enabling more reliable, constraint-aware generation and knowledge construction across domains.

Abstract: We introduce ontology-to-tools compilation as a proof-of-principle mechanism for coupling large language models (LLMs) with formal domain knowledge. Within The World Avatar (TWA), ontological specifications are compiled into executable tool interfaces that LLM-based agents must use to create and modify knowledge graph instances, enforcing semantic constraints during generation rather than through post-hoc validation. Extending TWA's semantic agent composition framework, the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and associated agents are integral components of the knowledge graph ecosystem, enabling structured interaction between generative models, symbolic constraints, and external resources. An agent-based workflow translates ontologies into ontology-aware tools and iteratively applies them to extract, validate, and repair structured knowledge from unstructured scientific text. Using metal-organic polyhedra synthesis literature as an illustrative case, we show how executable ontological semantics can guide LLM behaviour and reduce manual schema and prompt engineering, establishing a general paradigm for embedding formal knowledge into generative systems.

</details>


### [449] [The Dual Role of Abstracting over the Irrelevant in Symbolic Explanations: Cognitive Effort vs. Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03467)
*Zeynep G. Saribatur,Johannes Langer,Ute Schmid*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Abstracting symbolic explanations via clustering and removal in ASP-based explanations can improve understanding and reduce cognitive effort, supporting abstraction as a method for human-centered symbolic explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI explanations are often transparent in principle but cognitively demanding. The work seeks effective abstractions to reduce cognitive load while preserving or enhancing interpretability, leveraging symbolic, rule-based reasoning.

Method: Define a notion of irrelevant details within Answer Set Programming (ASP) and implement two abstractions: removal and clustering. Conduct cognitive experiments where participants classify stimuli across domains using explanations derived from an ASP, measuring understanding and cognitive effort.

Result: Clustering details significantly improves participants' understanding; removal of details significantly reduces cognitive effort; overall results support the hypothesis that abstraction enhances human-centered symbolic explanations.

Conclusion: Formal abstractions in symbolic explanations (via ASP) can balance transparency and cognitive load. Clustering aids comprehension; removal lowers effort, suggesting practical strategies for designing human-friendly explainable AI.

Abstract: Explanations are central to human cognition, yet AI systems often produce outputs that are difficult to understand. While symbolic AI offers a transparent foundation for interpretability, raw logical traces often impose a high extraneous cognitive load. We investigate how formal abstractions, specifically removal and clustering, impact human reasoning performance and cognitive effort. Utilizing Answer Set Programming (ASP) as a formal framework, we define a notion of irrelevant details to be abstracted over to obtain simplified explanations. Our cognitive experiments, in which participants classified stimuli across domains with explanations derived from an answer set program, show that clustering details significantly improve participants' understanding, while removal of details significantly reduce cognitive effort, supporting the hypothesis that abstraction enhances human-centered symbolic explanations.

</details>


### [450] [IntentRL: Training Proactive User-intent Agents for Open-ended Deep Research via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03468)
*Haohao Luo,Zexi Li,Yuexiang Xie,Wenhao Zhang,Yaliang Li,Ying Shen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: IntentRL introduces proactive, intent-clarification-driven DR agents to mitigate autonomy-interaction trade-off in long-horizon research. It trains via a shallow-to-deep intent refinement graph to expand data and a two-stage RL (offline then online with a user simulator) to improve intent handling and downstream performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DR agents enable long-horizon, evidence-grounded research beyond parametric knowledge but are computationally expensive and slow, especially when user intents are unclear. Clarifying latent intents before execution can improve efficiency and result quality.

Method: Propose IntentRL framework with: (1) a scalable shallow-to-deep intent refinement graph to expand seed samples into high-quality dialogue turns; (2) a two-stage RL: Stage I offline RL on dialogues to learn general user-interaction behavior; Stage II online rollouts with a user simulator to adapt to diverse feedback.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows IntentRL improves intent hit rate and downstream task performance, outperforming built-in clarify modules of closed-source DR agents and proactive LLM baselines.

Conclusion: Clarifying latent user intents prior to long-horizon research effectively reduces wasted computation and enhances DR agent performance; IntentRL offers a practical, scalable approach to balancing autonomy and interaction in long-horizon AI agents.

Abstract: Deep Research (DR) agents extend Large Language Models (LLMs) beyond parametric knowledge by autonomously retrieving and synthesizing evidence from large web corpora into long-form reports, enabling a long-horizon agentic paradigm. However, unlike real-time conversational assistants, DR is computationally expensive and time-consuming, creating an autonomy-interaction dilemma: high autonomy on ambiguous user queries often leads to prolonged execution with unsatisfactory outcomes. To address this, we propose IntentRL, a framework that trains proactive agents to clarify latent user intents before starting long-horizon research. To overcome the scarcity of open-ended research data, we introduce a scalable pipeline that expands a few seed samples into high-quality dialogue turns via a shallow-to-deep intent refinement graph. We further adopt a two-stage reinforcement learning (RL) strategy: Stage I applies RL on offline dialogues to efficiently learn general user-interaction behavior, while Stage II uses the trained agent and a user simulator for online rollouts to strengthen adaptation to diverse user feedback. Extensive experiments show that IntentRL significantly improves both intent hit rate and downstream task performance, outperforming the built-in clarify modules of closed-source DR agents and proactive LLM baselines.

</details>


### [451] [When Routing Collapses: On the Degenerate Convergence of LLM Routers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03478)
*Guannan Lai,Han-Jia Ye*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EquiRouter is a decision-aware router that directly learns model rankings to address routing collapse in LLM routing, achieving substantial cost savings (~17% on RouterBench at GPT-4-level performance) while maintaining quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Routing collapse: as budgets rise, routers prefer the strongest model even when cheaper models suffice, causing under-utilization of small models and wasted cost across unimodal and multimodal settings.

Method: Introduce EquiRouter, a ranking-based router that optimizes discrete model orderings rather than scalar performance scores, using a decision-aware objective (pairwise/triplet ranking) to align training with routing decisions.

Result: On RouterBench, EquiRouter reduces cost by about 17% at GPT-4-level performance compared to the strongest prior router; demonstrates restoration of small-model utility.

Conclusion: Treating routing as a ranking problem mitigates routing collapse, restoring the role of smaller models and improving cost-efficiency without sacrificing performance; code available at the authors' repository.

Abstract: LLM routing aims to achieve a favorable quality--cost trade-off by dynamically assigning easy queries to smaller models and harder queries to stronger ones. However, across both unimodal and multimodal settings, we uncover a pervasive yet underexplored failure mode in existing routers: as the user's cost budget increases, routers systematically default to the most capable and most expensive model even when cheaper models already suffice. As a result, current routers under-utilize small models, wasting computation and monetary cost and undermining the core promise of routing; we term this phenomenon routing collapse. We attribute routing collapse to an objective--decision mismatch: many routers are trained to predict scalar performance scores, whereas routing decisions ultimately depend on discrete comparisons among candidate models. Consequently, small prediction errors can flip relative orderings and trigger suboptimal selections. To bridge this gap, we propose EquiRouter, a decision-aware router that directly learns model rankings, restoring the role of smaller models and mitigating routing collapse. On RouterBench, EquiRouter reduces cost by about 17\% at GPT-4-level performance compared to the strongest prior router. Our code is available at https://github.com/AIGNLAI/EquiRouter.

</details>


### [452] [Group Selection as a Safeguard Against AI Substitution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03541)
*Qiankun Zhong,Thomas F. Eisenmann,Julian Garcia,Iyad Rahwan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI use strategies split the long-term cultural impact: substitute use dominates at the individual level, reducing variance more; complement use preserves variance and can be favored by group-level selection, suggesting policy to curb cultural collapse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how AI-driven content production affects cultural variance and cumulative evolution, and how different usage patterns shape long-term outcomes, including risks of cultural collapse.

Method: Agent-based modeling and evolutionary game theory comparing AI-complement (AI assists but humans remain main producers) versus AI-substitute (AI does most output) and tracking competition under evolutionary dynamics.

Result: AI-substitute users dominate via individual selection despite greater variance loss; AI-complement users can sustain exploration and may be favored under strong group selection; results highlight tension between individual vs group dynamics in AI adoption.

Conclusion: Findings inform policy and organizational design to mitigate cultural collapse risks, emphasizing strategies that preserve variance and group-level benefits while managing individual incentives.

Abstract: Reliance on generative AI can reduce cultural variance and diversity, especially in creative work. This reduction in variance has already led to problems in model performance, including model collapse and hallucination. In this paper, we examine the long-term consequences of AI use for human cultural evolution and the conditions under which widespread AI use may lead to "cultural collapse", a process in which reliance on AI-generated content reduces human variation and innovation and slows cumulative cultural evolution. Using an agent-based model and evolutionary game theory, we compare two types of AI use: complement and substitute. AI-complement users seek suggestions and guidance while remaining the main producers of the final output, whereas AI-substitute users provide minimal input, and rely on AI to produce most of the output. We then study how these use strategies compete and spread under evolutionary dynamics. We find that AI-substitute users prevail under individual-level selection despite the stronger reduction in cultural variance. By contrast, AI-complement users can benefit their groups by maintaining the variance needed for exploration, and can therefore be favored under cultural group selection when group boundaries are strong. Overall, our findings shed light on the long-term, population-level effects of AI adoption and inform policy and organizational strategies to mitigate these risks.

</details>


### [453] [Persona Generators: Generating Diverse Synthetic Personas at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03545)
*Davide Paglieri,Logan Cross,William A. Cunningham,Joel Z. Leibo,Alexander Sasha Vezhnevets*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Persona Generators that autonomously expand small persona descriptions into diverse synthetic populations using an iterative AlphaEvolve loop with LLMs as mutation operators, emphasizing coverage over density to capture long-tail behaviors; outperforms baselines on six diversity metrics and reveals rare trait combinations in held-out contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating AI systems that interact with humans requires representative user data. Collecting diverse real data is costly or infeasible, especially for novel tech or hypothetical scenarios. Existing density-focused synthetic methods miss long-tail behaviors. The work seeks scalable generation of context-tailored synthetic populations with broad trait coverage.

Method: Define lightweight Persona Generators that take small descriptions and generate diverse personas. Apply an iterative improvement loop (AlphaEvolve) where large language models act as mutation operators to refine the generator code over hundreds of iterations. Optimize for maximizing coverage of opinions and preferences across relevant diversity axes, evaluated in held-out contexts across six diversity metrics.

Result: Evolved generators substantially outperform baselines on six diversity metrics in held-out contexts, producing populations that span rare trait combinations difficult to achieve with standard LLM outputs.

Conclusion: The approach improves coverage of possible user behaviors for evaluating AI systems, enabling scalable, context-aware synthetic populations. Potential limitations include reliance on LLM quality and synthetic data biases; future work could refine diversity axes, validate against real-world analogs, and explore broader applications.

Abstract: Evaluating AI systems that interact with humans requires understanding their behavior across diverse user populations, but collecting representative human data is often expensive or infeasible, particularly for novel technologies or hypothetical future scenarios. Recent work in Generative Agent-Based Modeling has shown that large language models can simulate human-like synthetic personas with high fidelity, accurately reproducing the beliefs and behaviors of specific individuals. However, most approaches require detailed data about target populations and often prioritize density matching (replicating what is most probable) rather than support coverage (spanning what is possible), leaving long-tail behaviors underexplored. We introduce Persona Generators, functions that can produce diverse synthetic populations tailored to arbitrary contexts. We apply an iterative improvement loop based on AlphaEvolve, using large language models as mutation operators to refine our Persona Generator code over hundreds of iterations. The optimization process produces lightweight Persona Generators that can automatically expand small descriptions into populations of diverse synthetic personas that maximize coverage of opinions and preferences along relevant diversity axes. We demonstrate that evolved generators substantially outperform existing baselines across six diversity metrics on held-out contexts, producing populations that span rare trait combinations difficult to achieve in standard LLM outputs.

</details>


### [454] [EHRWorld: A Patient-Centric Medical World Model for Long-Horizon Clinical Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03569)
*Linjie Mu,Zhongzhen Huang,Yannian Gu,Shengqian Qin,Shaoting Zhang,Xiaofan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes EHRWorld, a causal, patient-centric medical world model trained on EHR data (EHRWorld-110K) that yields more stable, accurate long-horizon clinical simulations than standard LLM baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static LLM medical reasoning struggles to maintain coherent patient states under sequential interventions; error accumulates in long-horizon clinical simulations.

Method: Develop EHRWorld using a causal sequential training paradigm; construct EHRWorld-110K, a large-scale longitudinal EHR-derived dataset; evaluate against naive LLM baselines on long-horizon simulations and clinically sensitive events, assessing reasoning efficiency.

Result: EHRWorld significantly outperforms naive LLM baselines, achieving more stable long-horizon simulations, better modeling of clinically sensitive events, and favorable reasoning efficiency.

Conclusion: Training on causally grounded, temporally evolving clinical data is essential for reliable and robust medical world modeling; EHRWorld demonstrates the benefits of a causal sequential medical world model approach.

Abstract: World models offer a principled framework for simulating future states under interventions, but realizing such models in complex, high-stakes domains like medicine remains challenging. Recent large language models (LLMs) have achieved strong performance on static medical reasoning tasks, raising the question of whether they can function as dynamic medical world models capable of simulating disease progression and treatment outcomes over time. In this work, we show that LLMs only incorporating medical knowledge struggle to maintain consistent patient states under sequential interventions, leading to error accumulation in long-horizon clinical simulation. To address this limitation, we introduce EHRWorld, a patient-centric medical world model trained under a causal sequential paradigm, together with EHRWorld-110K, a large-scale longitudinal clinical dataset derived from real-world electronic health records. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that EHRWorld significantly outperforms naive LLM-based baselines, achieving more stable long-horizon simulation, improved modeling of clinically sensitive events, and favorable reasoning efficiency, highlighting the necessity of training on causally grounded, temporally evolving clinical data for reliable and robust medical world modeling.

</details>


### [455] [Can LLMs Do Rocket Science? Exploring the Limits of Complex Reasoning with GTOC 12](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03630)
*Iñaki del Campo,Pablo Cuervo,Victor Rodriguez-Fernandez,Roberto Armellin,Jack Yarndley*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs show improved strategic planning for an asteroid mining task in orbital mechanics but struggle with execution due to physics/implementation errors; LLMs act as domain facilitators rather than autonomous engineers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess limits of current AI agents in autonomous multi-stage planning in high-dimensional, physically constrained environments using the GTOC 12 asteroid mining challenge.

Method: Adapt the MLE-Bench framework to orbital mechanics; deploy an AIDE-based agent; use an LLM-as-a-Judge rubric evaluated by domain experts; compare models including GPT-4-Turbo, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and o3; analyze strategic viability and, critically, execution fidelity.

Result: Strategic viability scores have nearly doubled (9.3 to 17.2 out of 26) over two years across models; however, there is a persistent gap between strategy and execution, with failures in unit consistency, boundary conditions, and debugging loops.

Conclusion: LLMs provide strong domain knowledge and planning capabilities but remain limited by implementation barriers; they function as domain facilitators rather than fully autonomous engineers, highlighting the need for physics-aware verification and tighter integration between planning and execution.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable proficiency in code generation and general reasoning, yet their capacity for autonomous multi-stage planning in high-dimensional, physically constrained environments remains an open research question. This study investigates the limits of current AI agents by evaluating them against the 12th Global Trajectory Optimization Competition (GTOC 12), a complex astrodynamics challenge requiring the design of a large-scale asteroid mining campaign. We adapt the MLE-Bench framework to the domain of orbital mechanics and deploy an AIDE-based agent architecture to autonomously generate and refine mission solutions. To assess performance beyond binary validity, we employ an "LLM-as-a-Judge" methodology, utilizing a rubric developed by domain experts to evaluate strategic viability across five structural categories. A comparative analysis of models, ranging from GPT-4-Turbo to reasoning-enhanced architectures like Gemini 2.5 Pro, and o3, reveals a significant trend: the average strategic viability score has nearly doubled in the last two years (rising from 9.3 to 17.2 out of 26). However, we identify a critical capability gap between strategy and execution. While advanced models demonstrate sophisticated conceptual understanding, correctly framing objective functions and mission architectures, they consistently fail at implementation due to physical unit inconsistencies, boundary condition errors, and inefficient debugging loops. We conclude that, while current LLMs often demonstrate sufficient knowledge and intelligence to tackle space science tasks, they remain limited by an implementation barrier, functioning as powerful domain facilitators rather than fully autonomous engineers.

</details>


### [456] [Search-R2: Enhancing Search-Integrated Reasoning via Actor-Refiner Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03647)
*Bowei He,Minda Hu,Zenan Xu,Hongru Wang,Licheng Zong,Yankai Chen,Chen Ma,Xue Liu,Pluto Zhou,Irwin King*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Search-R2: an Actor-Refiner framework for search-integrated reasoning that uses a cut-and-regenerate Meta-Refiner and hybrid dense-reward signals to improve RL-based QA by addressing multi-scale credit assignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome sparse, trajectory-level rewards that obscure high-quality reasoning versus luck in search-enabled agents; improve data-efficient learning and reliability of search strategies.

Method: Actor generates initial reasoning; Meta-Refiner diagnoses and repairs flawed steps via cut-and-regenerate. Dense process reward based on information density of retrieved evidence; reward design couples outcome accuracy with evidence quality; formalization as a smoothed mixture policy; joint optimization.

Result: Outperforms strong RAG and RL baselines across model scales on general and multi-hop QA datasets; higher reasoning accuracy with minimal overhead.

Conclusion: Selective intervention in an Actor-Refiner loop provides strict gains over baselines; the hybrid reward and policy mixture enable better credit assignment for reasoning in search-augmented QA.

Abstract: Search-integrated reasoning enables language agents to transcend static parametric knowledge by actively querying external sources. However, training these agents via reinforcement learning is hindered by the multi-scale credit assignment problem: existing methods typically rely on sparse, trajectory-level rewards that fail to distinguish between high-quality reasoning and fortuitous guesses, leading to redundant or misleading search behaviors. To address this, we propose Search-R2, a novel Actor-Refiner collaboration framework that enhances reasoning through targeted intervention, with both components jointly optimized during training. Our approach decomposes the generation process into an Actor, which produces initial reasoning trajectories, and a Meta-Refiner, which selectively diagnoses and repairs flawed steps via a 'cut-and-regenerate' mechanism. To provide fine-grained supervision, we introduce a hybrid reward design that couples outcome correctness with a dense process reward quantifying the information density of retrieved evidence. Theoretically, we formalize the Actor-Refiner interaction as a smoothed mixture policy, proving that selective correction yields strict performance gains over strong baselines. Extensive experiments across various general and multi-hop QA datasets demonstrate that Search-R2 consistently outperforms strong RAG and RL-based baselines across model scales, achieving superior reasoning accuracy with minimal overhead.

</details>


### [457] [Mitigating Conversational Inertia in Multi-Turn Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03664)
*Yang Wan,Zheng Cao,Zhenhao Zhang,Zhengwen Zeng,Shuheng Shen,Changhua Meng,Linchao Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Context Preference Learning to mitigate conversational inertia in multiturn LLM agents by leveraging longer-context inertia to bias preferences toward low-inertia actions, with inference-time context management and validation across multiple environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Combat imitation bias and conversational inertia that arise when longer context environments amplify exploitation at the expense of exploration in few-shot LLM-driven agents.

Method: Perform attention analysis to define conversational inertia (diagonal attention to prior responses). Build environment-agnostic preference pairs from identical states with different context lengths. Propose Context Preference Learning (CPL) to calibrate model preferences toward low-inertia responses. Propose inference-time context management strategies to balance exploration and exploitation.

Result: Empirical evaluation across eight agentic environments and one deep research scenario shows reduced conversational inertia and improved performance when using CPL and context management.

Conclusion: Context-aware preference calibration can enable longer-context LLM agents to exploit feedback without becoming trapped by inertia, achieving better exploration-exploitation balance and performance.

Abstract: Large language models excel as few-shot learners when provided with appropriate demonstrations, yet this strength becomes problematic in multiturn agent scenarios, where LLMs erroneously mimic their own previous responses as few-shot examples. Through attention analysis, we identify conversational inertia, a phenomenon where models exhibit strong diagonal attention to previous responses, which is associated with imitation bias that constrains exploration. This reveals a tension when transforming few-shot LLMs into agents: longer context enriches environmental feedback for exploitation, yet also amplifies conversational inertia that undermines exploration. Our key insight is that for identical states, actions generated with longer contexts exhibit stronger inertia than those with shorter contexts, enabling construction of preference pairs without environment rewards. Based on this, we propose Context Preference Learning to calibrate model preferences to favor low-inertia responses over highinertia ones. We further provide context management strategies at inference time to balance exploration and exploitation. Experimental results across eight agentic environments and one deep research scenario validate that our framework reduces conversational inertia and achieves performance improvements.

</details>


### [458] [TodyComm: Task-Oriented Dynamic Communication for Multi-Round LLM-based Multi-Agent System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03688)
*Wenzhe Fan,Tommaso Tognoli,Henry Peng Zou,Chunyu Miao,Yibo Wang,Xinhua Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes TodyComm, a dynamic, task-driven communication topology that adapts every round in multi-round LLM-based multi-agent systems, achieving better task performance under varying adversaries and communication budgets while preserving scalability and token efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed communication topologies in multi-round LLM-based multi-agent systems fail to capture dynamic role changes, adversarial conditions, and bandwidth constraints. An adaptive topology can optimize collaboration per round to maximize task utility.

Method: Introduce TodyComm, a dynamic, behavior-driven topology algorithm optimized with policy gradient. At each round, the method constructs a topology by selecting communication links between agents based on current dynamics and budget, aiming to maximize task-specific utility.

Result: Experiments on five benchmarks show that TodyComm yields superior task effectiveness under dynamic adversary scenarios and communication budgets, while maintaining token efficiency and scalability compared to fixed-topology baselines.

Conclusion: Dynamic, per-round topology adaptation enhances multi-agent coordination in LLM-based systems, offering improved performance and efficiency in realistic settings with changing roles and resource constraints; future work could explore broader dynamics, efficiency, and robustness.

Abstract: Multi-round LLM-based multi-agent systems rely on effective communication structures to support collaboration across rounds. However, most existing methods employ a fixed communication topology during inference, which falls short in many realistic applications where the agents' roles may change \textit{across rounds} due to dynamic adversary, task progression, or time-varying constraints such as communication bandwidth. In this paper, we propose addressing this issue through TodyComm, a \textbf{t}ask-\textbf{o}riented \textbf{dy}namic \textbf{comm}unication algorithm. It produces behavior-driven collaboration topologies that adapt to the dynamics at each round, optimizing the utility for the task through policy gradient. Experiments on five benchmarks demonstrate that under both dynamic adversary and communications budgets, TodyComm delivers superior task effectiveness while retaining token efficiency and scalability.

</details>


### [459] [AOrchestra: Automating Sub-Agent Creation for Agentic Orchestration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03786)
*Jianhao Ruan,Zhihao Xu,Yiran Peng,Fashen Ren,Zhaoyang Yu,Xinbing Liang,Jinyu Xiang,Bang Liu,Chenglin Wu,Yuyu Luo,Jiayi Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A framework-agnostic agent abstraction and orchestrator that treats any agent as a 4-tuple (Instruction, Context, Tools, Model) to enable on-demand specialization; AOrchestra manages context, tools, and models and spawns executors dynamically, achieving Pareto-efficient trade-offs and improving benchmarks when paired with Gemini-3-Flash; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of dynamic, adaptable sub-agent abstractions in long-horizon, multi-turn task solving; enable plug-and-play, framework-agnostic composition of agents and reduce manual engineering.

Method: Define an agent as a compositional tuple (Instruction, Context, Tools, Model); develop an orchestrator that concretizes the tuple at each step by curating task-relevant context, selecting tools and models, and delegating execution via on-the-fly automatic agent creation.

Result: Achieves 16.28% relative improvement over the strongest baseline across GAIA, SWE-Bench, Terminal-Bench when paired with Gemini-3-Flash; code available at GitHub.

Conclusion: Provides a unified, framework-agnostic abstraction for sub-agents, enabling adaptive, cost-aware task solving with reduced human engineering; demonstrates empirical gains across challenging benchmarks.

Abstract: Language agents have shown strong promise for task automation. Realizing this promise for increasingly complex, long-horizon tasks has driven the rise of a sub-agent-as-tools paradigm for multi-turn task solving. However, existing designs still lack a dynamic abstraction view of sub-agents, thereby hurting adaptability. We address this challenge with a unified, framework-agnostic agent abstraction that models any agent as a tuple Instruction, Context, Tools, Model. This tuple acts as a compositional recipe for capabilities, enabling the system to spawn specialized executors for each task on demand. Building on this abstraction, we introduce an agentic system AOrchestra, where the central orchestrator concretizes the tuple at each step: it curates task-relevant context, selects tools and models, and delegates execution via on-the-fly automatic agent creation. Such designs enable reducing human engineering efforts, and remain framework-agnostic with plug-and-play support for diverse agents as task executors. It also enables a controllable performance-cost trade-off, allowing the system to approach Pareto-efficient. Across three challenging benchmarks (GAIA, SWE-Bench, Terminal-Bench), AOrchestra achieves 16.28% relative improvement against the strongest baseline when paired with Gemini-3-Flash. The code is available at: https://github.com/FoundationAgents/AOrchestra

</details>


### [460] [Understanding Agent Scaling in LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems via Diversity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03794)
*Yingxuan Yang,Chengrui Qu,Muning Wen,Laixi Shi,Ying Wen,Weinan Zhang,Adam Wierman,Shangding Gu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MAS performance is capped by intrinsic task uncertainty and effective channel count rather than agent count; heterogeneity yields substantial gains by providing complementary evidence; 2 diverse agents can match/exceed 16 homogeneous ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why simply increasing the number of homogeneous LLM agents yields diminishing returns and to uncover principled design principles for scalable MAS.

Method: Develop an information-theoretic framework linking performance to intrinsic uncertainty and an effective channel count K*, derive architecture-agnostic bounds, characterize the effects of homogeneity vs. heterogeneity, and validate empirically with diverse vs homogeneous agent configurations.

Result: The framework shows performance is bounded by intrinsic uncertainty and the number of effective channels. Homogeneous agents saturate due to high output correlation, while heterogeneous agents contribute complementary information. K* quantifies effective channels without ground-truth labels. Empirically, 2 diverse agents can match or exceed 16 homogeneous agents.

Conclusion: Diversity-aware MAS design yields more efficient and robust performance; use effective-channel count as a design objective; provide guidelines and release code/dataset for replication.

Abstract: LLM-based multi-agent systems (MAS) have emerged as a promising approach to tackle complex tasks that are difficult for individual LLMs. A natural strategy is to scale performance by increasing the number of agents; however, we find that such scaling exhibits strong diminishing returns in homogeneous settings, while introducing heterogeneity (e.g., different models, prompts, or tools) continues to yield substantial gains. This raises a fundamental question: what limits scaling, and why does diversity help? We present an information-theoretic framework showing that MAS performance is bounded by the intrinsic task uncertainty, not by agent count. We derive architecture-agnostic bounds demonstrating that improvements depend on how many effective channels the system accesses. Homogeneous agents saturate early because their outputs are strongly correlated, whereas heterogeneous agents contribute complementary evidence. We further introduce $K^*$, an effective channel count that quantifies the number of effective channels without ground-truth labels. Empirically, we show that heterogeneous configurations consistently outperform homogeneous scaling: 2 diverse agents can match or exceed the performance of 16 homogeneous agents. Our results provide principled guidelines for building efficient and robust MAS through diversity-aware design. Code and Dataset are available at the link: https://github.com/SafeRL-Lab/Agent-Scaling.

</details>


### [461] [Conformal Thinking: Risk Control for Reasoning on a Compute Budget](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03814)
*Xi Wang,Anushri Suresh,Alvin Zhang,Rishi More,William Jurayj,Benjamin Van Durme,Mehrdad Farajtabar,Daniel Khashabi,Eric Nalisnick*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A risk-control framework reframes adaptive reasoning budget setting for LLMs as stopping-time decision making, with an upper 'confidence' threshold and a parametric lower threshold to balance risk and compute, plus an efficiency_loss criterion for multiple budget criteria; empirically improves compute efficiency while meeting target risk.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Token-budgeted reasoning in LLMs trades accuracy for compute; setting budget/threshold is challenging; need a distribution-free method to guarantee risk constraints while minimizing cost.

Method: Introduce two stopping rules: an upper threshold that stops when the model is confident (risk of incorrect output) and a parametric lower threshold that stops unsolvable instances earlier; given target risk and a validation set, apply distribution-free risk control to determine thresholds; for multiple budget criteria, incorporate an efficiency_loss to select the most compute-efficient exiting mechanism; ensemble stopping mechanisms; empirical evaluation across tasks/models.

Result: Shows that risk-control stopping achieves desired risk while reducing compute; lower threshold and ensemble mechanisms provide efficiency gains; generalizable across diverse reasoning tasks and models.

Conclusion: Framing budget control as risk management enables predictable error-rate control and compute savings in adaptive reasoning, with a practical method for setting thresholds in a distribution-free way and extensibility to multiple budget criteria.

Abstract: Reasoning Large Language Models (LLMs) enable test-time scaling, with dataset-level accuracy improving as the token budget increases, motivating adaptive reasoning -- spending tokens when they improve reliability and stopping early when additional computation is unlikely to help. However, setting the token budget, as well as the threshold for adaptive reasoning, is a practical challenge that entails a fundamental risk-accuracy trade-off. We re-frame the budget setting problem as risk control, limiting the error rate while minimizing compute. Our framework introduces an upper threshold that stops reasoning when the model is confident (risking incorrect output) and a novel parametric lower threshold that preemptively stops unsolvable instances (risking premature stoppage). Given a target risk and a validation set, we use distribution-free risk control to optimally specify these stopping mechanisms. For scenarios with multiple budget controlling criteria, we incorporate an efficiency loss to select the most computationally efficient exiting mechanism. Empirical results across diverse reasoning tasks and models demonstrate the effectiveness of our risk control approach, demonstrating computational efficiency gains from the lower threshold and ensemble stopping mechanisms while adhering to the user-specified risk target.

</details>


### [462] [AutoFigure: Generating and Refining Publication-Ready Scientific Illustrations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03828)
*Minjun Zhu,Zhen Lin,Yixuan Weng,Panzhong Lu,Qiujie Xie,Yifan Wei,Sifan Liu,Qiyao Sun,Yue Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FigureBench: a new large-scale benchmark (3,300 pairs) for long-form scientific text-to-illustration generation; AutoFigure: an agentic framework that plans, recombines, and validates layouts to produce publication-ready scientific illustrations; strong performance vs baselines; code/data released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To remove the bottleneck of manually creating scientific illustrations by enabling automatic generation from long-form scientific text, improving structure, aesthetics, and fidelity.

Method: Construct FigureBench from diverse sources (papers, surveys, blogs, textbooks); define long-form text-to-illustration tasks; design AutoFigure that performs multi-step thinking, layout recombination, and validation before final rendering; benchmark AutoFigure against baselines; release code and data.

Result: AutoFigure consistently outperforms baselines, delivering publication-ready scientific illustrations; dataset and code are released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: FigureBench provides a large, diverse dataset and evaluation framework; AutoFigure introduces an agentic generation pipeline that achieves structurally complete and aesthetically refined figures, advancing automatic scientific illustration.

Abstract: High-quality scientific illustrations are crucial for effectively communicating complex scientific and technical concepts, yet their manual creation remains a well-recognized bottleneck in both academia and industry. We present FigureBench, the first large-scale benchmark for generating scientific illustrations from long-form scientific texts. It contains 3,300 high-quality scientific text-figure pairs, covering diverse text-to-illustration tasks from scientific papers, surveys, blogs, and textbooks. Moreover, we propose AutoFigure, the first agentic framework that automatically generates high-quality scientific illustrations based on long-form scientific text. Specifically, before rendering the final result, AutoFigure engages in extensive thinking, recombination, and validation to produce a layout that is both structurally sound and aesthetically refined, outputting a scientific illustration that achieves both structural completeness and aesthetic appeal. Leveraging the high-quality data from FigureBench, we conduct extensive experiments to test the performance of AutoFigure against various baseline methods. The results demonstrate that AutoFigure consistently surpasses all baseline methods, producing publication-ready scientific illustrations. The code, dataset and huggingface space are released in https://github.com/ResearAI/AutoFigure.

</details>
